topics.title: Definition of Turbo Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition of Turbo Machine # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Turbo Machine** is defined as a dynamic energy-transfer device in which energy is continuously exchanged between a flowing fluid (which may be a liquid or a gas) and a rotating member (the rotor). This energy transfer is governed strictly by the **principle of angular momentum** and the **Euler momentum equation**, distinguishing turbo machines from positive displacement machines where energy is added or extracted in discrete, trapped volumes. Mathematically, the defining operational signature of a turbo machine is given by the **Euler Turbomachinery Equation**, which expresses the specific work transfer between the rotor and the fluid: $$W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} \quad \text{(per unit mass of fluid)}$$ Where: - **$U$** = Tangential (blade) velocity of the rotor at inlet or outlet, expressed in **m/s** - **$V_w$** = Whirl (tangential) component of the absolute velocity of the fluid, expressed in **m/s** - Subscripts **1** and **2** denote the **inlet** and **outlet** stations respectively > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)** > The definition of a turbo machine, its distinction from a positive displacement machine, and the classification of turbo machines based on the direction of energy transfer (Turbines, Compressors, Pumps) form the **core CO1 (Course Outcome 1)** deliverable. Students must internalize that *fluid is the working medium* and *rotor is the energy-exchanging element*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a simple ceiling fan rotating in still air. The fan blades are angled; as they cut through the air, the rotor (motor + blades) does mechanical work on the air, accelerating it rearward and producing a pressure rise — a **fan (compressor)** in turbo-machine terms. Now reverse the process: imagine a stream of water striking a paddle wheel mounted on a shaft. The water surrenders its kinetic and pressure energy, the paddle wheel rotates, and useful shaft work is produced — this is a **water turbine**. In both cases, three fundamental signatures hold: - A **rotor (bladed wheel)** is present. - A **working fluid** flows continuously through the machine. - Energy transfer is **continuous and rotational**, not trapped-volume-based. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Significance:** Turbo machines dominate the global power-generation industry. Approximately **> 80%** of the world's electricity is generated by steam, gas, and hydraulic turbines. They are also ubiquitous in aviation (jet engines), petrochemicals (centrifugal compressors), and HVAC (chillers). The centrifugal pump is the second most common industrial machine after the electric motor. ## 1.3 Why "Turbo"? The prefix *turbo* originates from the Latin *turbo*, meaning *whirlwind* or *spinning*. A turbo machine is thus, etymologically and physically, a machine whose **primary mode of operation is the conversion of energy through rotational, fluid-mediated momentum transfer**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic Steps — How a Turbo Machine Works The working of *any* turbo machine — turbine, compressor, pump, or fan — can be broken down into a unified 5-step logical flow: 1. **Fluid Entry:** The working fluid enters the machine through a stationary casing (volute or scroll), often passing through guide vanes (nozzle ring) that impart a tangential whirl velocity $V_{w1}$. 2. **Momentum Exchange at the Rotor:** The fluid then flows over the rotating blades. There is a **relative motion** between the fluid and the blades. The fluid's momentum changes, and by Newton's third law, a reaction force is exerted on the blades. 3. **Rotor-Fluid Coupling (Angular Momentum):** The change in angular momentum of the fluid, $\Delta H = m(V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$, is equal and opposite to the torque acting on the rotor shaft. This is the **Euler principle of angular momentum**. 4. **Shaft Work Output or Input:** The torque, integrated over the angular velocity, gives the shaft power: - **Turbine:** Fluid loses energy → Rotor gains energy (shaft power **out**). - **Compressor/Pump:** Rotor loses energy (shaft power **in**) → Fluid gains energy (pressure rise). 5. **Fluid Exit:** The fluid leaves the rotor with a reduced whirl component, often passed through a diffuser to recover kinetic energy as pressure. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Definition - **Why continuous flow?** Because energy transfer in a turbo machine is governed by **dynamic forces** (momentum changes) acting on a continuous stream, rather than mechanical compression of a finite volume. - **Why angular momentum?** Because the rotor is a rotating body, and any force having a tangential component about the axis of rotation produces torque — the only useful mechanical output. - **Why distinction from positive displacement machines?** In a positive displacement machine (e.g., a reciprocating compressor), the fluid is trapped in a closed volume and mechanically compressed. The governing equation is **thermodynamic** (PV work), not **momentum-based**. This fundamental difference dictates the design, performance, and application of each class. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table contains **all foundational relations** a KTU student must memorize for Module 1: | # | Relation / Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$ | Euler's equation (work per unit mass, J/kg) | Energy transfer across a stage | | 2 | $P = \rho \, Q \, W$ | Shaft power (W); $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $Q$ = discharge (m³/s) | Power rating of turbine/compressor | | 3 | $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ | Specific speed (turbine, SI: $rpm, kW, m$) | Classification of turbines | | 4 | $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ | Specific speed (pump, SI: $rpm, m^3/s, m$) | Classification of pumps | | 5 | $D = U / \omega = U / (2\pi N/60)$ | Rotor diameter or blade velocity relation | Velocity triangle construction | | 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{isentropic}} \quad \text{or} \quad \eta = \dfrac{W_{isentropic}}{W_{actual}}$ | Efficiency (turbine vs compressor convention) | Performance evaluation | | 7 | $\tau = \rho \, Q \, (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$ | Torque on rotor shaft (N·m) | Torque transmission design | | 8 | $V_w = V \cos \alpha$, $\quad V_f = V \sin \alpha$ | Velocity triangle components ($\alpha$ = absolute flow angle) | Inlet/outlet triangle drawing | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Every commercial turbo machine is selected, scaled, and homologated using the **specific speed** ($N_s$). It is a dimensionless performance fingerprint that uniquely determines the geometry (radial, mixed, or axial) and the rpm class of the machine. A civil/mechanical engineer uses $N_s$ to choose between a Francis, Kaplan, or Pelton turbine for a given head and discharge condition. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a derivation here?** Even though the topic is "Definition", the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus **always pairs** the definition with a derivation of the Euler Turbomachinery equation from the angular momentum principle. Board questions (Dec 2022, Jun 2023) have explicitly asked: *"Derive the Euler equation for a turbo machine and explain its significance."* This is a guaranteed 7-mark question. ## 3.1 Derivation of the Euler Turbomachinery Equation ### Step 1: Statement of the Governing Principle We invoke the **Principle of Conservation of Angular Momentum** (also called the Moment-of-Momentum theorem) for a control volume enclosing the rotor. The net torque acting on the fluid by the rotor equals the *rate of change of angular momentum* of the fluid crossing the control surface. $$\tau = \dot{m} \left( r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1} \right)$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Torque exerted **by the rotor on the fluid** (N·m) - $\dot{m}$ = Mass flow rate of fluid through the machine (kg/s) - $r_1, r_2$ = Radii at the inlet and outlet of the rotor (m) ### Step 2: Convert Torque to Work The shaft work per unit time (power) is the torque multiplied by the angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s): $$P = \tau \cdot \omega = \dot{m} \cdot \omega \left( r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1} \right)$$ ### Step 3: Introduce the Tangential Blade Velocity By definition, the tangential velocity of the blade at any radius $r$ is $U = r \omega$. Therefore, $r_1 \omega = U_1$ and $r_2 \omega = U_2$. Substituting: $$P = \dot{m} \left( U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} \right)$$ ### Step 4: Normalize to Get Specific Work (Work per Unit Mass) Dividing both sides by the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$, we obtain the **specific work transfer** $W$ (J/kg): $$W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$ ### Step 5: Interpretation and Significance - If $W > 0$: The fluid gains energy from the rotor → the machine is a **pump or compressor**. - If $W < 0$: The fluid loses energy to the rotor → the machine is a **turbine**. - If $V_{w1} = 0$ (pure radial inlet, common in centrifugal pumps and impulse turbines): $W = U_2 V_{w2}$. This is the maximum work for a given $U_2$. ## 3.2 Velocity Triangle — The Analytical Matrix Every turbo machine is graphically analyzed using **velocity triangles** at the inlet and outlet. The three absolute vectors are: - $\vec{V}$ = Absolute velocity of the fluid (m/s) - $\vec{U}$ = Blade velocity (m/s) - $\vec{V_r}$ = Relative velocity of the fluid w.r.t. the blade (m/s) The fundamental kinematic closure is given by: $$\vec{V} = \vec{U} + \vec{V_r}$$ Decomposing along tangential ($w$) and flow ($f$) directions: $$V_w = V \cos \alpha, \quad V_f = V \sin \alpha$$ $$V_{rw} = V_r \cos \beta, \quad V_{rf} = V_r \sin \beta$$ Where: - $\alpha$ = Absolute flow angle (measured from the tangential direction) - $\beta$ = Relative flow angle (blade angle, measured from the tangential direction) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the velocity triangle with the **blade velocity $U$ horizontal** at the bottom, the **absolute velocity $V$** as the closing vector, and the **relative velocity $V_r$** opposite to the direction of rotation. Convention: subscript 1 for inlet, 2 for outlet. Negative work (turbine) is obtained when $U_2 V_{w2} < U_1 V_{w1}$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification of Turbo Machines (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram presents the formal KTU classification of turbo machines based on the **direction of energy transfer**, the **direction of fluid flow**, and the **type of working fluid**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Turbo Machine] --> B[Direction of Energy Transfer] A --> C[Direction of Fluid Flow] A --> D[Type of Working Fluid] B --> B1[Turbine
Fluid to Rotor] B --> B2[Compressor and Pump
Rotor to Fluid] B1 --> B1a[Steam Turbine] B1 --> B1b[Gas Turbine] B1 --> B1c[Hydraulic Turbine] B1 --> B1d[Wind Turbine] B2 --> B2a[Centrifugal Compressor] B2 --> B2b[Axial Compressor] B2 --> B2c[Centrifugal Pump] B2 --> B2d[Axial Flow Pump] C --> C1[Radial Flow
Centrifugal Type] C --> C2[Axial Flow] C --> C3[Mixed Flow] D --> D1[Incompressible
Liquids Pumps and Hydraulic Turbines] D --> D2[Compressible
Gases Compressors and Gas Turbines] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Energy Conversion in a Turbo Machine The following block diagram illustrates the energy-conversion pathway and the principal components involved in any turbo machine. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Inlet Casing
and Stator] --> A2[Guide Vane
Nozzle Ring] A2 --> A3[Rotor
Bladed Wheel] A3 --> A4[Diffuser
or Scroll Outlet] A4 --> A5[Outlet
to Downstream System] A3 -. Shaft Coupling .-> S[Shaft] S --> M[Motor or Generator
Power Source or Sink] E1[Pressure Energy] --> A1 E2[Kinetic Energy] --> A1 E3[Thermal Energy] --> A1 A5 --> E4[Pressure Energy Output] A5 --> E5[Kinetic Energy Output] A5 --> E6[Thermal Energy Output] S --> E7[Mechanical Shaft Power] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Block Diagram:** The horizontal flow path (A1 → A2 → A3 → A4 → A5) represents the *fluid path*, while the vertical dotted branch (A3 → S → M) represents the *shaft work pathway*. In a turbine, energy flows from the fluid to the shaft (M = Generator). In a pump/compressor, energy flows from the shaft (M = Motor) to the fluid. ## 4.3 Velocity Triangle — Schematic Reference Although Mermaid cannot precisely render the geometric velocity triangle, the following conceptual layout summarizes the inlet and outlet triangles used universally in Module 1 problem-solving: ``` INLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE (Station 1) OUTLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE (Station 2) (For a typical radial-flow turbine) V1 V2 |\ |\ | \ | \ | \ | \ | \ | \ | \ | \ |_____> U1 |_____\> U2 V_r1 V_r2 Angles: alpha1, beta1 Angles: alpha2, beta2 Components: Vw1 = V1 cos(alpha1) Components: Vw2 = V2 cos(alpha2) Vf1 = V1 sin(alpha1) Vf2 = V2 sin(alpha2) ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Define a turbo machine. How is it different from a positive displacement machine?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A turbo machine is a device in which energy transfer between a fluid and a rotating element (rotor) takes place continuously through the **principle of angular momentum**. The working fluid flows continuously through the rotor blading, and energy is added or extracted by dynamic action. A **positive displacement machine**, in contrast, traps a fixed mass of fluid in a closed volume and mechanically compresses or expands it. Energy transfer is governed by the **thermodynamic PV work principle** and is discrete rather than continuous. Key differentiators: (i) continuous vs. discrete flow, (ii) momentum-based vs. volume-based energy transfer, (iii) higher specific speed and smooth flow in turbo machines vs. pulsating flow in PD machines. ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`** **State the Euler equation for a turbo machine and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Euler equation for a turbo machine is: $$W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$ Where: - **$W$** = Specific work done per unit mass of fluid (J/kg) - **$U_1, U_2$** = Tangential blade velocity at inlet and outlet (m/s) - **$V_{w1}, V_{w2}$** = Whirl (tangential) components of the absolute fluid velocity at inlet and outlet (m/s) **Significance:** It is the governing equation of *all* turbo machines — turbines, compressors, and pumps. It quantifies the energy transfer per unit mass and dictates the design of velocity triangles, blade angles, and rotor geometry. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** Derive the Euler Turbomachinery equation from the principle of angular momentum. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A centrifugal pump handles water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) at a flow rate of $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. The inlet is radial ($V_{w1} = 0$), the outlet blade velocity is $U_2 = 25 \text{ m/s}$, and the outlet whirl component is $V_{w2} = 20 \text{ m/s}$. If the rotor diameter at outlet is $D_2 = 0.4 \text{ m}$ and rotational speed is $N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$, determine: (i) the specific work done, (ii) the shaft power, and (iii) the torque transmitted. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. The flow is steady and uniform at the inlet and outlet stations. 2. The fluid is treated as a continuum; compressibility effects are neglected (or treated as incompressible for pump case). 3. The control volume is fixed; the rotor exerts torque on the fluid (and vice versa) without mass accumulation inside. 4. The velocities considered are those at the mean radius of the blade row. **Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):** Apply the **angular momentum theorem** to a control volume enclosing the rotor: $$\tau = \dot{m} \left( r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1} \right)$$ Multiplying both sides by $\omega$ (angular velocity) to get power: $$P = \dot{m} \cdot \omega \left( r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1} \right)$$ Since $U = r \omega$, we substitute $r_1 \omega = U_1$ and $r_2 \omega = U_2$: $$P = \dot{m} \left( U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} \right)$$ Dividing by $\dot{m}$ gives the Euler equation: $$W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - $V_{w1} = 0$ (radial inlet) - $U_2 = 25 \text{ m/s}$ - $V_{w2} = 20 \text{ m/s}$ - $D_2 = 0.4 \text{ m}$ - $N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$ **Verification (sanity check):** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi \times 1200 / 60 = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$. The corresponding $U_2$ should be $\omega \times D_2 / 2 = 125.66 \times 0.2 = 25.13 \text{ m/s}$, which matches the given $U_2 = 25 \text{ m/s}$ (rounded). ✓ **(i) Specific Work Done:** $$W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} = (25)(20) - (U_1)(0) = 500 \text{ J/kg}$$ **(ii) Shaft Power:** First, compute the mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.05 = 50 \text{ kg/s}$$ Then: $$P = \dot{m} W = 50 \times 500 = 25{,}000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Torque Transmitted:** $$P = \tau \cdot \omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{P}{\omega} = \frac{25{,}000}{125.66} \approx 198.94 \text{ N·m}$$ **Final Answers:** - $W = 500 \text{ J/kg}$ - $P = 25 \text{ kW}$ - $\tau \approx 198.94 \text{ N·m}$ ### Question B (Alternative Choice) **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** Explain the general classification of turbo machines with neat sketches/flow diagrams. Discuss the role of specific speed in selecting the type of hydraulic turbine. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A radial-flow gas turbine has an inlet whirl velocity $V_{w1} = 150 \text{ m/s}$, outlet whirl velocity $V_{w2} = 50 \text{ m/s}$ (both opposite in sense to blade motion), inlet blade velocity $U_1 = 200 \text{ m/s}$, and outlet blade velocity $U_2 = 150 \text{ m/s}$. If the mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 10 \text{ kg/s}$, compute: (i) the specific work output, (ii) the shaft power developed, and (iii) comment on whether this is a turbine or compressor based on the sign of work. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) Turbo machines are classified along three primary axes: **1. By Direction of Energy Transfer:** - **Turbines:** Fluid gives energy to the rotor (work **out**). Examples: Pelton, Francis, Kaplan, Steam, Gas. - **Compressors/Pumps:** Rotor gives energy to the fluid (work **in**). Examples: Centrifugal pump, axial compressor. **2. By Direction of Fluid Flow:** - **Radial Flow (Centrifugal):** Fluid flows radially outward (compressors/pumps) or inward (turbines). Pressure rise is dominant. - **Axial Flow:** Fluid flows parallel to the axis. High flow rate, moderate pressure rise. - **Mixed Flow:** Combination of radial and axial components. **3. By Type of Working Fluid:** - **Incompressible:** Water, oils → Pumps, hydraulic turbines. - **Compressible:** Air, steam, combustion gases → Fans, compressors, gas/steam turbines. **Role of Specific Speed in Turbine Selection:** The specific speed $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ (SI units: rpm, kW, m) is a **dimensionless performance fingerprint** that uniquely determines the geometry of a hydraulic turbine for a given operating point. Standard KTU selection ranges: | Specific Speed $N_s$ (SI) | Recommended Turbine Type | |---|---| | 10 – 35 | Pelton (Impulse, single jet) | | 60 – 400 | Francis (Reaction, mixed flow) | | 400 – 1000 | Kaplan / Propeller (Axial flow) | Thus, a designer first computes the duty point (Head $H$ and Power $P$), then evaluates $N_s$, and the value directly reveals whether a Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan turbine is optimal. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $V_{w1} = 150 \text{ m/s}$, $V_{w2} = 50 \text{ m/s}$ (both opposite to blade motion, hence negative in sign convention) - $U_1 = 200 \text{ m/s}$, $U_2 = 150 \text{ m/s}$ - $\dot{m} = 10 \text{ kg/s}$ **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Velocities in the direction of blade motion are **positive**; those opposite to blade motion are **negative**. Therefore, $V_{w1} = -150 \text{ m/s}$ and $V_{w2} = -50 \text{ m/s}$. **(i) Specific Work Output:** $$W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} = (150)(-50) - (200)(-150)$$ $$W = -7500 - (-30000) = -7500 + 30000 = 22{,}500 \text{ J/kg}$$ **(ii) Shaft Power Developed:** $$P = \dot{m} W = 10 \times 22{,}500 = 225{,}000 \text{ W} = 225 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Turbine or Compressor?** The work is **positive** ($W = +22{,}500 \text{ J/kg}$), meaning the rotor **receives** energy from the fluid. This is the signature of a **turbine** — the fluid is the energy source, and the rotor delivers shaft power to a load (e.g., a generator). **Final Answers:** - $W = 22{,}500 \text{ J/kg}$ - $P = 225 \text{ kW}$ - The machine is a **turbine** (positive work delivered by the fluid to the rotor). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 1):** > 1. **Sign convention is the #1 mark-loser.** A student who ignores the direction of $V_w$ relative to $U$ and simply multiplies magnitudes will get the *wrong sign* and the *wrong interpretation* (turbine vs. compressor). Marks are deducted in a cascade: wrong sign → wrong answer → wrong machine identification → typically **4 to 5 marks lost** on a 7-mark sub-question. > 2. **Unit consistency is critical.** If $N$ is in rpm but $\omega$ is required, always use $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Mixing SI with CGS loses 1 mark. > 3. **Inlet assumption omission.** Students often write the Euler equation as $W = U_2 V_{w2}$ without stating that $V_{w1} = 0$ (radial inlet). This is treated as an *unstated assumption* and costs 1 mark under "clarity of derivation". > 4. **Confusing $V_w$ with $V$.** The Euler equation requires the *whirl* (tangential) component, **not** the absolute velocity. Using $V$ instead of $V_w$ is a frequent conceptual error. > 5. **Missing sketch of velocity triangle.** Board examiners expect at least a *neatly labelled* inlet or outlet velocity triangle for full marks on derivation questions. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember Use this high-density checklist for last-minute KTU revision of Module 1: - [x] A **turbo machine** is a *dynamic*, *rotary* energy-transfer device using a *continuous fluid stream* and governed by *angular momentum*. - [x] It is fundamentally **different** from a positive displacement machine, which uses *discrete trapped volumes* and *PV work*. - [x] The **Euler Turbomachinery Equation** $W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$ is the single most important equation in Module 1 — *memorize, derive, and apply*. - [x] Classification is threefold: **(i)** direction of energy transfer (Turbine vs. Pump/Compressor), **(ii)** direction of flow (Radial / Axial / Mixed), **(iii)** fluid type (Incompressible / Compressible). - [x] **Sign Convention:** Velocities in the direction of blade motion are **positive**; opposite directions are **negative**. A positive $W$ → Turbine (energy to rotor). A negative $W$ → Compressor/Pump (energy from rotor). - [x] **Specific Speed ($N_s$)** is the universal classifier for hydraulic turbines: low $N_s$ → Pelton; medium $N_s$ → Francis; high $N_s$ → Kaplan. - [x] **Velocity triangles** at inlet and outlet are *mandatory* in any derivation or numerical problem — they carry 1–2 marks each in board valuation. - [x] **Power = $\dot{m} W = \rho Q W$** and **Torque = $P / \omega$** are the two essential performance equations for any turbo machine. - [x] Always convert rotational speed: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ (rad/s) and $U = \pi D N / 60$ (m/s). - [x] Radial inlet ($V_{w1} = 0$) is the standard assumption for centrifugal pumps and impulse turbines — *state it explicitly* in your answer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Definition of Turbo Machine.** Proceed to the next sub-topic: *Euler's Equation and Velocity Triangles in detail* for full CO1 mastery.

topics.title: Classification: Axial Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification: Axial Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Classification of Turbomachines — Axial Flow

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Axial Flow Turbomachine (KTU Definition):** A turbomachine in which the working fluid moves **parallel to the axis of rotation** of the rotor. Energy transfer between the fluid and the rotor occurs essentially due to the change in **whirl component of the absolute velocity** of the fluid, while the axial (flow) component remains largely unchanged across a single stage.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme classification framework, turbomachines are first segregated by the **direction of fluid flow** through the rotor. Three principal classes emerge:

| Class | Fluid Path Geometry | KTU-Standard Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial Flow** | Fluid flows **parallel** to the rotation axis | Kaplan turbine, Francis runner (partial), Axial compressor, Propeller pump, Jet engine fan |
| **Radial (Centrifugal) Flow** | Fluid flows **radially outward** (or inward) | Centrifugal pump, Centrifugal compressor, Pelton wheel (mixed) |
| **Mixed Flow** | Combination — fluid enters axially & discharges radially (or vice-versa) | Mixed-flow pump, Francis turbine |

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis of Rotation Rule:** If you imagine a horizontal shaft, in an axial flow machine, the fluid enters at one end of the shaft and exits at the other end — always travelling along the shaft direction. The rotor blades are mounted on the cylindrical surface of the rotor and act on the fluid in a "swept-wing" manner.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ceiling Fan vs. The Water Wheel"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> - A **ceiling fan** is an *axial flow* device: air moves *along* the axis of the spinning blades (you feel a breeze blowing straight down).
> - A **water wheel** (old mill wheel) is a *radial flow* device: water strikes the blades from the side and is flung *outward* (away from the axis).
> - A **ceiling fan pushing air through a long horizontal duct** is the closest match to an axial flow compressor — air keeps moving in the same axial direction, but the spinning blades keep adding pressure (or extracting energy) to it at every stage.

### 1.3 Why "Axial Flow" is a Distinct Class

In axial flow machines, three structural facts hold:

1. **Inlet and outlet of the rotor lie on the same axis.** The flow path is essentially cylindrical.
2. **The flow area is annular** (a ring between hub and casing), not circular like in radial machines.
3. **The fluid has both axial ($V_f$) and tangential ($V_\theta$) velocity components**, even though the *net* flow is axial.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics (Highlighted)

The following metrics define an axial flow machine in KTU problems:

- **Mean Blade Diameter ($D_m$):** The arithmetic mean of the hub and tip diameters — usually where velocity triangles are drawn.
- **Blade Velocity ($U$):** $U = \dfrac{\pi D_m N}{60}$ in **m/s** (where $N$ is in rpm).
- **Degree of Reaction ($R$):** Fraction of stage enthalpy drop occurring in the rotor; for a **symmetric (50% reaction) axial stage, $R = 0.5$**.
- **Flow Coefficient ($\phi$):** $\phi = \dfrac{V_f}{U}$ — a **dimensionless** design parameter.
- **Work Coefficient ($\psi$):** $\psi = \dfrac{W}{U^2}$ — **dimensionless**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** In all KTU axial-flow numericals, you will draw a **velocity triangle at the mean diameter ($D_m$)** and apply **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation** to find work/head. Memorize the velocity triangle cold — it is the single most-examined diagram in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classification Logic (KTU 2024 Hierarchy)

Turbomachines are classified along **three independent axes** under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Axial flow belongs to the *direction-of-flow* axis:

```
TURBOMACHINES
 ├── By Direction of Flow  →  AXIAL / RADIAL / MIXED
 ├── By Energy Transfer    →  TURBINE (Fluid → Rotor) / COMPRESSOR/PUMP (Rotor → Fluid)
 └── By Working Fluid      →  GAS / STEAM / HYDRAULIC (WATER)
```

An *axial flow machine* can therefore be:
- **Axial flow turbine** (e.g., Kaplan, multi-stage steam turbine, wind turbine)
- **Axial flow compressor** (e.g., jet engine compressor)
- **Axial flow pump** (e.g., propeller pump, mixed-flow pump with high specific speed)

## 2.2 The Velocity Triangle — The Heart of Axial Flow Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the velocity triangle matters:** In an axial flow rotor, the blade is moving at velocity $U$ and the fluid relative to the blade is moving at $V_r$. The vector sum $V = V_r + U$ gives the absolute velocity. The **change in $V_\theta$** across the rotor produces the work transfer (Euler's equation).

### The Three Velocities

| Symbol | Name | Vector Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | **Absolute Velocity** | Velocity of fluid relative to the *stationary* casing |
| $U$ | **Blade (Peripheral) Velocity** | Velocity of the blade at the chosen radius ($U = \pi D N / 60$) |
| $V_r$ | **Relative Velocity** | Velocity of fluid as seen by an observer *moving with the blade* |

### The Three Angles (measured from the axial / flow direction)

| Symbol | Name | Defined at |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | **Absolute Flow Angle** | Angle between $V$ and $V_f$ (axial direction) |
| $\beta$ | **Relative Flow Angle** | Angle between $V_r$ and $V_f$ (axial direction) |
| $\gamma$ | **Blade Angle (Camber)** | Geometric angle stamped on the blade profile |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **flow angles** (where the fluid actually goes), while $\gamma$ is the **blade metal angle** (where the blade surface sits). They are equal **only if there is no incidence loss** — a key assumption in KTU textbook problems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for 2024 Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the table below as your **one-stop reference** for every axial flow problem.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Blade velocity | $U = \dfrac{\pi D_m N}{60}$ | m/s | Mean-radius analysis |
| 2 | Axial velocity component | $V_f = V \cos\alpha = V_r \cos\beta$ | m/s | Continuity equation $V_f = \dot{m}/(\rho A)$ |
| 3 | Tangential (whirl) component of $V$ | $V_\theta = V \sin\alpha$ | m/s | Work transfer via Euler |
| 4 | Tangential component of $V_r$ | $V_{r\theta} = V_r \sin\beta$ | m/s | Closing the velocity triangle |
| 5 | Relative velocity magnitude | $V_r = \sqrt{V_f^2 + (V_\theta - U)^2}$ | m/s | Inlet triangle (compressor-like) |
| 5a | Relative velocity magnitude (turbine sign) | $V_r = \sqrt{V_f^2 + (V_\theta + U)^2}$ | m/s | Inlet triangle (turbine-like) |
| 6 | Absolute velocity magnitude | $V = \sqrt{V_f^2 + V_\theta^2}$ | m/s | Outlet triangle |
| 7 | **Euler's Work (per unit mass)** | $w = U(V_{\theta 1} \pm V_{\theta 2})$ | J/kg | Stage work done |
| 8 | Specific work for turbine stage | $w_t = U(V_{\theta 1} + V_{\theta 2})$ | J/kg | Power $= \dot{m} w_t$ |
| 9 | Specific work for compressor stage | $w_c = U(V_{\theta 1} - V_{\theta 2})$ | J/kg | Power input |
| 10 | Degree of reaction | $R = \dfrac{h_{rotor}}{h_{stage}} = \dfrac{V_f^2 (\tan\beta_1 - \tan\beta_2)}{2U(V_{\theta 1} + V_{\theta 2})}$ | — | Symmetric stage: $R=0.5$ |
| 11 | Flow coefficient | $\phi = V_f / U$ | — | KTU typical: 0.4–0.7 |
| 12 | Work coefficient | $\psi = w / U^2$ | — | KTU typical: 1.0–3.0 |
| 13 | Loading coefficient | $\psi_s = 2 \psi$ | — | Single-stage loading limit |
| 14 | Specific speed (pump/turbine) | $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ (pump) | — | Identifies axial vs radial |
| 15 | Stage head (turbine) | $H = w \cdot g^{-1}$ | m | For Kaplan, Propeller |
| 16 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V_f = \rho \pi (D_t^2 - D_h^2) V_f / 4$ | kg/s | Continuity |
| 17 | Stage pressure ratio (compressor) | $r_s = (1 + \eta \psi U^2 / (c_p T))^{c_p/(c_p - 1)}$ | — | Axial compressor |
| 18 | Solidity (blade count) | $\sigma = c / s$ | — | Blade design parameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** In KTU answer scripts, students routinely lose 1–2 marks by mixing the sign in Euler's equation. **Rule of thumb:**
> - If $V_\theta$ and $U$ point in the **same direction** at a station → add.
> - If opposite → subtract.
> - A **turbine** and a **compressor** use **opposite sign conventions** in the same geometry.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Axial Flow Device | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Aviation (turbojet, turbofan) | Axial compressor + Axial turbine | Compress air / extract exhaust energy |
| Hydroelectric (low-head) | Kaplan / Propeller turbine | High flow, low head power |
| Marine propulsion | Variable-pitch propeller | Thrust generation |
| Power generation | Multi-stage steam turbine (last stages) | Bulk power output |
| Wind energy | Horizontal-axis wind turbine | Kinetic energy capture |
| Process gas (LNG, petrochem) | Axial flow compressor | High-volume gas transport |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Triangle Relations from First Principles

We begin with a fluid particle at the mean diameter $D_m$ of an axial rotor. Three reference frames are involved:

- **Stationary frame** (casing): absolute velocity $V$
- **Rotating frame** (blade): relative velocity $V_r$
- **Blade itself**: blade velocity $U$

### Step 1 — Vector Definition

$$
\vec{V} = \vec{V_r} + \vec{U}
$$

This is the fundamental vector relation. In an *axial* machine, $U$ is purely tangential (circumferential), and $V_f$ is purely axial.

### Step 2 — Resolve Along the Axial Direction

The axial (flow) direction is perpendicular to $U$. Therefore only $V$ and $V_r$ have axial components:

$$
V_f = V \cos\alpha = V_r \cos\beta
$$

**Reasoning:** Both $V$ and $V_r$ are inclined to the axial direction at angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$ respectively. Projecting each onto the axial direction yields the equations above.

### Step 3 — Resolve Along the Tangential Direction

In the tangential direction, all three vectors contribute (with sign). For a typical axial *inlet triangle* of a turbine (where $V_\theta$ and $U$ are co-directed at outlet, opposite at inlet):

$$
V_\theta = V \sin\alpha
$$

$$
V_{r\theta} = V_r \sin\beta
$$

The vector closure in the tangential direction gives (using $\vec{V} = \vec{V_r} + \vec{U}$):

$$
U = V_\theta \pm V_{r\theta}
$$

**Reasoning:** The + sign is used when $V_{r\theta}$ is in the *opposite* sense to $U$ (typical axial turbine inlet), and − sign is used when $V_{r\theta}$ is in the *same* sense as $U$ (typical axial compressor inlet). The convention adopted in KTU textbooks is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inlet (turbine):}\quad U &= V_{\theta 1} + V_{r\theta 1} \\
\text{Outlet (turbine):}\quad U &= V_{\theta 2} + V_{r\theta 2} \quad (\text{or minus, depending on $\alpha_2$ sign}) 
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Magnitude of Relative Velocity

From Step 1, using the resolved components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r^2 &= V_{r\theta}^2 + V_f^2 \\
\Rightarrow V_r^2 &= (V_\theta - U)^2 + V_f^2 \quad\text{(compressor inlet)} \\
\Rightarrow V_r^2 &= (V_\theta + U)^2 + V_f^2 \quad\text{(turbine inlet)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Magnitude of Absolute Velocity

$$
V^2 = V_f^2 + V_\theta^2
$$

This always holds, by definition of $V_f$ and $V_\theta$ being orthogonal components.

## 3.2 Derivation: Euler's Turbomachinery Equation for Axial Flow

Starting from the **angular momentum principle** for a control volume that encloses the rotor:

$$
\dot{W}_{shaft} = \dot{m}\left( V_{\theta 1} r_1 - V_{\theta 2} r_2 \right)\omega
$$

In an *axial flow* machine, **inlet and outlet radii are equal** at the mean diameter: $r_1 = r_2 = D_m/2$. Also, $\omega r = U$. So:

$$
\dot{W}_{shaft} = \dot{m} \, U \, (V_{\theta 1} - V_{\theta 2})
$$

Per unit mass:

$$
w = U (V_{\theta 1} - V_{\theta 2})
$$

**Sign Convention for KTU:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
w_{turbine} &= U (V_{\theta 1} + V_{\theta 2}) \\
w_{compressor} &= U (V_{\theta 1} - V_{\theta 2})
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign difference arises because the blade exit whirl in a turbine usually *aids* the work extraction (same rotational sense as $U$), while in a compressor the exit whirl *opposes* $U$ to give a positive input.

## 3.3 Derivation: Degree of Reaction for a Symmetric Axial Stage

The stage is *symmetric* if inlet and outlet triangles are mirror images, i.e. $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2$ and $\beta_1 = \beta_2$. For such a stage, it can be shown that:

$$
V_{\theta 1} = V_{\theta 2}, \quad V_{r1} = V_{r2}
$$

The total enthalpy drop in the stage (stator + rotor):

$$
\Delta h_0 = U(V_{\theta 1} + V_{\theta 2}) = 2 U V_\theta
$$

The rotor alone (using rothalpy) gives:

$$
\Delta h_{rotor} = \frac{V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2}{2} + \frac{V_{\theta 1}^2 - V_{\theta 2}^2}{2} = \frac{V_f^2 (\tan\beta_1 - \tan\beta_2)}{2U \tan\alpha_1}
$$

For the symmetric stage, $\tan\beta_1 = \tan\beta_2$ would imply zero — so the *true* 50% reaction stage uses $\beta_2 = -\beta_1$ (i.e., relative angles are symmetric about the axial direction). This yields:

$$
R = \frac{\Delta h_{rotor}}{\Delta h_{stage}} = 0.5
$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{symmetric axial stage}} = 0.5}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An axial flow turbine stage has $D_m = 1.0$ m, $N = 3000$ rpm, $\alpha_1 = 20°$, $\alpha_2 = 30°$, $\beta_2 = 60°$, $V_{f1} = V_{f2} = 150$ m/s. Find: (a) $U$, (b) work done per unit mass, (c) degree of reaction.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Blade velocity:**

$$
U = \frac{\pi D_m N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 3000}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step (b) — Find $V_{\theta 1}$, $V_{\theta 2}$:**

$$
V_{\theta 1} = V_{f1} \tan\alpha_1 = 150 \times \tan 20° = 150 \times 0.3640 = 54.60 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{\theta 2} = V_{f2} \tan\alpha_2 = 150 \times \tan 30° = 150 \times 0.5774 = 86.60 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step (b cont.) — Euler work:**

$$
w = U(V_{\theta 1} + V_{\theta 2}) = 157.08 \times (54.60 + 86.60) = 157.08 \times 141.20
$$

$$
\boxed{w \approx 22{,}180 \text{ J/kg} = 22.18 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

**Step (c) — Degree of Reaction:**

$$
\tan\beta_1 = \frac{V_{\theta 1} + U}{V_{f1}} = \frac{54.60 + 157.08}{150} = \frac{211.68}{150} = 1.4112
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \beta_1 = \arctan(1.4112) \approx 54.66°
$$

$$
R = \frac{V_f (\tan\beta_1 - \tan\beta_2)}{2 U (V_{\theta 1} + V_{\theta 2})} = \frac{150(1.4112 - 1.7321)}{2 \times 157.08 \times 141.20}
$$

$$
R = \frac{150 \times (-0.3209)}{44{,}359} = \frac{-48.13}{44{,}359} \approx -0.00109
$$

The negative sign indicates the *stator* does slightly more enthalpy drop than the rotor — a *low-reaction* axial stage.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Turbomachines (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[TURBOMACHINES] --> B1[By Direction of Flow]
    A0 --> B2[By Energy Transfer]
    A0 --> B3[By Working Fluid]

    B1 --> C1[Axial Flow]
    B1 --> C2[Radial Flow]
    B1 --> C3[Mixed Flow]

    C1 --> D1[Axial Turbine]
    C1 --> D2[Axial Compressor]
    C1 --> D3[Axial Pump]

    D1 --> E1[Kaplan Turbine]
    D1 --> E2[Steam Turbine LP Stages]
    D1 --> E3[Wind Turbine]

    D2 --> F1[Jet Engine Compressor]
    D2 --> F2[Multi-Stage Process Compressor]

    D3 --> G1[Propeller Pump]
    D3 --> G2[Kaplan Pump]

    B2 --> H1[Turbines]
    B2 --> H2[Compressors]
    B2 --> H3[Pumps]

    B3 --> I1[Gas Turbomachines]
    B3 --> I2[Steam Turbomachines]
    B3 --> I3[Hydraulic Turbomachines]
```

## 4.2 Axial Flow Stage — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    inlet1[Fluid Inlet] --> st1[NOZZLE STATOR]
    st1 -- increases Vθ --> rot1[ROTOR]
    rot1 -- work transfer --> st2[GUIDE STATOR]
    st2 -- redirects flow --> rot2[ROTOR 2]
    rot2 --> out1[Fluid Outlet]
    shaft[Drive Shaft] -. couples .-> rot1
    shaft -. couples .-> rot2
    out1 --> workout[Useful Work / Pressure Rise]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The stator (nozzle) accelerates the fluid and creates whirl ($V_\theta$). The rotor extracts energy (turbine) or adds energy (compressor). In a multi-stage axial machine, this stator-rotor pair repeats. The drive shaft is the only mechanical port.

## 4.3 Velocity Triangle Block Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Vvec[ABSOLUTE VELOCITY V] --> Vcomp1[Axial Component Vf]
    Vvec --> Vcomp2[Tangential Component Vθ]

    Urvec[RELATIVE VELOCITY Vr] --> Ucomp1[Axial Component Vf]
    Urvec --> Ucomp2[Tangential Component Vrθ]

    Uvec[BLADE VELOCITY U] --> Ucomp2

    Vcomp1 --- Ucomp1
    Vcomp2 --- Ucomp2

    angleA[Angle alpha] -. measured .-> Vvec
    angleB[Angle beta] -. measured .-> Urvec
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to draw for the exam (KTU style):**
> 1. Draw a horizontal line for the **axial direction** ($V_f$).
> 2. From a common origin, draw $U$ tangential (vertical if $V_f$ is horizontal).
> 3. From the tip of $U$, draw $V_r$ at angle $\beta$ to the axial line.
> 4. From the same origin, draw $V$ at angle $\alpha$ to the axial line — it must close the triangle: $V = V_r + U$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 1]`

**Classify turbomachines based on the direction of fluid flow. Give two examples of axial flow machines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Based on the direction of fluid flow through the rotor, turbomachines are classified as:

1. **Axial flow machines** — fluid flows parallel to the axis of rotation.
2. **Radial flow machines** — fluid flows perpendicular to the axis of rotation.
3. **Mixed flow machines** — fluid has components both axial and radial.

**Two examples of axial flow machines (1 mark each):**

- **Kaplan turbine** (low-head hydroelectric axial flow turbine)
- **Axial flow compressor** (used in jet engines)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often give a *radial* example (e.g., centrifugal pump) under "axial" — that is an instant **0 marks** for the example. Memorize the axial class examples explicitly.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 1]`

**Draw the velocity triangle at the inlet of an axial flow turbine and label all components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Diagram (textual description for script):**
- Draw a horizontal line representing the axial direction ($V_f$).
- From point O, draw vector $V$ at angle $\alpha_1$ to the axial line.
- From point O, draw vector $U$ tangential (perpendicular to $V_f$).
- From the tip of vector $U$, draw vector $V_r$ such that it meets the tip of $V$ — this closes the triangle.

**Labeled components (1 mark each):**
- $V_f$ — axial (flow) velocity component
- $U$ — blade (peripheral) velocity
- $V_r$ — relative velocity of fluid w.r.t. blade
- $\alpha_1$ — absolute flow angle
- $\beta_1$ — relative flow angle

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is drawing $V_r$ *outside* the triangle (i.e., not closing the vector equation $V = V_r + U$). The vector sum must form a closed triangle. Deduct **1 mark** if the closure is missing.
