Therefore, the energy transfer occurs purely by a **change in the velocity vector (momentum change) of the jet** — which is the textbook definition of *impulse*. **How does the energy transformation proceed?** The full thermodynamic / fluid-mechanical chain in a Pelton wheel installation is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Reservoir (PE)} &\xrightarrow{\text{Penstock (PE → KE)}} \text{Nozzle (PE → KE of jet)} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Bucket impact (KE → Mechanical Work)}} \text{Runner (Mechanical)} \end{aligned} $$ **Operational Sub-Steps:** 1. **Water Storage:** Water stored at a high-head reservoir possesses potential energy = $\rho g Q H$. 2. **Penstock Conveyance:** Water travels down a long penstock (pipe), converting *most* of its pressure head into kinetic head, minus small frictional losses $h_f$. 3. **Nozzle Conversion:** At the nozzle, the remaining head $H - h_f$ is **fully converted** to a high-velocity jet with velocity $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH_n}$, where $C_v$ is the nozzle velocity coefficient (typically **0.96 – 0.99**). 4. **Jet Impact:** The jet strikes the splitter ridge of the bucket and is divided into **two equal half-jets**. 5. **Bucket Deflection:** Each half-jet flows along the inner curved surface and is deflected through approximately **165° – 170°**, exiting almost back along its original path but shifted sideways. 6. **Momentum Change → Torque:** Because the jet's linear momentum changes direction, by Newton's second law an equal and opposite impulse acts on the bucket, producing a tangential force on the runner. 7. **Energy Rejection:** The spent water falls into a tailrace below. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).** | # | Quantity | Formula | Standard Value / Range | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Jet Velocity from Nozzle | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH_n}$ | $C_v = 0.96$ to $0.99$ | m/s | | 2 | Absolute Velocity of Whirl (inlet) | $V_{w1} = V_1$ (jet axial → becomes whirl at entry) | — | m/s | | 3 | Bucket (Blade) Velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | — | m/s | | 4 | Jet Velocity Ratio | $K = \dfrac{u}{V_1}$ | **Optimum = 0.45 to 0.46** | dimensionless | | 5 | Optimal Bucket Speed | $u = 0.45 \, V_1$ | Derived from Euler equation | m/s | | 6 | Wheel Peripheral Speed | $u = 0.45 \sqrt{2gH}$ | — | m/s | | 7 | Relative Velocity at Inlet | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u = V_1(1 - K)$ | — | m/s | | 8 | Relative Velocity at Outlet | $V_{r2} = K_r V_{r1}$ | $K_r = 0.80$ to $0.85$ (friction) | m/s | | 9 | Whirl Velocity at Outlet | $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta$ | $\beta \approx 10°$ (approx 0°) | m/s | | 10 | Work Done per kg | $W = u (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$ | — | J/kg or Nm/kg | | 11 | Specific Speed (metric) | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | **10 to 35 for Pelton** | — | | 12 | Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1+K\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$ | Max ≈ 0.985 (ideal) | — | | 13 | Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{(1+K\cos\beta)^2}{2}$ | At $K = 1$ | — | | 14 | Bucket Mean Diameter | $D = \dfrac{d_j + d_b}{0.5}$ (geometric relation) | — | m | | 15 | Number of Buckets | $n_b \geq 15 + \dfrac{D/d_j}{2}$ | Empirical (Bleich) | — | | 16 | Jet Diameter (Volumetric) | $d_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi C_v \sqrt{2gH_n} \cdot n}}$ | — | m | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Anti-Crash Rule:** KTU repeatedly tests the *optimum velocity ratio condition*. At $K = \dfrac{u}{V_1} = \dfrac{1}{2(1 + \cos\beta)} \approx 0.45$ (for small $\beta$), the hydraulic efficiency reaches its theoretical maximum. Memorize this **0.45** value! ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Pelton wheels drive **hydroelectric generators** in mountainous regions worldwide. Famous installations include: - **Bhatgar Dam (India):** Single-jet, vertical-shaft Pelton units rated ~25 MW each. - **Snowy Mountains Hydro Scheme (Australia):** Multi-jet Pelton units. - **Alpine European Plants (Switzerland, Norway):** 1000+ m head Pelton installations achieving > 92% runner efficiency. Their adoption is favored where the **head is exceptionally high but the discharge is moderate** — i.e., where mountain streams can be dammed at elevations of several hundred metres. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimum Velocity Ratio $K = u/V_1$ We start from the **Euler Turbomachinery Equation** applied between bucket inlet (1) and outlet (2): $$ W = u \, (V_{w1} - V_{w2}) \quad \text{(per kg of water per second)} $$ ### Step 1: Construct the Inlet Velocity Triangle Since the jet strikes the bucket tangentially, the **absolute velocity of water entering the bucket has no radial component**. Therefore: $$ V_{w1} = V_1 $$ The relative velocity at inlet, by vector subtraction: $$ V_{r1} = V_{w1} - u = V_1 - u $$ ### Step 2: Apply the Bucket Deflection Geometry The buckets are symmetrical double-cups. The relative jet enters and leaves with the *same magnitude* if the bucket were frictionless. Accounting for friction: $$ V_{r2} = K_r \, V_{r1} $$ where $K_r$ is the bucket friction coefficient (typically 0.80 to 0.85). The outlet whirl velocity is: $$ V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta $$ with $\beta$ being the bucket exit angle measured relative to the tangential direction (small, ~10°). For ideal frictionless flow with $\beta \to 0$: $$ V_{w2} = u - V_{r2} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify Work per kg becomes: $$ W = u\left[V_1 - (u - V_{r2})\right] = u(V_1 - u) + u V_{r2} $$ Substituting $V_{r2} = K_r(V_1 - u)$ and assuming $K_r = 1$, $K\cos\beta = 1$ (ideal bucket): $$ W = u(V_1 - u) + u(V_1 - u) = 2u(V_1 - u) $$ ### Step 4: Express as Efficiency Hydraulic efficiency $\eta_h$ is work done divided by input kinetic energy of the jet: $$ \eta_h = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)}{\frac{1}{2}V_1^2} = \frac{4u(V_1 - u)}{V_1^2} $$ ### Step 5: Optimize with respect to $u$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_1^2$ and letting $K = u/V_1$: $$ \eta_h = 4K(1 - K) $$ Differentiate with respect to $K$ and equate to zero: $$ \frac{d\eta_h}{dK} = 4(1 - 2K) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 $$ ### Step 6: Refine for Real Bucket Geometry For a real bucket with angle $\beta$, the same procedure yields: $$ K_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1}{2(1 + K\cos\beta)} \approx \frac{1}{2} \approx 0.45 \text{ to } 0.46 $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** The 0.45 value is derived assuming $K\cos\beta \approx 0.11$, giving the classical textbook optimum. Always state this in your derivations. ## 3.2 Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency Derivation Substituting $K = 1/(2(1+K\cos\beta))$ back into $\eta_h = \frac{2u(V_1-u)(1+K\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$ yields: $$ \eta_{h,\max} = \frac{(1 + K\cos\beta)^2}{2} $$ For an ideal bucket ($K_r = 1$, $\beta = 0$): $$ \eta_{h,\max} = \frac{(1+1)^2}{2} = 2.0 $$ This is **mathematically impossible as a fractional efficiency** — confirming that in real design with friction and finite bucket angle, $\eta_{h,\max}$ falls below 1.0. The numerical insight is that $K\cos\beta$ must be less than $\sqrt{2} - 1$ for physical realizability. ## 3.3 Derivation of Bucket Count Formula To ensure **continuous coverage** of the jet by buckets, no jet stream should pass between buckets without striking a cup. The empirical correlation (Bleich / KTU reference) is: $$ n_b \geq 15 + \frac{D/d_j}{2} $$ **Derivation Logic:** A bucket spans an arc length $\approx d_j + (\text{bucket thickness})$. The wheel circumference is $\pi D$. Dividing these yields the minimum number needed so that the arc between two successive buckets is always less than $d_j$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pelton Wheel — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reservoir
High Head H] B[Penstock
Pipe with Head Loss hf] C[Needle Valve
Flow Regulation] D[Nozzle
Pressure to Kinetic Energy] E[High-Velocity Jet
Velocity V1] F[Bucket Splitter
Divides Jet into Two Halves] G[Bucket Curved Cups
Deflection 165 to 170 deg] H[Runner Disc
Rotates with Speed u] I[Deflected Jet Exits
Low Velocity V2] J[Tailrace
Waste Water Discharge] K[Shaft Output
Mechanical Power] L[Generator
Electrical Power] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J H --> K K --> L style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style D fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000 style H fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000 style L fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#be185d,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Classification Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pelton Wheel
Impulse Turbine] A --> B1[By Shaft Axis] A --> B2[By Number of Jets] A --> B3[By Specific Speed] A --> B4[By Construction] B1 --> B1a[Horizontal Shaft
Low/Medium Head] B1 --> B1b[Vertical Shaft
Very High Head] B2 --> B2a[Single Jet
Ns = 10 to 20] B2 --> B2b[Multi Jet 2 to 6
Ns = 20 to 35] B3 --> B3a[Low Ns
Single Jet] B3 --> B3b[Higher Ns
Multi Jet] B4 --> B4a[Single Stage
Most Common] B4 --> B4b[Multi Stage
Very Rare] style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000 style B2a fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000 style B2b fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Inlet and Outlet Velocity Triangles (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TRI1[Inlet Velocity Triangle] V1[V1 Jet Absolute Velocity] U1[u Bucket Velocity] VR1[Vr1 Relative Velocity at Inlet] V1 --- VR1 V1 --- U1 U1 --- VR1 end subgraph TRI2[Outlet Velocity Triangle] U2[u Bucket Velocity] VR2[Vr2 Relative Velocity at Outlet] VW2[Vw2 Whirl Component at Outlet] U2 --- VR2 VR2 --- VW2 U2 --- VW2 end TRI1 --> TRI2 style TRI1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style TRI2 fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#be185d,color:#000000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Triangle (KTU Board Pattern):** At the inlet, $V_1$, $u$, and $V_{r1}$ form a closed triangle since $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$. At the outlet, the relative velocity is deflected through angle $(180° - \beta)$ from the direction of $u$ — making $V_{w2}$ small (ideally zero in a perfect bucket). This is the geometric origin of the famous "165° deflection". ## 4.4 Components of a Pelton Wheel Assembly ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Nozzle Assembly] A2[Needle Valve Stem] A3[Deflector Plate] A4[Spear / Needle Tip] A5[Buckets on Runner] A6[Runner Disc] A7[Brake Jet] A8[Casing / Housing] A9[Guide Bearing] A1 --> A4 A2 --> A4 A3 --> A1 A5 --> A6 A6 --> A9 A8 --> A6 A7 --> A6 style A4 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000 style A5 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000 style A8 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Auxiliary Components (KTU Frequent Topic):** > - **Needle / Spear Valve:** Controls the discharge (and hence power) by axial movement inside the nozzle. > - **Deflector Plate:** Deflects the jet away from the buckets during sudden load rejection (governor action). > - **Brake Jet:** A small auxiliary jet striking the back of the buckets to bring the runner to rest for inspection. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each in strict KTU style; here 3 marks as per new scheme) ### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Classify Pelton wheel based on (a) shaft orientation and (b) number of nozzles.** **Model Answer:** (a) **Shaft Orientation:** 1. *Horizontal-shaft Pelton Wheel* — used for low/medium heads; runner is mounted on a horizontal shaft coupled directly to a horizontal-shaft generator. 2. *Vertical-shaft Pelton Wheel* — used for very high heads; the jet strikes the runner from above and the generator is coupled vertically below. Multiple jets can be arranged symmetrically around the runner. (b) **Number of Nozzles:** 1. *Single-jet Pelton Wheel* — one nozzle supplies one jet to the runner; used when specific speed demand is low. 2. *Multi-jet Pelton Wheel* — two, three, four, or six nozzles supply multiple jets; used to handle medium heads (60–300 m) and increase the specific speed without increasing the bucket dimensions. --- ### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the principle of working of a Pelton wheel turbine.** **Model Answer:** The Pelton wheel works on the **principle of impulse**. The entire pressure head of water is first converted into a high-velocity kinetic-energy jet by a nozzle. This jet strikes the curved double-cupped buckets mounted on the periphery of the runner and is deflected through approximately 165° to 170°. The change in momentum of the water jet produces an impulsive force on the buckets, causing the runner to rotate. The kinetic energy of the jet is thus converted into mechanical (rotational) energy of the shaft. The key principle: **Pressure energy → Kinetic energy (in nozzle) → Mechanical work (in runner).** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice) ### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]` **(a) Derive the expression for the optimum velocity ratio of a Pelton wheel. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the classification of Pelton wheel based on (i) shaft axis, (ii) number of jets, and (iii) specific speed. (7 marks)** ### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 — Euler Equation:** Work done per kg of water per second is given by the Euler turbomachinery equation: $$ W = u(V_{w1} - V_{w2}) $$ **Step 2 — Inlet Velocity Triangle:** The jet enters the bucket tangentially, so the whirl velocity at inlet equals the jet velocity: $$ V_{w1} = V_1 $$ The relative velocity at inlet is: $$ V_{r1} = V_1 - u $$ **Step 3 — Outlet Velocity Triangle (Ideal Bucket):** For a frictionless bucket, $V_{r2} = V_{r1} = V_1 - u$, and assuming $\beta \to 0$, the outlet whirl velocity becomes: $$ V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta \approx u - (V_1 - u) = 2u - V_1 $$ **Step 4 — Work Expression:** $$ W = u[V_1 - (2u - V_1)] = u(2V_1 - 2u) = 2u(V_1 - u) $$ **Step 5 — Hydraulic Efficiency:** $$ \eta_h = \frac{W}{\frac{1}{2}V_1^2} = \frac{4u(V_1 - u)}{V_1^2} $$ Let $K = u/V_1$: $$ \eta_h = 4K(1 - K) $$ **Step 6 — Optimization:** $$ \frac{d\eta_h}{dK} = 4(1 - 2K) = 0 \implies K = 0.5 $$ For a real bucket with angle $\beta$: $$ K_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1}{2(1 + K_r\cos\beta)} \approx 0.45 \text{ to } 0.46 $$ **Hence the optimum velocity ratio is $K = u/V_1 \approx 0.45$.** ### Model Solution to (b): **(i) Shaft Axis Classification:** - **Horizontal-shaft Pelton wheel** — Suitable for low and medium heads (30–300 m). The runner is mounted horizontally and is coupled directly to a horizontal-shaft generator. Single-jet is most common. - **Vertical-shaft Pelton wheel** — Suitable for high and very high heads (above 300 m). The jet strikes the runner vertically downward from above, and the generator is placed below the runner. Multi-jet configurations are common to balance lateral thrust. **(ii) Number of Jets Classification:** - **Single-jet Pelton wheel** — Only one nozzle and one jet. Specific speed range: $N_s = 10$ to $20$ (SI). Used for very high heads and low discharges. - **Multi-jet Pelton wheel** — 2, 3, 4, or 6 nozzles supplying multiple jets spaced symmetrically. Increases $N_s$ to 20–35 without enlarging bucket size. Used for medium heads (60–300 m) where a single jet would result in uneconomically large wheel diameter. **(iii) Specific Speed Classification:** - **Low specific speed** ($N_s = 10$ to $20$): Single-jet, horizontal-shaft machines. - **Higher specific speed** ($N_s = 20$ to $35$): Multi-jet, vertical-shaft machines. Higher specific speed reduces the wheel diameter and raises rotational speed, allowing direct coupling to standard generators. --- ### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]` **(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Pelton wheel. (7 marks)** **(b) Derive the expression for work done per second per unit weight of water striking the buckets of a Pelton wheel. (7 marks)** ### Model Solution to (a): **Working Principle (Refer to Block Diagram in Section 4):** 1. Water from a high-head reservoir is conveyed through a long penstock to the powerhouse. 2. The penstock ends in a **nozzle assembly** containing a needle valve (spear). By adjusting the needle, the cross-sectional area of the jet is varied, regulating the discharge and hence the power output. 3. Inside the nozzle, the entire pressure head of water is converted into a high-velocity jet: $$ V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH_n} $$ 4. This jet emerges in the form of a cylindrical column and strikes the **double-cupped buckets** mounted on the periphery of the runner. 5. Each bucket has a central **splitter ridge** that divides the jet into two equal half-jets. The curved inner surfaces of the cups deflect each half-jet through an angle of approximately **165° to 170°**, reversing its direction almost completely. 6. The **change in momentum** of the water produces an impulsive force on the buckets. This force, acting at a distance from the axis, produces a **torque** that rotates the runner. 7. The runner, mounted on a shaft, drives a generator that converts the mechanical energy into electrical energy. 8. The deflected water falls into the tailrace below the runner. **Key Points:** - Pressure throughout the runner is **atmospheric**. - Energy transfer is purely by **change of velocity (impulse)**, not by change of pressure. - A deflector plate is provided to deflect the jet away from the buckets during emergency shutdown. ### Model Solution to (b): **Derivation of Work Done per kg of Water:** Consider the inlet and outlet velocity triangles of water at a bucket. **Step 1 — Mass flow rate of water:** If $A_j$ is the jet area, $\rho$ is water density, and $V_1$ is jet velocity, then mass flow rate is: $$ \dot{m} = \rho A_j V_1 $$ **Step 2 — Inlet whirl velocity:** Since the jet strikes the bucket tangentially: $$ V_{w1} = V_1 $$ **Step 3 — Outlet whirl velocity:** After deflection through the bucket, assuming $\beta$ is small and $K_r \approx 1$: $$ V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta $$ **Step 4 — Apply Euler Turbomachinery Equation:** $$ W = u(V_{w1} - V_{w2}) \quad \text{(work per unit weight, J/kg)} $$ Substituting: $$ W = u\big[V_1 - (u - V_{r2}\cos\beta)\big] $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $V_{r2} = K_r(V_1 - u)$:** $$ W = u\big[V_1 - u + K_r(V_1 - u)\cos\beta\big] = u(V_1 - u)(1 + K_r\cos\beta) $$ **Step 6 — Mass flow form (work per second):** $$ \dot{W} = \rho A_j V_1 \cdot u(V_1 - u)(1 + K_r\cos\beta) $$ If $Q = A_j V_1$ is the volumetric discharge, the work per second is: $$ \dot{W} = \rho Q \cdot u(V_1 - u)(1 + K_r\cos\beta) $$ For an ideal frictionless bucket ($K_r = 1$, $\beta = 0$): $$ \dot{W} = 2\rho Q \, u(V_1 - u) $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT forget the factor $(1 + K_r\cos\beta)$** in the work expression. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for omitting this. > 2. **Velocity Triangle Direction:** Many students confuse $V_{w2}$ direction. At the outlet, $V_{w2}$ is **opposite** to $V_{w1}$ because the bucket reverses the jet. Always draw the triangle carefully with arrows. > 3. **Units Confusion:** $W$ in J/kg (per kg) and $\dot{W}$ in W or kW (per second) — note the difference. > 4. **Optimal $K$ Value:** The value 0.45 (not 0.5) is the textbook optimum for *real* Pelton wheels. Using 0.5 in design problems may be penalized unless the bucket is ideal. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Cram Sheet — Pelton Wheel Classification & Working** - [x] **Pelton Wheel** = Impulse turbine (pressure drop only in nozzle; runner at atmospheric pressure). - [x] **Working Principle:** PE → KE (in nozzle) → Mechanical Work (in bucket) — by *jet deflection*. - [x] **Bucket Deflection Angle:** 165° – 170° (near-complete reversal of jet). - [x] **Optimum Velocity Ratio:** $K = u/V_1 \approx 0.45$ (real) or 0.5 (ideal frictionless). - [x] **Head Range:** 30 m – 1800 m (very high heads preferred). - [x] **Specific Speed Range:** $N_s = 10$ to $35$ (lowest among all turbines). - [x] **Classification #1 — Shaft Axis:** Horizontal vs Vertical. - [x] **Classification #2 — Number of Jets:** Single-jet vs Multi-jet (2, 3, 4, 6). - [x] **Classification #3 — Specific Speed:** Low Ns (single-jet) vs Higher Ns (multi-jet). - [x] **Classification #4 — Construction:** Single-stage vs Multi-stage (multi-stage very rare). - [x] **Key Formula:** $V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH_n}$, $u = \pi DN/60$, $W = u(V_1 - u)(1 + K_r\cos\beta)$. - [x] **Components to Draw:** Nozzle, Needle valve, Deflector, Brake jet, Buckets, Runner, Casing. - [x] **Euler Equation (must memorize):** $W = u(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$ — sign depends on direction of whirl change. - [x] **Governing Mechanism:** Needle valve (primary) + Deflector plate (emergency) + Brake jet (parking). - [x] **Number of Buckets Formula:** $n_b \geq 15 + (D/d_j)/2$. - [x] **Why Pelton for high head?** Jet velocity $V_1 \propto \sqrt{H}$; only impulse turbines can handle enormous $V_1$ without runner cavitation. - [x] **Common Mistake:** Saying "pressure changes inside runner" — WRONG! Pelton runner is at *constant atmospheric* pressure. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Classification of Pelton Wheel & Principle of Working**

topics.title: Velocity Triangles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Velocity Triangles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — VELOCITY TRIANGLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Triangles** are vector diagrams that graphically represent the **absolute velocity**, **relative velocity**, and **blade (or rotor) velocity** of a fluid particle as it enters and leaves the rotor of a turbomachine. They are constructed by the parallelogram law of vector addition at the **inlet (station 1)** and **outlet (station 2)** of the impeller/rotor blade passages.

The three fundamental velocity vectors involved are:

| Symbol | Velocity | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **C** | Absolute Velocity | Velocity of fluid relative to the stationary casing (fixed frame) |
| **W** | Relative Velocity | Velocity of fluid relative to the rotating blade (moving frame) |
| **U** | Blade/Rotor Velocity | Linear tangential velocity of the blade at the mean radius |

These three vectors are related by the **fundamental vector triangle equation**:

$$\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$$

or equivalently,

$$\vec{W} = \vec{C} - \vec{U}$$

Each velocity triangle is characterized by:
- **α (alpha)** → Absolute flow angle: angle between **C** and **U**
- **β (beta)** → Relative flow angle: angle between **W** and the negative direction of **U**
- **θ (theta) / φ (phi)** → Blade angle: the angle made by the blade surface with the tangential direction

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — Rain on a moving car windshield:**
>
> Imagine you are standing on a footpath watching rain fall **vertically** (this is the **absolute velocity C**). Now imagine sitting in a moving car — the rain appears to strike the windshield at an **inclined angle** (this is the **relative velocity W**, the rain's velocity relative to the moving car). The car's own motion creates a **horizontal velocity U** that combines with the rain's vertical fall to give you the inclined strike. Velocity triangles apply this exact same idea to fluid flowing over rotating turbomachine blades.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need velocity triangles?** They allow engineers to **decompose complex 3-D fluid motion** at the rotor inlet/outlet into measurable vector components. From these components, we can directly compute the **work transfer, hydraulic efficiency, slip factor, and degree of reaction** using Euler's turbomachinery equation. **Without velocity triangles, designing a turbine or compressor blade profile is impossible.**

### 📐 Classification of Velocity Triangles by Direction

| Classification | Direction of Flow at Inlet | Examples |
|----------------|----------------------------|----------|
| **Tangential / Impulse** | Flow enters radially or tangentially | Pelton wheel |
| **Radial / Centrifugal** | Flow enters axially and exits radially | Radial flow centrifugal pump/compressor |
| **Axial** | Flow remains essentially axial | Axial flow turbine, axial compressor |
| **Mixed Flow** | Combination of radial and axial | Francis turbine, mixed flow pump |

### 🧩 Components of Each Velocity Vector

The absolute velocity **C** is resolved into:
- **Cw (whirl/tangential component)** → in the direction of blade motion **U**
- **Cf (flow/axial-radial component)** → perpendicular to blade motion (along meridional direction)

Similarly, the relative velocity **W** is resolved into:
- **Ww (relative whirl)**
- **Wf (relative flow component)**

By definition, **Wf = Cf** because the blade has no velocity component in the meridional direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition — Continuity of flow component:**
>
> The flow component (axial or radial) of the velocity is **identical** in both the absolute and relative frames because the rotor only has tangential velocity. Therefore:
> $$C_f = W_f$$
> This is a **fundamental invariant** used in nearly all velocity triangle problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Velocity Triangles

The construction of a velocity triangle follows a strict geometric logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the reference frame.**
Two frames exist:
- **Stationary (stator) frame** → vectors are absolute velocities (**C**)
- **Rotating (rotor) frame** → vectors are relative velocities (**W**)

**Step 2 — Draw the blade velocity vector.**
Vector **U** = πDN/60 is drawn first as the reference. It is always **tangential** to the mean rotor radius.

**Step 3 — Locate the flow component.**
The axial/radial flow velocity **Cf** is drawn perpendicular to **U**. This sets the geometric constraint that Cf is preserved in both frames.

**Step 4 — Construct the relative velocity.**
**W** is drawn from the tip of **U**, such that its flow component equals Cf. The angle **β** between W and −U direction defines the relative flow angle.

**Step 5 — Close the triangle with the absolute velocity.**
**C** is drawn from the origin (start of U) to the tip of W. The angle **α** between C and U is the absolute flow angle.

**Why does this work?**
Because of vector addition: $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$. The triangle is closed geometrically when all three vectors are drawn head-to-tail.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Gold)

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Blade velocity (m/s), D = diameter (m), N = rpm |
| 2 | $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ | Fundamental triangle relation |
| 3 | $C_f = W_f$ | Continuity of meridional/flow component |
| 4 | $C_w = U + W \cos\beta$ | Whirl component in terms of relative velocity |
| 5 | $C_w = C \cos\alpha$ | Whirl component in terms of absolute velocity |
| 6 | $C_f = C \sin\alpha$ | Flow component in terms of absolute velocity |
| 7 | $C_f = W \sin\beta$ | Flow component in terms of relative velocity |
| 8 | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{C_f}{C_w}$ | Absolute flow angle relation |
| 9 | $\tan\beta = \dfrac{C_f}{U - C_w}$ | Relative flow angle relation |
| 10 | $\dfrac{W}{U} = \dfrac{\sin\alpha}{\sin\beta}$ | Sine rule application to triangle |
| 11 | $\dfrac{C}{U} = \dfrac{\sin\beta}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)}$ | Sine rule application |
| 12 | $V_{r1}^2 = V_{r2}^2 \cdot \dfrac{r_1}{r_2}$ | Radial equilibrium (free vortex) |
| 13 | $W_s = \pm (U_1 C_{w1} \pm U_2 C_{w2})$ | **Euler's equation** (turbine sign convention) |
| 14 | $W_s = U \cdot \Delta C_w$ (for ΔCw alone) | Specific work in axial machines |
| 15 | $\eta_h = \dfrac{U \Delta C_w}{\dfrac{C_1^2 - C_2^2}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)}$ | Hydraulic efficiency |
| 16 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{w2}}{C_{w1}}$ | Degree of reaction for axial machines |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Alert (KTU Board Standard):**
>
> For **turbines**: Work is **done BY the fluid ON the blade** → $W_s = +U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}$ (if U1, U2, Cw1, Cw2 are positive magnitudes, subtract Cw2 since swirl at exit is often reduced or reversed).
>
> For **compressors/pumps**: Work is **done ON the fluid BY the blade** → $W_s = -(U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1})$ → magnitude: $W_s = U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}$.
>
> Always **state the sign convention explicitly** in your solution. Losing the sign = losing 2 marks.

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Turbine Design (Francis, Kaplan, Pelton):** Velocity triangles dictate **runner blade shape, blade angles, and number of blades**.
- **Jet Engine & Steam/Gas Turbine:** Axial turbine blades are designed using velocity triangles to maximize specific work output and minimize leaving losses.
- **Centrifugal Pumps & Compressors:** Inlet velocity triangles determine **pre-whirl** at impeller entry, directly affecting NPSH and efficiency.
- **Wind Turbines & Turboprop Aircraft:** Modern aerodynamic design relies on velocity triangles to optimize blade pitch for varying wind/freestream conditions.
- **Hydraulic Transmission Systems (Torque Converters):** Same triangle principle governs fluid coupling between impeller and turbine.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** In a **well-designed turbine**, the exit absolute velocity **C2** should be as nearly axial (purely Cf2) as possible so that the kinetic energy at exit is **NOT lost** (no whirl remaining to extract). This is called **"reaction leaving the runner axially"** or **"minimum kinetic energy at exit."** This principle drives the design of Francis and Kaplan runners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1: Component Resolution of C from the Inlet Velocity Triangle

**Given:**
- Absolute velocity **C1** at inlet
- Blade velocity **U1**
- Relative velocity **W1** (the fluid velocity as seen from the rotating blade)

**To Prove:** Component relationships $C_w = C \cos\alpha$ and $C_f = C \sin\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Draw the inlet velocity triangle.**
Place the origin O at the point where blade enters the fluid stream. Draw **U1** horizontally (tangent to rotor mean radius). At the tip of **U1**, draw **W1** making angle β with the negative U direction. From origin O, draw **C1** to close the triangle.

**Step 2 — Identify the right-angled components.**
Drop a perpendicular from the tip of W1 to the horizontal (U-axis). This perpendicular length = **Cf** (axial/radial flow component). The horizontal projection of C1 along U = **Cw** (tangential whirl component).

**Step 3 — Write the trigonometric ratios.**
In the right triangle formed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\alpha &= \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{C_w}{C_1} \\
\therefore C_w &= C_1 \cos\alpha \\[6pt]
\sin\alpha &= \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{C_f}{C_1} \\
\therefore C_f &= C_1 \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Result.**
$$C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1, \qquad C_f = C_1 \sin\alpha_1$$

**Conclusion:** The whirl and flow components are obtained by **projecting the absolute velocity vector** along the tangential and meridional directions respectively. This is the foundation for computing work transfer via Euler's equation.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Derivation of Euler's Turbomachinery Equation

**Statement:** The work done per unit weight of fluid (specific work) by a turbomachine is:
$$W_s = \pm(U_1 C_{w1} \pm U_2 C_{w2})$$

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's second law in tangential direction.**
The tangential force exerted by the blade on the fluid equals the **rate of change of tangential momentum** of the fluid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \dot{m}(C_{w2} - C_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate (kg/s) and the subscripts 1, 2 denote inlet and outlet.

**Step 2 — Work done per second (power).**
Power = Tangential force × Blade velocity:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_t \cdot U = \dot{m} U (C_{w2} - C_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific work (work per unit weight).**
Divide by weight flow rate $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot g$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= \frac{P}{\dot{m} \cdot g} = \frac{U(C_{w2} - C_{w1})}{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

In head form (work per unit weight in metres):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{U(C_{w2} - C_{w1})}{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalization to radial machines.**
For radial-flow machines, blade velocities differ at inlet and outlet ($U_1 \neq U_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= \frac{1}{g}(U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

For turbines: $C_{w2} < C_{w1}$ (whirl decreases), giving positive work output.
For pumps/compressors: $C_{w2} > C_{w1}$, giving positive work input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is Euler's Equation — the most fundamental and high-yield equation in the entire Turbo Machinery course.** It directly links velocity triangles to work transfer. You can lose up to **4 marks** in Part B if you state it incorrectly.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Velocity Ratio Form Using Sine Rule

**Given:** Velocity triangle with sides C, U, W and angles α, β, (α − β).

**Applying the Sine Rule** to the triangle:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{\sin\beta} &= \frac{U}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)} = \frac{W}{\sin\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for the flow coefficient φ.**
Flow coefficient is defined as $\phi = \dfrac{C_f}{U}$. Since $C_f = C \sin\alpha = W \sin\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{U} &= \frac{\sin\beta}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)} \\
\phi &= \frac{C_f}{U} = \frac{C \sin\alpha}{U} = \frac{\sin\alpha \cdot \sin\beta}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the blade-speed-to-jet-speed ratio.**
For a Pelton wheel with jet velocity $V_j$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_u &= \frac{U}{V_j} = \frac{\sin\beta_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 - \beta_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The sine rule transforms geometric triangle data into **dimensionless coefficients** that allow comparison between machines of different sizes.

---

### 📋 Analytical Matrix — Common Velocity Triangle Configurations

| Configuration | Inlet β₁ | Outlet β₂ | Remarks |
|---------------|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Impulse (Pelton)** | Very small (≈ 0°) | ≈ 165°–170° | Whirl nearly completely reversed; pressure drop in nozzle only |
| **Francis turbine (slow)** | ≈ 70°–90° | ≈ 20°–30° | Reaction ≈ 50%; mixed flow |
| **Francis turbine (fast)** | ≈ 25°–30° | ≈ 15°–20° | Nearly axial; reaction ≈ 50% |
| **Kaplan turbine** | ≈ 30°–45° | ≈ 15°–25° | Axial; reaction ≈ 50–60% |
| **Axial turbine (Curtis)** | small | small | Multi-stage impulse |
| **Centrifugal pump** | 90° (radial entry) | < 90° | Pre-whirl = 0 at design flow |

---

### 📝 Worked Example (Typical 14-mark Question Pattern)

> **Problem:** Steam enters a turbine stage at absolute velocity $C_1 = 150$ m/s at an angle $\alpha_1 = 20°$. The mean blade speed is $U = 75$ m/s. The exit relative velocity makes angle $\beta_2 = 30°$ with the tangential direction, and $W_2 = 90$ m/s. Determine: (a) Inlet relative velocity **W₁** and its angle **β₁**; (b) Outlet absolute velocity **C₂** and its angle **α₂**; (c) Specific work done per kg of steam using Euler's equation.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Step (a) — Find W₁ and β₁:**

**Step (i) — Compute inlet whirl and flow components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{w1} &= C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 150 \cos 20° = 150 \times 0.9397 \\
C_{w1} &= 140.95 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
C_f &= C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 150 \sin 20° = 150 \times 0.3420 \\
C_f &= 51.30 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Apply velocity triangle closure.**
Since $C_{w1} = U + W_1 \cos\beta_1$ (when β is measured from −U direction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 \cos\beta_1 &= C_{w1} - U = 140.95 - 75 = 65.95 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also, $W_1 \sin\beta_1 = C_f = 51.30$ m/s.

**Step (iii) — Compute magnitude and angle.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \sqrt{(W_1\cos\beta_1)^2 + (W_1\sin\beta_1)^2} \\
W_1 &= \sqrt{(65.95)^2 + (51.30)^2} = \sqrt{4349.4 + 2631.7} \\
W_1 &= \sqrt{6981.1} \approx 83.55 \text{ m/s} \\[6pt]
\tan\beta_1 &= \frac{W_1 \sin\beta_1}{W_1 \cos\beta_1} = \frac{51.30}{65.95} = 0.7779 \\
\beta_1 &= \tan^{-1}(0.7779) \approx 37.88°
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Step (b) — Find C₂ and α₂:

**Step (i) — Compute outlet whirl and flow components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{w2} &= W_2 \cos\beta_2 = 90 \cos 30° = 90 \times 0.8660 = 77.94 \text{ m/s} \\
W_{f2} &= W_2 \sin\beta_2 = 90 \sin 30° = 90 \times 0.5 = 45.00 \text{ m/s} \\
C_{w2} &= U - W_{w2} = 75 - 77.94 = -2.94 \text{ m/s} \\
C_f &= W_{f2} = 45.00 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **negative** $C_{w2}$ indicates the whirl has been **reversed** in direction (energy extracted).

**Step (ii) — Compute magnitude of C₂ and its angle.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= \sqrt{C_{w2}^2 + C_f^2} = \sqrt{(-2.94)^2 + (45)^2} \\
C_2 &= \sqrt{8.64 + 2025} = \sqrt{2033.64} \approx 45.10 \text{ m/s} \\[6pt]
\tan\alpha_2 &= \frac{C_f}{C_{w2}} = \frac{45}{-2.94} = -15.31 \\
\alpha_2 &\approx 180° - 86.26° = 93.74° \text{ (i.e., reverse whirl)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Step (c) — Specific work from Euler's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= U(C_{w1} - C_{w2}) = 75 \times [140.95 - (-2.94)] \\
W_s &= 75 \times 143.89 \\
W_s &= 10791.75 \text{ J/kg} \approx 10.79 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $W_1$ | 83.55 m/s |
| $\beta_1$ | 37.88° |
| $C_2$ | 45.10 m/s |
| $\alpha_2$ | 93.74° (reverse whirl) |
| $W_s$ | 10,791.75 J/kg |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board tip:** Always include a **neatly drawn velocity triangle** (inlet and outlet) before writing any equations. Drawing the triangle first earns you **1–2 marks** even before the calculation begins, because it demonstrates conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1: Block Diagram — Velocity Triangle Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Station 1 or 2] --> B[Define Three Velocities: C, W, U]
    B --> C[Compute Blade Velocity: U = pi D N / 60]
    C --> D[Determine Flow Component Cf from Mass Flow]
    D --> E{Choose Reference Frame}
    E -->|Stationary| F[Draw Vector U Tangential to Mean Radius]
    E -->|Rotating| G[Draw Vector Cf Perpendicular to U]
    F --> H[Mark Tip of U Vector]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Locate W at angle beta from -U direction with Wf equal Cf]
    I --> J[Close Triangle: Draw C from Origin to Tip of W]
    J --> K[Measure alpha: Angle between C and U]
    K --> L[Resolve C into Cw and Cf]
    L --> M[Apply Euler Equation: Ws = U times delta Cw]
    M --> N[Compute Specific Work and Efficiency]
    N --> O[End: Output Work Transfer and Blade Profile Data]
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 2: Schematic — Inlet and Outlet Velocity Triangles for an Axial Turbine Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INL["INLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE - STATION 1"]
        U1["Vector U1 - Blade Speed Inlet"]
        C1["Vector C1 - Absolute Inlet Velocity"]
        W1["Vector W1 - Relative Inlet Velocity"]
        A1["alpha1 - Absolute Flow Angle"]
        B1["beta1 - Relative Flow Angle"]
        U1 --- C1
        U1 --- W1
        C1 --- W1
    end

    subgraph OUTL["OUTLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE - STATION 2"]
        U2["Vector U2 - Blade Speed Outlet"]
        C2["Vector C2 - Absolute Outlet Velocity"]
        W2["Vector W2 - Relative Outlet Velocity"]
        A2["alpha2 - Absolute Flow Angle"]
        B2["beta2 - Relative Flow Angle"]
        U2 --- C2
        U2 --- W2
        C2 --- W2
    end

    INL -->|Flow through Rotor Passages| OUTL
    OUTL -->|Ws = U times Cw1 - Cw2| P["Power Output Per Unit Mass"]
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 3: Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Velocity Triangle and Machine Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VELTRI[Velocity Triangle Inputs: C, W, U, alpha, beta] --> WHIRL[Compute Whirl Components: Cw1 and Cw2]
    VELTRI --> FLOW[Compute Flow Components: Cf1 and Cf2]
    WHIRL --> EULER[Euler Equation: Ws = U times delta Cw]
    FLOW --> CONT[Continuity Check: Cf1 equals Cf2]
    EULER --> PERF1[Specific Work Output]
    EULER --> PERF2[Blade Loading]
    CONT --> PERF3[Axial Velocity Check]
    PERF1 --> HYDRO[Hydraulic Efficiency]
    PERF2 --> BLADE[Blade Profile and Number Selection]
    PERF3 --> LOSS[Kinetic Energy Leaving Loss]
    HYDRO --> FINAL[Machine Performance Evaluation]
    BLADE --> FINAL
    LOSS --> FINAL
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 4: Classification Flowchart of Turbomachines by Velocity Triangle Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Turbomachine] --> DIR{Flow Direction at Inlet?}
    DIR -->|Tangential or Radial| TANG[Impulse Type - Pelton Wheel]
    DIR -->|Axial| AX{Internal Reaction}
    DIR -->|Mixed| MIX[Mixed Flow - Francis Turbine]

    AX -->|Low Reaction below 50 percent| AXIALIMP[Axial Impulse Curtis Stage]
    AX -->|Reaction around 50 percent| AXIAL50[Axial Reaction - Kaplan Type]

    TANG --> V1[Velocity Triangle: beta1 near 0, beta2 near 165 deg]
    MIX --> V2[Velocity Triangle: Inlet Radial, Outlet Axial]
    AXIALIMP --> V3[Velocity Triangle: Small angles, Symmetric]
    AXIAL50 --> V4[Velocity Triangle: Beta1 and Beta2 comparable]
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 5: Decision Flow for Solving Velocity Triangle Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Given Velocities and Angles]
    B --> C{Is Cf given or Computable?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Cf equals C sin alpha equals W sin beta]
    C -->|No but mass flow given| E[Compute Cf from Q divided by A]
    D --> F[Compute Cw from C cos alpha or U plus W cos beta]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Euler Equation for Work]
    G --> H{Output Required?}
    H -->|Work| I[Report Ws in J per kg or kJ per kg]
    H -->|Efficiency| J[Compute eta as Useful Work divided by Energy Supplied]
    H -->|Blade Angle| K[Use beta to specify blade profile]
    I --> DONE[Solution Complete]
    J --> DONE
    K --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use these diagrams in exams:**
>
> - Diagram 1 and 2 are the **core conceptual scaffolds** — draw the **physical velocity triangles** directly on your answer sheet with a pencil and ruler.
> - Diagrams 3, 4, 5 are **analytical flowcharts** that help you decide **which equation to apply** in long KTU Part-B questions.
> - Mermaid is used here only as a **functional/architectural mapping** because a precise vector-accurate velocity triangle must be hand-drawn on graph paper during the exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define velocity triangle. Explain the terms absolute velocity, relative velocity, and blade velocity with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **velocity triangle** is a vector diagram showing the relative magnitudes and directions of three velocities of a fluid particle at the inlet or outlet of a turbomachine rotor.

- **Absolute Velocity (C):** The velocity of the fluid measured relative to a **stationary** reference frame (the casing of the machine).
- **Relative Velocity (W):** The velocity of the fluid measured relative to the **rotating** blade, i.e., as observed by an observer moving with the rotor.
- **Blade Velocity (U):** The tangential linear velocity of the blade at the mean radius, given by $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$.

[Diagram: standard inlet triangle with U horizontal, W at angle β from −U, C from origin making angle α with U, closed by vector addition C = W + U.]

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explaining all three velocities, 1 mark for diagram.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State Euler's equation for a turbomachine and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Euler's equation for the specific work done by a fluid in a turbomachine is:

$$W_s = \frac{1}{g}(U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1})$$

(For turbines, work output is positive; for compressors/pumps, work input is positive with the opposite sign convention.)

**Significance:**
1. It is the **fundamental energy-transfer equation** of all turbomachines — it directly gives the work output of a turbine or work input of a pump/compressor.
2. It uses only the **tangential (whirl) components** of the absolute velocities, which are read directly from velocity triangles.
3. It forms the **basis for computing hydraulic and mechanical efficiency**.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for correct equation, 1 mark for sign convention, 1 mark for two significance points.)*

---

### 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose EITHER A or B

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the velocity triangle at the inlet of an axial flow turbomachine. Establish the relationship between absolute velocity C₁, relative velocity W₁, and blade velocity U₁. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a single-stage axial turbine, steam enters with absolute velocity $C_1 = 200$ m/s at angle $\alpha_1 = 25°$ to the tangential direction. The mean blade speed is $U = 100$ m/s. The relative velocity at exit is $W_2 = 120$ m/s at $\beta_2 = 35°$ from the tangential direction (in the opposite sense to U). Determine:
- (i) Inlet relative velocity $W_1$ and angle $\beta_1$.
- (ii) Outlet absolute velocity $C_2$ and angle $\alpha_2$.
- (iii) Work done per kg of steam per second. **(7 marks)**

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Draw the inlet velocity triangle showing U horizontal, W at angle β from the −U direction, and C as the closing vector.

**Step 2:** Apply the parallelogram law:
$$\vec{C_1} = \vec{W_1} + \vec{U_1}$$

**Step 3:** Resolve into components:
- **Tangential:** $C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = U + W_1 \cos\beta_1$
- **Meridional:** $C_f = C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = W_1 \sin\beta_1$

**Step 4:** Solve for $W_1$:
$$W_1 = \sqrt{C_1^2 + U_1^2 - 2 C_1 U_1 \cos\alpha_1}$$
(from cosine rule on triangle where angle between C₁ and U₁ is α₁ and the opposite side is W₁).

**Step 5:** Angle β₁:
$$\tan\beta_1 = \frac{C_f}{U_1 - C_{w1}}$$

*(Marks: 1 for diagram, 2 for vector equation + resolution, 2 for W₁ formula, 2 for β₁ formula.)*

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $C_1 = 200$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 25°$, $U = 100$ m/s, $W_2 = 120$ m/s, $\beta_2 = 35°$ (opposite to U).

**Step 1: Compute inlet whirl and flow components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{w1} &= C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 200 \cos 25° = 200 \times 0.9063 = 181.26 \text{ m/s} \\
C_f &= C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 200 \sin 25° = 200 \times 0.4226 = 84.52 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply triangle closure for inlet.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 \cos\beta_1 &= C_{w1} - U = 181.26 - 100 = 81.26 \text{ m/s} \\
W_1 \sin\beta_1 &= C_f = 84.52 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \sqrt{(81.26)^2 + (84.52)^2} = \sqrt{6603.2 + 7143.6} \\
W_1 &= \sqrt{13746.8} \approx 117.25 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
\beta_1 &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{84.52}{81.26}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.0401) \approx 46.12°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Outlet whirl and flow components.**
Since $\beta_2$ is in the opposite sense to U:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{w2} &= -W_2 \cos\beta_2 = -120 \cos 35° = -98.30 \text{ m/s} \\
W_{f2} &= W_2 \sin\beta_2 = 120 \sin 35° = 68.83 \text{ m/s} \\
C_{w2} &= U + W_{w2} = 100 - 98.30 = 1.70 \text{ m/s} \\
C_f &= W_{f2} = 68.83 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Outlet absolute velocity and angle.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= \sqrt{(1.70)^2 + (68.83)^2} = \sqrt{2.89 + 4737.6} \approx 68.85 \text{ m/s} \\
\alpha_2 &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{68.83}{1.70}\right) \approx 88.59°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Work done per kg per second (Euler equation).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= U(C_{w1} - C_{w2}) = 100 \times (181.26 - 1.70) \\
W_s &= 100 \times 179.56 = 17{,}956 \text{ J/kg} \approx 17.96 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $W_1$ | 117.25 m/s |
| $\beta_1$ | 46.12° |
| $C_2$ | 68.85 m/s |
| $\alpha_2$ | 88.59° |
| $W_s$ | 17,956 J/kg |

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Euler's equation for the work done per unit weight of fluid flowing through a turbomachine. State clearly the sign convention used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The velocity triangle of a Francis turbine has the following data at the inlet: mean radius $r_1 = 1.25$ m, $N = 300$ rpm, $C_1 = 30$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 60°$, $\beta_1 = 30°$. At the outlet, $r_2 = 1.0$ m, $C_2 = 15$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 30°$. Determine:
- (i) The whirl components at inlet and outlet.
- (ii) The work done per unit weight of water.
- (iii) The hydraulic efficiency of the turbine. **(7 marks)**

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's second law in the tangential direction. The tangential force on the fluid equals the rate of change of angular momentum:
$$F_t = \dot{m}(C_{w2} - C_{w1})$$

**Step 2:** Power transferred to blade = $F_t \cdot U$:
$$P = \dot{m} \cdot U \cdot (C_{w2} - C_{w1})$$

**Step 3:** Specific work (per unit weight = per Newton-force equivalent) gives:
$$W_s = \frac{U(C_{w2} - C_{w1})}{g} \quad \text{(J/N = m, in metres of head)}$$

**Step 4:** For radial machines with $U_1 \neq U_2$:
$$W_s = \frac{U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}}{g}$$

**Sign Convention:**
- **Turbine:** $W_s = \dfrac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g}$ (positive = work output).
- **Pump/Compressor:** $W_s = \dfrac{U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}}{g}$ (positive = work input).

*(Marks: 2 for momentum equation, 2 for power, 2 for work, 1 for sign convention.)*

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $r_1 = 1.25$ m, $r_2 = 1.0$ m, $N = 300$ rpm, $C_1 = 30$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 60°$, $\beta_1 = 30°$, $C_2 = 15$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 30°$.

**Step 1: Blade velocities.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{2\pi r_1 N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1.25 \times 300}{60} = 39.27 \text{ m/s} \\
U_2 &= \frac{2\pi r_2 N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1.0 \times 300}{60} = 31.42 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Whirl components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{w1} &= C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 30 \cos 60° = 30 \times 0.5 = 15 \text{ m/s} \\
C_{w2} &= C_2 \cos\alpha_2 = 15 \cos 30° = 15 \times 0.866 = 12.99 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Work done per unit weight.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= \frac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g} = \frac{(39.27)(15) - (31.42)(12.99)}{9.81} \\
W_s &= \frac{589.05 - 408.15}{9.81} = \frac{180.90}{9.81} \approx 18.44 \text{ m of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Hydraulic efficiency.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_h &= \frac{\text{Useful work per unit weight}}{\text{Kinetic energy input per unit weight}} \\
\text{Numerator} &= U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2} = 180.90 \text{ (units: m²/s², i.e., J/kg)} \\
\text{Energy input (Bernoulli KE + pressure head drop)} &= \frac{C_1^2 - C_2^2}{2g} + \Delta z + \Delta(p/\rho g)
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume the inlet and outlet conditions are at the same elevation and neglecting pressure potential (or, given only velocities, use kinetic-energy basis):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_h &= \frac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{(C_1^2/2g)} \\
\eta_h &= \frac{180.90}{(30^2/2 \times 9.81)} = \frac{180.90}{45.87} \approx 3.94
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the result exceeds 1, this confirms that energy is **not** purely kinetic — pressure energy is also converted. In practice, the **change in pressure head** across the runner must be included in the denominator for a realistic hydraulic efficiency value. The KTU board expects students to **state this limitation** and acknowledge that without pressure data, only the Euler-based comparison is meaningful.

**Refined hydraulic efficiency with assumed $\Delta p / \rho g = 18$ m** (illustrative):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_h &= \frac{180.90}{(C_1^2 - C_2^2)/2 + g\Delta z + \Delta p/\rho g} \\
&= \frac{180.90}{(900 - 225)/2 + 0 + 18} = \frac{180.90}{337.5 + 18} = \frac{180.90}{355.5} \\
\eta_h &\approx 0.509 \text{ or } 50.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $U_1, U_2$ | 39.27 m/s, 31.42 m/s |
| $C_{w1}, C_{w2}$ | 15 m/s, 12.99 m/s |
| $W_s$ | 18.44 m of water |
| $\eta_h$ | ≈ 50.9% |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks in Velocity Triangle Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention in Euler's equation** — losing 2 marks. Always write the equation with explicit + or − signs and label "turbine" or "compressor."
>
> 2. **Mixing up α (absolute) and β (relative)** — β is the angle of W with the **negative U direction**, not with U. Drawing the triangle incorrectly propagates errors throughout.
>
> 3. **Not drawing the velocity triangle first** — the board examiner awards **at least 1 mark** for a correctly drawn triangle with labeled vectors. Skipping this costs you easy marks.
>
> 4. **Using $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ with wrong units** — N must be in **rpm** and D in **metres**. Mixing up these units is a frequent mistake.
>
> 5. **Confusing inlet and outlet blade velocities in a radial machine** — $U_1 \neq U_2$. Use the **correct radius** at each station; this error leads to a chain of wrong calculations.
>
> 6. **Rounding intermediate results too early** — keep 4–5 decimal places during intermediate steps; round only the final answer to 2 decimals. Excessive rounding in intermediate steps leads to wrong final answers.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Velocity Triangles (Module 3):**
>
> ✅ Velocity triangle = vector diagram showing **C, W, U** at rotor inlet/outlet.
>
> ✅ Fundamental equation: $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$
>
> ✅ Continuity invariant: $C_f = W_f$ (flow component is same in both frames).
>
> ✅ **Euler's equation (most important):**
> - Turbine: $W_s = \dfrac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g}$
> - Compressor/Pump: $W_s = \dfrac{U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}}{g}$
>
> ✅ **Blade velocity:** $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ (axial machines) or $U = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{60}$ (radial machines).
>
> ✅ **Component formulas:**
> - $C_w = C \cos\alpha = U + W \cos\beta$ (when β measured from −U)
> - $C_f = C \sin\alpha = W \sin\beta$
> - $\tan\beta = \dfrac{C_f}{U - C_w}$
>
> ✅ **Sine rule:** $\dfrac{C}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{U}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)} = \dfrac{W}{\sin\alpha}$
>
> ✅ **Degree of reaction** for axial machines: $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{w2}}{C_{w1}}$
>
> ✅ **Free vortex condition:** $C_w \cdot r = \text{constant}$ → used in Francis/Kaplan design.
>
> ✅ **Maximum specific work** in a turbine stage occurs when **α₂ = 90°** (no whirl exit loss), and is $W_s = U_1 C_{w1}$ (when Cw2 = 0).
>
> ✅ **Velocity triangles must always be drawn first** in any solution — they earn easy marks and prevent calculation errors.
>
> ✅ **For Impulse machines (Pelton):** Pressure drop occurs in the **nozzle**, not in the rotor.
>
> ✅ **For Reaction machines (Francis/Kaplan):** Pressure drop is **shared** between stator and rotor.
>
> ✅ **Range of blade angles to remember:**
> - Pelton inlet β₁ ≈ 0° to 5°
> - Pelton outlet β₂ ≈ 165° to 175°
> - Kaplan inlet β₁ ≈ 30° to 45°
> - Centrifugal pump inlet β₁ = 90° (radial entry, no pre-whirl)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiencies and Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Efficiencies and Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Efficiencies and Losses in Turbo Machinery?

In the context of **Turbo Machinery** (compressors, turbines, and combined systems), **Efficiencies** are dimensionless performance metrics that quantify how effectively a machine converts input energy into useful work (or vice versa) under real, irreversible conditions. **Losses**, conversely, are the penalties paid due to irreversibilities such as friction, turbulence, leakage, and heat transfer that prevent the machine from achieving the ideal **isentropic** (constant entropy) performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Efficiency* in turbo machinery is defined as the ratio of the **isentropic enthalpy change (work)** to the **actual enthalpy change (work)** for a process. A higher efficiency means a smaller deviation from the ideal isentropic process, implying fewer losses.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Bucket

Imagine you are filling a swimming pool using a bucket with a small hole at the bottom. 

- **Ideal Case (Isentropic)**: A perfectly sealed bucket carries 100% of the water to the pool. This is the "ideal work" or "isentropic work."
- **Real Case (Actual)**: The hole lets water leak out, so you must carry extra water and pump harder to deliver the same net amount. The "extra effort" you spend is the **loss**.

In turbo machinery, the "leak" takes the form of:
- Fluid friction (viscous losses)
- Turbulence and flow separation
- Leakage past clearances
- Heat transfer to/from surroundings
- Mechanical friction in bearings

The **efficiency** simply tells you: *"Of the total energy I put in, what percentage actually came out as useful work?"*

### Why is this Topic KTU-Critical?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMET411 Module 3):**
> The KTU 2024 scheme dedicates significant weightage to efficiencies because:
> 1. **Design Selection** — Engineers must select compressors/turbines based on isentropic vs. polytropic efficiency.
> 2. **Performance Prediction** — Real machines deviate from ideal; efficiency bridges the gap.
> 3. **Loss Estimation** — Stage-by-stage loss analysis (profile loss, clearance loss, etc.) is essential for preliminary design.
> 4. **Combined Cycle & Reheat Analysis** — Reheat factor, stage efficiency, and overall efficiency are direct exam topics.

### Key Performance Metrics at a Glance

- **Isentropic Efficiency ($\eta_{isen}$)** — The most fundamental; compares actual work to ideal isentropic work.
- **Polytropic Efficiency ($\eta_{poly}$)** — Stage-by-stage efficiency, more accurate for multi-stage machines.
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)** — Accounts for bearing friction, windage, and seal losses.
- **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$)** — Important for reciprocating compressors; less so for turbo compressors.
- **Internal (Hydraulic/Aerodynamic) Efficiency** — Combines thermodynamic and fluid losses.
- **Overall (Thermal) Efficiency** — For complete systems like gas turbine plants.

### Physical Constants and Standard Parameters

- **Specific Heat Ratio ($\gamma = C_p / C_v$)**: For air, $\gamma \approx \mathbf{1.4}$.
- **Gas Constant for Air ($R$)**: $R = \mathbf{0.287 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$.
- **Reference Conditions (KTU Standard)**: $T_0 = 288.15 \; K$, $P_0 = 101.325 \; kPa$ (used in numerical problems unless stated).
- **Stage Pressure Ratio** — A key performance indicator; high stage pressure ratio typically lowers isentropic efficiency.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Losses in Turbo Machinery

Losses are categorized based on their **origin** and **location** in the machine:

| Category | Type of Loss | Typical Location |
|----------|--------------|------------------|
| Aerodynamic | Profile, Secondary, Tip Clearance | Blade rows |
| Mechanical | Bearing friction, Windage, Seal | Shaft, seals |
| Thermodynamic | Heat transfer, Leakage | Casing, clearances |
| Hydraulic | Incidence, Diffusion | Stator, Rotor |
| Incidental | Part-load, Surge | Operating regime |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question on losses, always classify them into *Internal* vs. *External* and *Aerodynamic* vs. *Mechanical* — examiners award marks for **structured presentation**.

---

## 1.3 Roadmap of This Study Note

This study note will:
1. Define each efficiency formally with KTU-syllabus terminology.
2. Derive the key efficiency relations step-by-step.
3. Provide a comprehensive formula sheet (without breaking markdown table syntax).
4. Map the relationships between efficiencies using a structured diagram.
5. Furnish model exam questions (Part A and Part B with internal choice) with full solutions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Isentropic Efficiency of a Compressor

A **compressor** consumes work to raise the pressure of a gas. The ideal (isentropic) work is the *minimum* work required. Real compressors always consume more.

### Derivation of the Definition

Consider a compressor with:
- Inlet state: $1$ (with $h_1$, $P_1$)
- Ideal outlet (isentropic): $2s$ (with $h_{2s}$, $P_2$)
- Actual outlet: $2$ (with $h_2$, $P_2$)

The actual specific work done on the gas is:
$$W_{act} = h_2 - h_1$$

The minimum (isentropic) specific work required is:
$$W_{isen} = h_{2s} - h_1$$

The **isentropic efficiency of a compressor** is defined as:
$$\eta_{c,isen} = \frac{W_{isen}}{W_{act}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$$

### Why is it always less than 1?

Because $h_2 > h_{2s}$ in a real compressor (entropy increases), the denominator is larger than the numerator. Thus:
$$\eta_{c,isen} \le 1$$

## 2.2 Isentropic Efficiency of a Turbine

A **turbine** extracts work from a high-energy fluid. The actual work output is *less* than the ideal isentropic work.

Using state points (inlet $1$, isentropic outlet $2s$, actual outlet $2$):
- Actual work output: $W_{act} = h_1 - h_2$
- Isentropic work output: $W_{isen} = h_1 - h_{2s}$

Since $h_2 > h_{2s}$ (real outlet is hotter due to losses), the actual work is less:
$$\eta_{t,isen} = \frac{W_{act}}{W_{isen}} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the difference:** Compressor efficiency uses *ideal in denominator*; turbine efficiency also uses *ideal in denominator*. The numerator differs because the direction of work transfer is opposite.

## 2.3 Polytropic Efficiency

For a **multi-stage** machine, the isentropic efficiency of the whole machine is **not** equal to the isentropic efficiency of each stage. This is because each stage has its own losses, and they accumulate. The **polytropic efficiency** is a fictitious efficiency that *if applied to each infinitesimal stage* would yield the same overall isentropic efficiency.

### Definition (Compressor):
$$\eta_{poly,c} = \frac{\int dW_{isen}}{\int dW_{act}}$$

For an ideal gas undergoing a polytropic process ($P v^n = \text{const}$):
$$W_{act} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

$$W_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

### Relationship between Polytropic and Isentropic Efficiency

For a compressor:
$$\eta_{isen,c} = \frac{\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}}} - 1}$$

For a turbine:
$$\eta_{isen,t} = \frac{1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}}}}{1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The polytropic efficiency is essentially *constant* across stages for many machines, while the isentropic efficiency *decreases* as overall pressure ratio increases. This is why high-pressure-ratio compressors quote polytropic efficiency.

## 2.4 Mechanical Efficiency

The mechanical efficiency accounts for losses in bearings, seals, and any auxiliary drives:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{W_{shaft,useful}}{W_{shaft,total}}$$

In a turbine:
$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{W_{net}}{W_{gross}}$$

where $W_{gross}$ is the aerodynamic work and $W_{net}$ is the work available at the coupling.

## 2.5 Blade (Stage) Efficiency

The **blade efficiency** relates the work actually obtained to the work that would be obtained if there were no friction in the blade passages. It is given by:
$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{\Delta h_{actual}}{\Delta h_{ideal}}$$

## 2.6 Reheat Factor

In multi-stage turbines, friction in early stages heats the fluid, which then expands *more* in later stages than predicted by an isentropic line. The **reheat factor (RF)** is:

$$RF = \frac{\sum (\text{Actual stage enthalpy drop})}{\text{Total isentropic enthalpy drop}}$$

$$RF = \frac{\sum \Delta h_{i,act}}{(h_1 - h_{2s})}$$

For a turbine:
$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{stage} \times RF$$

## 2.7 Degree of Reaction (Recap from Velocity Triangles)

While covered in Module 2, the **degree of reaction ($R$)** is foundational for efficiency analysis:

$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{rotor}}{\Delta h_{stage}}$$

- $R = 0.5$: Symmetric (50-50) reaction (Parsons turbine).
- $R = 0$: Pure impulse (de Laval turbine).
- $R \approx 0.9$: Modern high-reaction axial turbines.

The relation to the **stage isentropic work**:
$$\Delta h_{stage,isen} = U \Delta V_w$$

where $U$ is the mean blade speed and $\Delta V_w$ is the change in tangential velocity.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT reference for KTU exams on this topic.** Memorize the structure; understand the logic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Conditions / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | Isentropic Efficiency (Compressor) | $\eta_{c,isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | $h_2 > h_{2s}$ always |
| 2 | Isentropic Efficiency (Turbine) | $\eta_{t,isen} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | dimensionless | $h_2 > h_{2s}$ always |
| 3 | Polytropic Efficiency (Compressor) | $\eta_{poly,c} = \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(P_2/P_1)}{\ln(T_2/T_1)}$ | dimensionless | $T$ in K, $P$ same units |
| 4 | Polytropic Efficiency (Turbine) | $\eta_{poly,t} = \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(T_2/T_1)}{\ln(P_2/P_1)}$ | dimensionless | For $P_1 > P_2$ in turbines |
| 5 | Stage Temperature Ratio (Compressor) | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}}}$ | ratio | Polytropic relation |
| 6 | Overall Isentropic from Polytropic (Comp.) | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}}} - 1}$ | dimensionless | $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ |
| 7 | Reheat Factor (Turbine) | $RF = \dfrac{\sum \Delta h_{stage,act}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | ratio > 1 | For multi-stage turbines |
| 8 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{shaft}}{W_{gross}}$ | dimensionless | 0.95 - 0.99 typical |
| 9 | Internal Efficiency (Turbine) | $\eta_{int} = \eta_{isen} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | dimensionless | Combined losses |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{ov} = \eta_{stage} \times RF$ | dimensionless | $RF > 1$ compensates |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** The vertical bars `$ \vert $` in $\vert h_2 - h_1 \vert$ are written with `\vert` LaTeX command to avoid breaking the table rendering.

## 2.9 Engineering Real-World Utility

1. **Gas Turbine Design (GE, Rolls-Royce, Siemens):** Polytropic efficiency is the design metric; every stage is rated to a target $\eta_{poly}$ (typically **88-92%**).
2. **Aircraft Engine Compressors:** Compressor isentropic efficiency directly affects Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC).
3. **Steam Turbines (NTPC, BHEL):** Reheat factor is critical; often $RF = 1.03 - 1.06$.
4. **Compressor Stations (ONGC, GAIL):** Mechanical efficiency determines auxiliary power consumption.
5. **Cryogenic Turboexpanders (LNG plants):** Very high isentropic efficiency ($>85\%$) is required for low temperatures.

---

## 2.10 Loss Categories — Detailed Classification

### A. Internal Losses (within fluid)
- **Profile (Blade) Loss** — Friction on blade surfaces.
- **Secondary Loss** — Vortices at blade hubs and tips.
- **Tip Clearance Loss** — Leakage over blade tips.
- **Annulus Loss** — Boundary layer on casing/hub.
- **Incidence Loss** — Loss from non-optimal angle of attack.

### B. External Losses
- **Mechanical Losses** — Bearing friction, windage.
- **Leakage Losses** — Through seals and clearances.
- **Heat Transfer Losses** — Cooling/heating from casing.

### C. Stage Losses Quantification

The **loss coefficient ($\zeta$)** relates kinetic energy to pressure loss:
$$\Delta P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \zeta$$

where $V$ is a representative velocity (often the mean blade speed or exit velocity).

For a typical axial turbine stage: $\zeta_{total} \approx 0.15 - 0.25$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Loss coefficients are often quoted as a percentage of dynamic head. $\zeta = 0.2$ means "20% of dynamic head is lost."

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Isentropic Efficiency for an Ideal Gas Compressor

### Given:
- Inlet: $T_1$, $P_1$
- Outlet: $P_2$
- Process: Polytropic with index $n$, but actual process is irreversible.
- $\gamma = C_p / C_v$

### Goal:
Express $\eta_{isen}$ in terms of measurable temperatures and pressure ratio.

### Step 1: Write the actual work for an irreversible compressor

For a real (polytropic) compression from state 1 to state 2:
$$W_{act} = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

### Step 2: Write the isentropic work

For an isentropic process from the same $T_1$, $P_1$ to $P_2$:
$$W_{isen} = C_p (T_{2s} - T_1)$$

with
$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

### Step 3: Form the efficiency ratio

$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{W_{isen}}{W_{act}} = \frac{C_p (T_{2s} - T_1)}{C_p (T_2 - T_1)} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$

### Step 4: Eliminate $T_{2s}$ using the isentropic relation

$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]}{T_2 - T_1}$$

### Step 5: Final compact form

Let $r_p = P_2 / P_1$. Then:
$$\boxed{\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_1 \left( r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right)}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Polytropic Efficiency in Terms of $T_2/T_1$ and $P_2/P_1$

### Step 1: Recall polytropic process temperature-pressure relation

For a polytropic process with index $n$:
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n - 1}{n}}$$

### Step 2: Polytropic work input

$$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} R (T_2 - T_1)$$

### Step 3: Isentropic work from $T_1$, $P_1$ to $P_2$

$$W_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

### Step 4: Polytropic efficiency is the ratio for a *single* stage

For a single stage treated as polytropic:
$$\eta_{poly} = \frac{W_{isen,stage}}{W_{poly,stage}}$$

Solving for the polytropic index $n$ in terms of $\eta_{poly}$:

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{poly}}$$

### Step 5: Substituting back

Using the relation:
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}}}$$

### Step 6: Logarithmic form

Taking natural logs:
$$\ln\left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right) = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}} \ln\left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\eta_{poly} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{\ln(P_2 / P_1)}{\ln(T_2 / T_1)}}$$

This is the **most common formula** for calculating polytropic efficiency from measured $T$ and $P$ data.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Reheat Factor for a 3-Stage Turbine

### Setup
- Inlet state: 1 ($T_1$, $P_1$)
- After stage 1 (actual): 2 ($T_2$, $P_2$)
- After stage 2 (actual): 3 ($T_3$, $P_3$)
- After stage 3 (actual): 4 ($T_4$, $P_4$)
- Isentropic state from 1 to $P_4$: $4s$

### Step 1: Total actual enthalpy drop

$$\Delta h_{actual,total} = C_p (T_1 - T_4)$$

### Step 2: Total isentropic enthalpy drop

$$\Delta h_{isen,total} = C_p (T_1 - T_{4s})$$

with
$$T_{4s} = T_1 \left( \frac{P_4}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

### Step 3: Reheat factor definition

$$RF = \frac{\Delta h_{actual,total}}{\Delta h_{isen,total}} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{T_1 - T_{4s}}$$

### Step 4: Relation to stage efficiency

For each stage, the isentropic stage efficiency is:
$$\eta_{stage} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\sum_{i=1}^{3} (T_{i} - T_{i+1})_{act}}$$

Wait — the correct formulation is:
$$\eta_{stage} = \frac{\Delta h_{isen,stage}}{\Delta h_{actual,stage}}$$

Summing all stages:
$$\sum \Delta h_{act,stage} = C_p (T_1 - T_4)$$

$$\sum \Delta h_{isen,stage} = \Delta h_{isen,total} = C_p (T_1 - T_{4s})$$

The overall efficiency is:
$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\sum \Delta h_{act,stage}}{\Delta h_{isen,total}} = \eta_{stage} \times RF$$

### Step 5: Final compact relation

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \eta_{stage} \times RF}$$

$$RF = \frac{\eta_{overall}}{\eta_{stage}}$$

Since $RF > 1$, the overall efficiency is *greater* than the stage efficiency — reheat partially compensates for losses.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Efficiency

| Scenario | Best Efficiency to Use | Reason |
|----------|------------------------|--------|
| Single-stage compressor | Isentropic | Direct comparison, simple |
| Multi-stage compressor design | Polytropic | Constant per stage, easier scaling |
| Single-stage turbine | Isentropic | Standard reporting metric |
| Multi-stage turbine | Stage efficiency + Reheat factor | Captures cumulative effect |
| Complete gas turbine plant | Overall (thermal) efficiency | Includes combustion, mechanical |
| Compressor with gearbox | Mechanical + Isentropic | Decouples shaft from fluid work |
| Aircraft engine SFC calculation | Propulsive + Thermal | Combines thrust and fuel |

---

## 3.5 Solved Example — Comprehensive KTU Style

### Problem:
Air enters a compressor at $T_1 = 300 \; K$, $P_1 = 100 \; kPa$, and exits at $P_2 = 500 \; kPa$, $T_2 = 470 \; K$. Calculate:
1. Polytropic efficiency
2. Isentropic efficiency
3. Compare

**Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$.**

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute isentropic exit temperature**
$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times \left( \frac{500}{100} \right)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}}$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times (5)^{0.2857}$$

Computing $5^{0.2857}$:
$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$
$$0.2857 \times 1.6094 = 0.4601$$
$$e^{0.4601} = 1.5842$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.5842 = 475.26 \; K$$

**Step 2: Isentropic efficiency**

$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{475.26 - 300}{470 - 300} = \frac{175.26}{170} = 1.0309$$

**Wait! This is greater than 1!**

This indicates the problem data is physically inconsistent (actual temperature is *less* than isentropic). Let me re-check — perhaps $T_2$ should be greater.

**Corrected Problem:** Use $T_2 = 500 \; K$.

$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{475.26 - 300}{500 - 300} = \frac{175.26}{200} = 0.876 \; \text{or} \; 87.6\%$$

**Step 3: Polytropic efficiency**

$$\eta_{poly} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{\ln(P_2 / P_1)}{\ln(T_2 / T_1)}$$

$$\eta_{poly} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} \cdot \frac{\ln(5)}{\ln(500/300)}$$

$$\eta_{poly} = 0.2857 \cdot \frac{1.6094}{\ln(1.6667)}$$

$$\ln(1.6667) = 0.5108$$

$$\eta_{poly} = 0.2857 \cdot \frac{1.6094}{0.5108} = 0.2857 \cdot 3.1511 = 0.9005 \; \text{or} \; 90.05\%$$

**Step 4: Comparison**

- $\eta_{poly} = 90.05\%$
- $\eta_{isen} = 87.6\%$

The polytropic efficiency is **higher** than the isentropic efficiency. This is **always true** for compressors and reflects that the overall isentropic efficiency is "diluted" by the cumulative effect of losses over the entire pressure ratio.

### Insight:
> A high polytropic efficiency with a moderate isentropic efficiency indicates a machine with many stages (high $r_p$). For aircraft engines, $\eta_{poly} \approx 90\%$ is excellent.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of Efficiencies in Turbo Machinery

The following diagram maps the hierarchical relationship between the various efficiencies and the underlying losses that drive them. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as per KTU standards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputEnergy[Input Energy: Fuel, Shaft Work, or Kinetic Energy]:::input
    RealProcess[Real Process: Irreversible with Entropy Generation]:::process
    IdealProcess[Ideal Isentropic Process: Reversible, Constant Entropy]:::ideal

    InputEnergy --> RealProcess
    InputEnergy --> IdealProcess

    RealProcess --> LossesBlock[Losses]:::loss
    LossesBlock --> AerodynLoss[Aerodynamic Losses: Profile, Tip Clearance, Secondary]:::loss
    LossesBlock --> MechLoss[Mechanical Losses: Bearing, Windage, Seal]:::loss
    LossesBlock --> ThermoLoss[Thermodynamic Losses: Heat Transfer, Leakage]:::loss
    LossesBlock --> IncidLoss[Incidence Losses: Non-optimal Angle of Attack]:::loss

    RealProcess --> IsenEff[Isentropic Efficiency]:::eff
    RealProcess --> PolyEff[Polytropic Efficiency]:::eff
    RealProcess --> StageEff[Stage Efficiency]:::eff
    RealProcess --> MechEff[Mechanical Efficiency]:::eff

    IdealProcess -.comparison.-> IsenEff
    IdealProcess -.comparison.-> PolyEff
    IdealProcess -.comparison.-> StageEff

    IsenEff --> OverallEff[Overall Efficiency]:::final
    PolyEff --> OverallEff
    StageEff --> ReheatFact[Reheat Factor RF]:::aux
    ReheatFact --> OverallEff
    MechEff --> OverallEff

    OverallEff --> OutputWork[Useful Output Work]:::output
    OverallEff --> Performance[Performance Metrics: SFC, Power Output]:::output

    classDef input fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ideal fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#9370db,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#dc143c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eff fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#98fb98,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### Reading the Diagram:

1. **Top (Blue/Orange):** The two "parallel universes" — *Real Process* and *Ideal Process*. All efficiencies emerge from comparing these.
2. **Middle-Left (Red):** The four major loss categories feeding the real process.
3. **Middle (Yellow):** Individual efficiencies — isentropic, polytropic, stage, mechanical.
4. **Middle-Right (Dark Yellow):** Reheat factor as an auxiliary multiplier.
5. **Bottom (Green):** Overall efficiency — the *convergence point*.
6. **Bottom (Purple):** Useful work and performance metrics — the *final output*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "explain the role of efficiencies in turbo machinery," trace this diagram verbally — *Input → Real/Ideal split → Losses → Efficiencies → Overall → Output*. Examiners reward **structured flow**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: T-s Diagram of Real vs. Isentropic Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    State1[State 1: Inlet, T1, P1]:::state
    State2s[State 2s: Isentropic Outlet]:::ideal
    State2[State 2: Actual Outlet, higher entropy]:::real
    State1 -->|Isentropic: constant s| State2s
    State1 -->|Real: increasing s| State2

    State1 --> Compressor[Compressor: W act > W isen]:::process
    State2s -. comparison .-> Compressor
    State2 -. produces .-> Compressor
    Compressor --> IsenEffBox[Isentropic Efficiency]:::eff

    State1 --> Turbine[Turbine: W act < W isen]:::process
    State2 -. produces .-> Turbine
    State2s -. comparison .-> Turbine
    Turbine --> IsenEffBoxT[Turbine Efficiency]:::eff

```
