## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Multi-Stage Turbine with Reheat

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Inlet1[Inlet 1: T1, P1]:::state
    Stage1[Stage 1: Actual h drop isen1]:::stage
    Outlet2[Outlet 2: T2 > T2s, P2]:::state
    Stage2[Stage 2: Reheated expansion]:::stage
    Outlet3[Outlet 3: T3 > T3s, P3]:::state
    Stage3[Stage 3: Final expansion]:::stage
    Outlet4[Outlet 4: T4, P4]:::state
    IsenFinal[Isentropic final: T4s at P4]:::ideal

    Inlet1 --> Stage1 --> Outlet2
    Outlet2 --> Stage2 --> Outlet3
    Outlet3 --> Stage3 --> Outlet4
    Inlet1 -. parallel isentropic line .-> IsenFinal
    Outlet4 -. actual end state .-> IsenFinal

    Stage1 --> SumActual[Sum of Actual Stage h drops]:::sum
    Stage2 --> SumActual
    Stage3 --> SumActual
    SumActual --> ReheatCalc[Reheat Factor RF = Sum Actual / Total Isentropic]:::rf

    Inlet1 --> IsenFinal
    IsenFinal --> TotalIsen[Total Isentropic h drop]:::sum
    ReheatCalc --> OverallEff[Overall Efficiency = Stage Eff x RF]:::final

    classDef state fill:#e0ffff,stroke:#008b8b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ideal fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#9370db,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rf fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#ff6347,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#98fb98,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### How to Interpret:

- The **solid line** path (blue/cyan) is the *actual expansion* with rising entropy.
- The **dashed line** (purple) is the *isentropic* path from inlet to final pressure.
- The Reheat Factor is the *ratio of areas* (or enthalpy drops) shown.
- $RF > 1$ because the actual final temperature $T_4$ is *higher* than $T_{4s}$, making the total actual enthalpy drop *less* than the sum of stage isentropic drops.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "isentropic efficiency" of a compressor and a turbine. Why are they defined differently?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (Compressor):** The isentropic efficiency of a compressor is the ratio of the *minimum work input* (isentropic) to the *actual work input*:
$$\eta_{c,isen} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Definition (Turbine):** The isentropic efficiency of a turbine is the ratio of the *actual work output* to the *ideal work output* (isentropic):
$$\eta_{t,isen} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

**Why Different (1 mark):** In both cases, the *ideal (isentropic) process* is in the denominator because it represents the *best achievable* performance benchmark. For a compressor, the ideal is the minimum work (numerator); for a turbine, the ideal is the maximum work (numerator). The convention is unified — *ideal in denominator* — but the sign convention of work direction flips the numerator.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**What is reheat factor? Why is it greater than unity for a multi-stage turbine?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The reheat factor (RF) of a multi-stage turbine is defined as the ratio of the sum of the actual enthalpy drops in all stages to the total isentropic enthalpy drop:
$$RF = \frac{\sum \Delta h_{act,stage}}{\Delta h_{isen,total}}$$

**Why > 1 (1.5 marks):** In a multi-stage turbine, friction in the early stages causes the fluid to leave at a *higher temperature* (and entropy) than the isentropic value. This hotter fluid then *re-expands* in subsequent stages, doing *more work* than the isentropic prediction. Therefore, the cumulative actual work is *less reduced* by losses than a simple per-stage analysis suggests. The ratio of total actual to total isentropic work is thus greater than 1, typically $RF = 1.02$ to $1.06$ for modern turbines.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is for **14 marks**, with sub-parts typically carrying **7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE** of two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define polytropic efficiency. Derive the relationship between polytropic efficiency and isentropic efficiency for a multi-stage compressor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal compressor compresses air from $1 \; bar$, $300 \; K$ to $5 \; bar$. If the isentropic efficiency is 85%, calculate:
1. The actual exit temperature.
2. The polytropic efficiency.
3. The work input per kg of air.

**Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$.**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Polytropic efficiency is the efficiency of an *infinitesimal* stage of a multi-stage machine. It is defined as:
$$\eta_{poly} = \frac{\text{Isentropic work for an infinitesimal stage}}{\text{Actual work for an infinitesimal stage}}$$

It is essentially *constant* for all stages of a machine, whereas the overall isentropic efficiency drops as the total pressure ratio increases.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For an isentropic process from $P_1$ to $P_2$:
$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

For a real (polytropic) process with polytropic index $n$:
$$T_2 = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$$

The actual work input:
$$W_{act} = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

The isentropic work input:
$$W_{isen} = C_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = C_p T_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

Therefore, the isentropic efficiency is:
$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1}$$

For the polytropic process:
$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{poly}}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\eta_{isen} = \frac{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \eta_{poly}}} - 1}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1 \; bar$, $T_1 = 300 \; K$
- $P_2 = 5 \; bar$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.85$
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$

**Step 1: Calculate isentropic exit temperature (1 mark)**
$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 300 \times (5)^{0.2857}$$

Computing $(5)^{0.2857}$:
$$(5)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln 5} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.4601} = 1.5842$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.5842 = 475.26 \; K$$

**Step 2: Calculate actual exit temperature (2 marks)**

Using:
$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$

$$0.85 = \frac{475.26 - 300}{T_2 - 300} = \frac{175.26}{T_2 - 300}$$

$$T_2 - 300 = \frac{175.26}{0.85} = 206.19$$

$$T_2 = 506.19 \; K$$

**Step 3: Calculate polytropic efficiency (2 marks)**

$$\eta_{poly} = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{\ln(P_2 / P_1)}{\ln(T_2 / T_1)}$$

$$\eta_{poly} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} \cdot \frac{\ln(5)}{\ln(506.19/300)}$$

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$

$$\ln(506.19/300) = \ln(1.6873) = 0.5231$$

$$\eta_{poly} = 0.2857 \times \frac{1.6094}{0.5231} = 0.2857 \times 3.0768 = 0.8790$$

$$\eta_{poly} \approx 87.9\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate work input per kg (2 marks)**

$$W_{act} = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (506.19 - 300) = 1.005 \times 206.19 = 207.22 \; kJ/kg$$

**Final Answers:**
1. Actual exit temperature: $T_2 \approx 506.2 \; K$
2. Polytropic efficiency: $\eta_{poly} \approx 87.9\%$
3. Work input: $W_{act} \approx 207.2 \; kJ/kg$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Definition:** Students often write $\eta_c = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_{2s} - T_1)$ — this is the *inverse*! Always *ideal in denominator*.
> 2. **Power vs. Energy:** Forgetting to multiply by mass flow rate. Question asks per kg, so this is safe, but read carefully.
> 3. **Logarithm Calculation:** $\ln$ (natural) vs. $\log_{10}$. For polytropic efficiency, ALWAYS use natural log. Using $\log_{10}$ gives a wrong answer by factor 2.3026.
> 4. **Units:** $T$ in K (not °C) for all gas law applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various losses in axial flow turbines. Discuss how they affect the isentropic efficiency of a turbine stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-stage turbine receives steam at $20 \; bar$, $400^\circ C$ and exhausts at $0.1 \; bar$. The isentropic efficiency of each stage is 80%. Calculate:
1. The reheat factor.
2. The overall isentropic efficiency.

**Use steam tables as needed.**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Losses in Axial Flow Turbines:**

**Classification (3 marks):** The major losses in axial flow turbines are:

1. **Nozzle (Stator) Losses:**
   - **Friction Loss:** Friction between steam and nozzle walls; reduces exit kinetic energy.
   - **Shock Loss:** When nozzle is not fully filled (partial admission).
   - **Windage Loss:** Rotor blades moving through stationary steam.

2. **Rotor (Moving Blade) Losses:**
   - **Blade Friction Loss:** Friction on the rotor blade surface.
   - **Tip Leakage Loss:** Steam leaks past blade tips due to pressure difference.
   - **Secondary Flow Loss:** Vortices in blade passages due to end-wall effects.

3. **Stage Losses:**
   - **Incidence Loss:** Non-optimal angle of attack at rotor inlet.
   - **Leaving Loss:** Residual kinetic energy at rotor exit.
   - **Clearance Loss:** Steam leakage through labyrinth seals.

4. **Mechanical Losses:**
   - **Bearing Friction:** In shaft bearings.
   - **Disc Friction (Windage):** Rotor disc rotating in steam.

**Effect on Isentropic Efficiency (4 marks):**

Each loss manifests as an *increase in entropy* or a *reduction in work output*. The isentropic efficiency:
$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{\text{Actual work}}{\text{Isentropic work}}$$

is *reduced* by:
- **Profile Loss** — Increases $\Delta h_{loss}$ per stage. A typical value: $\Delta h_{loss} / (U^2/2) = 0.05$ to $0.15$.
- **Tip Clearance Loss** — Strongly dependent on clearance/blade-height ratio. Doubling the clearance can drop $\eta_{isen}$ by 2-3%.
- **Incidence Loss** — Non-optimal $i$ angle. Loss $\propto i^3$ for small $i$.
- **Leaving Loss** — Kinetic energy not converted to work. Critical at low $U/C_0$ ratios.

The combined effect typically gives stage efficiencies of 80-90% for modern axial turbines. In multi-stage machines, the reheat factor partially compensates for these losses.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Inlet: $P_1 = 20 \; bar$, $T_1 = 400^\circ C$
- Exit: $P_2 = 0.1 \; bar$
- 3 stages
- $\eta_{stage} = 0.80$ per stage

**From Steam Tables:**

At $20 \; bar$, $400^\circ C$ (superheated):
$$h_1 = 3247.6 \; kJ/kg, \quad s_1 = 7.129 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$$

At $0.1 \; bar$ (saturated):
- $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_g = 8.1502$
- $s_{fg} = 7.5009$
- $h_f = 191.8$, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$

**Step 1: Calculate isentropic exit state (1 mark)**

Since $s_1 = 7.129 < s_g = 8.1502$, the steam is wet at exit.

$$x_{2s} = \frac{s_1 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{7.129 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{6.4797}{7.5009} = 0.8639$$

$$h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 191.8 + 0.8639 \times 2392.8$$
$$h_{2s} = 191.8 + 2067.16 = 2258.96 \; kJ/kg$$

**Step 2: Total isentropic enthalpy drop (1 mark)**

$$\Delta h_{isen,total} = h_1 - h_{2s} = 3247.6 - 2258.96 = 988.64 \; kJ/kg$$

**Step 3: Calculate actual exit state and reheat factor (3 marks)**

For a single stage with isentropic efficiency 80%:
$$\eta_{stage} = \frac{\Delta h_{act,stage}}{\Delta h_{isen,stage}}$$

If all stages had the same isentropic drop and equal efficiency, the overall isentropic efficiency would also be 80% (for an ideal isentropic process split equally). However, due to reheat, the actual work is more than 80% of the isentropic work.

**Using the relation:** For equal stages with reheat, the actual final exit state lies on a higher entropy line. The reheat factor can be estimated as:
$$RF = 1 + (1 - \eta_{stage}) \cdot \frac{\text{Number of stages effect factor}}{\text{Approximation}}$$

A more accurate approach: For 3 equal stages with $\eta_{stage} = 0.8$, the reheat factor is typically:

$$RF \approx 1.03 \text{ to } 1.05$$

For this calculation, using a typical reheat factor for a 3-stage turbine with $\eta_{stage} = 0.8$:

$$RF = \frac{\text{Total actual work}}{\text{Total isentropic work}}$$

Since $\eta_{stage} = 0.8$ would be the overall efficiency *if* the actual exit state were on the isentropic line. With reheat:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{stage} \times RF$$

The overall isentropic efficiency is:
$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2,act}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

**Step 4: For a typical 3-stage steam turbine, $RF \approx 1.05$ (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.80 \times 1.05 = 0.84 \; \text{or} \; 84\%$$

**Step 5: Calculate actual work output:**

$$\Delta h_{act,total} = \eta_{overall} \times \Delta h_{isen,total} = 0.84 \times 988.64 = 830.46 \; kJ/kg$$

**Final Answers:**
1. Reheat factor: $RF \approx 1.05$
2. Overall isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{overall} \approx 84\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Steam Table Errors:** Mixing up $s$ vs $h$. Always verify with both saturation and superheat tables.
> 2. **Quality Calculation:** $x_{2s}$ may exceed 1 (superheated exit) for high pressure ratios. Check before assuming wet steam.
> 3. **Reheat Factor Sign:** $RF > 1$ for turbines but $RF < 1$ for compressors (losses make exit hotter, so cumulative actual work is *less* than isentropic in compressors).
> 4. **Stage vs. Overall:** Don't confuse $\eta_{stage}$ with $\eta_{overall}$. They differ by the reheat factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3, Efficiencies and Losses**

### Core Definitions
- [x] **Isentropic efficiency** — ratio of ideal work to actual work (compressor) or actual work to ideal work (turbine).
- [x] **Polytropic efficiency** — efficiency of an infinitesimal stage; constant across stages.
- [x] **Mechanical efficiency** — accounts for bearing, seal, and windage losses.
- [x] **Reheat factor** — ratio of cumulative actual work to cumulative isentropic work; $RF > 1$ for turbines, $RF < 1$ for compressors.
- [x] **Degree of reaction** — ratio of rotor enthalpy drop to stage enthalpy drop.

### Critical Numerical Formulas
- [x] $\eta_{c,isen} = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ (Compressor)
- [x] $\eta_{t,isen} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{2s}}$ (Turbine)
- [x] $\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(P_2/P_1)}{\ln(T_2/T_1)}$ (Universal)
- [x] $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{stage} \times RF$
- [x] $T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ (Isentropic relation)

### Loss Categories (Memorize for 7-Mark Questions)
- [x] **Aerodynamic:** Profile, Tip Clearance, Secondary, Annulus, Incidence.
- [x] **Mechanical:** Bearing Friction, Windage, Seal Friction.
- [x] **Thermodynamic:** Heat Transfer, Leakage.
- [x] **Stage Losses:** Nozzle loss, Rotor loss, Leaving loss.

### Common Sign Conventions
- [x] **Compressor:** $T_2 > T_{2s}$, $W_{act} > W_{isen}$, $\eta = W_{isen}/W_{act} < 1$.
- [x] **Turbine:** $T_2 > T_{2s}$ (actual hotter), $W_{act} < W_{isen}$, $\eta = W_{act}/W_{isen} < 1$.
- [x] **Reheat:** $RF > 1$ for turbines, $RF < 1$ for compressors (due to entropy generation direction).

### KTU High-Yield Insights
- [x] $\eta_{poly} > \eta_{isen}$ for compressors (always).
- [x] For turbines, $\eta_{poly} > \eta_{isen}$ as well; RF compensates.
- [x] High pressure ratio → low $\eta_{isen}$ (with same $\eta_{poly}$).
- [x] Modern aero-engine compressors: $\eta_{poly} \approx 90\%$.
- [x] Steam turbines: $\eta_{stage} \approx 80-85\%$, $RF \approx 1.03-1.06$.

### Examiner's Top Pitfalls
- [x] Forgetting to convert $^\circ C$ to $K$ for gas law calculations.
- [x] Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ in polytropic efficiency formula.
- [x] Inverting the efficiency definition (ideal in numerator instead of denominator).
- [x] Confusing reheat factor for turbines vs. compressors.
- [x] Mixing up compressor and turbine formulas (sign errors).

### Cross-Connections to Other Modules
- [x] **Module 1 (Thermodynamics):** Isentropic relations, $T$-$s$ diagrams.
- [x] **Module 2 (Velocity Triangles):** Degree of reaction, blade efficiency.
- [x] **Module 4 (Design):** Loss coefficients, stage loading, flow coefficient.

> **End of Notes — Efficiencies and Losses (PEMET411 Module 3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reaction Turbines - Francis Turbine: Principle of Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reaction Turbines - Francis Turbine: Principle of Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Francis Turbine** is a **mixed-flow, reaction-type hydraulic turbine** in which water enters the runner radially (at the inlet) and leaves axially (at the outlet), undergoing a pressure drop across the runner blades. It is the most widely used hydro-turbine in the world, designed to operate efficiently over a **medium head range of 30 m to 600 m** and with **discharges ranging from 1 m³/s to 700 m³/s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET411 Module 3):** A Francis Turbine is classified as a **Reaction Turbine** because the working fluid (water) undergoes a **continuous pressure drop** as it flows through the rotor. This is fundamentally different from an Impulse Turbine (e.g., Pelton Wheel), where the entire pressure drop occurs in the nozzle and the runner operates at atmospheric pressure.

The Francis Turbine is named after **James B. Francis** (1815–1892), an American engineer who developed the first mixed-flow reaction turbine in **1849** at the Lowell Hydraulic Experiments in Massachusetts, USA.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a giant **underground spinning merry-go-round** (the runner) sitting inside a sealed water pipe system:
- **Spiral Casing =** The spiral staircase-shaped entry hall that distributes water evenly to every part of the merry-go-round.
- **Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates) =** The turnstile gates at a stadium entrance, which direct the water inward and control how much flow enters.
- **Runner =** The spinning blades of the merry-go-round, which catch the incoming water and get pushed by it (just like a windmill catches wind).
- **Draft Tube =** The exit slide at the end of a water park, which slows the water down so it leaves gently, recovering the leftover kinetic energy.

The water enters with **high pressure and high velocity**, gives up its energy to spin the runner, and exits with **low pressure and low velocity** at the tailrace.

## 1.3 Classification of Francis Turbine

| Parameter | Classification |
|---|---|
| Type of Action | Reaction Turbine |
| Direction of Flow at Inlet | Radial (or slightly axial-inward) |
| Direction of Flow at Outlet | Axial |
| Head Range | **30 m to 600 m** (Medium) |
| Specific Speed ($N_s$) | 60 to 450 (SI Units) |
| Number of Blades | 14 to 24 (Modern designs) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The specific speed of a Francis Turbine is **$N_s$ = 60 to 450** (in metric units), which mathematically separates it from Kaplan Turbines ($N_s$ > 450) and Pelton Turbines ($N_s$ = 8 to 30).

## 1.4 Major Components of a Francis Turbine

1. **Spiral Casing (Scroll Casing):** A spiral-shaped passage of gradually decreasing cross-section that delivers water uniformly around the entire circumference of the guide vane ring.
2. **Stay Ring / Speed Ring:** A stationary ring supporting the guide vanes and providing a fixed reference.
3. **Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates):** Movable vanes that regulate the flow rate and the direction of water entering the runner. They are controlled by a **regulator**.
4. **Runner:** The rotating heart of the turbine consisting of curved blades. Water flows between these blades and transfers its energy to the runner.
5. **Draft Tube:** A gradually expanding passage at the runner outlet that converts the residual kinetic energy into pressure energy and discharges water to the tailrace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Draft Tube?** It allows the turbine to be installed **above the tailrace water level** (suction head) without losing the head corresponding to that elevation, while simultaneously recovering the kinetic energy of the water leaving the runner.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Working (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Francis Turbine operates on the principle of **impulse-reaction** combined with **pressure drop within the runner**. The step-by-step energy conversion sequence is:

1. **High-Pressure Water Entry:** Water from the penstock enters the **spiral casing** at high pressure. The casing is designed with a **uniform velocity of flow ($V_f$)** at all radial sections, meaning the flow speed does not change even though the cross-section area changes (this is achieved by decreasing the cross-sectional area as the spiral wraps tighter).

2. **Flow Regulation & Direction Change:** Water then enters the **guide vane ring (wicket gates)**, where the stationary guide vanes convert a portion of the **pressure energy into kinetic energy** and direct the water tangentially onto the runner blades. The angle of the guide vanes can be adjusted to control the flow rate.

3. **Energy Transfer in the Runner:** Water enters the runner **radially** through the space between the guide vanes and the runner. As it flows over the curved runner blades, **both pressure energy and kinetic energy** are transferred to the runner. The pressure at the outlet of the runner is **below atmospheric** (suction pressure).

4. **Exit via Draft Tube:** Water leaves the runner **axially** and enters the **draft tube**, which has a **gradually increasing cross-sectional area**. According to Bernoulli's principle, the velocity decreases and the pressure rises (regaining some useful head). The water finally discharges to the **tailrace** at atmospheric pressure.

5. **Energy Conversion Summary:**
$$\text{Total Head} (H) = \text{Kinetic Head} + \text{Pressure Head} + \text{Head Loss}$$

## 2.2 Pressure and Velocity Variations Along the Flow Path

| Location | Velocity of Flow ($V_f$) | Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| Penstock Inlet | Low | Very High |
| Spiral Casing | Constant (designed) | Decreases gradually |
| Guide Vane Outlet | High | Decreases further |
| Runner Inlet | High | Below atmospheric at outlet |
| Runner Outlet | Decreasing along blade | Lowest (sub-atmospheric) |
| Draft Tube Outlet | Very Low | Atmospheric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The pressure at the runner outlet ($P_2$) is **less than atmospheric pressure**. This is what distinguishes a reaction turbine from an impulse turbine, where the runner operates entirely at atmospheric pressure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H$ | Net head available to turbine | $H = H_g - h_f$ | m |
| $V_1$ | Absolute velocity of water at runner inlet | $V_1 = \sqrt{V_{f1}^2 + V_{w1}^2}$ | m/s |
| $V_2$ | Absolute velocity of water at runner outlet | $V_2 = \sqrt{V_{f2}^2 + V_{w2}^2}$ | m/s |
| $V_{w1}$ | Whirl velocity at inlet | $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$ | m/s |
| $V_{w2}$ | Whirl velocity at outlet | $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos \alpha_{90} = 0$ (axial exit) | m/s |
| $V_{f1}$ | Flow velocity at inlet | $V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1$ | m/s |
| $V_{f2}$ | Flow velocity at outlet | $V_{f2} = V_2 \sin \beta_2$ | m/s |
| $u_1$ | Blade velocity at inlet | $u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ | m/s |
| $u_2$ | Blade velocity at outlet | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | m/s |
| $W$ | Work done per second per unit weight | $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m |
| $\eta_h$ | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g H}$ | — |
| $R$ | Degree of reaction | $R = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{2gH} + 1$ (varies) | — |
| $N_s$ | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | SI Units |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention in Formula:** Use **negative sign** for **$V_{w2} u_2$** when whirl at outlet is in the **opposite direction** to that at inlet (typical Francis case with $\beta_2 < 90°$). Use **positive** when whirl is in the same direction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Francis Turbine is the **workhorse of global hydroelectric power**, accounting for approximately **60% to 70%** of all installed hydro-turbine capacity worldwide. Notable applications include:

- **Large Hydropower Dams:** Hoover Dam (USA), Three Gorges Dam (China), Bhakra Nangal (India), Idukki (Kerala), and Koyna (Maharashtra).
- **Medium-Head Pumped Storage Plants:** Used in reversible pump-turbine configurations for grid energy storage.
- **Run-of-the-River Plants:** Small and micro-hydro installations in hilly regions like Kerala, Himachal Pradesh, and Uttarakhand.

The efficiency of a modern Francis Turbine routinely exceeds **90%**, making it the most efficient prime mover ever designed by humans.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Work Done (Euler's Equation for Francis Turbine)

We start from **Euler's Turbomachine Equation** applied to the control volume of the Francis Runner.

**Step 1 — State Euler's General Equation:**

The work done per unit weight of water (in meters of head) is given by:

$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to Francis Turbine (Axial Discharge):**

For a Francis Turbine, water leaves the runner **axially**. This means the absolute velocity at exit $V_2$ is purely axial. Therefore, the whirl component at exit is:

$$
V_{w2} = V_2 \cos 90^{\circ} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Work Equation:**

Substituting $V_{w2} = 0$ into Euler's equation:

$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Express Whirl Velocity in Terms of Absolute Velocity and Guide Vane Angle:**

From the inlet velocity triangle, the whirl component is:

$$
V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1
$$

where $\alpha_1$ is the guide vane angle at the inlet.

**Step 5 — Substitute Back into the Work Equation:**

$$
W = \frac{V_1 \cos \alpha_1 \cdot u_1}{g}
$$

**Step 6 — Hydraulic Efficiency:**

Hydraulic efficiency is defined as the ratio of work done to the energy supplied per unit weight:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{\text{Work done per unit weight}}{\text{Head available}} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g H}
$$

**Step 7 — Blade Velocity at Inlet:**

The peripheral (blade) velocity at the inlet diameter $D_1$ for a runner rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute is:

$$
u_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60}
$$

This is a key result for KTU problems. Combining Steps 5, 6, and 7:

$$
W = \frac{V_1 \cos \alpha_1 \cdot \pi D_1 N}{60 \, g}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Degree of Reaction for Francis Turbine

The **Degree of Reaction ($R$)** is defined as the ratio of the change in pressure energy (or head) across the rotor to the total change in head.

**Step 1 — Starting Definition:**

$$
R = \frac{\text{Change in pressure head across runner}}{\text{Total change in head across runner}}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Generalized Formula (Khan Equation):**

For a turbomachine with both whirl and flow components, the degree of reaction is expressed as:

$$
R = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{2gH} + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Francis Turbine Condition ($V_{w2} = 0$):**

$$
R = \frac{0 \cdot u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{2gH} + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
R = 1 - \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{2gH}
$$

**Step 5 — Note on $\beta_2$ Angle:**

For an ideal Francis Turbine, the relative velocity at exit is designed to be **purely radial** (i.e., $\beta_2 = 90°$ from the tangential direction). This gives $V_{w2} = 0$ and also makes the outlet triangle symmetric. In this ideal case, the degree of reaction is exactly **0.5 (50%)**, which means equal parts of the energy conversion are due to pressure change and kinetic energy change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** For a **standard Francis Turbine**, the degree of reaction **R lies between 0.4 and 0.6**, with the ideal design value being **R = 0.5**. This is a frequently asked 2-mark or 3-mark question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Other Turbines

| Parameter | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Impulse | Reaction | Reaction |
| Head Range | High (300 m – 2000 m) | Medium (30 m – 600 m) | Low (below 30 m) |
| Flow Direction | Tangential | Radial → Axial | Axial |
| Specific Speed ($N_s$) | 8 – 30 | 60 – 450 | 450 – 1200 |
| Runner Blades | Bucket-shaped | Curved, fixed | Adjustable (propeller) |
| Draft Tube | Not required | Essential | Essential |
| Degree of Reaction | 0 | 0.4 – 0.6 | > 0.6 |
| Pressure in Runner | Atmospheric | Sub-atmospheric | Sub-atmospheric |

## 3.4 Specific Speed Derivation Hint

The specific speed of a Francis Turbine is given by:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

This is derived by dimensional analysis of the three governing variables (speed $N$, power $P$, head $H$) and is the key parameter used to **classify** a turbine as Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Francis Turbine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RESERVOIR PENSTOCK] --> A2[SPIRAL CASING]
    A2 --> A3[STAY RING]
    A3 --> A4[GUIDE VANES WICKET GATES]
    A4 --> A5[RUNNER MIXED FLOW]
    A5 --> A6[DRAFT TUBE]
    A6 --> A7[TAILRACE ATMOSPHERIC]

    A1 -- High Pressure Water --> A2
    A2 -- Constant Vf Uniform Distribution --> A4
    A4 -- Tangential Entry Controlled Flow --> A5
    A5 -- Pressure Drop Energy Transfer --> A6
    A6 -- Velocity Reduction Pressure Recovery --> A7

    A5 -- Rotational Shaft --> A8[GENERATOR]
    A8 -- Electrical Power --> A9[GRID SUPPLY]

    style A1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style A3 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A5 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style A6 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style A7 fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style A8 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style A9 fill:#404040,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Inlet Velocity Triangle (Francis Turbine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[BLADE VELOCITY U1 TANGENTIAL] --> B2[ABSOLUTE VELOCITY V1 AT ANGLE ALPHA1]
    B2 --> B3[RELATIVE VELOCITY W1 AT ANGLE BETA1]
    B3 --> B4[FLOW VELOCITY VF1 RADIAL]
    B4 --> B5[WHIRL VELOCITY VW1 = V1 COS ALPHA1]

    style B1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style B4 fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style B5 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Inlet Triangle:** $V_1$ enters at the guide vane angle $\alpha_1$ to the tangential direction. It splits into:
> - $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$ (tangential whirl component, the one that does work)
> - $V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1$ (radial flow component, perpendicular to blade motion)
>
> The relative velocity $W_1$ is the vector difference: $\vec{W_1} = \vec{V_1} - \vec{u_1}$.

## 4.3 Outlet Velocity Triangle (Francis Turbine — Axial Exit)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[BLADE VELOCITY U2] --> C2[ABSOLUTE VELOCITY V2 PURELY AXIAL]
    C2 --> C3[VW2 = ZERO COS 90 DEG]
    C2 --> C4[VF2 = V2 AXIAL]
    C4 --> C5[RELATIVE VELOCITY W2 AT ANGLE BETA2]

    style C1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style C3 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style C4 fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style C5 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Diagram:** The outlet velocity triangle is the signature feature of the Francis Turbine. Because $V_{w2} = 0$ (axial exit), the work equation simplifies to $W = V_{w1} u_1 / g$. This is the single most important deduction in this module.

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional View of Francis Turbine (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[SPIRAL CASING TOP INLET] --> D2[STAY RING GUIDE VANE ASSEMBLY]
    D2 --> D3[RUNNER BLADES MIXED FLOW]
    D3 --> D4[DRAFT TUBE FUNNEL SHAPED]
    D4 --> D5[TAILRACE OUTLET BOTTOM]

    D2 -.- D2A[STATIONARY]
    D3 -.- D3A[ROTATING]
    D4 -.- D4A[STATIONARY CONICAL]

    style D1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style D2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style D3 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style D4 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style D5 fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style D2A fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666,color:#000
    style D3A fill:#ffe699,stroke:#666,color:#000
    style D4A fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the principle of working of a Francis Turbine. Why is it called a mixed-flow turbine?**

**Model Answer:**

A Francis Turbine is a reaction-type hydraulic turbine where water enters the runner **radially** and leaves **axially**, hence the name **mixed-flow turbine**.

**Principle of Working:**
1. Water from the penstock enters the **spiral casing**, which delivers it uniformly around the runner circumference at a **constant velocity of flow ($V_f$)**.
2. Water then passes through the **guide vanes (wicket gates)**, which convert pressure energy to kinetic energy and direct the water tangentially onto the runner blades at a pre-determined angle.
3. Inside the **runner**, the water flows over the curved blades. As it follows the curved path, it exerts a force on the blades, causing the runner to rotate. The **pressure of water decreases** as it flows through the runner.
4. Water leaves the runner **axially** and enters the **draft tube**, which converts the residual kinetic energy into pressure energy before discharging to the tailrace.

It is called mixed-flow because the water **enters radially and exits axially** within the same runner.

*(Marks Distribution: Definition – 1, Working Steps – 1.5, Reason for "mixed-flow" – 0.5)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List the main components of a Francis Turbine and state the function of the draft tube.**

**Model Answer:**

**Main Components of a Francis Turbine:**
1. Spiral Casing
2. Stay Ring
3. Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates)
4. Runner with Blades
5. Draft Tube
6. Shaft and Bearings

**Function of the Draft Tube:**
- It is a **gradually expanding passage** at the runner outlet.
- It converts the **kinetic energy** of water leaving the runner into **pressure energy** (suction head recovery).
- It allows the turbine to be set **above the tailrace level**, reducing civil construction cost.
- It increases the **net head** available to the turbine, thereby improving efficiency.

*(Marks Distribution: Components list – 1, Draft tube functions – 2)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, and an **internal choice** is provided. Below, both alternatives are solved completely.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the work done per second per unit weight of water passing through a Francis Turbine. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A Francis Turbine has an inlet diameter of 1.2 m and an outlet diameter of 0.9 m. The velocity of whirl at inlet is 25 m/s and at outlet is 0 m/s. The blade velocity at inlet is 20 m/s. If the net head available is 50 m, calculate: (i) Work done per unit weight, (ii) Hydraulic efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

For a Francis Turbine, water exits the runner **axially**, so the whirl component at outlet is zero:
$$
V_{w2} = V_2 \cos 90^{\circ} = 0
$$

Applying **Euler's turbomachine equation** to the control volume of the runner:
$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g}
$$

Substituting $V_{w2} = 0$ and choosing the appropriate sign:
$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}
$$

Expressing the whirl velocity in terms of the guide vane angle:
$$
V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1
$$

Therefore:
$$
W = \frac{V_1 \cos \alpha_1 \cdot u_1}{g}
$$

**Assumptions Made:**
1. The flow through the runner is **steady, incompressible, and one-dimensional**.
2. Water leaves the runner **purely axially** ($V_{w2} = 0$).
3. There are **no friction losses** in the ideal analysis.
4. The velocity triangles are defined at the **mean diameter** of the runner.

*(7 marks: Derivation – 4, Assumptions – 3)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Inlet diameter $D_1 = 1.2$ m
- Outlet diameter $D_2 = 0.9$ m
- $V_{w1} = 25$ m/s
- $V_{w2} = 0$ m/s
- $u_1 = 20$ m/s
- $H = 50$ m

**(i) Work done per unit weight:**

Applying the simplified Euler's equation for a Francis Turbine:
$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g} = \frac{25 \times 20}{9.81}
$$

$$
W = \frac{500}{9.81} = 50.97 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Hydraulic efficiency:**

$$
\eta_h = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{50.97}{50}
$$

$$
\eta_h = 1.0194 = 101.94\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Excess Efficiency Alert:** A value greater than 100% indicates that the assumed $V_{w1}$ and $u_1$ are **not physically realizable** for the given head. In a real exam, state this clearly: *'The hydraulic efficiency exceeding 100% implies the assumed velocity values are inconsistent with the given head; this is typically a sign that the data in the problem may need reinterpretation, or velocity coefficients should be applied.'*

*(7 marks: Work done – 3, Efficiency – 3, Comment on validity – 1)*

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a velocity triangle diagram the energy transfer in a Francis Turbine runner. What is the significance of the outlet velocity triangle being purely axial?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Francis Turbine operates at 300 rpm under a head of 60 m. The runner inlet diameter is 1.0 m. If the guide vane angle is 20° and the flow ratio $\psi = V_{f1}/u_1 = 0.25$, determine: (i) Blade velocity at inlet, (ii) Absolute velocity of water at inlet, (iii) Whirl velocity at inlet, (iv) Work done per unit weight of water.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Energy Transfer in Francis Runner:**

The velocity triangle at the inlet of the Francis Runner consists of three vectors:
- $\vec{V_1}$ — Absolute velocity of water entering the runner (at angle $\alpha_1$ to tangential direction)
- $\vec{u_1}$ — Blade (peripheral) velocity of the runner at the inlet
- $\vec{W_1}$ — Relative velocity of water with respect to the moving blade (at angle $\beta_1$ to tangential direction)

The **vector relationship** is:
$$
\vec{V_1} = \vec{u_1} + \vec{W_1}
$$

**Component breakdown:**
- **Whirl component** (tangential): $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$ — responsible for doing work on the runner.
- **Flow component** (radial): $V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1$ — this is the flow passing through the blade channels without doing tangential work.

The **outlet velocity triangle** has its absolute velocity $V_2$ purely axial, meaning $V_{w2} = 0$. The relative velocity $W_2$ leaves the blade at angle $\beta_2$ (which is often 90° in the ideal design, making $W_2$ purely radial).

**Significance of Purely Axial Outlet:**
1. It **simplifies Euler's equation** to $W = V_{w1} u_1 / g$.
2. It ensures **no residual kinetic energy** in the whirl direction — maximum energy is extracted.
3. The axial exit is naturally aligned with the **draft tube**, allowing smooth flow recovery.
4. The relative velocity at exit is then purely radial, which means **smooth entry** into the draft tube without recirculation losses.

*(7 marks: Diagram description – 3, Energy transfer logic – 2, Significance – 2)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Speed $N = 300$ rpm
- Net head $H = 60$ m
- Inlet diameter $D_1 = 1.0$ m
- Guide vane angle $\alpha_1 = 20°$
- Flow ratio $\psi = V_{f1}/u_1 = 0.25$
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Blade velocity at inlet:**

$$
u_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 300}{60}
$$

$$
u_1 = \frac{300 \pi}{60} = 5\pi \approx 15.708 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Absolute velocity of water at inlet:**

From the flow ratio, $V_{f1} = 0.25 \, u_1 = 0.25 \times 15.708 = 3.927$ m/s.

From the inlet velocity triangle:
$$
\tan \alpha_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{w1} = \frac{V_{f1}}{\tan \alpha_1}
$$

$$
V_{w1} = \frac{3.927}{\tan 20°} = \frac{3.927}{0.36397} = 10.79 \text{ m/s}
$$

Absolute velocity:
$$
V_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{\sin \alpha_1} = \frac{3.927}{\sin 20°} = \frac{3.927}{0.3420} = 11.48 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Whirl velocity at inlet:**

Already computed in step (ii):
$$
V_{w1} = 10.79 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Work done per unit weight of water:**

$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g} = \frac{10.79 \times 15.708}{9.81}
$$

$$
W = \frac{169.49}{9.81} = 17.28 \text{ m}
$$

**Verification of Hydraulic Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_h = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{17.28}{60} = 0.288 = 28.8\%
$$

*(7 marks: Each sub-part approximately 1.5–2 marks; final answer with units – 1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the whirl component at outlet when $\beta_2 < 90°$ — this flips the sign of the work term.
> 2. **Mixing $V_1$ with $V_{w1}$** in the work equation. Only the **whirl component** $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$ does work, NOT the full $V_1$.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert** $N$ from rpm to rad/s or using $\pi D N / 60$ correctly for blade velocity.
> 4. **Confusing $V_f$ (flow velocity)** with $V_w$ (whirl velocity) in the velocity triangle.
> 5. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks for missing SI units.
> 6. **Hydraulic efficiency > 100%** is a physically impossible red flag — always verify the sanity of the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Francis Turbine is a mixed-flow reaction turbine** — water enters radially, exits axially.
- It operates in the **medium head range (30–600 m)** and has a **specific speed $N_s$ between 60 and 450**.
- The **pressure at the runner outlet is below atmospheric** — this is the defining feature of reaction turbines.
- **Euler's equation for Francis Turbine** simplifies to $W = V_{w1} u_1 / g$ because $V_{w2} = 0$ (axial exit).
- **Degree of Reaction (R) for Francis** lies between **0.4 and 0.6**, with the ideal design value being **R = 0.5**.
- The **spiral casing maintains a constant velocity of flow ($V_f$)** at all radial sections, ensuring uniform water distribution.
- The **draft tube** serves three purposes: (1) velocity reduction, (2) pressure recovery, (3) safe installation above tailrace.
- **Guide vanes (wicket gates)** regulate flow rate and direct water tangentially to the runner.
- The **velocity triangle** has three vectors: $V_1$ (absolute), $u_1$ (blade), $W_1$ (relative) at inlet; $V_2$ (axial), $u_2$ (blade), $W_2$ (relative) at outlet.
- The **relative velocity at outlet** is designed to be radial ($\beta_2 = 90°$) for ideal energy extraction.
- **Blade velocity** at any section: $u = \pi D N / 60$ (where $D$ is the local runner diameter).
- **Hydraulic efficiency:** $\eta_h = V_{w1} u_1 / (g H)$.
- **Specific speed:** $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ is the key parameter for turbine selection.
- **Francis turbines are the most widely used hydro-turbines worldwide** (60–70% of installed hydro capacity).
- **Order of classification by $N_s$:** Pelton (8–30) → Francis (60–450) → Kaplan (450–1200).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Triangles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Velocity Triangles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — VELOCITY TRIANGLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Triangles** are vector diagrams that graphically represent the **absolute velocity**, **relative velocity**, and **blade (or rotor) velocity** of a fluid particle as it enters and leaves the rotor of a turbomachine. They are constructed by the parallelogram law of vector addition at the **inlet (station 1)** and **outlet (station 2)** of the impeller/rotor blade passages.

The three fundamental velocity vectors involved are:

| Symbol | Velocity | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **C** | Absolute Velocity | Velocity of fluid relative to the stationary casing (fixed frame) |
| **W** | Relative Velocity | Velocity of fluid relative to the rotating blade (moving frame) |
| **U** | Blade/Rotor Velocity | Linear tangential velocity of the blade at the mean radius |

These three vectors are related by the **fundamental vector triangle equation**:

$$\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$$

or equivalently,

$$\vec{W} = \vec{C} - \vec{U}$$

Each velocity triangle is characterized by:
- **α (alpha)** → Absolute flow angle: angle between **C** and **U**
- **β (beta)** → Relative flow angle: angle between **W** and the negative direction of **U**
- **θ (theta) / φ (phi)** → Blade angle: the angle made by the blade surface with the tangential direction

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — Rain on a moving car windshield:**
>
> Imagine you are standing on a footpath watching rain fall **vertically** (this is the **absolute velocity C**). Now imagine sitting in a moving car — the rain appears to strike the windshield at an **inclined angle** (this is the **relative velocity W**, the rain's velocity relative to the moving car). The car's own motion creates a **horizontal velocity U** that combines with the rain's vertical fall to give you the inclined strike. Velocity triangles apply this exact same idea to fluid flowing over rotating turbomachine blades.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need velocity triangles?** They allow engineers to **decompose complex 3-D fluid motion** at the rotor inlet/outlet into measurable vector components. From these components, we can directly compute the **work transfer, hydraulic efficiency, slip factor, and degree of reaction** using Euler's turbomachinery equation. **Without velocity triangles, designing a turbine or compressor blade profile is impossible.**

### 📐 Classification of Velocity Triangles by Direction

| Classification | Direction of Flow at Inlet | Examples |
|----------------|----------------------------|----------|
| **Tangential / Impulse** | Flow enters radially or tangentially | Pelton wheel |
| **Radial / Centrifugal** | Flow enters axially and exits radially | Radial flow centrifugal pump/compressor |
| **Axial** | Flow remains essentially axial | Axial flow turbine, axial compressor |
| **Mixed Flow** | Combination of radial and axial | Francis turbine, mixed flow pump |

### 🧩 Components of Each Velocity Vector

The absolute velocity **C** is resolved into:
- **Cw (whirl/tangential component)** → in the direction of blade motion **U**
- **Cf (flow/axial-radial component)** → perpendicular to blade motion (along meridional direction)

Similarly, the relative velocity **W** is resolved into:
- **Ww (relative whirl)**
- **Wf (relative flow component)**

By definition, **Wf = Cf** because the blade has no velocity component in the meridional direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition — Continuity of flow component:**
>
> The flow component (axial or radial) of the velocity is **identical** in both the absolute and relative frames because the rotor only has tangential velocity. Therefore:
> $$C_f = W_f$$
> This is a **fundamental invariant** used in nearly all velocity triangle problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Velocity Triangles

The construction of a velocity triangle follows a strict geometric logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the reference frame.**
Two frames exist:
- **Stationary (stator) frame** → vectors are absolute velocities (**C**)
- **Rotating (rotor) frame** → vectors are relative velocities (**W**)

**Step 2 — Draw the blade velocity vector.**
Vector **U** = πDN/60 is drawn first as the reference. It is always **tangential** to the mean rotor radius.

**Step 3 — Locate the flow component.**
The axial/radial flow velocity **Cf** is drawn perpendicular to **U**. This sets the geometric constraint that Cf is preserved in both frames.

**Step 4 — Construct the relative velocity.**
**W** is drawn from the tip of **U**, such that its flow component equals Cf. The angle **β** between W and −U direction defines the relative flow angle.

**Step 5 — Close the triangle with the absolute velocity.**
**C** is drawn from the origin (start of U) to the tip of W. The angle **α** between C and U is the absolute flow angle.

**Why does this work?**
Because of vector addition: $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$. The triangle is closed geometrically when all three vectors are drawn head-to-tail.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Gold)

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Blade velocity (m/s), D = diameter (m), N = rpm |
| 2 | $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ | Fundamental triangle relation |
| 3 | $C_f = W_f$ | Continuity of meridional/flow component |
| 4 | $C_w = U + W \cos\beta$ | Whirl component in terms of relative velocity |
| 5 | $C_w = C \cos\alpha$ | Whirl component in terms of absolute velocity |
| 6 | $C_f = C \sin\alpha$ | Flow component in terms of absolute velocity |
| 7 | $C_f = W \sin\beta$ | Flow component in terms of relative velocity |
| 8 | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{C_f}{C_w}$ | Absolute flow angle relation |
| 9 | $\tan\beta = \dfrac{C_f}{U - C_w}$ | Relative flow angle relation |
| 10 | $\dfrac{W}{U} = \dfrac{\sin\alpha}{\sin\beta}$ | Sine rule application to triangle |
| 11 | $\dfrac{C}{U} = \dfrac{\sin\beta}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)}$ | Sine rule application |
| 12 | $V_{r1}^2 = V_{r2}^2 \cdot \dfrac{r_1}{r_2}$ | Radial equilibrium (free vortex) |
| 13 | $W_s = \pm (U_1 C_{w1} \pm U_2 C_{w2})$ | **Euler's equation** (turbine sign convention) |
| 14 | $W_s = U \cdot \Delta C_w$ (for ΔCw alone) | Specific work in axial machines |
| 15 | $\eta_h = \dfrac{U \Delta C_w}{\dfrac{C_1^2 - C_2^2}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)}$ | Hydraulic efficiency |
| 16 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{w2}}{C_{w1}}$ | Degree of reaction for axial machines |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Alert (KTU Board Standard):**
>
> For **turbines**: Work is **done BY the fluid ON the blade** → $W_s = +U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}$ (if U1, U2, Cw1, Cw2 are positive magnitudes, subtract Cw2 since swirl at exit is often reduced or reversed).
>
> For **compressors/pumps**: Work is **done ON the fluid BY the blade** → $W_s = -(U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1})$ → magnitude: $W_s = U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}$.
>
> Always **state the sign convention explicitly** in your solution. Losing the sign = losing 2 marks.

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Turbine Design (Francis, Kaplan, Pelton):** Velocity triangles dictate **runner blade shape, blade angles, and number of blades**.
- **Jet Engine & Steam/Gas Turbine:** Axial turbine blades are designed using velocity triangles to maximize specific work output and minimize leaving losses.
- **Centrifugal Pumps & Compressors:** Inlet velocity triangles determine **pre-whirl** at impeller entry, directly affecting NPSH and efficiency.
- **Wind Turbines & Turboprop Aircraft:** Modern aerodynamic design relies on velocity triangles to optimize blade pitch for varying wind/freestream conditions.
- **Hydraulic Transmission Systems (Torque Converters):** Same triangle principle governs fluid coupling between impeller and turbine.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** In a **well-designed turbine**, the exit absolute velocity **C2** should be as nearly axial (purely Cf2) as possible so that the kinetic energy at exit is **NOT lost** (no whirl remaining to extract). This is called **"reaction leaving the runner axially"** or **"minimum kinetic energy at exit."** This principle drives the design of Francis and Kaplan runners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1: Component Resolution of C from the Inlet Velocity Triangle

**Given:**
- Absolute velocity **C1** at inlet
- Blade velocity **U1**
- Relative velocity **W1** (the fluid velocity as seen from the rotating blade)

**To Prove:** Component relationships $C_w = C \cos\alpha$ and $C_f = C \sin\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Draw the inlet velocity triangle.**
Place the origin O at the point where blade enters the fluid stream. Draw **U1** horizontally (tangent to rotor mean radius). At the tip of **U1**, draw **W1** making angle β with the negative U direction. From origin O, draw **C1** to close the triangle.

**Step 2 — Identify the right-angled components.**
Drop a perpendicular from the tip of W1 to the horizontal (U-axis). This perpendicular length = **Cf** (axial/radial flow component). The horizontal projection of C1 along U = **Cw** (tangential whirl component).

**Step 3 — Write the trigonometric ratios.**
In the right triangle formed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\alpha &= \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{C_w}{C_1} \\
\therefore C_w &= C_1 \cos\alpha \\[6pt]
\sin\alpha &= \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{C_f}{C_1} \\
\therefore C_f &= C_1 \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Result.**
$$C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1, \qquad C_f = C_1 \sin\alpha_1$$

**Conclusion:** The whirl and flow components are obtained by **projecting the absolute velocity vector** along the tangential and meridional directions respectively. This is the foundation for computing work transfer via Euler's equation.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Derivation of Euler's Turbomachinery Equation

**Statement:** The work done per unit weight of fluid (specific work) by a turbomachine is:
$$W_s = \pm(U_1 C_{w1} \pm U_2 C_{w2})$$

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's second law in tangential direction.**
The tangential force exerted by the blade on the fluid equals the **rate of change of tangential momentum** of the fluid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \dot{m}(C_{w2} - C_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate (kg/s) and the subscripts 1, 2 denote inlet and outlet.

**Step 2 — Work done per second (power).**
Power = Tangential force × Blade velocity:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_t \cdot U = \dot{m} U (C_{w2} - C_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific work (work per unit weight).**
Divide by weight flow rate $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot g$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= \frac{P}{\dot{m} \cdot g} = \frac{U(C_{w2} - C_{w1})}{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

In head form (work per unit weight in metres):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{U(C_{w2} - C_{w1})}{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalization to radial machines.**
For radial-flow machines, blade velocities differ at inlet and outlet ($U_1 \neq U_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= \frac{1}{g}(U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

For turbines: $C_{w2} < C_{w1}$ (whirl decreases), giving positive work output.
For pumps/compressors: $C_{w2} > C_{w1}$, giving positive work input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is Euler's Equation — the most fundamental and high-yield equation in the entire Turbo Machinery course.** It directly links velocity triangles to work transfer. You can lose up to **4 marks** in Part B if you state it incorrectly.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Velocity Ratio Form Using Sine Rule

**Given:** Velocity triangle with sides C, U, W and angles α, β, (α − β).

**Applying the Sine Rule** to the triangle:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{\sin\beta} &= \frac{U}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)} = \frac{W}{\sin\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for the flow coefficient φ.**
Flow coefficient is defined as $\phi = \dfrac{C_f}{U}$. Since $C_f = C \sin\alpha = W \sin\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{U} &= \frac{\sin\beta}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)} \\
\phi &= \frac{C_f}{U} = \frac{C \sin\alpha}{U} = \frac{\sin\alpha \cdot \sin\beta}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the blade-speed-to-jet-speed ratio.**
For a Pelton wheel with jet velocity $V_j$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_u &= \frac{U}{V_j} = \frac{\sin\beta_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 - \beta_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The sine rule transforms geometric triangle data into **dimensionless coefficients** that allow comparison between machines of different sizes.

---

### 📋 Analytical Matrix — Common Velocity Triangle Configurations

| Configuration | Inlet β₁ | Outlet β₂ | Remarks |
|---------------|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Impulse (Pelton)** | Very small (≈ 0°) | ≈ 165°–170° | Whirl nearly completely reversed; pressure drop in nozzle only |
| **Francis turbine (slow)** | ≈ 70°–90° | ≈ 20°–30° | Reaction ≈ 50%; mixed flow |
| **Francis turbine (fast)** | ≈ 25°–30° | ≈ 15°–20° | Nearly axial; reaction ≈ 50% |
| **Kaplan turbine** | ≈ 30°–45° | ≈ 15°–25° | Axial; reaction ≈ 50–60% |
| **Axial turbine (Curtis)** | small | small | Multi-stage impulse |
| **Centrifugal pump** | 90° (radial entry) | < 90° | Pre-whirl = 0 at design flow |

---

### 📝 Worked Example (Typical 14-mark Question Pattern)

> **Problem:** Steam enters a turbine stage at absolute velocity $C_1 = 150$ m/s at an angle $\alpha_1 = 20°$. The mean blade speed is $U = 75$ m/s. The exit relative velocity makes angle $\beta_2 = 30°$ with the tangential direction, and $W_2 = 90$ m/s. Determine: (a) Inlet relative velocity **W₁** and its angle **β₁**; (b) Outlet absolute velocity **C₂** and its angle **α₂**; (c) Specific work done per kg of steam using Euler's equation.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Step (a) — Find W₁ and β₁:**

**Step (i) — Compute inlet whirl and flow components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{w1} &= C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 150 \cos 20° = 150 \times 0.9397 \\
C_{w1} &= 140.95 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
C_f &= C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 150 \sin 20° = 150 \times 0.3420 \\
C_f &= 51.30 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Apply velocity triangle closure.**
Since $C_{w1} = U + W_1 \cos\beta_1$ (when β is measured from −U direction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 \cos\beta_1 &= C_{w1} - U = 140.95 - 75 = 65.95 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also, $W_1 \sin\beta_1 = C_f = 51.30$ m/s.

**Step (iii) — Compute magnitude and angle.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \sqrt{(W_1\cos\beta_1)^2 + (W_1\sin\beta_1)^2} \\
W_1 &= \sqrt{(65.95)^2 + (51.30)^2} = \sqrt{4349.4 + 2631.7} \\
W_1 &= \sqrt{6981.1} \approx 83.55 \text{ m/s} \\[6pt]
\tan\beta_1 &= \frac{W_1 \sin\beta_1}{W_1 \cos\beta_1} = \frac{51.30}{65.95} = 0.7779 \\
\beta_1 &= \tan^{-1}(0.7779) \approx 37.88°
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Step (b) — Find C₂ and α₂:

**Step (i) — Compute outlet whirl and flow components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{w2} &= W_2 \cos\beta_2 = 90 \cos 30° = 90 \times 0.8660 = 77.94 \text{ m/s} \\
W_{f2} &= W_2 \sin\beta_2 = 90 \sin 30° = 90 \times 0.5 = 45.00 \text{ m/s} \\
C_{w2} &= U - W_{w2} = 75 - 77.94 = -2.94 \text{ m/s} \\
C_f &= W_{f2} = 45.00 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **negative** $C_{w2}$ indicates the whirl has been **reversed** in direction (energy extracted).

**Step (ii) — Compute magnitude of C₂ and its angle.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= \sqrt{C_{w2}^2 + C_f^2} = \sqrt{(-2.94)^2 + (45)^2} \\
C_2 &= \sqrt{8.64 + 2025} = \sqrt{2033.64} \approx 45.10 \text{ m/s} \\[6pt]
\tan\alpha_2 &= \frac{C_f}{C_{w2}} = \frac{45}{-2.94} = -15.31 \\
\alpha_2 &\approx 180° - 86.26° = 93.74° \text{ (i.e., reverse whirl)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Step (c) — Specific work from Euler's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= U(C_{w1} - C_{w2}) = 75 \times [140.95 - (-2.94)] \\
W_s &= 75 \times 143.89 \\
W_s &= 10791.75 \text{ J/kg} \approx 10.79 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $W_1$ | 83.55 m/s |
| $\beta_1$ | 37.88° |
| $C_2$ | 45.10 m/s |
| $\alpha_2$ | 93.74° (reverse whirl) |
| $W_s$ | 10,791.75 J/kg |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board tip:** Always include a **neatly drawn velocity triangle** (inlet and outlet) before writing any equations. Drawing the triangle first earns you **1–2 marks** even before the calculation begins, because it demonstrates conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1: Block Diagram — Velocity Triangle Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Station 1 or 2] --> B[Define Three Velocities: C, W, U]
    B --> C[Compute Blade Velocity: U = pi D N / 60]
    C --> D[Determine Flow Component Cf from Mass Flow]
    D --> E{Choose Reference Frame}
    E -->|Stationary| F[Draw Vector U Tangential to Mean Radius]
    E -->|Rotating| G[Draw Vector Cf Perpendicular to U]
    F --> H[Mark Tip of U Vector]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Locate W at angle beta from -U direction with Wf equal Cf]
    I --> J[Close Triangle: Draw C from Origin to Tip of W]
    J --> K[Measure alpha: Angle between C and U]
    K --> L[Resolve C into Cw and Cf]
    L --> M[Apply Euler Equation: Ws = U times delta Cw]
    M --> N[Compute Specific Work and Efficiency]
    N --> O[End: Output Work Transfer and Blade Profile Data]
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 2: Schematic — Inlet and Outlet Velocity Triangles for an Axial Turbine Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INL["INLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE - STATION 1"]
        U1["Vector U1 - Blade Speed Inlet"]
        C1["Vector C1 - Absolute Inlet Velocity"]
        W1["Vector W1 - Relative Inlet Velocity"]
        A1["alpha1 - Absolute Flow Angle"]
        B1["beta1 - Relative Flow Angle"]
        U1 --- C1
        U1 --- W1
        C1 --- W1
    end

    subgraph OUTL["OUTLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE - STATION 2"]
        U2["Vector U2 - Blade Speed Outlet"]
        C2["Vector C2 - Absolute Outlet Velocity"]
        W2["Vector W2 - Relative Outlet Velocity"]
        A2["alpha2 - Absolute Flow Angle"]
        B2["beta2 - Relative Flow Angle"]
        U2 --- C2
        U2 --- W2
        C2 --- W2
    end

    INL -->|Flow through Rotor Passages| OUTL
    OUTL -->|Ws = U times Cw1 - Cw2| P["Power Output Per Unit Mass"]
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 3: Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Velocity Triangle and Machine Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VELTRI[Velocity Triangle Inputs: C, W, U, alpha, beta] --> WHIRL[Compute Whirl Components: Cw1 and Cw2]
    VELTRI --> FLOW[Compute Flow Components: Cf1 and Cf2]
    WHIRL --> EULER[Euler Equation: Ws = U times delta Cw]
    FLOW --> CONT[Continuity Check: Cf1 equals Cf2]
    EULER --> PERF1[Specific Work Output]
    EULER --> PERF2[Blade Loading]
    CONT --> PERF3[Axial Velocity Check]
    PERF1 --> HYDRO[Hydraulic Efficiency]
    PERF2 --> BLADE[Blade Profile and Number Selection]
    PERF3 --> LOSS[Kinetic Energy Leaving Loss]
    HYDRO --> FINAL[Machine Performance Evaluation]
    BLADE --> FINAL
    LOSS --> FINAL
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 4: Classification Flowchart of Turbomachines by Velocity Triangle Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Turbomachine] --> DIR{Flow Direction at Inlet?}
    DIR -->|Tangential or Radial| TANG[Impulse Type - Pelton Wheel]
    DIR -->|Axial| AX{Internal Reaction}
    DIR -->|Mixed| MIX[Mixed Flow - Francis Turbine]

    AX -->|Low Reaction below 50 percent| AXIALIMP[Axial Impulse Curtis Stage]
    AX -->|Reaction around 50 percent| AXIAL50[Axial Reaction - Kaplan Type]

    TANG --> V1[Velocity Triangle: beta1 near 0, beta2 near 165 deg]
    MIX --> V2[Velocity Triangle: Inlet Radial, Outlet Axial]
    AXIALIMP --> V3[Velocity Triangle: Small angles, Symmetric]
    AXIAL50 --> V4[Velocity Triangle: Beta1 and Beta2 comparable]
```

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 5: Decision Flow for Solving Velocity Triangle Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Given Velocities and Angles]
    B --> C{Is Cf given or Computable?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Cf equals C sin alpha equals W sin beta]
    C -->|No but mass flow given| E[Compute Cf from Q divided by A]
    D --> F[Compute Cw from C cos alpha or U plus W cos beta]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Euler Equation for Work]
    G --> H{Output Required?}
    H -->|Work| I[Report Ws in J per kg or kJ per kg]
    H -->|Efficiency| J[Compute eta as Useful Work divided by Energy Supplied]
    H -->|Blade Angle| K[Use beta to specify blade profile]
    I --> DONE[Solution Complete]
    J --> DONE
    K --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use these diagrams in exams:**
>
> - Diagram 1 and 2 are the **core conceptual scaffolds** — draw the **physical velocity triangles** directly on your answer sheet with a pencil and ruler.
> - Diagrams 3, 4, 5 are **analytical flowcharts** that help you decide **which equation to apply** in long KTU Part-B questions.
> - Mermaid is used here only as a **functional/architectural mapping** because a precise vector-accurate velocity triangle must be hand-drawn on graph paper during the exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define velocity triangle. Explain the terms absolute velocity, relative velocity, and blade velocity with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **velocity triangle** is a vector diagram showing the relative magnitudes and directions of three velocities of a fluid particle at the inlet or outlet of a turbomachine rotor.

- **Absolute Velocity (C):** The velocity of the fluid measured relative to a **stationary** reference frame (the casing of the machine).
- **Relative Velocity (W):** The velocity of the fluid measured relative to the **rotating** blade, i.e., as observed by an observer moving with the rotor.
- **Blade Velocity (U):** The tangential linear velocity of the blade at the mean radius, given by $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$.

[Diagram: standard inlet triangle with U horizontal, W at angle β from −U, C from origin making angle α with U, closed by vector addition C = W + U.]

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explaining all three velocities, 1 mark for diagram.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State Euler's equation for a turbomachine and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Euler's equation for the specific work done by a fluid in a turbomachine is:

$$W_s = \frac{1}{g}(U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1})$$

(For turbines, work output is positive; for compressors/pumps, work input is positive with the opposite sign convention.)

**Significance:**
1. It is the **fundamental energy-transfer equation** of all turbomachines — it directly gives the work output of a turbine or work input of a pump/compressor.
2. It uses only the **tangential (whirl) components** of the absolute velocities, which are read directly from velocity triangles.
3. It forms the **basis for computing hydraulic and mechanical efficiency**.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for correct equation, 1 mark for sign convention, 1 mark for two significance points.)*

---

### 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose EITHER A or B

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the velocity triangle at the inlet of an axial flow turbomachine. Establish the relationship between absolute velocity C₁, relative velocity W₁, and blade velocity U₁. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a single-stage axial turbine, steam enters with absolute velocity $C_1 = 200$ m/s at angle $\alpha_1 = 25°$ to the tangential direction. The mean blade speed is $U = 100$ m/s. The relative velocity at exit is $W_2 = 120$ m/s at $\beta_2 = 35°$ from the tangential direction (in the opposite sense to U). Determine:
- (i) Inlet relative velocity $W_1$ and angle $\beta_1$.
- (ii) Outlet absolute velocity $C_2$ and angle $\alpha_2$.
- (iii) Work done per kg of steam per second. **(7 marks)**

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Draw the inlet velocity triangle showing U horizontal, W at angle β from the −U direction, and C as the closing vector.

**Step 2:** Apply the parallelogram law:
$$\vec{C_1} = \vec{W_1} + \vec{U_1}$$

**Step 3:** Resolve into components:
- **Tangential:** $C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = U + W_1 \cos\beta_1$
- **Meridional:** $C_f = C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = W_1 \sin\beta_1$

**Step 4:** Solve for $W_1$:
$$W_1 = \sqrt{C_1^2 + U_1^2 - 2 C_1 U_1 \cos\alpha_1}$$
(from cosine rule on triangle where angle between C₁ and U₁ is α₁ and the opposite side is W₁).

**Step 5:** Angle β₁:
$$\tan\beta_1 = \frac{C_f}{U_1 - C_{w1}}$$

*(Marks: 1 for diagram, 2 for vector equation + resolution, 2 for W₁ formula, 2 for β₁ formula.)*

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $C_1 = 200$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 25°$, $U = 100$ m/s, $W_2 = 120$ m/s, $\beta_2 = 35°$ (opposite to U).

**Step 1: Compute inlet whirl and flow components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{w1} &= C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 200 \cos 25° = 200 \times 0.9063 = 181.26 \text{ m/s} \\
C_f &= C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 200 \sin 25° = 200 \times 0.4226 = 84.52 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply triangle closure for inlet.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 \cos\beta_1 &= C_{w1} - U = 181.26 - 100 = 81.26 \text{ m/s} \\
W_1 \sin\beta_1 &= C_f = 84.52 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \sqrt{(81.26)^2 + (84.52)^2} = \sqrt{6603.2 + 7143.6} \\
W_1 &= \sqrt{13746.8} \approx 117.25 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
\beta_1 &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{84.52}{81.26}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.0401) \approx 46.12°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Outlet whirl and flow components.**
Since $\beta_2$ is in the opposite sense to U:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{w2} &= -W_2 \cos\beta_2 = -120 \cos 35° = -98.30 \text{ m/s} \\
W_{f2} &= W_2 \sin\beta_2 = 120 \sin 35° = 68.83 \text{ m/s} \\
C_{w2} &= U + W_{w2} = 100 - 98.30 = 1.70 \text{ m/s} \\
C_f &= W_{f2} = 68.83 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Outlet absolute velocity and angle.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= \sqrt{(1.70)^2 + (68.83)^2} = \sqrt{2.89 + 4737.6} \approx 68.85 \text{ m/s} \\
\alpha_2 &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{68.83}{1.70}\right) \approx 88.59°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Work done per kg per second (Euler equation).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= U(C_{w1} - C_{w2}) = 100 \times (181.26 - 1.70) \\
W_s &= 100 \times 179.56 = 17{,}956 \text{ J/kg} \approx 17.96 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $W_1$ | 117.25 m/s |
| $\beta_1$ | 46.12° |
| $C_2$ | 68.85 m/s |
| $\alpha_2$ | 88.59° |
| $W_s$ | 17,956 J/kg |

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Euler's equation for the work done per unit weight of fluid flowing through a turbomachine. State clearly the sign convention used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The velocity triangle of a Francis turbine has the following data at the inlet: mean radius $r_1 = 1.25$ m, $N = 300$ rpm, $C_1 = 30$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 60°$, $\beta_1 = 30°$. At the outlet, $r_2 = 1.0$ m, $C_2 = 15$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 30°$. Determine:
- (i) The whirl components at inlet and outlet.
- (ii) The work done per unit weight of water.
- (iii) The hydraulic efficiency of the turbine. **(7 marks)**

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's second law in the tangential direction. The tangential force on the fluid equals the rate of change of angular momentum:
$$F_t = \dot{m}(C_{w2} - C_{w1})$$

**Step 2:** Power transferred to blade = $F_t \cdot U$:
$$P = \dot{m} \cdot U \cdot (C_{w2} - C_{w1})$$

**Step 3:** Specific work (per unit weight = per Newton-force equivalent) gives:
$$W_s = \frac{U(C_{w2} - C_{w1})}{g} \quad \text{(J/N = m, in metres of head)}$$

**Step 4:** For radial machines with $U_1 \neq U_2$:
$$W_s = \frac{U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}}{g}$$

**Sign Convention:**
- **Turbine:** $W_s = \dfrac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g}$ (positive = work output).
- **Pump/Compressor:** $W_s = \dfrac{U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}}{g}$ (positive = work input).

*(Marks: 2 for momentum equation, 2 for power, 2 for work, 1 for sign convention.)*

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $r_1 = 1.25$ m, $r_2 = 1.0$ m, $N = 300$ rpm, $C_1 = 30$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 60°$, $\beta_1 = 30°$, $C_2 = 15$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 30°$.

**Step 1: Blade velocities.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{2\pi r_1 N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1.25 \times 300}{60} = 39.27 \text{ m/s} \\
U_2 &= \frac{2\pi r_2 N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1.0 \times 300}{60} = 31.42 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Whirl components.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{w1} &= C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 30 \cos 60° = 30 \times 0.5 = 15 \text{ m/s} \\
C_{w2} &= C_2 \cos\alpha_2 = 15 \cos 30° = 15 \times 0.866 = 12.99 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Work done per unit weight.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= \frac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g} = \frac{(39.27)(15) - (31.42)(12.99)}{9.81} \\
W_s &= \frac{589.05 - 408.15}{9.81} = \frac{180.90}{9.81} \approx 18.44 \text{ m of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Hydraulic efficiency.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_h &= \frac{\text{Useful work per unit weight}}{\text{Kinetic energy input per unit weight}} \\
\text{Numerator} &= U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2} = 180.90 \text{ (units: m²/s², i.e., J/kg)} \\
\text{Energy input (Bernoulli KE + pressure head drop)} &= \frac{C_1^2 - C_2^2}{2g} + \Delta z + \Delta(p/\rho g)
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume the inlet and outlet conditions are at the same elevation and neglecting pressure potential (or, given only velocities, use kinetic-energy basis):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_h &= \frac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{(C_1^2/2g)} \\
\eta_h &= \frac{180.90}{(30^2/2 \times 9.81)} = \frac{180.90}{45.87} \approx 3.94
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the result exceeds 1, this confirms that energy is **not** purely kinetic — pressure energy is also converted. In practice, the **change in pressure head** across the runner must be included in the denominator for a realistic hydraulic efficiency value. The KTU board expects students to **state this limitation** and acknowledge that without pressure data, only the Euler-based comparison is meaningful.

**Refined hydraulic efficiency with assumed $\Delta p / \rho g = 18$ m** (illustrative):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_h &= \frac{180.90}{(C_1^2 - C_2^2)/2 + g\Delta z + \Delta p/\rho g} \\
&= \frac{180.90}{(900 - 225)/2 + 0 + 18} = \frac{180.90}{337.5 + 18} = \frac{180.90}{355.5} \\
\eta_h &\approx 0.509 \text{ or } 50.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $U_1, U_2$ | 39.27 m/s, 31.42 m/s |
| $C_{w1}, C_{w2}$ | 15 m/s, 12.99 m/s |
| $W_s$ | 18.44 m of water |
| $\eta_h$ | ≈ 50.9% |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks in Velocity Triangle Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention in Euler's equation** — losing 2 marks. Always write the equation with explicit + or − signs and label "turbine" or "compressor."
>
> 2. **Mixing up α (absolute) and β (relative)** — β is the angle of W with the **negative U direction**, not with U. Drawing the triangle incorrectly propagates errors throughout.
>
> 3. **Not drawing the velocity triangle first** — the board examiner awards **at least 1 mark** for a correctly drawn triangle with labeled vectors. Skipping this costs you easy marks.
>
> 4. **Using $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ with wrong units** — N must be in **rpm** and D in **metres**. Mixing up these units is a frequent mistake.
>
> 5. **Confusing inlet and outlet blade velocities in a radial machine** — $U_1 \neq U_2$. Use the **correct radius** at each station; this error leads to a chain of wrong calculations.
>
> 6. **Rounding intermediate results too early** — keep 4–5 decimal places during intermediate steps; round only the final answer to 2 decimals. Excessive rounding in intermediate steps leads to wrong final answers.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Velocity Triangles (Module 3):**
>
> ✅ Velocity triangle = vector diagram showing **C, W, U** at rotor inlet/outlet.
>
> ✅ Fundamental equation: $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$
>
> ✅ Continuity invariant: $C_f = W_f$ (flow component is same in both frames).
>
> ✅ **Euler's equation (most important):**
> - Turbine: $W_s = \dfrac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g}$
> - Compressor/Pump: $W_s = \dfrac{U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1}}{g}$
>
> ✅ **Blade velocity:** $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ (axial machines) or $U = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{60}$ (radial machines).
>
> ✅ **Component formulas:**
> - $C_w = C \cos\alpha = U + W \cos\beta$ (when β measured from −U)
> - $C_f = C \sin\alpha = W \sin\beta$
> - $\tan\beta = \dfrac{C_f}{U - C_w}$
>
> ✅ **Sine rule:** $\dfrac{C}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{U}{\sin(\alpha - \beta)} = \dfrac{W}{\sin\alpha}$
>
> ✅ **Degree of reaction** for axial machines: $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{w2}}{C_{w1}}$
>
> ✅ **Free vortex condition:** $C_w \cdot r = \text{constant}$ → used in Francis/Kaplan design.
>
> ✅ **Maximum specific work** in a turbine stage occurs when **α₂ = 90°** (no whirl exit loss), and is $W_s = U_1 C_{w1}$ (when Cw2 = 0).
>
> ✅ **Velocity triangles must always be drawn first** in any solution — they earn easy marks and prevent calculation errors.
>
> ✅ **For Impulse machines (Pelton):** Pressure drop occurs in the **nozzle**, not in the rotor.
>
> ✅ **For Reaction machines (Francis/Kaplan):** Pressure drop is **shared** between stator and rotor.
>
> ✅ **Range of blade angles to remember:**
> - Pelton inlet β₁ ≈ 0° to 5°
> - Pelton outlet β₂ ≈ 165° to 175°
> - Kaplan inlet β₁ ≈ 30° to 45°
> - Centrifugal pump inlet β₁ = 90° (radial entry, no pre-whirl)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiencies and Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Efficiencies and Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Efficiencies and Losses in Turbo Machinery?

In the context of **Turbo Machinery** (compressors, turbines, and combined systems), **Efficiencies** are dimensionless performance metrics that quantify how effectively a machine converts input energy into useful work (or vice versa) under real, irreversible conditions. **Losses**, conversely, are the penalties paid due to irreversibilities such as friction, turbulence, leakage, and heat transfer that prevent the machine from achieving the ideal **isentropic** (constant entropy) performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Efficiency* in turbo machinery is defined as the ratio of the **isentropic enthalpy change (work)** to the **actual enthalpy change (work)** for a process. A higher efficiency means a smaller deviation from the ideal isentropic process, implying fewer losses.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Bucket

Imagine you are filling a swimming pool using a bucket with a small hole at the bottom.
