topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theoretical Head - Capacity Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Head (H_th):** The rise in pressure head of the fluid per unit weight across the impeller of a turbo machine, derived purely from the **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation**, assuming **no hydraulic losses, no leakage, no slip, and an infinite number of blades** (i.e., ideal flow conditions).

The **Theoretical Head – Capacity Relationship** expresses the functional dependence of the theoretical head (H_th) developed by a centrifugal machine on its **discharge (Q)** — the volumetric flow rate (capacity). Mathematically, for a centrifugal pump with an exit blade angle β₂ and an outer diameter D₂:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \frac{u_2 \cot\beta_2}{\pi D_2 B_2 \, g} \cdot Q$$

This is a **linear relationship of the form H_th = A − B·Q**, whose slope is dictated by the geometry (blade angle β₂, width B₂) and the peripheral speed (u₂).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Garden Hose on a Merry-Go-Round:**
> Imagine you swing a hose sideways (giving it angular momentum) and let water exit from its tip. If the hose is short, the water leaves with low speed; if the hose is long, it leaves fast — the longer the "blade," the more lift you get.
> Now imagine adding a second adjustable nozzle (a "blade vane") at the tip:
> - If the vane is **angled backward** (like a fan blade tilted back), water leaves with reduced horizontal push — more water out means *less* head.
> - If the vane is **straight radial**, the head stays constant regardless of how much water flows.
> - If the vane is **angled forward** (scoop-shaped), more water means *more* head (rarely used because it causes unstable operation).
>
> The theoretical head-capacity graph is just this relationship drawn on paper — a straight line that slopes **downward, flat, or upward** depending on blade design.

## 1.3 Underlying Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Nature |
|:------:|:---------|:-------------:|:------:|
| g | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | Constant |
| ρ | Density of fluid | kg/m³ | Fluid property |
| Q | Discharge / Capacity | **m³/s** | Variable (x-axis) |
| H_th | Theoretical head | **m of fluid** | Variable (y-axis) |
| u₂ | Blade tip speed at outlet | m/s | Geometric |
| β₂ | Outlet blade angle | degrees / radians | Geometric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic belongs to Module 4 ("Centrifugal Pumps and Compressors") and forms the analytical backbone for deriving the **characteristic curves** of pumps. Mastery of Euler's equation and the **velocity triangle** at the impeller outlet is **mandatory** before attempting any numerical problem on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Premise — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation

The entire head-capacity relationship is built upon the work done per unit weight by the impeller blades on the fluid, originally derived by **Leonhard Euler** in 1754.

For a turbo machine with no pre-whirl (V_w1 = 0, common assumption for centrifugal pumps):

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} \, u_2}{g}$$

where:
- **V_w2** = Whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity at outlet
- **u₂** = Peripheral (blade tip) velocity at outlet = π·D₂·N/60
- **g** = Gravitational acceleration

## 2.2 The Velocity Triangle at Outlet — The Heart of the Relationship

The whirl velocity V_w2 is resolved from the outlet velocity triangle:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cot\beta_2$$

where **V_r2** is the **radial (relative) velocity** component at the outlet, which is directly linked to discharge Q through the **flow area** at the impeller periphery.

## 2.3 Linking V_r2 to Discharge (Q)

The flow area at outlet is approximated as the **cylindrical surface area** swept by the blade width:

$$A_2 = \pi D_2 B_2$$

Therefore, by continuity:

$$V_{r2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}$$

## 2.4 Assembly — Deriving the Linear Head-Capacity Relation

Substituting V_w2 and V_r2 into Euler's equation:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2}{g}\left(u_2 - \frac{Q \cot\beta_2}{\pi D_2 B_2}\right)$$

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \frac{u_2 \cot\beta_2}{\pi D_2 B_2 \, g} \cdot Q$$

Recognizing this as a straight line **y = c − mx** in the H_th vs. Q plane, the parameters are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|:---------:|:------:|:----------|:-----------------|
| Intercept (at Q = 0, shut-off head) | **A** | $u_2^2 / g$ | Head at zero flow |
| Slope (rate of head drop with Q) | **B** | $u_2 \cot\beta_2 / (\pi D_2 B_2 g)$ | Geometric stiffness of characteristic |
| Dependent Variable | **H_th** | H_th = A − B·Q | Theoretical head (m) |
| Independent Variable | **Q** | Given / computed | Capacity (m³/s) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **authoritative, exam-ready summary** of every formula you will need for the Theoretical Head – Capacity Relationship. Memorize the table; apply it in derivations.

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|:-:|:--------|:------------------------|
| 1 | $H_{th} = (V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1)/g$ | **Euler's turbomachinery equation** (general form) |
| 2 | $H_{th} = V_{w2}u_2 / g$ | With zero pre-whirl (V_w1 = 0) |
| 3 | $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cot\beta_2$ | Outlet velocity triangle resolution |
| 4 | $V_{r2} = Q / (\pi D_2 B_2)$ | Radial velocity from continuity (V_f2 = V_r2) |
| 5 | $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$ | Blade tip speed (N in rpm) |
| 6 | $H_{th} = u_2^2/g - [u_2\cot\beta_2/(\pi D_2 B_2 g)] \cdot Q$ | **Final head-capacity equation** |
| 7 | $A = u_2^2 / g$ | Shut-off theoretical head (at Q = 0) |
| 8 | $B = u_2\cot\beta_2 / (\pi D_2 B_2 g)$ | Slope of H_th vs. Q line |
| 9 | $P_{th} = \rho g Q H_{th}$ | Theoretical fluid power (watts) |
| 10 | $\eta_h = H_{th,act} / H_{th,ideal}$ | Hydraulic (Euler) efficiency reference |

## 2.6 Effect of Blade Outlet Angle (β₂) on the Slope

The sign of **cot β₂** controls whether the characteristic line is downward-sloping, flat, or upward-sloping:

| Blade Type | β₂ Range | cot β₂ | Slope of H_th vs. Q | Behavior |
|:-----------|:--------:|:------:|:-------------------:|:--------|
| **Backward Curved** | β₂ < 90° | Positive | **Negative** ↓ | H drops as Q rises — most stable, most used |
| **Radial** | β₂ = 90° | Zero (cot 90° = 0) | **Zero** → | H is constant, independent of Q |
| **Forward Curved** | β₂ > 90° | Negative | **Positive** ↑ | H rises as Q rises — unstable, rare |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The head-capacity relationship is the **design DNA** of every centrifugal pump, blower, and compressor manufactured globally. Practical applications include:
> - **Pump selection:** Engineers plot system resistance curves (H_sys = H_static + K·Q²) on the same chart as H_th vs. Q to find the **operating point** (their intersection).
> - **Performance prediction:** By knowing β₂, D₂, B₂, and N, designers predict head before building a single prototype.
> - **Stability assessment:** Forward-curved blades produce rising characteristics, which can lead to **surge** in compressors and **hunting** in pumps — hence backward-curved blades dominate industry.
> - **Cavitation margin analysis:** The H_th − Q line must be combined with NPSH available to avoid cavitation at high Q.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Theoretical Head – Capacity Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Assumptions (standard for KTU problems):**
> 1. The impeller has an **infinite number of blades**, so that fluid follows the blade contour exactly (no deviation).
> 2. **No pre-whirl** at the inlet: V_w1 = 0.
> 3. **Inlet whirl is purely radial** at entry: α₁ = 90° (V_f1 = V1).
> 4. Flow is **steady, incompressible, and axisymmetric**.
> 5. The flow area at outlet is the cylindrical annulus area: A_2 = π·D₂·B₂.

### Step 1 — Begin with Euler's Turbomachinery Equation (general form)

The Euler head for any turbo machine, derived from the angular momentum balance between inlet and outlet, is:

$$H_{th} = \frac{1}{g}\left(V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1\right)$$

**Logic:** This equation states that the work done per unit weight by the blades on the fluid equals the change in the tangential component of absolute velocity, multiplied by the blade speed, divided by g.

### Step 2 — Apply the Zero Pre-Whirl Assumption

If the fluid enters the impeller **purely radially** (no tangential component at inlet), then V_w1 = 0. The equation reduces to:

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2}u_2}{g}$$

**Logic:** All the head rise is produced by the impeller exit, since the inlet adds no swirl energy.

### Step 3 — Express the Whirl Velocity V_w2 from the Outlet Velocity Triangle

The outlet velocity triangle (composed of V₂ absolute, V_r2 relative, and u₂ blade velocity) yields the trigonometric relationship:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cot\beta_2$$

**Logic:** In the triangle, V_w2 is the horizontal projection of V_r2 along the direction of u₂. The relative velocity V_r2 makes an angle β₂ with the tangential direction (negative, in the direction opposite to u₂ for backward-curved blades), so its tangential projection is V_r2·cot β₂.

### Step 4 — Substitute V_w2 into Euler's Equation

Substituting Step 3's result into Step 2:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2}{g}\left(u_2 - V_{r2}\cot\beta_2\right)$$

Expanding the bracket:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \frac{u_2 V_{r2}\cot\beta_2}{g}$$

**Logic:** The first term represents the head developed at zero flow (shut-off head); the second term subtracts a quantity proportional to V_r2 (and hence Q), producing the linear droop.

### Step 5 — Apply the Continuity Equation at the Impeller Outlet

The radial velocity V_r2 is the volume flow rate divided by the flow area at outlet:

$$V_{r2} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}$$

**Logic:** The fluid leaves the impeller through the cylindrical area swept by the blade width B₂ at diameter D₂. Incompressible flow ensures V·A = Q is constant.

### Step 6 — Substitute V_r2 into the Head Equation

Replacing V_r2 in the expression from Step 4:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \frac{u_2\cot\beta_2}{g}\left(\frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}\right)$$

Rearranging in the standard linear form:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \left(\frac{u_2\cot\beta_2}{\pi D_2 B_2 g}\right) Q$$

**Logic:** This is the **final, exam-ready form**. Recognizing it as y = A − B·Q with:
- **A = u₂²/g** (intercept, the theoretical head at Q = 0)
- **B = u₂·cot β₂ / (π·D₂·B₂·g)** (slope)

### Step 7 — Special Case: Radial Blades (β₂ = 90°)

When β₂ = 90°, cot β₂ = 0, so:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} = \text{constant}$$

**Logic:** The head is **independent of Q**. The line on the H-Q plot is perfectly horizontal.

### Step 8 — Special Case: Backward Curved Blades (e.g., β₂ = 60°)

When β₂ = 60°, cot β₂ = 0.577 (positive), so:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \left(\frac{0.577 \, u_2}{\pi D_2 B_2 g}\right) Q$$

**Logic:** The line slopes downward — as Q increases, V_r2 increases, V_w2 decreases (because V_w2 = u₂ − V_r2·cot β₂), and hence H_th decreases.

### Step 9 — Special Case: Forward Curved Blades (e.g., β₂ = 120°)

When β₂ = 120°, cot β₂ = −0.577 (negative), so:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} + \left(\frac{0.577 \, u_2}{\pi D_2 B_2 g}\right) Q$$

**Logic:** The line slopes upward — but such blades cause flow instability and are avoided.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Key Relationships

| Step # | Physical Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Interpretation |
|:------:|:-----------------|:----------------------|:---------------------------|
| 1 | Euler's general equation | $H_{th} = (V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1)/g$ | Net work per unit weight |
| 2 | Zero pre-whirl simplification | $H_{th} = V_{w2}u_2/g$ | Standard for centrifugal pump |
| 3 | Outlet velocity triangle | $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cot\beta_2$ | Links geometry to whirl |
| 4 | Substitution | $H_{th} = u_2^2/g - (u_2V_{r2}\cot\beta_2)/g$ | Pre-continuity form |
| 5 | Continuity equation | $V_{r2} = Q/(\pi D_2 B_2)$ | Volume conservation |
| 6 | Final form | $H_{th} = (u_2^2/g) - [u_2\cot\beta_2/(\pi D_2 B_2 g)] \cdot Q$ | **Linear H-Q law** |
| 7 | Radial blade (β₂ = 90°) | $H_{th} = u_2^2/g$ = const | Horizontal characteristic |
| 8 | Backward curved (β₂ < 90°) | Slope B > 0, line falls | Stable, widely used |
| 9 | Forward curved (β₂ > 90°) | Slope B < 0, line rises | Unstable, rarely used |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Derivation Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Centrifugal Pump with infinite blades"] --> B["Euler's General Equation H_th = V_w2 u2 - V_w1 u1 divided by g"]
    B --> C["Apply V_w1 equals 0: Zero Pre-whirl"]
    C --> D["H_th equals V_w2 u2 divided by g"]
    D --> E["Outlet Velocity Triangle: V_w2 equals u2 minus V_r2 cot beta2"]
    E --> F["Substitute into H_th expression"]
    F --> G["Apply Continuity: V_r2 equals Q divided by pi D2 B2"]
    G --> H["Final Linear Form: H_th equals A minus B times Q"]
    H --> I["Case 1: beta2 less than 90: Backward Curved, Slope Negative"]
    H --> J["Case 2: beta2 equals 90: Radial, Slope Zero"]
    H --> K["Case 3: beta2 greater than 90: Forward Curved, Slope Positive"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Head-Capacity Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Geometric Inputs: D2, B2, beta2, N"] --> U2["Compute u2 = pi D2 N divided by 60"]
    U2 --> PARAMA["Compute A = u2 squared divided by g: Shut-off Head"]
    INPUT --> PARAMB["Compute B = u2 cot beta2 divided by pi D2 B2 g: Slope"]
    PARAMA --> EQN["Linear Equation: H_th equals A minus B times Q"]
    PARAMB --> EQN
    EQN --> OUT1["Q = 0 point: A"]
    EQN --> OUT2["Q equals Q_design point: H_op"]
    EQN --> OUT3["Maximum Q: Theoretical Cut-off"]
    OUT1 --> PLOT["Plot H_th vs Q Characteristic Curve"]
    OUT2 --> PLOT
    OUT3 --> PLOT
    PLOT --> SEL["Pump Selection and Operating Point Matching"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Effect of β₂ on Characteristic Shape

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SHAPE["Outlet Blade Geometry"] --> BC["Backward Curved: beta2 less than 90"]
    SHAPE --> RD["Radial: beta2 equals 90"]
    SHAPE --> FC["Forward Curved: beta2 greater than 90"]
    BC --> OBC["cot beta2 greater than 0, Slope B greater than 0, H_th falls with Q"]
    RD --> ORD["cot beta2 equals 0, Slope B equals 0, H_th constant with Q"]
    FC --> OFC["cot beta2 less than 0, Slope B less than 0, H_th rises with Q"]
    OBC --> USE1["Industry Standard: Stable Operation"]
    ORD --> USE2["Used in Specific Process Pumps"]
    OFC --> USE3["Avoided: Surge and Instability"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain Euler's equation for the theoretical head developed by a centrifugal pump. State clearly the assumption of zero pre-whirl.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Euler's turbomachinery equation, governing the energy transfer between the impeller and the fluid, is given by:

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1}{g}$$

where **V_w1, V_w2** are the whirl components of absolute velocity at inlet and outlet, and **u₁, u₂** are the blade speeds at inlet and outlet. Under the assumption of **zero pre-whirl (V_w1 = 0)**, the fluid enters the impeller purely radially, so the equation reduces to **H_th = V_w2·u₂/g**, indicating that the head is generated entirely by the outlet whirl-tangential interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for meaning of each variable, 1 mark for zero pre-whirl simplification.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**With the help of the velocity triangle at the impeller outlet, show that V_w2 = u₂ − V_r2·cot β₂.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

From the outlet velocity triangle, the relative velocity V_r2 is inclined at an angle β₂ to the tangential (negative u₂) direction. The tangential component of V_r2 is V_r2·cos β₂ along u₂, and the radial component is V_r2·sin β₂. Therefore:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 = u_2 - V_{r2}\cot\beta_2 \cdot \sin\beta_2 / \sin\beta_2$$

Final simplified form: **V_w2 = u₂ − V_r2·cot β₂** (3 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) Part B rules for Module 4:
> - Each question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.
> - Student must attempt **one full question** (either Option A or Option B).

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from Euler's equation, derive the theoretical head-capacity relationship **H_th = A − B·Q** for a centrifugal pump. Clearly identify the terms A and B in terms of impeller geometry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump running at **1450 rpm** has an impeller of outer diameter **D₂ = 0.30 m** and blade width at outlet **B₂ = 0.025 m**. The outlet blade angle is **β₂ = 60°**. Assuming zero pre-whirl and no slip, compute: (i) the theoretical head at shut-off (Q = 0), (ii) the slope B, and (iii) the theoretical head at **Q = 0.05 m³/s**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1 of these notes** for the complete derivation. The student is expected to write the following steps:

1. **Euler's equation (1 mark):** $H_{th} = (V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1)/g$
2. **Zero pre-whirl assumption (1 mark):** $H_{th} = V_{w2}u_2/g$
3. **Outlet velocity triangle (2 marks):** $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cot\beta_2$
4. **Continuity at outlet (1 mark):** $V_{r2} = Q/(\pi D_2 B_2)$
5. **Final linear form (2 marks):** $H_{th} = (u_2^2/g) - [u_2\cot\beta_2/(\pi D_2 B_2 g)] \cdot Q$, with A = u₂²/g and B = u_2·cot β₂/(π·D₂·B₂·g).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute u₂ (1 mark):**
$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60} = 22.78 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute cot β₂ (1 mark):**
$$\cot 60° = \frac{1}{\tan 60°} = \frac{1}{1.732} = 0.5774$$

**Step 3 — Shut-off head A (1 mark):**
$$A = \frac{u_2^2}{g} = \frac{(22.78)^2}{9.81} = \frac{518.93}{9.81} = 52.90 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Slope B (2 marks):**
$$B = \frac{u_2 \cot\beta_2}{\pi D_2 B_2 g} = \frac{22.78 \times 0.5774}{\pi \times 0.30 \times 0.025 \times 9.81} = \frac{13.15}{0.2312} = 56.88 \text{ m/(m³/s)}$$

**Step 5 — Head at Q = 0.05 m³/s (2 marks):**
$$H_{th} = A - B \cdot Q = 52.90 - 56.88 \times 0.05 = 52.90 - 2.844 = 50.06 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** Shut-off head = **52.90 m**, Slope B = **56.88 m·s/m³**, Head at Q = 0.05 m³/s = **50.06 m**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A centrifugal pump has the following parameters: D₂ = 0.40 m, B₂ = 0.030 m, β₂ = 45°, N = 1000 rpm. The pump delivers **Q = 0.08 m³/s** of water. Find the theoretical head developed assuming zero pre-whirl. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the theoretical head-capacity characteristics of centrifugal pumps with **backward-curved, radial, and forward-curved** impeller blades, with neat sketches of the H-Q curves. State the engineering significance of each type. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute u₂ (1 mark):**
$$u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.40 \times 1000}{60} = 20.94 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute cot β₂ (1 mark):**
$$\cot 45° = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute A (1 mark):**
$$A = \frac{u_2^2}{g} = \frac{(20.94)^2}{9.81} = \frac{438.5}{9.81} = 44.70 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute B (2 marks):**
$$B = \frac{u_2 \cot\beta_2}{\pi D_2 B_2 g} = \frac{20.94 \times 1.0}{\pi \times 0.40 \times 0.030 \times 9.81} = \frac{20.94}{0.3698} = 56.62 \text{ m·s/m³}$$

**Step 5 — Compute H_th (2 marks):**
$$H_{th} = 44.70 - 56.62 \times 0.08 = 44.70 - 4.530 = 40.17 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** Theoretical head ≈ **40.17 m**.

**Part (b) — Comparative Discussion with Sketches (7 marks):**

| Blade Type | β₂ | cot β₂ | Slope | H-Q Curve Shape | Significance |
|:-----------|:---:|:------:|:-----:|:---------------:|:-------------|
| Backward Curved | β₂ < 90° | Positive | Negative slope | Line falls with Q | **Most stable, overload-safe, industry standard** |
| Radial | β₂ = 90° | 0 | Zero slope | Horizontal line | Used in **process pumps** where head must remain constant |
| Forward Curved | β₂ > 90° | Negative | Positive slope | Line rises with Q | **Unstable, prone to surge; rarely used** in pumps |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QC["Q-axis horizontal"] --> GRAPH["H vs Q graph"]
    BC["Backward Curved: Line falls from H max at Q=0 to H min at Q max"] --> GRAPH
    RD["Radial: Horizontal line at constant H"] --> GRAPH
    FC["Forward Curved: Line rises from H min at Q=0 to H max at Q max"] --> GRAPH
```

**Engineering Significance (2 marks):**
- **Backward curved:** Flattening characteristic gives overload protection — motor can never be overloaded at high Q. **Used in 90% of centrifugal pumps.**
- **Radial:** Constant head suits **boiler feed pumps** and **high-pressure applications**.
- **Forward curved:** Rising characteristic gives **non-overloading** behavior but causes **surge** — used mainly in **fans and blowers**, not pumps.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner's Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by g:** Euler's head is per unit weight, so the **factor of g (9.81 m/s²) is mandatory** in the denominator. Marks are deducted if g is omitted.
> 2. **Confusing cot β₂ with tan β₂:** For β₂ = 60°, students often write tan 60° = 1.732. The correct value is **cot 60° = 0.577**. The relationship is **V_w2 = u₂ − V_r2·cot β₂**, not tan.
> 3. **Wrong flow area:** The outlet area is **π·D₂·B₂** (cylindrical, not annular). Many students incorrectly subtract hub diameter.
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Q must be in **m³/s** (not L/s or LPM) and u₂ in **m/s** (from N in rpm via u = πDN/60).
> 5. **Assuming V_f2 ≠ V_r2 at outlet:** For backward-curved radial-flow machines, the radial component of absolute velocity **V_f2 equals V_r2**, and the flow area is the **cylindrical** surface — do not confuse with the meridional component.
> 6. **Sign of cot β₂:** For β₂ > 90°, cot is **negative** — the slope becomes positive, which is a classic KTU trap.
> 7. **Unit of slope B:** B has units of **m·s/m³** (or m per unit discharge), not m²/s. Show unit cancellation explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Reading):**

- ✅ The **Theoretical Head H_th** is the head developed by an **ideal, loss-free, infinite-bladed** impeller.
- ✅ **Euler's equation:** $H_{th} = (V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1)/g$ — start every derivation here.
- ✅ **Zero pre-whirl** assumption reduces it to: $H_{th} = V_{w2}u_2/g$.
- ✅ From the **outlet velocity triangle**: $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cot\beta_2$.
- ✅ From **continuity** at outlet: $V_{r2} = Q/(\pi D_2 B_2)$.
- ✅ Combining gives the **linear law**: $H_{th} = (u_2^2/g) - [u_2\cot\beta_2/(\pi D_2 B_2 g)] \cdot Q$.
- ✅ The constants are: **Intercept A = u₂²/g** and **Slope B = u₂·cot β₂/(π·D₂·B₂·g)**.
- ✅ **Backward-curved blades (β₂ < 90°):** cot β₂ > 0 → line falls → **most stable, used industrially**.
- ✅ **Radial blades (β₂ = 90°):** cot β₂ = 0 → line horizontal → constant head.
- ✅ **Forward-curved blades (β₂ > 90°):** cot β₂ < 0 → line rises → **unstable, used in fans only**.
- ✅ Always compute **u₂ = π·D₂·N/60** before plugging in.
- ✅ The **shut-off head** (H at Q = 0) equals **A = u₂²/g** — a clean closed-form check.
- ✅ For real pumps, multiply H_th by hydraulic efficiency η_h to get **actual head H = η_h · H_th**.
- ✅ The H_th vs. Q line is **ideal**; actual pump curves drop further due to **mechanical, volumetric, and hydraulic losses** (covered in Module 4 next topics).
- ✅ **Slip factor σ** (next sub-topic) corrects V_w2 for the finite-blade effect: $V_{w2,actual} = \sigma \cdot V_{w2,ideal}$.
- ✅ Master the **velocity triangle** — every KTU question in this area tests it, often with changed blade angle or flow rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Speed for Starting the Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Minimum Speed for Starting the Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In turbo machinery, the **Minimum Speed for Starting the Flow** is defined as the lowest rotational speed of the impeller at which the head developed by a centrifugal pump first becomes equal to (or just exceeds) the **static head ($H_s$)** of the delivery system, thereby initiating the discharge of fluid through the delivery pipe.

Mathematically, it is the speed at which:

$$H_{manometric} \geq H_s \quad \text{at} \quad Q \rightarrow 0$$

In other words, it is the threshold speed below which the pump cannot lift the water column in the delivery pipe, and consequently, no flow can be initiated regardless of how long the pump is run.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This concept is critical for the design of pumps in high-lift applications such as boiler feed pumps, deep-well pumps, and high-rise building water supply systems where the static head is very large.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw & Glass" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to drink water from a deep glass using a straw. The deeper the water column, the harder you must suck to initiate the flow. Below a certain suction effort (analogous to pump speed), no water moves up the straw, no matter how long you try.

Similarly, a centrifugal pump's impeller acts like the "suction mechanism." The impeller's tangential velocity ($u_2$) must reach a critical value before it can overcome the static column of water in the delivery pipe. Below this critical speed, the impeller simply churns the water inside the casing without delivering it.

**Key Analogy Mapping:**

| Human Drinking Scenario | Centrifugal Pump Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Depth of water in glass | **Static Head ($H_s$)** |
| Suction effort applied | **Tangential velocity of impeller ($u_2$)** |
| Minimum suction to start flow | **Minimum starting speed ($N_{min}$)** |
| Flow rate up the straw | **Discharge ($Q$)** |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **bold-highlighted** constants and parameters are universally used in KTU board examinations for this topic:

- **Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$**
- **Static head: $H_s$ (in meters of fluid)**
- **Outer impeller diameter: $D_2$ (in meters)**
- **Blade outlet angle: $\beta_2$ (in degrees)**
- **Minimum speed: $N_{min}$ (in RPM)**
- **Tangential velocity at impeller exit: $u_2$ (in m/s)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The minimum starting speed is independent of the manometric efficiency when the analysis is restricted to the condition of zero flow, since slip and hydraulic losses become negligible when $Q = 0$.

---

## 1.4 Why Is This Concept Important?

In practical engineering scenarios, especially in:

1. **Cold start-up of boiler feed pumps** — The boiler pressure provides a massive static head; the pump must reach a threshold speed before any water enters the boiler.
2. **Fire-fighting pumps in high-rise buildings** — The vertical column of water in standpipes represents a high static head.
3. **Irrigation pumps in hilly terrains** — The elevation difference dictates the minimum operational speed.
4. **Nuclear reactor coolant pumps** — Where safety demands that flow commences only above a certain rotational speed.

If the impeller runs below $N_{min}$ for prolonged periods, it can lead to:
- **Overheating** (churning without cooling flow)
- **Cavitation damage**
- **Energy wastage** with zero useful work output

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The derivation of the minimum starting speed rests on a sequence of fundamental fluid mechanics principles. Let us break this down:

### Step 1: Establish the Head Condition for Flow Initiation

For flow to begin in the delivery pipe, the pressure energy imparted by the impeller at the pump outlet must exceed the potential energy of the water column in the delivery pipe.

> **Condition:** $\text{Pump head developed} \geq \text{Static head of the system}$

This is the **threshold condition** for flow.

### Step 2: Apply Euler's Turbomachinery Equation

For a centrifugal pump impeller, the manometric head developed is given by Euler's equation:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{1}{g}(V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

At the moment of starting, since the water is essentially stationary in the impeller eye (no pre-whirl), we set $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Zero-Discharge Velocity Triangle

At the moment of starting ($Q \approx 0$):
- The flow velocity at the impeller exit, $V_{f2} \approx 0$
- The relative velocity $V_{r2}$ is purely tangential (aligned with the blade direction at outlet)
- The absolute whirl velocity $V_{w2}$ becomes approximately equal to the tangential velocity $u_2$ (for backward-curved blades with $\beta_2 < 90°$)

Therefore, at starting condition:

$$V_{w2} \approx u_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Justification:** At $Q = 0$, there is no axial flow component. The water entering the impeller blade channel is simply carried around by the blade itself. The whirl component then equals the blade speed at the trailing edge.

### Step 4: Equate Pump Head to Static Head

Setting the head developed by the impeller equal to the static head:

$$\frac{u_2 \cdot u_2}{g} = H_s$$

$$\frac{u_2^2}{g} = H_s$$

### Step 5: Solve for Minimum Tangential Velocity

$$u_{2, min} = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

### Step 6: Convert Tangential Velocity to Rotational Speed

Using the relation $u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$:

$$\frac{\pi D_2 N_{min}}{60} = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2} \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{mano} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | Euler's head (no pre-whirl) | m | At $Q = 0$ |
| 2 | $u_{2,min} = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$ | Minimum tangential velocity | m/s | Threshold condition |
| 3 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{60}{\pi D_2} \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$ | Minimum starting speed | RPM | $V_{w1} = 0$, $V_{f2} = 0$ |
| 4 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{60 \cdot u_{2,min}}{\pi D_2}$ | Speed-velocity conversion | RPM | General |
| 5 | $H_{mano} \propto N^2$ | Affinity law for head | — | Geometrically similar pumps |
| 6 | $N_{min,2} = N_{min,1} \sqrt{\dfrac{H_{s,2}}{H_{s,1}}}$ | Scaling of minimum speed | RPM | Same pump, varying head |
| 7 | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | Blade speed at outlet | m/s | General turbomachinery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** All absolute value notations and vertical bars are written as $\vert$ to maintain markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The minimum starting speed formula is used in:

1. **Pump Selection for High-Rise Buildings:** Engineers use this to ensure the chosen pump can start delivering water at the lowest service speed, avoiding dead-head operation.
2. **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) Programming:** VFDs must be programmed with a minimum frequency threshold based on $N_{min}$ to prevent dry-running damage.
3. **Boiler Feed Pump (BFP) Commissioning:** During cold start-up, the BFP speed is gradually raised from zero; crossing $N_{min}$ is the operational milestone that confirms flow into the boiler.
4. **Cavitation Prevention:** Operating below $N_{min}$ means no flow and therefore no cooling, leading to rapid cavitation and seal damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Minimum Speed for Starting the Flow

We shall derive the formula from first principles using Euler's equation combined with the velocity triangle analysis at the threshold condition.

### **Given:**
- Static head of the system: $H_s$ (m)
- Outer diameter of impeller: $D_2$ (m)
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- Blade outlet angle: $\beta_2$ (backward curved, typically $20°$ to $40°$)
- No pre-whirl at inlet ($V_{w1} = 0$)

### **Find:**
- Minimum speed $N_{min}$ at which flow commences.

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1: Start with Euler's Turbomachinery Equation**

The general Euler equation for the work done per unit weight of fluid (manometric head) in a centrifugal pump impeller is:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{1}{g}(V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

**Explanation:** This equation represents the change in angular momentum of the fluid between the impeller inlet (suffix 1) and outlet (suffix 2), divided by the gravitational acceleration. $V_w$ is the whirl component of the absolute velocity, and $u$ is the blade speed.

**Step 2: Apply the Inlet Condition (No Pre-whirl)**

At the impeller eye, the water typically enters radially without any tangential component. Thus:

$$V_{w1} = 0$$

This simplifies Euler's equation to:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}$$

**Step 3: Analyze the Outlet Velocity Triangle at Q = 0**

The exit velocity triangle of a centrifugal pump impeller has the following components at design flow:

- $u_2$ = tangential blade speed at exit
- $V_{f2}$ = flow velocity (axial-radial component) at exit
- $V_{r2}$ = relative velocity at exit
- $V_{w2}$ = whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity at exit
- $\beta_2$ = blade angle at exit measured from tangential direction

The geometric relation is:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$$

**Step 4: Apply the Zero-Discharge Condition**

At the moment of starting, no water flows through the impeller, so the flow velocity vanishes:

$$V_{f2} = 0$$

Substituting this into the velocity triangle relation:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - 0 \cdot \cot \beta_2 = u_2$$

**Step 5: Substitute Back into the Head Equation**

$$H_{mano} = \frac{u_2 \cdot u_2}{g} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$

**Step 6: Equate to Static Head for Flow Initiation**

For water to just begin flowing out of the delivery pipe, the manometric head must equal the static head:

$$\frac{u_2^2}{g} = H_s$$

**Step 7: Solve for the Minimum Tangential Velocity**

Squaring both sides' roots:

$$u_2 = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

This is the **minimum blade tip speed** at the impeller outlet.

**Step 8: Convert Tangential Velocity to Rotational Speed**

The tangential velocity of any point on the impeller at radius $r_2 = D_2/2$ is related to the rotational speed by:

$$u_2 = \frac{2 \pi r_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

**Explanation:** This is the standard kinematic relationship where $N$ is in revolutions per minute (RPM), and the factor $60$ converts minutes to seconds.

Solving for $N$:

$$N = \frac{60 \cdot u_2}{\pi D_2}$$

**Step 9: Substitute $u_2 = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$**

$$N_{min} = \frac{60 \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}}{\pi D_2}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2} \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

### **Problem Statement:**

A centrifugal pump is required to lift water from a sump to an overhead tank. The vertical height between the water surfaces is **18 meters**. The outer diameter of the impeller is **300 mm**. Assuming no pre-whirl and zero flow velocity at starting, calculate the minimum speed at which the pump will start delivering water.

### **Given Data:**
- Static head, $H_s = 18 \ \text{m}$
- Outer diameter of impeller, $D_2 = 300 \ \text{mm} = 0.3 \ \text{m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the formula to use**

$$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2} \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the term $\sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$**

$$\sqrt{g \cdot H_s} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 18}$$

Computing the product:

$$9.81 \times 18 = 176.58 \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

Taking the square root:

$$\sqrt{176.58} = 13.289 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator $\pi D_2$**

$$\pi D_2 = \pi \times 0.3 = 0.3 \pi = 0.9425 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Compute $N_{min}$**

$$N_{min} = \frac{60 \times 13.289}{0.9425}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$60 \times 13.289 = 797.34$$

Dividing by the denominator:

$$N_{min} = \frac{797.34}{0.9425} = 845.98 \ \text{RPM}$$

**Step 5: Round off to appropriate significant figures**

$$\boxed{N_{min} \approx 846 \ \text{RPM}}$$

---

## 3.3 Effect of Pre-whirl on Minimum Speed (Advanced Analysis)

If the water enters the impeller with a pre-whirl $V_{w1} \neq 0$ (which occurs in some specialized designs), Euler's equation becomes:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

At zero discharge, $V_{w2} = u_2$, and the minimum speed condition becomes:

$$\frac{u_2^2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g} = H_s$$

$$u_2^2 = g \cdot H_s + V_{w1} u_1$$

$$u_{2, min} = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s + V_{w1} u_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** A positive pre-whirl ($V_{w1}$ in the direction of $u_1$) **reduces** the required minimum speed because the fluid already has some angular momentum. Conversely, a negative pre-whirl (counter-swirl) would **increase** the required minimum speed.

---

## 3.4 Effect of Blade Angle on Minimum Speed

The previous analysis assumed $V_{f2} = 0$ at starting. However, in real pumps, even at zero discharge, a small residual flow may exist due to leakage. For a more general case:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$$

The minimum speed equation generalizes to:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{u_2 (u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2)}{g} = H_s$$

This shows that **blade angle $\beta_2$ influences $N_{min}$** when residual flow exists. For backward-curved blades ($\beta_2 < 90°$), $\cot \beta_2 > 0$, which means a higher $N_{min}$ is needed compared to the idealized zero-flow assumption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: Minimum Speed Determination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Pump Analysis] --> B[Identify System Static Head Hs]
    B --> C[Identify Impeller Geometry: D2, beta2]
    C --> D{Determine Flow Condition at Start}
    D -->|Q Approx 0| E[Apply Zero-Flow Velocity Triangle]
    D -->|Q Greater 0| F[Include Flow Velocity Vf2]
    E --> G[Vw2 equals u2 at Q equals 0]
    F --> H[Compute Vw2 from velocity triangle]
    G --> I[Apply Euler Equation: Hmano equals Vw2 times u2 divided by g]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Equate Hmano to Hs]
    J --> K[Solve for u2 min]
    K --> L[Convert u2 to Nmin using kinematic relation]
    L --> M[Output: Nmin in RPM]
    M --> N[Validate: Operating N must exceed Nmin]
    N --> O[END]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Components Affecting Minimum Speed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUMP[Centrifugal Pump System]
        P1[Impeller Geometry: D2, beta2] --> P2[Blade Tip Velocity u2]
        P2 --> P3[Manometric Head Hmano]
    end
    subgraph SYS[Hydraulic Delivery System]
        S1[Static Head Hs] --> S2[System Resistance Curve]
        S2 --> S3[Operating Point Intersection]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Speed Control Subsystem]
        C1[Motor Drive] --> C2[Rotational Speed N]
        C2 --> P2
        C2 --> C3{Compare N with Nmin}
        C3 -->|N Below Nmin| C4[No Flow: Churning Mode]
        C3 -->|N Above Nmin| C5[Flow Established: Delivery Mode]
    end
    P3 --> S3
    S3 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Velocity Triangle Schematic at Starting Condition (Q = 0)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TRIANGLE[Exit Velocity Triangle at Starting Condition]
        T1[Blade Speed u2] --> T2[Relative Velocity Vr2 aligned tangentially]
        T2 --> T3[Absolute Velocity V2 equals Vw2 equals u2]
        T3 --> T1
    end
    T1 --> T4[Since Vf2 equals 0, the triangle degenerates to a line]
    T4 --> T5[Consequence: Vw2 equals u2]
    T5 --> T6[Hmano equals u2 squared divided by g]
```

## 4.4 Operating Characteristic Curve Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CURVE[Pump H-Q Characteristic and System Curve]
        R1[Rotational Speed N] --> R2[Head Developed Hmano decreases with Q]
        R2 --> R3[At Q equals 0: Hmano equals u2 squared divided by g]
        R3 --> R4[System Static Head Line: Horizontal at Hs]
        R4 --> R5[Intersection Point determines minimum operating speed]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "minimum speed for starting the flow" as applied to a centrifugal pump. State the formula used to calculate it.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):** The minimum speed for starting the flow in a centrifugal pump is defined as the lowest rotational speed of the impeller at which the head developed by the pump just equals the static head of the delivery system, causing the fluid to commence flowing through the delivery pipe.

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2} \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

Where $D_2$ is the impeller outer diameter, $H_s$ is the static head, and $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**What is the role of the velocity triangle at the impeller exit in determining the minimum starting speed of a centrifugal pump?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Role of Velocity Triangle (3 Marks):**

At the moment of starting the flow, the discharge $Q$ is approximately zero. This means the flow velocity component $V_{f2}$ at the impeller exit is also zero. The velocity triangle at the exit degenerates, and the whirl component $V_{w2}$ becomes equal to the tangential blade speed $u_2$.

This simplification is critical because Euler's equation then reduces to:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$

Equating this to the static head $H_s$ directly yields the minimum tangential velocity:

$$u_{2, min} = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

Which is then converted to $N_{min}$ using $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive an expression for the minimum speed at which a centrifugal pump will start delivering water against a given static head. State all assumptions clearly.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A centrifugal pump with an impeller diameter of **350 mm** at the outlet is to deliver water against a static head of **25 m**. Calculate the minimum speed at which the pump will start the flow. Take $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. No pre-whirl at the impeller inlet ($V_{w1} = 0$).
2. Zero flow velocity at the impeller exit ($V_{f2} = 0$).
3. The fluid is incompressible and flow is steady.
4. Blade outlet angle is backward-curved ($\beta_2 < 90°$).

**Derivation (6 Marks):**

Starting with Euler's turbomachinery equation:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{1}{g}(V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

Applying $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

At $Q = 0$, the exit velocity triangle gives $V_{w2} = u_2$:

$$H_{mano} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$

For flow initiation:

$$\frac{u_2^2}{g} = H_s$$

$$u_{2, min} = \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

Converting to rotational speed:

$$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi D_2} \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $D_2 = 350 \ \text{mm} = 0.35 \ \text{m}$
- $H_s = 25 \ \text{m}$
- $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

**Calculation (6 Marks):**

$$\sqrt{g \cdot H_s} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 25} = \sqrt{245.25} = 15.66 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$\pi D_2 = \pi \times 0.35 = 1.0996 \ \text{m}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{60 \times 15.66}{1.0996}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{939.6}{1.0996} = 854.4 \ \text{RPM}$$

**Answer:** $N_{min} \approx 855 \ \text{RPM}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A centrifugal pump is to deliver water against a static head of **12 m**. The impeller has an outer diameter of **250 mm** and runs at a normal operating speed of **1450 RPM**. Determine whether the pump will start delivering water at its operating speed or not. Take $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the static head is increased to **20 m** in the same pump, what will be the new minimum speed required to start the flow? Comment on the operational implications.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $H_s = 12 \ \text{m}$
- $D_2 = 250 \ \text{mm} = 0.25 \ \text{m}$
- $N = 1450 \ \text{RPM}$
- $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Calculate the actual blade tip speed at the operating condition (1 Mark):**

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 1450}{60} = \frac{1138.83}{60} = 18.98 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the head developed at starting (Q = 0) (1 Mark):**

$$H_{mano, start} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} = \frac{(18.98)^2}{9.81} = \frac{360.24}{9.81} = 36.72 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Compare with static head (2 Marks):**

Since $H_{mano, start} = 36.72 \ \text{m} \gg H_s = 12 \ \text{m}$, the pump will **comfortably start delivering water** at 1450 RPM.

**Alternative verification (2 Marks):** Calculate $N_{min}$:

$$N_{min} = \frac{60}{\pi \times 0.25} \sqrt{9.81 \times 12} = \frac{60}{0.7854} \times \sqrt{117.72}$$

$$N_{min} = 76.39 \times 10.85 = 828.7 \ \text{RPM}$$

Since $N_{operating} = 1450 \ \text{RPM} > N_{min} = 828.7 \ \text{RPM}$, the pump will start the flow. ✓

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $H_{s, new} = 20 \ \text{m}$
- Same pump, $D_2 = 0.25 \ \text{m}$

**Step 1: Calculate new $N_{min}$ (3 Marks):**

$$N_{min, new} = \frac{60}{\pi \times 0.25} \sqrt{9.81 \times 20}$$

$$\sqrt{9.81 \times 20} = \sqrt{196.2} = 14.007 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$N_{min, new} = \frac{60 \times 14.007}{0.7854} = \frac{840.42}{0.7854} = 1070.2 \ \text{RPM}$$

**Step 2: Operational Implications (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Observation |
|---|---|
| New $N_{min}$ | 1070 RPM |
| Operating $N$ | 1450 RPM |
| Margin | 1450 - 1070 = 380 RPM (still sufficient) |
| Risk | Margin reduced; any drop in motor speed could stall flow |
| Recommendation | Pump is adequate but operates with reduced safety margin; if head exceeds ~28 m, the pump would fail to start at 1450 RPM |

**Conclusion:** At $H_s = 20 \ \text{m}$, the pump can still start the flow at 1450 RPM, but the operational margin is reduced. For $H_s > 28 \ \text{m}$ (calculated as $u_2^2 / g = 18.98^2 / 9.81 \approx 36.7 \ \text{m}$ theoretical maximum), the pump would fail to deliver water.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $D_2$ must be in **meters**, not mm, before substituting into the formula. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if unit conversion is missed.
>
> 2. **Omitting "$\pi$" in the formula:** A frequent error is writing $N_{min} = (60 / D_2) \sqrt{g H_s}$ without $\pi$. Always write the full formula.
>
> 3. **Confusing static head with manometric head:** The condition $H_{mano} = H_s$ applies **only at starting**. For normal operation, the pump must overcome $H_s$ plus friction losses.
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** In derivation questions, KTU examiners specifically look for the assumption "$V_{w1} = 0$" and "$V_{f2} = 0$ at Q = 0". Omitting these costs **at least 1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Using $g = 10 \ \text{m/s}^2$ inconsistently:** If the problem statement specifies $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$, use that exact value. Do not mix constants.
>
> 6. **Incorrect rounding:** Do not round off intermediate values. Compute $\sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$ as a single expression to avoid compounding rounding errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- [x] **Minimum starting speed** is the threshold speed at which pump head = static head, initiating flow.
- [x] The governing formula is $N_{min} = \dfrac{60}{\pi D_2} \sqrt{g \cdot H_s}$.
- [x] The derivation rests on **Euler's equation** with the simplification that at $Q = 0$, $V_{w2} = u_2$.
- [x] Two critical assumptions: **(i)** No pre-whirl at inlet, **(ii)** Zero flow velocity at exit.
- [x] Minimum speed is **independent** of blade angle $\beta_2$ under ideal zero-flow conditions.
- [x] Minimum speed depends **only on** $D_2$, $H_s$, and $g$ — not on discharge or efficiency.
- [x] For a **given pump**, $N_{min} \propto \sqrt{H_s}$ — doubling the head requires $\sqrt{2}$ times the speed.
- [x] For a **given head**, $N_{min} \propto 1/D_2$ — larger impellers need lower speeds to start flow.
- [x] Real-world operation must maintain $N_{operating} > N_{min}$ to ensure sustained flow.
- [x] Below $N_{min}$, the pump "churns" the fluid without delivery, leading to overheating and damage.
- [x] The unit of $H_s$ in the formula must be in **meters of fluid** (specific to the liquid being pumped).
- [x] For **highly viscous fluids**, the actual minimum speed is higher than predicted by this formula due to viscous head losses, which have been neglected in the ideal derivation.
- [x] **Affinity law application:** If the speed is doubled, the head developed becomes four times — useful for analyzing VFD-controlled pumps.
- [x] In KTU board exams, always show **all substitution steps** and clearly mark the **final boxed answer** for full marks.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Minimum Speed for Starting the Flow**
**Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) Standards**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Suction Lift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maximum Suction Lift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maximum Suction Lift ($H_{s,max}$)** is the greatest possible vertical height, measured from the free liquid surface in the sump (reservoir) to the centerline of the pump impeller eye, at which a pump (primarily a **centrifugal pump**) can lift and deliver liquid without undergoing **cavitation**, losing its prime, or suffering a breakdown in continuous flow operation.

Mathematically, it represents the upper geometric bound of the suction-side installation imposed jointly by:

- **Atmospheric pressure** ($P_{atm}$)
- **Vapor pressure** of the liquid at the operating temperature ($P_v$)
- **Suction pipe friction losses** ($h_f$)
- **Acceleration head** ($h_{acc}$)
- **Net Positive Suction Head available** ($NPSH_a$) versus the **required NPSH** ($NPSH_r$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Maximum suction lift is the highest possible vertical distance a pump can draw water from a source below its centerline, limited by atmospheric pressure, vapor pressure, friction losses, and cavitation threshold."*

### 1.2 The Universal Cap — Why ~10.33 m?

The most counter-intuitive fact every B.Tech student must memorize is that **no pump on Earth can theoretically lift water higher than 10.33 meters at sea level under standard atmospheric conditions**, regardless of impeller size, rotational speed, or power input. This is governed purely by **Torricelli's principle** and atmospheric physics — not by the pump itself.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Drinking Straw

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Drinking Straw**
>
> Imagine trying to drink a milkshake through a long straw. The maximum height you can pull the milkshake up the straw is limited by:
> 1. **Your lung's suction pressure** (analogous to atmospheric pressure pushing the liquid up)
> 2. **The weight of the milkshake column** (analogous to the hydrostatic head)
> 3. **The friction along the straw walls** (analogous to pipe friction losses)
> 4. **Air bubbles forming if you pull too hard** (analogous to cavitation)
>
> If you try to pull harder than the liquid can sustain without vaporizing, the milkshake "cavitates" — air pockets form and flow becomes erratic. This is *exactly* what happens inside a centrifugal pump when suction lift is pushed beyond the maximum.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Atmospheric Pressure** ($P_{atm}$) | **101.325 kPa** or **10.33 m of water** | kPa / m |
| **Density of water at 4 °C** ($\rho$) | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| **Acceleration due to gravity** ($g$) | **9.81** | m/s² |
| **Vapor pressure of water at 20 °C** ($P_v$) | **2.34 kPa** ≈ 0.24 m of water | kPa / m |
| **Vapor pressure of water at 100 °C** ($P_v$) | **101.325 kPa** (equals atmospheric) | kPa |
| **Maximum theoretical suction lift (cold water, sea level)** | **≈ 10.33 m** | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Logic — Why Suction Lift Is Limited

When a centrifugal pump starts, it does **not** "suck" water upward. Instead, atmospheric pressure acting on the **free liquid surface** in the sump **pushes** the liquid up into the pump's low-pressure eye region. The pump merely creates a partial vacuum at its suction.

This creates a clear causal chain:

1. **Atmospheric pressure pushes** water up the suction pipe
2. Water has **weight**, so each meter of vertical lift "consumes" ~9.81 kPa of pressure
3. Water has **vapor pressure** — if the local pressure falls below $P_v$, the water **boils** at ambient temperature → **cavitation** occurs
4. The suction pipe has **friction**, which further reduces the available pressure head
5. Liquid entering the rotating impeller must be **accelerated**, costing additional head (acceleration head)

> [!TIP]
> **Why can't a more powerful pump increase suction lift?**
> Because the limiting pressure (atmospheric) is **external** to the pump. A bigger impeller can deliver more *flow* and more *pressure on the discharge side*, but it cannot pull liquid higher than what the surrounding atmosphere can push up. This is why pumps at high altitudes (e.g., in mountains or Denver, Colorado at 1600 m) have *lower* maximum suction lifts.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{atm} = \rho g H_{atm}$ | Atmospheric pressure head ($H_{atm} \approx 10.33$ m water at sea level) | m |
| 2 | $H_{s,th(max)} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g}$ | **Maximum theoretical suction lift** (no losses) | m |
| 3 | $H_{s,prac(max)} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_f - h_{acc} - NPSH_r$ | **Maximum practical suction lift** (with losses + safety) | m |
| 4 | $NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \pm H_s - h_f$ | Net Positive Suction Head **available** at pump suction | m |
| 5 | $NPSH_r$ (from manufacturer) | Net Positive Suction Head **required** by pump to avoid cavitation | m |
| 6 | Cavitation-free condition: $NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r$ | Essential design check | — |
| 7 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g d}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss in suction pipe | m |
| 8 | $h_{acc} = \dfrac{V^2}{2 g}$ | Velocity head / acceleration head at impeller eye | m |
| 9 | $H_{s,th(max)} = 10.33 - 0.24 = 10.09$ m | Numerical value for cold water at sea level | m |
| 10 | $P_{atm}(h) = 10.33 \left(1 - \dfrac{h}{44300}\right)^{5.256}$ | Atmospheric pressure at altitude $h$ (m above MSL) | m of water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Note (KTU standard):**
> - $H_s$ is **positive** when the pump is located **above** the sump (suction lift)
> - $H_s$ is **negative** when the pump is located **below** the sump (suction head / flooded suction)
> - In the $NPSH_a$ formula, use **+** for suction head and **−** for suction lift

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding maximum suction lift is critical in:

- **Municipal water supply** — designing sump locations relative to pump house elevations
- **Boiler feed pumps** — preventing cavitation in high-temperature condensate systems
- **HVAC chilled water systems** — avoiding pump cavitation at low temperatures
- **Oil & gas pipelines** — handling hydrocarbon liquids with low vapor pressures
- **Marine engineering** — bilge and seawater pumping systems
- **Hydroelectric stations** — drafting tubes and tail-race conditions
- **Chemical process plants** — pumping volatile or hot fluids

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry rule of thumb:** Engineers typically limit practical suction lift to **6 to 7 meters** for cold water at sea level, leaving a generous safety margin of 3–4 m below the theoretical 10.33 m cap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Theoretical Suction Lift

Consider a centrifugal pump drawing water from a sump. Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the free liquid surface in the sump (point 1) and the impeller eye (point 2), assuming:

- Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow
- Both points exposed to atmospheric or near-atmospheric pressure
- No friction (ideal case)

**Step 1:** Write Bernoulli's equation between the sump surface and the pump eye:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + Z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + Z_2$$

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions. At the free surface of the sump:
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (open to atmosphere)
- $V_1 \approx 0$ (large reservoir, negligible velocity)
- $Z_1 = 0$ (reference datum at sump surface)

At the pump suction eye:
- $P_2 = ?$ (the pressure we want to find)
- $V_2 = V$ (velocity of liquid entering impeller)
- $Z_2 = H_s$ (the suction lift we want to find)

**Step 3:** Substitute the conditions:

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + H_s$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the pressure at the pump eye, $P_2$:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V^2}{2g} - H_s$$

**Step 5:** For **cavitation-free operation**, the pressure at the impeller eye $P_2$ must **never fall below** the vapor pressure $P_v$ of the liquid. Set $P_2 = P_v$ as the limiting condition:

$$\dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V^2}{2g} - H_{s,max}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate $H_{s,max}$:

$$H_{s,max} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 7:** For the **theoretical maximum** (ignoring velocity head, i.e., quasi-static consideration):

$$\boxed{H_{s,th(max)} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g}}$$

**Step 8:** Numerical evaluation at sea level for cold water at 20 °C:

$$H_{s,th(max)} = \dfrac{101325 - 2340}{1000 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{98985}{9810} = 10.09 \text{ m}$$

If we ignore vapor pressure (treat water as non-volatile):

$$H_{s,th(max)} = \dfrac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.33 \text{ m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Practical Maximum Suction Lift

In real installations, additional head losses occur. The complete energy balance at the pump suction flange becomes:

$$H_{s,prac(max)} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_f - h_{acc} - NPSH_r$$

**Step-by-step expansion of each term:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g d} \quad \text{(Darcy–Weisbach friction loss in suction pipe)} \\[6pt]
h_{acc} &= \dfrac{V^2}{2 g} \quad \text{(Kinetic energy head at impeller eye)} \\[6pt]
NPSH_r &= \text{Pump-specific value from manufacturer} \quad \text{(Safety margin against local cavitation)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Effect of Altitude on Maximum Suction Lift

Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude. Using the **ISA (International Standard Atmosphere)** model:

$$P_{atm}(h) = P_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{L h}{T_0}\right)^{\dfrac{gM}{RL}}$$

For practical KTU-level work, the simplified form suffices:

$$P_{atm}(h) \approx 10.33 - 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \cdot h \quad \text{(h in meters, valid up to 3000 m)}$$

**Worked comparison:**

| Location | Altitude (m) | $P_{atm}$ (m of water) | $H_{s,th(max)}$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea level (Kochi) | 0 | 10.33 | 10.09 |
| Bengaluru | 920 | 9.20 | 8.96 |
| Delhi | 216 | 10.06 | 9.82 |
| Leh, Ladakh | 3500 | 6.80 | 6.56 |
| Mt. Everest Base Camp | 5364 | 5.04 | 4.80 |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump draws water at 30 °C ($P_v = 4.25$ kPa) from a sump located 4 m below the pump centerline. The suction pipe is 8 m long with diameter 75 mm, friction factor $f = 0.025$, and the velocity in the pipe is 2.5 m/s. The pump requires $NPSH_r = 3.5$ m. Atmospheric pressure is 101 kPa. Check whether cavitation will occur.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute atmospheric pressure head:

$$H_{atm} = \dfrac{101000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute vapor pressure head:

$$H_v = \dfrac{4250}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.433 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute friction head loss:

$$h_f = \dfrac{4 \times 0.025 \times 8 \times (2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075} = \dfrac{5.0}{1.4715} = 3.40 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute velocity head:

$$h_{acc} = \dfrac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.319 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Compute NPSH available (with suction lift $H_s = 4$ m):

$$NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_v - H_s - h_f = 10.30 - 0.433 - 4 - 3.40 = 2.47 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with NPSH required:

$$NPSH_a = 2.47 \text{ m} \;\;<\;\; NPSH_r = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** Since $NPSH_a < NPSH_r$, **cavitation WILL occur**. The pump installation is inadequate. To fix it, the engineer must either:
> - Lower the pump closer to the sump (reduce $H_s$)
> - Use a larger diameter suction pipe (reduce $V$ and $h_f$)
> - Reduce the flow rate
> - Select a pump with lower $NPSH_r$

### 3.5 Maximum Suction Lift from Manufacturer's NPSH Chart (Analytical Matrix)

Manufacturers plot $NPSH_r$ versus flow rate $Q$ for each pump. The maximum suction lift at any operating point is:

$$H_{s,max} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - NPSH_r - h_f - h_{acc}$$

| Flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) | $NPSH_r$ (m) | $V$ (m/s) | $h_f$ (m) | $H_{s,max}$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.005 | 1.2 | 1.13 | 0.18 | 8.18 |
| 0.010 | 1.8 | 2.26 | 0.72 | 7.15 |
| 0.015 | 2.5 | 3.39 | 1.62 | 5.49 |
| 0.020 | 3.5 | 4.52 | 2.88 | 3.23 |

> [!TIP]
> Notice how $H_{s,max}$ **decreases** with flow rate. This is why pumps operated at low flows can have higher suction lifts, while high-flow operations demand either flooded suction or short suction lines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Block Diagram — Components Limiting Suction Lift

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Atm["Atmospheric Pressure Source\nP_atm pushes water upward"]
    Sump["Open Sump / Reservoir\nFree liquid surface"]
    Pipe["Suction Pipe\nlength L, diameter d"]
    Fric["Friction Loss h_f\nproportional to V^2"]
    Acc["Acceleration Head h_acc\nV^2 over 2g"]
    PumpEye["Pump Impeller Eye\nP_2 must remain above P_v"]
    Cav["Cavitation Check\nNPSH_a versus NPSH_r"]
    Out["Maximum Suction Lift\nH_s,max achieved"]
    
    Atm --> Sump
    Sump --> Pipe
    Pipe --> Fric
    Fric --> Acc
    Acc --> PumpEye
    PumpEye --> Cav
    Cav --> Out
    
    Vap["Vapor Pressure P_v\ntemperature dependent"] -.-> PumpEye
    NPSHr["Manufacturer NPSH_r"] -.-> Cav
    Alt["Altitude Effect\nreduces P_atm"] -.-> Atm
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Suction Lift Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1\nIdentify P_atm at site altitude"] --> B["Step 2\nDetermine P_v at liquid temperature"]
    B --> C["Step 3\nCompute theoretical max\nH_th = P_atm - P_v over rho g"]
    C --> D["Step 4\nCompute pipe friction h_f"]
    D --> E["Step 5\nCompute velocity head h_acc"]
    E --> F["Step 6\nObtain NPSH_r from pump curve"]
    F --> G["Step 7\nCalculate H_s,prac max\nH_th minus h_f minus h_acc minus NPSH_r"]
    G --> H{"Step 8\nIs H_s,actual within H_s,prac,max?"}
    H -- "YES" --> I["DESIGN OK\nNo cavitation expected"]
    H -- "NO" --> J["REDESIGN NEEDED\nLower pump, enlarge pipe, or change pump"]
```

### 4.3 Pressure Profile Along the Suction Line (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Sump Surface\nP = P_atm\nH = 0 m"]
    P2["Just inside suction pipe entrance\nP slightly less than P_atm\nH = small entrance loss"]
    P3["Along horizontal pipe length\nP drops linearly with friction"]
    P4["At pump suction flange\nP = P_atm minus rho g H_s minus h_f"]
    P5["At impeller eye\nP_2 = P at flange minus h_acc"]
    Pv["Vapor Pressure Line P_v\nCAVITATION THRESHOLD"]
    
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    Pv -.-> P5
    note["If P_2 falls below P_v line\nCAVITATION OCCURS"]
    Pv -.- note
```

### 4.4 Cavitation-Free Operating Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Zone1["SAFE ZONE\nNPSH_a greater than NPSH_r plus safety margin\nH_s below H_s,max"]
    Zone2["MARGINAL ZONE\nNPSH_a slightly above NPSH_r\nReduced pump life, noise, vibration"]
    Zone3["CAVITATION ZONE\nNPSH_a less than NPSH_r\nPitting, erosion, loss of head"]
    
    Zone1 --> Zone2
    Zone2 --> Zone3
    
    Boundary1["Boundary line 1: NPSH_a equals NPSH_r"] -.-> Zone2
    Boundary2["Boundary line 2: NPSH_a equals NPSH_r minus 1 m safety"] -.-> Zone1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
Define the term **Maximum Suction Lift** of a centrifugal pump. State the factors on which it depends.

**Model Answer:**

Maximum suction lift is the maximum vertical distance between the free liquid surface in the sump and the centerline of the pump impeller, at which the pump can draw liquid continuously without cavitation.

It depends on:
1. **Atmospheric pressure** at the installation site (which decreases with altitude)
2. **Vapor pressure** of the liquid (which increases with temperature)
3. **Friction losses** in the suction pipe
4. **Velocity head** (acceleration head) at the impeller eye
5. **NPSH required** by the pump (manufacturer-specified)
6. **Density of the liquid**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
Why is the maximum theoretical suction lift of a pump at sea level limited to approximately 10.33 m of water?

**Model Answer:**

The limit arises from **atmospheric pressure**. Standard atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa) can support a water column of height:

$$H = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81} \approx 10.33 \text{ m}$$

Since the pump does not "suck" water but merely creates a partial vacuum while atmospheric pressure pushes the water up the suction pipe, no pump — regardless of power, size, or design — can lift water higher than what the surrounding atmosphere can push up. This is a **thermodynamic / fluid-static limit**, not a pump design limit.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the maximum theoretical suction lift of a centrifugal pump. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal pump handles water at 40 °C ($P_v = 7.38$ kPa) from a sump 5 m below the pump. The suction pipe is 10 m long, 80 mm diameter, with friction factor $f = 0.022$. Water velocity in the pipe is 2 m/s. The pump requires $NPSH_r = 2.8$ m. Atmospheric pressure is 100 kPa. Determine the $NPSH_a$ and state whether the pump will cavitate.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow
2. Inviscid (no friction initially — theoretical case)
3. Pump suction eye velocity is negligible
4. No acceleration head considered in the theoretical case
5. Sump is open to atmosphere

**Derivation:** (See Section 3.1 for full derivation)

Applying Bernoulli's equation between sump surface (point 1) and impeller eye (point 2):

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0 + H_s$$

For cavitation-free operation, $P_2 \geq P_v$, so setting $P_2 = P_v$ as the limiting case:

$$H_{s,th(max)} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g}$$

Numerically (cold water, sea level): $H_{s,th(max)} \approx 10.09$ to 10.33 m.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1:** Atmospheric pressure head

$$H_{atm} = \dfrac{100000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.194 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Vapor pressure head (water at 40 °C)

$$H_v = \dfrac{7380}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.752 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Friction loss in suction pipe

$$h_f = \dfrac{4 \times 0.022 \times 10 \times (2)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.08} = \dfrac{3.52}{1.5696} = 2.243 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Velocity / acceleration head

$$h_{acc} = \dfrac{(2)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.204 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $NPSH_a$ (note: pump is above sump, so suction lift $H_s = 5$ m)

$$NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_v - H_s - h_f = 10.194 - 0.752 - 5 - 2.243 = 2.199 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with $NPSH_r$

$$NPSH_a = 2.20 \text{ m} \;\;<\;\; NPSH_r = 2.8 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** Since $NPSH_a < NPSH_r$, **cavitation will occur**. The system is inadequately designed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch how the maximum suction lift of a pump is affected by (i) altitude of the installation, (ii) temperature of the liquid, and (iii) friction in the suction pipe.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pump installed at an altitude of 1500 m above mean sea level is required to deliver water at 25 °C ($P_v = 3.17$ kPa). The suction pipe is 6 m long, 50 mm diameter, $f = 0.026$, and the flow velocity is 1.8 m/s. If the manufacturer specifies $NPSH_r = 2.2$ m, calculate the maximum permissible suction lift.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Skecth Description:** (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3)

**Effects on Maximum Suction Lift:**

| Factor | Effect on $P_{atm}$ or Losses | Effect on $H_{s,max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Higher altitude | $P_{atm}$ decreases | $H_{s,max}$ **decreases** |
| (ii) Higher liquid temperature | $P_v$ increases | $H_{s,max}$ **decreases** |
| (iii) Higher suction pipe friction | $h_f$ increases | $H_{s,max}$ **decreases** |

**Explanation:**

**(i) Altitude:** As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure decreases (thinner air column above). At 1500 m, $P_{atm} \approx 8.6$ m of water, reducing the available pushing force. Maximum suction lift drops from ~10.33 m at sea level to ~8.3 m at 1500 m.

**(ii) Liquid temperature:** As temperature rises, vapor pressure of the liquid rises exponentially. At 100 °C, $P_v = P_{atm}$, so the maximum suction lift becomes **zero** — the pump must be installed below the liquid level (flooded suction).

**(iii) Friction:** Higher pipe friction (longer pipe, smaller diameter, rougher walls, higher flow rate) consumes more of the available head, leaving less for the actual vertical lift.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1:** Atmospheric pressure at 1500 m altitude (using simplified formula):

$$P_{atm} = 10.33 - 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1500 = 10.33 - 0.18 = 10.15 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 2:** Vapor pressure head at 25 °C

$$H_v = \dfrac{3170}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.323 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical maximum suction lift (no losses)

$$H_{s,th(max)} = 10.15 - 0.323 = 9.827 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Friction loss

$$h_f = \dfrac{4 \times 0.026 \times 6 \times (1.8)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{2.020}{0.981} = 2.059 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Velocity head

$$h_{acc} = \dfrac{(1.8)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.165 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Practical maximum suction lift

$$H_{s,prac(max)} = 9.827 - 2.059 - 0.165 - 2.2 = 5.403 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_{s,prac(max)} \approx 5.40 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mistake 1:** Forgetting to subtract $P_v$ when computing theoretical lift, leading to an answer of 10.15 m instead of 9.83 m. **Marks lost: 1–2**
> - **Most common mistake 2:** Wrong sign convention for $H_s$ in the $NPSH$ formula. Always use **subtraction** when pump is **above** the sump (suction lift). **Marks lost: 2**
> - **Most common mistake 3:** Ignoring the $NPSH_r$ term in the practical lift calculation. This is a **mandatory** safety margin. **Marks lost: 2–3**
> - **Most common mistake 4:** Unit inconsistency — using $P$ in kPa and $\rho g$ in different units. Always convert $P/\rho g$ into "m of water" first. **Marks lost: 1**
> - **Most common mistake 5:** Failing to state the **cavitation condition** explicitly. Always conclude with: *"Since $NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r$, the design is safe / not safe."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maximum Suction Lift**

- **Definition:** Maximum vertical height from sump free surface to pump eye without cavitation.
- **Theoretical Limit:** $H_{s,th(max)} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \approx 10.33$ m (cold water, sea level).
- **Practical Limit:** $H_{s,prac(max)} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_f - h_{acc} - NPSH_r$.
- **Master Equation:** $NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \pm H_s - h_f$ (use **−** for lift, **+** for head).
- **Cavitation Rule:** Pump is safe **iff** $NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r$.
- **Altitude effect:** $P_{atm}$ decreases ~12 Pa per meter of altitude gain; $H_{s,max}$ drops.
- **Temperature effect:** $P_v$ rises exponentially; at $T_{boil}$, $H_{s,max} = 0$ → flooded suction mandatory.
- **Pipe friction:** $h_f \propto L V^2 / d$. Use shorter, larger-diameter suction pipes.
- **Velocity head:** $h_{acc} = V^2/2g$. Reduce by lowering velocity (larger pipe diameter).
- **Industry practice:** Limit suction lift to 5–7 m for cold water, 2–3 m for hot water.
- **Flooded suction preferred** for hot liquids, volatile hydrocarbons, and high-altitude installations.
- **Atmospheric pressure head** is the **only** natural force pushing liquid into the pump — pumps don't "suck."
- **Maximum suction lift is a property of the installation + liquid + pump**, not of the pump alone.
- **The 10.33 m cap** is a physical ceiling, not a design guideline — practical values are always lower.
- **Thomas' cavitation parameter** $\sigma = \dfrac{NPSH_r}{H}$ is also used in KTU syllabus as a related concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
