> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Net Positive Suction Head

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is the total head available at the suction port of a pump (or the inlet of a turbine) **above the vapor pressure of the liquid**, measured in meters of the liquid. It represents the margin by which the absolute pressure at the pump inlet exceeds the vapor pressure of the fluid being pumped, thereby preventing **cavitation**.

The term is universally classified into two distinct engineering quantities:

| Quantity | Symbol | Also Known As | Determined By |
|----------|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Net Positive Suction Head Available | **NPSH<sub>A</sub>** | Available NPSH, Site NPSH | System installation (site conditions) |
| Net Positive Suction Head Required | **NPSH<sub>R</sub>** | Required NPSH, Pump NPSH, NPSH<sub>3%</sub> | Pump/turbine manufacturer (test data) |

**Fundamental Operational Criterion (Golden Rule of NPSH):**

$$\boxed{\text{NPSH}_A \;>\; \text{NPSH}_R \;+\; \text{Safety Margin}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Safety Margin in Industry Practice:** A margin of **0.5 m to 1.5 m** above NPSH<sub>R</sub> is recommended for pumps handling cold water, and **1.0 m to 3.0 m** for hot liquids or fluids near saturation temperature.

---

## Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Straw in a Juice Box" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are drinking juice from a sealed juice box using a straw. As you suck harder, the pressure inside the straw drops. If you create too much vacuum, the juice **vaporizes** inside the straw, forming bubbles — and your drink comes out in spurts, making a weird noise.
>
> This is exactly what happens inside a pump. The liquid is pushed by atmospheric pressure (or pump suction conditions) into the pump eye. If the local pressure inside the impeller eye falls **below the vapor pressure**, the liquid flashes into vapor bubbles. When these bubbles travel to regions of higher pressure (impeller exit), they collapse violently — this is **cavitation**.
>
> **NPSH<sub>A</sub>** = the "energy budget" of pressure available above vapor pressure that the system can deliver to the pump.
> **NPSH<sub>R</sub>** = the "minimum budget" the pump demands to function without cavitation.
> If you provide more than what the pump needs, you are safe. If not — **cavitation damage**, noise, vibration, and efficiency loss occur.

---

## Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Standards to Remember:**
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **P<sub>atm</sub> = 101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water (at 4 °C)**
> - **Vapor Pressure of Water at 20 °C:** **P<sub>v</sub> = 2.34 kPa = 0.24 m of water**
> - **Vapor Pressure of Water at 100 °C:** **P<sub>v</sub> = 101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water** (equals atmospheric — this is why pumps cannot lift boiling water)
> - **Specific Weight of Water:** **γ = ρg = 9.81 kN/m³** (at standard conditions)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Cavitation Occurs — The Underlying Physics

Cavitation is fundamentally a **thermodynamic phase-change phenomenon** governed by the pressure-temperature relationship of the liquid. The process occurs in three stages:

1. **Pressure Drop Stage:** As fluid flows from the suction pipe into the pump eye (impeller inlet), the velocity increases and the static pressure drops (Bernoulli's principle).
2. **Vapor Bubble Formation (Nucleation):** When local static pressure falls **below vapor pressure (P<sub>v</sub>)**, dissolved gases come out of solution and the liquid itself vaporizes, forming tiny vapor cavities/bubbles.
3. **Bubble Collapse (Implosion):** As these bubbles convect into regions of higher pressure (toward the impeller exit or volute), they collapse violently, generating **localized shock waves** with pressures up to **1000 MPa**, causing pitting, erosion, noise, and structural damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Cavitation does NOT occur at the suction flange — it occurs at the **impeller eye (low-pressure region)**, where additional pressure drops occur due to flow acceleration and pre-rotation.

---

## 2.2 Physical Significance of NPSH Components

| Component | Physical Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **P<sub>atm</sub>/ρg** | Atmospheric pressure head pushing fluid into suction pipe | Depends on altitude (lower at high elevations) |
| **P<sub>v</sub>/ρg** | Vapor pressure head of liquid at operating temperature | Increases exponentially with temperature |
| **h<sub>s</sub>** | Static suction head (positive if liquid level is above pump centerline) | Determines whether pump is "flooded" or has "suction lift" |
| **h<sub>f</sub>** | Friction losses in suction piping, fittings, valves | Reduces available energy |
| **v²/2g** | Velocity head at suction flange | Usually small and often neglected in KTU problems |

---

## 2.3 KTU Master Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$ | **NPSH Available (General Form)** | m of liquid |
| 2 | $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f$ (flooded suction, $h_s$ positive) | Flooded suction case | m |
| 3 | $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f$ (suction lift, $h_s$ negative) | Suction lift case | m |
| 4 | $\text{NPSH}_A = H_s \pm \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - h_f$ | Energy equation form (using total suction head $H_s$) | m |
| 5 | $\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{H}}$ | **Thoma's Cavitation Parameter** | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\boxed{N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(g \cdot \text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}}}$ | **Suction Specific Speed** (metric form) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{NPSH}_R = \left(\dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{N_s}\right)^{4/3} / g$ | Rearranged suction specific speed for NPSH<sub>R</sub> | m |
| 8 | $\text{NPSH}_R \propto Q^{2/3}$ (from affinity laws) | Required NPSH scales with flow for geometrically similar pumps | m |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\text{NPSH}_{R,2}}{\text{NPSH}_{R,1}} = \left(\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{2/3} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$ | NPSH scaling for homologous pumps | — |
| 10 | $\Delta h_{max} = \text{NPSH}_R - \text{NPSH}_A$ | **Deficit causing cavitation** (must be ≤ 0 for safety) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** In Indian engineering textbooks (Punmia, Modi, Rajput, KTU references):
> - **h<sub>s</sub> = +ve** when the source liquid level is **above** the pump centerline → **flooded suction** (adds head).
> - **h<sub>s</sub> = −ve** when the source is **below** the pump centerline → **suction lift** (reduces head).
> - Always take the sign of h<sub>s</sub> explicitly based on geometry — examiners deduct marks if sign convention is wrong.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pump Installation Design:** NPSH dictates the **maximum permissible suction lift** or **minimum submergence** required to avoid cavitation.
2. **Cooling Water Pumps (Power Plants):** With hot condensate (~80–95 °C), vapor pressure is high — pumps must be located **below the source** (flooded) to maintain adequate NPSH<sub>A</sub>.
3. **Boiler Feed Pumps (BFPs):** Operate with water near saturation, so NPSH<sub>A</sub> margin is very small — specialized BFP designs use **inducer stages** to reduce NPSH<sub>R</sub>.
4. **High-Altitude Installations:** At altitudes > 1000 m, P<sub>atm</sub> drops significantly (e.g., 8.5 m at 3000 m), reducing NPSH<sub>A</sub> drastically.
5. **Marine & Aerospace:** Special low-NPSH pumps are used in submarines, aircraft, and spacecraft.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of NPSH<sub>A</sub> from the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

**Setup:** Consider a pump drawing water from a reservoir through a suction pipe. Apply the steady flow energy equation between **Point 1** (free surface of reservoir) and **Point 2** (pump suction flange/centerline).

**Schematic Coordinate System:**

- Point 1: Reservoir free surface (P<sub>1</sub> = P<sub>atm</sub>, v<sub>1</sub> ≈ 0, elevation = z<sub>1</sub>)
- Point 2: Pump suction centerline (P<sub>2</sub> = P<sub>s</sub>, v<sub>2</sub> = v<sub>s</sub>, elevation = z<sub>2</sub>)

**Step 1 — Write the Steady Flow Energy Equation between 1 and 2:**

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions (free surface of reservoir):**

Since the reservoir surface is large, velocity is negligible (v<sub>1</sub> ≈ 0), and pressure equals atmospheric.

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + z_1 = \dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

**Step 3 — Define the static suction head:**

The static suction head h<sub>s</sub> is the elevation difference between the source liquid level and the pump centerline.

$$h_s = z_1 - z_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute h<sub>s</sub> into the equation:**

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + h_s = \dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} + h_f$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the absolute pressure at the suction flange (P<sub>s</sub>):**

$$\dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + h_s - \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} - h_f$$

**Step 6 — Convert P<sub>s</sub> to absolute pressure head (NPSH concept begins here):**

The NPSH at the suction is defined as the total head (absolute pressure head + velocity head) **above vapor pressure**:

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \left(\dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g}\right) - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute P<sub>s</sub>/ρg from Step 5 into Step 6:**

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \left[\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + h_s - \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} - h_f\right] + \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Step 8 — Cancel the velocity head terms (this is the elegant cancellation):**

The $-\dfrac{v_s^2}{2g}$ and $+\dfrac{v_s^2}{2g}$ terms cancel out, leaving:

$$\boxed{\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Notice that the **velocity head cancels out completely**. This is why the suction pipe diameter (and hence velocity) does **not** appear in the final NPSH<sub>A</sub> equation. The velocity head affects the absolute pressure at the flange, but the NPSH definition includes the velocity head by convention, leading to its disappearance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Thoma's Cavitation Parameter (σ)

**Objective:** To obtain a **dimensionless number** that characterizes cavitation susceptibility of a pump independent of fluid properties.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of NPSH<sub>R</sub> in terms of head:**

$$\text{NPSH}_R = \dfrac{P_{in,min} - P_v}{\rho g}$$

where P<sub>in,min</sub> is the minimum pressure at the impeller eye.

**Step 2 — Define total head developed by the pump H:**

$$H = \dfrac{P_{out} - P_{in}}{\rho g} \approx \text{head developed per unit weight of fluid}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of NPSH<sub>R</sub> to H (both in meters, hence dimensionless):**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{H}$$

**Step 4 — Critical cavitation parameter σ<sub>c</sub> is found experimentally:**

At the onset of cavitation (3% head drop in standard ISO 9906 testing), σ attains a critical value.

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{H} \bigg|_{cavitation \; onset}$$

**Step 5 — Typical σ<sub>c</sub> values:**

| Pump Type | Typical σ<sub>c</sub> Range |
|-----------|----------------------------|
| Radial flow | 0.05 – 0.15 |
| Mixed flow | 0.15 – 0.30 |
| Axial flow | 0.30 – 0.60 |
| Specific speed (N<sub>s</sub>) range | σ<sub>c</sub> increases with N<sub>s</sub> |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** High specific speed pumps (axial flow) have **higher σ<sub>c</sub>** → they are **more susceptible to cavitation**. This is why axial flow pumps are always installed in **flooded suction** (positive h<sub>s</sub>).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Suction Specific Speed (N<sub>ss</sub>)

**Objective:** To create a single dimensionless parameter characterizing the **cavitation performance** of a pump family.

**Step 1 — Start with the standard specific speed N<sub>s</sub>:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

**Step 2 — Replace H in the denominator with NPSH<sub>R</sub>/σ (from cavitation parameter):**

$$H = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{\sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into N<sub>s</sub>:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{\left(\text{NPSH}_R/\sigma\right)^{3/4}} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q} \cdot \sigma^{3/4}}{(\text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}}$$

**Step 4 — The suction specific speed is defined by isolating σ:**

$$N_{ss} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(\text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}} = \dfrac{N_s}{\sigma^{3/4}}$$

**Step 5 — Final form with gravitational constant (for dimensional homogeneity in metric units):**

$$\boxed{N_{ss} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(g \cdot \text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step explanation of each step:**
> - **Step 1:** Standard specific speed uses head developed by the pump.
> - **Step 2:** Cavitation parameter relates NPSH<sub>R</sub> to H.
> - **Step 3:** Algebraic substitution of H with NPSH<sub>R</sub>/σ.
> - **Step 4:** The grouping $N\sqrt{Q}/\text{NPSH}_R^{3/4}$ is the suction specific speed, a measure of how much flow a pump can ingest for a given NPSH.
> - **Step 5:** For SI units, g is included to maintain dimensionless form (since Q is in m³/s, N in rpm).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (Standard KTU Problem Types)

| KTU Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation(s) to Use |
|------------------|-------|------|------------------------|
| Type 1: Cavitation safety check | h<sub>s</sub>, h<sub>f</sub>, P<sub>atm</sub>, P<sub>v</sub>, NPSH<sub>R</sub> | Whether cavitation occurs | Compute NPSH<sub>A</sub>, compare to NPSH<sub>R</sub> |
| Type 2: Maximum suction lift | P<sub>atm</sub>, P<sub>v</sub>, h<sub>f</sub>, NPSH<sub>R</sub>, safety margin | Maximum permissible h<sub>s</sub> | $h_s = \text{NPSH}_R + \text{margin} + h_f - \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g}$ |
| Type 3: Minimum submergence | NPSH<sub>R</sub>, h<sub>f</sub>, P<sub>atm</sub>, P<sub>v</sub> | Minimum h<sub>s</sub> (flooded) | $\text{NPSH}_A = \text{NPSH}_R$, solve for h<sub>s</sub> |
| Type 4: Thoma's parameter | NPSH<sub>R</sub>, H | σ | $\sigma = \text{NPSH}_R / H$ |
| Type 5: Suction specific speed | N, Q, NPSH<sub>R</sub> | N<sub>ss</sub> | $N_{ss} = N\sqrt{Q}/(g \cdot \text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}$ |
| Type 6: Affinity law application | NPSH<sub>R,1</sub>, Q<sub>1</sub>, Q<sub>2</sub> | NPSH<sub>R,2</sub> at new flow | $\text{NPSH}_{R,2} = \text{NPSH}_{R,1} (Q_2/Q_1)^{2/3}$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NPSH Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RESERVOIR OR SOURCE TANK] --> B[SUCTION PIPE WITH FITTINGS]
    B --> C[PUMP SUCTION FLANGE P_s]
    C --> D[IMPELLER EYE LOW PRESSURE REGION]
    D --> E[IMPELLER EXIT HIGH PRESSURE REGION]
    E --> F[VOLUTE OR DIFFUSER]
    F --> G[DISCHARGE FLANGE]

    H[ATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE P_atm] --> B
    I[FLUID VAPOR PRESSURE P_v] --> D
    J[STATIC SUCTION HEAD h_s] --> C
    K[FRICTION LOSS h_f] --> B

    L[NPSH_A CALCULATION ENGINE] --> M{IS NPSH_A GREATER THAN NPSH_R?}

    D --> N[VAPOR BUBBLE FORMATION]
    N --> O[BUBBLE COLLAPSE CAVITATION]
    O --> P[PUMP DAMAGE NOISE VIBRATION]

    Q[SAFETY MARGIN TYPICALLY 0.5 TO 1.5 M] --> M
    M -->|YES| R[SAFE OPERATION PUMP FUNCTIONS NORMALLY]
    M -->|NO| O
```

**Block Interpretation Guide:**

| Block | Role in NPSH Framework |
|-------|------------------------|
| A → B → C | Energy transfer path from source to pump suction |
| D | Critical low-pressure zone where cavitation initiates |
| E → F → G | Pressure recovery (normal pumping action) |
| H, I, J, K | System inputs that combine to determine NPSH<sub>A</sub> |
| L | Mathematical model computing NPSH<sub>A</sub> |
| M | Decision logic gate — cavitation safety check |
| N → O → P | Cavitation failure chain (consequence of NPSH<sub>A</sub> < NPSH<sub>R</sub>) |
| R | Successful operation regime |

---

## 4.2 NPSH Decision Tree for Cavitation Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([START: PUMP OPERATING CONDITIONS KNOWN]) --> Step1[COMPUTE NPSH_A FROM SITE DATA]
    Step1 --> Step2[GET NPSH_R FROM MANUFACTURER CATALOG]
    Step2 --> Step3[APPLY SAFETY MARGIN 0.5 M TO 1.5 M]
    Step3 --> Compare{NPSH_A IS GREATER THAN NPSH_R PLUS MARGIN?}
    Compare -->|YES| Safe[PUMP OPERATES WITHOUT CAVITATION]
    Compare -->|NO| Danger[CAVITATION WILL OCCUR]
    Danger --> Action[ACTIONS: REDUCE SUCTION LIFT / LOWER PUMP / INCREASE PIPE DIAMETER / COOL FLUID / USE INDUCER]
    Action --> Reevaluate[RECOMPUTE NPSH_A AND REPEAT]
    Reevaluate --> Step1
    Safe --> Monitor[CONTINUOUS MONITORING OF VIBRATION AND NOISE]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH). Distinguish between NPSH<sub>A</sub> and NPSH<sub>R</sub>.**

**Model Answer (Point-Wise):**

1. **Definition:** NPSH is the total head (in meters of liquid) available at the pump suction above the vapor pressure of the liquid being pumped. It is the margin by which the absolute pressure at the pump inlet exceeds the vapor pressure.

2. **NPSH Available (NPSH<sub>A</sub>):** Determined by the **system/installation**. It is a function of atmospheric pressure, vapor pressure, static suction head, and suction line friction losses.
$$\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$$

3. **NPSH Required (NPSH<sub>R</sub>):** Determined by the **pump manufacturer** through cavitation testing. It is the minimum NPSH that must be provided to the pump at a given flow rate to prevent cavitation.

4. **Criterion:** For safe operation, **NPSH<sub>A</sub> > NPSH<sub>R</sub>** (plus a safety margin of 0.5–1.5 m).

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**State and explain Thoma's cavitation parameter. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Statement:** Thoma's cavitation parameter σ is defined as the **dimensionless ratio** of the required NPSH to the head developed by the pump:
$$\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{H}$$

2. **Critical value:** σ<sub>c</sub> corresponds to the onset of cavitation (typically defined as a 3% drop in head, per ISO 9906).

3. **Significance:**
   - It is **dimensionless** and **independent of fluid properties**, making it a universal cavitation indicator.
   - It increases with specific speed — high N<sub>s</sub> pumps (axial flow) have higher σ<sub>c</sub> and are more cavitation-prone.
   - It is used for **model-to-prototype scaling** of cavitation behavior.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign convention errors on h<sub>s</sub>:** If the source is **above** pump → use **+h<sub>s</sub>** (flooded); if **below** → use **−h<sub>s</sub>** (suction lift). Mixing these up is the #1 cause of full-mark loss in KTU NPSH problems.
> - **Forgetting to subtract vapor pressure:** Many students write NPSH = P<sub>atm</sub>/ρg ± h<sub>s</sub> − h<sub>f</sub> and miss the **−P<sub>v</sub>/ρg** term. This gives a high (incorrect) value and falsely declares the pump safe.
> - **Not applying the safety margin:** KTU expects you to add at least 0.5 m margin when comparing NPSH<sub>A</sub> with NPSH<sub>R</sub>.
> - **Confusing pump NPSH with turbine NPSH:** For **turbines**, the formula uses the tailrace elevation differently — read the question carefully.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Module 4]**

**A centrifugal pump draws water from a sump 3 m below the pump centerline. The suction pipe is 100 mm diameter, 8 m long, with a friction factor f = 0.025 (Darcy). The pump delivers 0.05 m³/s at 1450 rpm. The vapor pressure of water is 2.5 kPa and atmospheric pressure is 98 kPa. The manufacturer's catalog indicates NPSH<sub>R</sub> = 4.0 m at this operating point. Determine:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The NPSH available at the pump suction. Is the pump safe from cavitation? Justify with a 1 m safety margin.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The maximum permissible suction lift if the friction head loss is to be limited to 0.5 m, with all other data unchanged.

---

#### **Model Solution (a): NPSH Available Calculation**

**Step 1 — Calculate the velocity in the suction pipe:**

$$A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

$$v_s = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} = \dfrac{0.05}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.366 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the friction head loss (Darcy-Weisbach equation):**

$$h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} = 0.025 \cdot \dfrac{8}{0.10} \cdot \dfrac{(6.366)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.025 \times 80 \times \dfrac{40.53}{19.62} = 2.0 \times 2.066 = 4.13 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Determine static suction head h<sub>s</sub>:**

Since the sump is **3 m below** the pump centerline → this is a **suction lift** → use **−h<sub>s</sub>** = −3.0 m.

**Step 4 — Compute the atmospheric and vapor pressure heads:**

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{98 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = 9.99 \; \text{m of water}$$

$$\dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.255 \; \text{m of water}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the NPSH<sub>A</sub> formula:**

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f$$

$$\text{NPSH}_A = 9.99 - 0.255 - 3.0 - 4.13 = 2.605 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Cavitation safety check with 1 m margin:**

$$\text{NPSH}_{A, \, \text{effective}} = 2.605 - 1.0 = 1.605 \; \text{m}$$

$$\text{NPSH}_R = 4.0 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

Since $\text{NPSH}_A (1.605 \; \text{m}) < \text{NPSH}_R (4.0 \; \text{m})$, **cavitation WILL occur**. ❌

**Conclusion:** The pump is **NOT safe**. Remedial actions needed: reduce suction lift, increase pipe diameter, or relocate the pump closer to the sump.

---

#### **Model Solution (b): Maximum Permissible Suction Lift**

**Step 1 — Set the cavitation condition (with safety margin):**

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \text{NPSH}_R + \text{safety margin}$$

$$9.99 - 0.255 - h_s - 0.5 = 4.0 + 1.0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for h<sub>s</sub>:**

$$9.235 - h_s = 5.0$$

$$h_s = 9.235 - 5.0 = 4.235 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{h_{s, \, \text{max}} = 4.235 \; \text{m (suction lift)}}$$

The pump can be located at most **4.235 m above the sump water level** to operate safely under the given conditions.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2022, Supplementary Exam]**

**A double-suction centrifugal pump handles 0.25 m³/s of water at 960 rpm with a head of 30 m. The manufacturer's NPSH<sub>R</sub> at this duty point is 5.5 m. Atmospheric pressure is 100 kPa, vapor pressure of water is 7.4 kPa, and the suction pipe friction loss is 0.8 m. The pump centerline is 2 m above the water level in the sump.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Check whether the pump operates without cavitation. If not, by how much should the pump be lowered?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate Thoma's cavitation parameter and the suction specific speed for this pump.

---

#### **Model Solution (a): Cavitation Check**

**Step 1 — Pressure head terms:**

$$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.194 \; \text{m}$$

$$\dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{7.4 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.754 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Suction head with sign convention:**

Since pump is **2 m above** the sump water level → **suction lift** → $h_s = -2.0 \; \text{m}$

**Step 3 — Compute NPSH<sub>A</sub>:**

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_f$$

$$\text{NPSH}_A = 10.194 - 0.754 - 2.0 - 0.8 = 6.64 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with NPSH<sub>R</sub> + safety margin (using 0.5 m):**

$$\text{NPSH}_R + \text{margin} = 5.5 + 0.5 = 6.0 \; \text{m}$$

Since $\text{NPSH}_A (6.64 \; \text{m}) > 6.0 \; \text{m}$ → **Pump is SAFE** ✓

**Step 5 — Find how much the pump can be raised (not lowered) further:**

The current pump centerline is 2 m above the sump. We have a surplus of:

$$\Delta h = 6.64 - 6.0 = 0.64 \; \text{m}$$

So the pump can be raised by **0.64 m more** (i.e., placed 2.64 m above the sump) and still remain safe.

**If the question demands "lowering," the answer is: NO lowering is required; the pump is already safe.**

---

#### **Model Solution (b): Thoma's Parameter and Suction Specific Speed**

**Step 1 — Thoma's cavitation parameter:**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{H} = \dfrac{5.5}{30} = 0.1833$$

**Step 2 — Suction specific speed (metric, with g = 9.81 m/s²):**

$$N_{ss} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(g \cdot \text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}}$$

$$N_{ss} = \dfrac{960 \times \sqrt{0.25}}{(9.81 \times 5.5)^{3/4}}$$

**Numerator:** $960 \times 0.5 = 480$

**Denominator:** 
$(9.81 \times 5.5) = 53.955$

$(53.955)^{3/4} = (53.955)^{0.75}$

Computing: $53.955^{0.5} = 7.345$, $53.955^{0.25} = (7.345)^{0.5} = 2.710$

Therefore: $(53.955)^{0.75} = 7.345 \times 2.710 = 19.91$

$$N_{ss} = \dfrac{480}{19.91} = 24.11 \; \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\sigma = 0.183, \quad N_{ss} = 24.11}$$

A suction specific speed of ~24 indicates a **mixed flow** type pump, which is consistent with typical double-suction centrifugal designs.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Master These Before the Exam:**

### **Core Conceptual Points**
- ☐ NPSH = total head at suction above **vapor pressure**, measured in meters of liquid.
- ☐ Two types: **NPSH<sub>A</sub>** (site/system) and **NPSH<sub>R</sub>** (pump/manufacturer).
- ☐ **Golden rule:** $\text{NPSH}_A > \text{NPSH}_R + \text{safety margin (0.5–1.5 m)}$.
- ☐ Cavitation occurs at the **impeller eye**, NOT at the suction flange.
- ☐ Vapor pressure increases **exponentially** with temperature — hot liquids cavitate easily.

### **Formula Mastery (Must memorize)**
- ☐ $\text{NPSH}_A = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$
- ☐ **Flooded suction** → +h<sub>s</sub>; **Suction lift** → −h<sub>s</sub>
- ☐ $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH}_R}{H}$ (Thoma's parameter, dimensionless)
- ☐ $N_{ss} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(g \cdot \text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}}$ (suction specific speed)

### **Numerical Values to Remember**
- ☐ P<sub>atm</sub> at sea level = **101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water**
- ☐ P<sub>v</sub> of water at 20 °C = **2.34 kPa = 0.24 m**
- ☐ P<sub>v</sub> of water at 100 °C = **101.325 kPa** (equals atmospheric)
- ☐ ρg for water = **9.81 kN/m³**

### **Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ☐ Don't forget the **−P<sub>v</sub>/ρg** term — it's the most common mistake.
- ☐ Don't mix up signs of h<sub>s</sub> — read the geometry carefully.
- ☐ Don't confuse pump NPSH analysis with **turbine** NPSH analysis.
- ☐ Always include the **safety margin** when reporting whether a pump is safe.

### **Quick-Reference Comparison Table**

| Feature | Pump | Turbine |
|---------|------|---------|
| Cavitation location | Impeller eye (inlet) | Runner exit (draft tube) |
| Energy equation direction | Suction to discharge | Headrace to tailrace |
| Setting elevation rule | Lower NPSH<sub>A</sub> by reducing h<sub>s</sub> | Increase draft tube height (limited by atmospheric) |
| Typical σ range | 0.05 (radial) to 0.60 (axial) | 0.02 to 0.10 (Francis), 0.10 to 0.40 (Kaplan) |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cavitation** is a hydrodynamic phenomenon occurring in turbomachines handling liquids (centrifugal pumps, axial flow pumps, hydraulic turbines, propellers, etc.) wherein the **local static pressure** of the liquid at certain points inside the machine drops to or below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. This causes the formation of vapor-filled cavities or bubbles within the liquid. When these bubbles are swept into regions of higher pressure downstream, they collapse violently, generating high-pressure shock waves that erode metal surfaces, generate noise and vibration, and degrade the hydraulic performance of the machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: Cavitation is the formation, growth, and sudden collapse of vapor bubbles in a flowing liquid when the local static pressure falls below the saturated vapor pressure of the liquid at the operating temperature. The collapse of these bubbles produces destructive pitting on impeller/blade surfaces and loss of efficiency.

### 1.2 Key Pressure References in Cavitation Analysis

For any hydraulic turbomachine, three reference pressures are crucial:

| Pressure Reference | Symbol | Typical Value (Water, 25°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric / Barometric Pressure | $p_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Vapor Pressure of Liquid | $p_v$ | **3.17 kPa** |
| Local Static Pressure inside Machine | $p$ | Varies (must stay $> p_v$) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine boiling water in a sealed but flexible container. When the pressure inside drops (or temperature rises), bubbles form — this is essentially cavitation. Now imagine those bubbles being violently squeezed back into liquid water by high pressure — that collapse is what destroys the impeller blades of a pump running with insufficient suction pressure. Another classic analogy is the "popcorn effect": kernels pop (vapor bubbles form) when heated (pressure drops), and the burst sound mimics the collapse damage inside a hydraulic machine.

In simpler terms:
- A pump impeller spinning fast creates low-pressure regions on its suction side (like air over an airplane wing creating lift, except here it "sucks" too hard).
- If this low pressure drops below the water's vapor pressure, water literally "boils" at room temperature.
- These vapor pockets (bubbles) are then hurled into the high-pressure discharge region, where they **implode**.
- The implosion is so energetic that it pits, cracks, and eventually destroys the impeller surface.

### 1.4 Where Cavitation Occurs

| Machine Type | Location of Cavitation |
|---|---|
| Centrifugal Pump | Suction side of impeller eye, near the impeller inlet vane tips |
| Axial Flow Pump | Suction side of runner blades |
| Hydraulic Turbine (Reaction) | Runner blade trailing edges (Francis), blade tips (Kaplan) |
| Propeller | Blade suction surface near tip |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cavitation Number / Thoma's Cavitation Coefficient ($\sigma$)

The **Thoma cavitation parameter** is a dimensionless number that quantifies the margin of safety against cavitation.

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_{ref} - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2}
$$

Where:
- $p_{ref}$ = reference pressure (usually $p_{atm}$ for pumps)
- $p_v$ = vapor pressure of the liquid at operating temperature
- $\rho$ = density of the liquid
- $V$ = reference velocity (usually the peripheral velocity of the impeller, $U_1$, or meridional velocity at impeller inlet, $V_{m1}$)

**KTU Convention** (follows the textbook by Modi & Seth / Som & Biswas used in Kerala):
- For **pumps**: $\sigma = \dfrac{p_{inlet} - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2}$ (using impeller inlet velocity)
- For **turbines**: $\sigma = \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g H}$ (using net head $H$)

### 2.2 Critical Cavitation Condition

Cavitation begins when the local static pressure reaches the vapor pressure. The condition at which cavitation **just starts** is called the **critical cavitation condition**, characterized by:
- **Critical cavitation parameter** ($\sigma_c$ or $\sigma_{cr}$)
- **Critical Net Positive Suction Head** (NPSH$_{\text{required}}$)

### 2.3 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

The NPSH is the total head available at the pump suction flange above the vapor pressure head. It has two forms:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{available} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{p_s}{\rho g} \pm H_s - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}
$$

Where:
- $\dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g}$ = atmospheric pressure head
- $\dfrac{p_s}{\rho g}$ = gauge pressure at the suction reservoir surface
- $H_s$ = static suction lift (positive if liquid is below pump centerline, negative if flooded suction)
- $\dfrac{p_v}{\rho g}$ = vapor pressure head

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for $H_s$**: $H_s$ is taken **positive** when suction lift exists (liquid below pump) and **negative** when suction head exists (liquid above pump). The most common convention used in KTU problems: $+H_s$ for lift, $-H_s$ for head.

The **NPSH$_{required}$** (or NPSH$_R$) is set by the pump manufacturer and depends on:
- Impeller geometry
- Flow rate $Q$
- Rotational speed $N$

The cavitation-free operation rule:
$$
\text{NPSH}_{available} \geq \text{NPSH}_{required} + \text{Safety Margin (typically 0.5 to 1.0 m)}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Thoma Cavitation Parameter (Pump) | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_1 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2}$ | Dimensionless analysis |
| Thoma Cavitation Parameter (Turbine) | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g H}$ | For hydraulic turbines |
| NPSH$_{available}$ | $\dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{p_s}{\rho g} \pm H_s - \dfrac{p_v}{\rho g}$ | Pump installations |
| NPSH$_{margin}$ | $\text{NPSH}_{av} - \text{NPSH}_{req}$ | Safety margin |
| Suction Specific Speed | $N_{ss} = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(NPSH_R)^{3/4}}$ | Pump comparison |
| Minimum Submergence Depth | $S_{min} = \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g} - \text{NPSH}_R - h_f$ | Open-sump installations |
| Critical Cavitation Velocity | $V_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_{atm} - p_v)}{\rho}}$ | Threshold for cavitation onset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: The cavitation parameter $\sigma$ allows engineers to predict whether a given machine will cavitate under specific operating conditions. It also enables model-to-prototype scaling in hydraulic model testing — i.e., testing a small-scale pump model in a laboratory and predicting cavitation behavior of the full-scale prototype. This is a standard tool in hydraulic machine design labs in IITs/NITs and is examinable in KTU Module 4.

### 2.5 Effects of Cavitation

1. **Pitting and Erosion**: Bubble collapses erode blade surfaces, reducing life.
2. **Vibration and Noise**: Characteristic high-frequency "rattle" sound.
3. **Loss of Head / Efficiency**: Performance curves drop sharply after cavitation onset.
4. **Reduction in Discharge** (pumps) or **interruption of flow** (turbines).
5. **Structural Damage**: In severe cases, complete failure of blades.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Thoma's Cavitation Coefficient from Euler's Equation

Consider the flow entering a centrifugal pump impeller at section 1 (inlet). Applying the **steady-flow energy equation** between the suction reservoir (point $S$) and the impeller inlet (point 1), both taken as free liquid surfaces or reference points:

$$
\frac{p_S}{\rho g} + \frac{V_S^2}{2g} + z_S = \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_L
$$

**Step 1**: Simplify assuming $V_S \approx 0$ (large reservoir) and ignoring minor head loss $h_L$ for ideal analysis. Also take $z_1 - z_S = -H_s$ where $H_s$ is the suction lift.

$$
\frac{p_S}{\rho g} = \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - H_s
$$

**Step 2**: Rearrange to express $p_1$ (the static pressure at impeller inlet):

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{p_S}{\rho g} + H_s - \frac{V_1^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3**: The pressure at the suction reservoir is the atmospheric pressure (if open) plus any pressurization $p_s$ (gauge). For a typical installation: $p_S = p_{atm}$.

$$
p_1 = p_{atm} + \rho g H_s - \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4**: Cavitation just begins when $p_1 = p_v$ (local static pressure equals vapor pressure). Substitute:

$$
p_v = p_{atm} + \rho g H_s - \frac{\rho V_{1,cr}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5**: Solve for the critical inlet velocity $V_{1,cr}$ at which cavitation commences:

$$
\frac{\rho V_{1,cr}^2}{2} = p_{atm} - p_v + \rho g H_s
$$

$$
V_{1,cr} = \sqrt{\frac{2(p_{atm} - p_v)}{\rho} + 2gH_s}
$$

**Step 6**: Define Thoma's cavitation parameter using the absolute velocity at impeller inlet $V_1$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v + \rho g H_s}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2}
$$

**Step 7**: The **critical** value of $\sigma$ is reached when $V_1 = V_{1,cr}$:

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v + \rho g H_s}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{1,cr}^2} = 1 \quad \text{(at the threshold)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: If $\sigma_{operating} > \sigma_c$, the machine operates safely without cavitation. If $\sigma_{operating} < \sigma_c$, cavitation occurs. This dimensionless ratio allows direct comparison between machines of different sizes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of NPSH from Energy Equation

Starting from the suction reservoir to the pump suction flange (point $F$):

**Step 1**: Write Bernoulli's equation between free surface of suction sump ($S$) and pump suction flange ($F$):

$$
\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + z_S = \frac{p_F}{\rho g} + \frac{V_F^2}{2g} + z_F + h_{f,s}
$$

**Step 2**: Take $z_S - z_F = H_s$ (suction lift) and rearrange:

$$
\frac{p_F}{\rho g} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s - \frac{V_F^2}{2g} - h_{f,s}
$$

**Step 3**: Write Bernoulli's equation from pump suction flange ($F$) to the impeller eye ($E$) where the pressure becomes minimum:

$$
\frac{p_F}{\rho g} + \frac{V_F^2}{2g} + z_F = \frac{p_E}{\rho g} + \frac{V_E^2}{2g} + z_E + h_{f,F-E}
$$

**Step 4**: The minimum pressure point $E$ is at the impeller eye. For cavitation onset, $p_E = p_v$. Combining the two equations:

$$
\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s - h_{f,s} - h_{f,F-E} = \frac{p_v}{\rho g} + \frac{V_E^2}{2g} + (z_E - z_F)
$$

**Step 5**: Defining NPSH$_{available}$ as the total head available at the pump suction above vapor pressure head, after subtracting all friction losses and accounting for the velocity head at the eye:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{av} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{p_s}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}
$$

Where $h_f$ represents total friction losses in suction piping.

**Step 6**: The pump manufacturer specifies NPSH$_{required}$ = $V_E^2 / (2g) + (z_E - z_F)$ + internal losses. For cavitation-free operation:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{av} > \text{NPSH}_{req}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Suction Specific Speed Limit (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1**: Thoma proposed an empirical correlation for maximum permissible suction specific speed:

$$
N_{ss,max} = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(NPSH_R)^{3/4}} \leq K
$$

Where $K$ depends on impeller type:
- Single-suction centrifugal pump: $K \approx 11{,}000$ to $14{,}000$ (SI: rpm, m³/s, m)
- Double-suction centrifugal pump: $K \approx 14{,}000$ to $20{,}000$
- Axial flow pump: $K \approx 15{,}000$ to $25{,}000$

**Step 2**: Rearranging for the minimum NPSH required:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{R,min} = \left[\frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{K}\right]^{4/3}
$$

**Step 3**: For a given $N$ and $Q$, this gives the minimum NPSH the pump must operate at to avoid cavitation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cavitation in Pumps vs Turbines

| Aspect | Centrifugal Pump | Hydraulic Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Impeller eye / inlet vane tips | Runner blade trailing edges |
| $\sigma$ definition | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_1 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2}$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g H}$ |
| Effect of cavitation | Loss of discharge, head drops, noise | Loss of power, efficiency drop, vibration |
| Prevention | Reduce $H_s$, lower $T$, increase $p_{atm}$ | Reduce tailwater level, avoid overloading |
| Critical parameter | NPSH$_{R}$ | $\sigma_c$ (Thoma's critical sigma) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cavitation Formation Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Enters Suction Pipe] --> B[Pressure Decreases as Velocity Increases]
    B --> C{Local Pressure vs Vapor Pressure}
    C -->|p greater pv| D[Normal Flow No Cavitation]
    C -->|p less pv| E[Vapor Bubbles Form Cavities]
    E --> F[Bubbles Carried to High Pressure Zone]
    F --> G[Bubble Collapse Implosion]
    G --> H[Surface Erosion Pitting]
    G --> I[Vibration and Noise]
    G --> J[Efficiency Loss]
    H --> K[Component Failure]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Machine Performance Degradation]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture for NPSH Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Suction Reservoir Conditions]
        A1[Atmospheric Pressure patm]
        A2[Liquid Temperature T]
        A3[Static Suction Head Hs]
        A4[Friction Losses hf]
    end
    subgraph B[Liquid Properties]
        B1[Liquid Density rho]
        B2[Vapor Pressure pv]
    end
    subgraph C[NPSH Calculation Engine]
        C1[NPSH available Formula]
        C2[Compare with NPSH required]
    end
    subgraph D[Decision Output]
        D1[Safe Operation]
        D2[Cavitation Risk]
    end
    A --> C1
    B --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
```

### 4.3 Cavitation Onset Threshold Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Operating Point Setup] --> S1[Define Velocity V1]
    S1 --> S2[Compute p1 using Bernoulli]
    S2 --> S3{Is p1 less than pv}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[CAVITATION OCCURS]
    S3 -->|No| S5[SAFE NO CAVITATION]
    S4 --> S6[Reduce Hs or V1]
    S6 --> S1
    S5 --> S7[Continue Operation]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description of Cavitation in a Centrifugal Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Schematic Description**: A centrifugal pump with vertical suction lift $H_s$ draws liquid from a sump. The pressure decreases progressively from atmospheric at the sump surface to a minimum at the impeller eye (point $E$). Beyond point $E$, the impeller adds energy and pressure rises rapidly through the volute to the discharge. Cavitation bubbles form at the eye when the pressure falls below $p_v$ and collapse as they move into the higher-pressure impeller passages and volute, causing erosion on the impeller vane leading edges.

```
Sump Surface (patm)
        |
        |  Hs (suction lift)
        v
+-------+
| PUMP  | --(eye, p=pv minimum)--+
|       |                       |
+-------+                       v
                          Volute (high p)
                          Bubbles collapse here
                          Causing blade erosion
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Define cavitation. State the conditions under which cavitation occurs in a centrifugal pump.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation of vapor bubbles (cavities) in a flowing liquid when the local static pressure falls below the vapor pressure of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. The bubbles subsequently collapse in regions of higher pressure, producing noise, vibration, erosion of surfaces, and loss of efficiency.

**Conditions (1.5 marks):** Cavitation occurs when:
1. The local static pressure $p$ at any point inside the pump falls below the vapor pressure $p_v$ of the liquid.
2. Suction lift $H_s$ is excessive, NPSH$_{available}$ falls below NPSH$_{required}$.
3. Liquid temperature is high, raising $p_v$ and reducing NPSH margin.
4. The pump operates at higher speeds or flows beyond design point.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Explain the term Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH). Why is NPSH important in pump selection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** NPSH$_{available}$ is the total head available at the pump suction flange above the vapor pressure head of the liquid. Mathematically:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{av} = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g} + H_s - h_f
$$

where $H_s$ is the static suction head (positive if flooded) and $h_f$ is friction loss in suction piping.

**Importance (2 marks):**  
1. NPSH$_{available}$ must exceed NPSH$_{required}$ (set by manufacturer) for cavitation-free operation.
2. If NPSH$_{available}$ < NPSH$_{required}$, cavitation occurs leading to pitting, vibration, and efficiency loss.
3. It dictates the maximum permissible suction lift, pipe diameter, and installation altitude.
4. It is essential in selecting pumps for hot liquids, high-altitude installations, and long suction lines.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**  
*(a)* Define Thoma's cavitation coefficient. Derive an expression for the cavitation parameter for a centrifugal pump.  
*(b)* A centrifugal pump delivers water at a rate of $0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ against a head of $20 \text{ m}$ at $1450 \text{ rpm}$. The diameter of the impeller is $0.30 \text{ m}$ and the velocity of flow at inlet is $2.5 \text{ m/s}$. If the cavitation parameter is to be limited to $0.10$ and atmospheric pressure is $10.3 \text{ m}$ of water, find the maximum allowable value of suction lift. Take vapor pressure head = $0.24 \text{ m}$ of water.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Thoma's cavitation coefficient $\sigma$ is a dimensionless parameter that represents the ratio of the net pressure head available above vapor pressure to the velocity head at the impeller inlet.

**Derivation (5 marks):** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the suction reservoir surface (point $S$) and the impeller inlet (point 1):

$$
\frac{p_S}{\rho g} + \frac{V_S^2}{2g} + z_S = \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_L
$$

With $V_S \approx 0$, $h_L \approx 0$, and $z_S - z_1 = H_s$:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{p_S}{\rho g} + H_s - \frac{V_1^2}{2g}
$$

Substituting $p_S = p_{atm}$:

$$
p_1 = p_{atm} + \rho g H_s - \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2}
$$

Cavitation occurs when $p_1 = p_v$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v + \rho g H_s}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2}
$$

This is **Thoma's cavitation parameter**.

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 20 \text{ m}$, $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$
- $D = 0.30 \text{ m}$, $V_{f1} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$
- $\sigma = 0.10$
- $\dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} = 10.3 \text{ m}$, $\dfrac{p_v}{\rho g} = 0.24 \text{ m}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the peripheral velocity of impeller:

$$
U_1 = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60}
$$

$$
U_1 = \frac{\pi \times 435}{60} = \frac{1366.76}{60} = 22.78 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Take reference velocity as $V_1 \approx V_{f1}$ (meridional velocity for cavitation parameter as per KTU convention):

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_{atm} - p_v + \rho g H_s}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $g/\rho g$ to convert to head form:

$$
\sigma = \frac{\dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{p_v}{\rho g} + H_s}{\dfrac{V_1^2}{2g}}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $\dfrac{V_1^2}{2g}$:

$$
\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 0.3186 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\sigma = 0.10$ and solve for $H_s$:

$$
0.10 = \frac{10.3 - 0.24 + H_s}{0.3186}
$$

$$
0.10 \times 0.3186 = 10.06 + H_s
$$

$$
0.03186 = 10.06 + H_s
$$

$$
H_s = 0.03186 - 10.06 = -10.03 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret result:

The negative value of $H_s$ indicates that the pump must be installed with a **suction head** of about $10.03 \text{ m}$ below the water surface (i.e., the pump must be **flooded** by placing the sump liquid level $10.03 \text{ m}$ above the pump centerline).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: A very common mistake students make is forgetting that in Thoma's formula, **$H_s$ is signed** — positive for suction lift (water below pump) and negative for suction head (water above pump). Many students write $|H_s| = 10.03 \text{ m}$ without indicating the direction, leading to loss of marks. Also, ensure you use the correct reference velocity — $V_1$ (absolute inlet velocity) versus $V_{f1}$ (meridional velocity). The KTU convention in most solved problems is $V_1 = V_{f1}$ when the pre-whirl is zero.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – July 2023 – Alternate Choice]**  
*(a)* What is NPSH? Explain the difference between NPSH$_{available}$ and NPSH$_{required}$.  
*(b)* A pump is to be installed at an elevation of $1500 \text{ m}$ above sea level. The barometric pressure at this altitude is $84.5 \text{ kPa}$. The pump handles water at $40°C$ ($p_v = 7.38 \text{ kPa}$). The suction pipe has a friction loss of $0.50 \text{ m}$ of water, and the static suction lift is $3.0 \text{ m}$. The pump manufacturer's data gives NPSH$_{required}$ = $4.0 \text{ m}$. Determine whether the pump will cavitate. If so, suggest two remedial measures.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – NPSH Concept (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) is the total suction head available at the pump inlet above the vapor pressure head of the liquid.

**Differences (5 marks):**

| Aspect | NPSH$_{available}$ | NPSH$_{required}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Head available at pump suction flange above vapor pressure | Minimum head required by the pump to avoid cavitation |
| Determined by | System design (atmospheric pressure, temperature, suction lift, friction) | Pump design (impeller eye geometry, speed, flow) |
| Formula | $\dfrac{p_{atm} - p_v}{\rho g} \pm H_s - h_f$ | Set by manufacturer (from cavitation tests) |
| Control | Can be increased by reducing $H_s$ or pipe friction | Decreased by special impeller design (e.g., suction impeller) |
| Operation rule | Must be ≥ NPSH$_{required}$ for safe operation | — |

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Altitude = $1500 \text{ m}$, $p_{atm} = 84.5 \text{ kPa}$
- Water at $40°C$: $p_v = 7.38 \text{ kPa}$
- Friction loss: $h_f = 0.50 \text{ m}$
- Static suction lift: $H_s = 3.0 \text{ m}$ (positive since water is below pump)
- NPSH$_{required} = 4.0 \text{ m}$

**Step 1:** Convert pressures to heads using $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$
\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{84.5 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{84500}{9810} = 8.614 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\frac{p_v}{\rho g} = \frac{7.38 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{7380}{9810} = 0.752 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate NPSH$_{available}$:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{av} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f - \frac{p_v}{\rho g}
$$

$$
\text{NPSH}_{av} = 8.614 - 3.0 - 0.50 - 0.752
$$

$$
\text{NPSH}_{av} = 4.362 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with NPSH$_{required}$:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{av} = 4.362 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{NPSH}_{req} = 4.0 \text{ m}
$$

Margin = $4.362 - 4.0 = 0.362 \text{ m}$

**Step 4:** Conclusion:

The pump will **NOT cavitate** under normal conditions, but the margin ($0.362 \text{ m}$) is small. Recommended safety margin is at least **$0.6 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ m}$**. Hence, there is a risk of cavitation if conditions deviate.

**Step 5:** Remedial measures if cavitation is anticipated:

1. **Reduce suction lift** — Place the pump closer to the sump level or below the water surface (flooded suction).
2. **Reduce friction losses** — Use larger diameter suction pipe, minimize bends and fittings.
3. **Cool the liquid** — Lower temperature reduces $p_v$, increasing NPSH margin.
4. **Use a booster pump** — A small pump upstream increases suction pressure.
5. **Select a low-NPSH pump** — Replace with a pump having lower NPSH$_{required}$ or use an inducer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes**: (i) Many students forget to convert kPa to metres of water head (divide by $\rho g = 9810$). (ii) Students often add $H_s$ instead of subtracting when it is a suction lift. (iii) KTU expects units throughout — silent conversions without showing work cost marks. (iv) Marks are awarded for interpretation/conclusion, not just the calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Cavitation**: Local static pressure falls below vapor pressure → vapor bubbles form → collapse in high-pressure zones → erosion, noise, vibration.
- **Three Critical Pressures**: $p_{atm}$, $p_v$, and local static $p$. Always $p > p_v$ for safe operation.
- **Thoma's Cavitation Parameter**: $\sigma = \dfrac{p_{ref} - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$ — dimensionless indicator of cavitation risk.
- **NPSH$_{available}$**: $\dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm H_s - h_f - \dfrac{p_v}{\rho g}$ — depends on installation.
- **NPSH$_{required}$**: Set by pump manufacturer based on impeller geometry, $N$, and $Q$.
- **Safe Operation Rule**: NPSH$_{av} \geq$ NPSH$_{req} + 0.6$ to $1.0 \text{ m}$ safety margin.
- **Pump Cavitation Location**: Impeller eye, suction side of vane tips.
- **Turbine Cavitation Location**: Runner blade trailing edges, draft tube region.
- **Critical Velocity**: $V_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_{atm} - p_v)}{\rho}}$ — maximum permissible inlet velocity.
- **Suction Specific Speed**: $N_{ss} = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(\text{NPSH}_R)^{3/4}}$ — bound by Thoma limit ($K \approx 11{,}000$–$25{,}000$ depending on pump type).
- **Prevention Strategies**:
  - Minimize suction lift
  - Cool the liquid (lower $p_v$)
  - Increase suction pipe diameter (lower friction)
  - Use flooded suction where possible
  - Select low-NPSH pump / use inducer
  - Avoid operation at very low or very high flow rates
- **High Altitude Effect**: Atmospheric pressure drops → NPSH$_{av}$ drops → cavitation risk rises.
- **Hot Liquids**: Vapor pressure rises → NPSH$_{av}$ drops → cavitation risk rises.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip for KTU Exam**: Always state the **direction** of $H_s$ clearly in numericals. Always **convert all pressures to consistent units** (Pa or metres of water). Always show the **final comparison** (NPSH$_{av}$ vs NPSH$_{req}$) and a **concluding sentence** ("safe / unsafe operation") — this fetches the interpretation marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Priming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Need for Priming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Priming** in turbomachinery refers to the preparatory operation of completely filling the **impeller casing** (volute) and the **suction pipe** of a centrifugal pump with the working liquid **before** the pump is started.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, priming is the act of **expelling air (or any gaseous medium) trapped inside the pump suction line and casing**, and replacing it with the actual liquid to be pumped. Until this is done, the pump cannot develop the required suction lift or discharge head, and continuous operation is impossible.

The physical justification is rooted in the **Euler turbomachinery equation**, where the specific work done by the impeller is directly proportional to the density of the fluid being accelerated:

$$w = U_2 \, C_{\theta 2} - U_1 \, C_{\theta 1} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho}$$

If the fluid inside the casing is **air** rather than water, the dynamic pressure rise $\Delta P$ generated by the impeller is so small (because $\rho_{air} \approx \dfrac{1}{800}\,\rho_{water}$) that it cannot overcome the **suction lift**, **friction losses**, and the **weight of the water column** to be lifted. The pump simply churns the air in place — a condition known as **air-binding** or **air-locking**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of priming as "wet-the-straw" before you drink.**

Imagine you try to drink a milkshake through a long straw, but the straw is dry and full of air. No matter how hard you suck, the milkshake will not rise — because the air inside expands and contracts, but never lets the dense liquid climb the column.

Now dip the straw fully into the milkshake so that the entire tube is **filled with liquid first**. Suddenly, even a gentle sip pulls the milkshake upward easily. This is exactly what priming does for a centrifugal pump.

| Property | Dry Straw (Air inside) | Primed Straw (Liquid inside) |
|---|---|---|
| Density of fluid | **1.2 kg/m³** | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Can lift water? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Suction pull effect | Almost zero | Strong |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Density of water at 4 °C: **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Density of air at STP: **ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Density ratio: **ρ_air / ρ_water ≈ 1/816**
> - Atmospheric pressure head of water: **H_a ≈ 10.33 m**
> - Vapor pressure head of water at 30 °C: **H_v ≈ 0.44 m**

These constants govern *why* a tiny pressure rise on air cannot lift water, and *how much* priming is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Logic — Why Priming Is Mandatory

A centrifugal pump works on the principle of converting **kinetic energy** imparted by the impeller into **pressure energy** at the volute outlet. For the pump to "pull" water up from a sump located below its centreline, two conditions must be satisfied **simultaneously**:

1. The impeller must generate enough pressure to overcome the **suction lift** ($H_s$), the **friction loss** in the suction pipe ($H_{fs}$), the **acceleration head** ($H_a$), and the **vapor pressure head** of water ($H_v$).

2. The fluid column in the suction line must be **continuous and liquid**, with no entrapped air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage-by-stage operational logic:**

- **Stage 1 — Empty casing (air-filled):** When the pump is dry-started, the impeller rotates in air. The relative velocity triangle generates a centrifugal pressure rise $\Delta P = \rho \cdot \dfrac{(v_2^2 - v_1^2)}{2}$. With $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, this $\Delta P$ is **816 times smaller** than what water would produce. The pump cannot lift even a 10 cm column.

- **Stage 2 — Air-binding phenomenon:** Even a few bubbles of trapped air in the suction line cause **cavitation-like discontinuity**. The bubbles expand in the low-pressure eye of the impeller and collapse in the high-pressure region, breaking the continuity of the fluid column.

- **Stage 3 — Priming operation:** Liquid is introduced (manually, by vacuum, or by auxiliary pump) until **100 % of the suction pipe and volute casing** is filled. Air is displaced through a vent valve at the highest point of the casing.

- **Stage 4 — Continuous operation:** Once primed, the impeller accelerates the (now continuous) liquid column. The momentum transfer from impeller to liquid creates a low-pressure zone at the impeller eye (suction), drawing more liquid up from the sump.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Reasoning

> [!TIP]
> **Why can't air be pumped?** Because pressure head $H = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$ is what lifts water. If $\rho$ drops by a factor of 816, then for the same $\Delta P$, the head drops by the same factor — meaning a 10 m water column would need the impeller to do **816 × 10 = 8160 m** of air-head. Physically impossible for any single-stage centrifugal pump.

> **How is priming done?**
> - **Manual priming** — pour water through a priming port until casing overflows.
> - **Vacuum priming** — use an external vacuum pump to suck air out of the casing; atmospheric pressure pushes water up the suction pipe.
> - **Ejector priming** — use a steam or water-jet ejector (Venturi) to evacuate air.
> - **Self-priming pumps** — built-in reservoir and re-circulation chamber that retains liquid between cycles.
> - **Foot valve priming** — a non-return valve at the sump end keeps the suction line full when the pump stops.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Priming is **non-negotiable** in:
- **Boiler feed pumps** in thermal power stations
- **Fire-fighting pumps** in high-rise buildings
- **Agricultural irrigation pumps** drawing water from open wells
- **Marine bilge and ballast pumps**
- **Cooling water circulation pumps** in process industries

Failure to prime leads to **cavitation damage, seal failure, bearing overheating**, and total **mechanical seizure** of the impeller in extreme cases.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure rise across impeller | $\Delta P \;=\; \rho \cdot \dfrac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2}$ | Pa | Depends on fluid density |
| 2 | Head developed | $H \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$ | m of fluid | Inverse with $\rho$ |
| 3 | Maximum suction lift (with priming) | $H_s^{max} \;=\; H_a \;-\; H_v \;-\; H_{fs} \;-\; H_{acc}$ | m | $H_a \approx 10.33$ m at sea level |
| 4 | Atmospheric pressure head | $H_a \;=\; \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g}$ | m | Water = 10.33 m, Mercury = 0.76 m |
| 5 | NPSH available | $NPSH_a \;=\; H_a \;\pm\; H_s \;-\; H_v \;-\; H_{fs}$ | m | $(+)$ if suction below datum, $(-)$ if flooded suction |
| 6 | Priming effectiveness ratio | $\eta_{prime} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{liquid}}{V_{casing} + V_{suction\ pipe}}$ | dimensionless | Ideal $= 1.0$ |
| 7 | Density ratio (air/water) | $\rho_{air} / \rho_{water} \;\approx\; 1/816$ | — | At standard conditions |
| 8 | Cavitation parameter (Thoma) | $\sigma \;=\; \dfrac{NPSH_a}{H}$ | dimensionless | Cavitation begins when $\sigma \leq \sigma_{critical}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition for priming success:** The volumetric fraction of air remaining in the casing must be **less than 1 %** of the total internal volume for stable continuous operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Air Cannot Be Pumped (Quantitative Proof)

We start from the Euler turbomachinery equation for the impeller, considering absolute and relative velocity components at the impeller **inlet (1)** and **outlet (2)**.

The specific work done per unit mass of fluid by the impeller, assuming **radial-entry** ($C_{\theta 1} = 0$) and a backward-curved blade, is:

$$
w \;=\; U_2 \, C_{\theta 2} \;-\; U_1 \, C_{\theta 1}
$$

Since at the inlet $C_{\theta 1} = 0$, the equation reduces to:

$$
w \;=\; U_2 \, C_{\theta 2}
$$

The pressure rise produced by this work, using the steady-flow energy equation and neglecting losses, is:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \rho \, w \;=\; \rho \, U_2 \, C_{\theta 2}
$$

The corresponding **head** developed (in metres of the fluid being pumped) is:

$$
H \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g} \;=\; \dfrac{U_2 \, C_{\theta 2}}{g}
$$

> **Critical observation:** The *head* $H$ is **independent of density** under ideal conditions, but the *pressure rise* $\Delta P$ is **directly proportional to $\rho$**. This is the key asymmetry that breaks priming.

### Worked Numerical Comparison — Air vs Water

Let a centrifugal pump impeller operate at:
- Blade tip speed $U_2 = 30$ m/s
- Tangential velocity component $C_{\theta 2} = 25$ m/s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate the head developed (same for both fluids):**

$$
H \;=\; \dfrac{U_2 \, C_{\theta 2}}{g} \;=\; \dfrac{30 \times 25}{9.81} \;=\; \dfrac{750}{9.81}
$$

$$
H \;\approx\; 76.45 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure rise when pumping water ($\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³):**

$$
\Delta P_w \;=\; \rho_w \, g \, H \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 76.45
$$

$$
\Delta P_w \;\approx\; 7.50 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \;=\; 7.50 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure rise when pumping air ($\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³):**

$$
\Delta P_a \;=\; \rho_a \, g \, H \;=\; 1.225 \times 9.81 \times 76.45
$$

$$
\Delta P_a \;\approx\; 918.8 \text{ Pa} \;=\; 0.00919 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 4 — Suction lift capability for water (against gravity + 2 m friction + 0.5 m vapor head):**

$$
H_s^{max} \;\approx\; H_a \;-\; H_v \;-\; H_{fs} \;\approx\; 10.33 \;-\; 0.44 \;-\; 2.0
$$

$$
H_s^{max} \;\approx\; 7.89 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Step 5 — Suction lift capability for air (against gravity + friction in air column):**

A 1 m column of air exerts a head of only $\dfrac{1.225}{1000} \times 1 = 0.001225$ m of water equivalent. To lift even **1 mm** of water, the air column would need to climb approximately **816 mm**. This is why a dry pump cannot self-prime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** The head developed ($H$) is identical in both cases (~76 m), but the *ability to physically lift water* depends on pressure $\Delta P$, which collapses by a factor of **816** when air is the working fluid. Hence priming is mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Suction Lift with Priming (Bernoulli-Based)

Consider the suction pipe from the sump (point **s**) to the impeller eye (point **i**). Applying Bernoulli's equation between these two points:

$$
\dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} \;+\; \dfrac{v_s^2}{2g} \;+\; z_s \;=\; \dfrac{P_i}{\rho g} \;+\; \dfrac{v_i^2}{2g} \;+\; z_i \;+\; H_{fs}
$$

Here:
- $P_s = P_{atm}$ (sump open to atmosphere)
- $z_i - z_s = H_s$ (suction lift, positive when pump is above sump)
- $v_s \approx 0$ (large sump, negligible velocity)
- $P_i = P_{vapor}$ at the threshold of cavitation

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \;=\; \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \;+\; \dfrac{v_i^2}{2g} \;+\; H_s \;+\; H_{fs}
$$

Solving for maximum suction lift:

$$
H_s^{max} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} \;-\; \dfrac{v_i^2}{2g} \;-\; H_{fs}
$$

$$
H_s^{max} \;=\; H_a \;-\; H_v \;-\; H_{acc} \;-\; H_{fs}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** This equation sets the upper limit on how far above the sump the pump can be mounted, **provided the suction line is fully primed**. Any air pocket reduces the effective $H_a$ and lowers the permissible $H_s$ dramatically.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Incomplete Priming on Pump Performance

| Priming Quality | Air Volume Fraction | Effective $\rho$ in Casing | Head Developed | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fully primed | 0 % | **1000 kg/m³** | 100 % of design | ✅ Safe |
| Mostly primed | 5 % | ~ 775 kg/m³ | ~ 77 % of design | ⚠️ Mild loss |
| Partially primed | 20 % | ~ 500 kg/m³ | ~ 50 % of design | ⚠️ Cavitation risk |
| Poorly primed | 50 % | ~ 250 kg/m³ | ~ 25 % of design | ❌ Severe damage |
| Unprimed (dry) | 100 % | **1.225 kg/m³** | ~ 0.12 % of design | ❌ No pumping |

> [!TIP]
> **Take-away:** Even a 5 % air pocket cuts the design head by nearly one-quarter. This is why KTU examiners love asking about the **percentage loss in head due to incomplete priming**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Priming Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional sequence** of the priming process in a centrifugal pump installation, from the dry-state to fully-primed continuous operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pump OFF, Casing DRY] --> B[Open Priming Port Valve]
    B --> C[Pour Liquid or Activate Vacuum Pump]
    C --> D{Air Displaced Through Vent?}
    D -- No --> E[Continue Priming - Watch Vent Valve]
    E --> D
    D -- Yes --> F[Close Priming Port and Vent Valve]
    F --> G[Verify Liquid Level in Casing = 100 percent]
    G --> H{Casing Fully Liquid-Filled?}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Start Main Pump Motor]
    I --> J[Impeller Accelerates Liquid Column]
    J --> K[Suction Lift Overcome by rho x g x H]
    K --> L[Continuous Liquid Delivery Achieved]
    L --> M[Stable Operating Point Reached]
    M --> N[Monitor NPSHa vs NPSHr Continuously]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Functional Architecture — Priming Methods

This Mermaid block maps the **decision tree for selecting the appropriate priming technique** based on installation type and duty requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Centrifugal Pump Installation] --> Q{Suction Condition}
    Q --> R[Below Sump - Flooded Suction]
    Q --> S[Above Sump - Dry Suction]
    R --> T[No Priming Required]
    S --> U{Pump Type}
    U --> V[Conventional Centrifugal]
    U --> W[Self Priming Design]
    V --> X{Priming Method}
    X --> Y[Manual Pour-Through]
    X --> Z[Vacuum Pump Evacuation]
    X --> AA[Steam Ejector Venturi]
    X --> AB[Foot Valve Plus Refill]
    W --> AC[Built-in Reservoir Recirculation]
    AC --> AD[Automatic Reprime on Start]
    Y --> AE[Operator Dependent]
    Z --> AF[Reliable Industrial Standard]
    AA --> AG[Used in Thermal Plants]
    AB --> AH[Common in Agricultural Pumps]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Pressure Build-Up Sequence After Priming

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[t = 0 s: Motor ON, Liquid in Casing] --> P1[t = 0.1 s: Impeller Spins Up]
    P1 --> P2[t = 0.5 s: Low Pressure at Impeller Eye]
    P2 --> P3[t = 1.0 s: Atmospheric Pressure Pushes Liquid Up Suction Pipe]
    P3 --> P4[t = 2.0 s: Liquid Reaches Impeller Eye]
    P4 --> P5[t = 2.5 s: Kinetic Energy Conversion Begins]
    P5 --> P6[t = 3.0 s: Pressure Rises in Volute]
    P6 --> P7[t = 5.0 s: Discharge Valve Opens, Flow Stabilises]
    P7 --> P8[Steady State Operation Achieved]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each node represents a **functional state** of the pumping system, not a physical location. The arrows represent causal transitions governed by fluid dynamics equations (Bernoulli, Euler).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define priming of a centrifugal pump. Why is it necessary?

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Priming is the process of filling the impeller casing and the suction pipe completely with the liquid to be pumped, before starting the centrifugal pump. (1 mark)

It is necessary because a centrifugal pump cannot develop sufficient pressure to lift water when air is present inside the casing, since air has a density only about **1/816th** that of water. (1 mark)

The pressure rise produced by the impeller ($\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$) is too small for air to overcome the suction lift, friction losses, and the weight of the water column. Therefore, without priming, the pump simply churns air without delivering any liquid. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. State any three methods of priming a centrifugal pump.

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Manual priming** — pouring liquid directly into the casing through a priming port until it overflows.
2. **Vacuum priming** — using an external vacuum pump to evacuate air from the casing; atmospheric pressure then pushes liquid up the suction pipe.
3. **Self-priming pump** — built-in liquid reservoir and recirculation chamber that automatically re-primes the pump at each start.

*(Other acceptable answers: ejector priming using a Venturi nozzle; foot valve with manual refill.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **need for priming** in a centrifugal pump with neat functional sketches. Discuss the role of fluid density and atmospheric pressure in determining the priming capability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Priming is the preparatory filling of the suction line and pump casing with the working liquid. Without priming, a centrifugal pump cannot establish continuous flow because air, being 816 times less dense than water, cannot transmit sufficient pressure to lift the water column.

**Physical reasoning (2 marks):** The Euler turbomachinery equation gives the pressure rise as $\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$. When $\rho$ drops from 1000 to 1.225 kg/m³, $\Delta P$ collapses by the same factor. The pump's ability to lift water against suction head ($H_s$), friction ($H_{fs}$), and vapor pressure ($H_v$) becomes vanishingly small.

**Atmospheric pressure role (2 marks):** Atmospheric pressure (~10.33 m of water head) is the prime mover during priming. When the casing is evacuated by vacuum or filled by gravity, atmospheric pressure acting on the sump surface pushes water up the suction pipe. Maximum suction lift is given by:

$$
H_s^{max} \;=\; H_a \;-\; H_v \;-\; H_{fs} \;-\; H_{acc}
$$

**Functional sketch description (1 mark):** A block diagram showing sump → foot valve → suction pipe → pump casing → vent valve → discharge. Air is evacuated from the casing; liquid fills progressively from the sump upward.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Priming is indispensable for any centrifugal pump installation where the suction source is below the pump centreline. Modern self-priming pumps automate this process but rely on the same physical principles.

---

**(b)** A centrifugal pump is required to deliver water at a suction lift of **5 m** from a sump. The suction pipe has a friction loss of **1.2 m** and an acceleration head of **0.4 m**. The water temperature is **30 °C** (vapor pressure head = 0.44 m of water). Determine: **(i)** Whether priming will be sufficient at **sea level** ($H_a = 10.33$ m), and **(ii)** the maximum permissible suction lift under these conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Suction lift $H_s = 5$ m
- Friction head loss $H_{fs} = 1.2$ m
- Acceleration head $H_{acc} = 0.4$ m
- Vapor pressure head $H_v = 0.44$ m
- Atmospheric head $H_a = 10.33$ m (sea level)

**Step 1 — Apply the suction lift equation (1 mark):**

$$
H_s^{max} \;=\; H_a \;-\; H_v \;-\; H_{fs} \;-\; H_{acc}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$
H_s^{max} \;=\; 10.33 \;-\; 0.44 \;-\; 1.2 \;-\; 0.4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$
H_s^{max} \;=\; 10.33 \;-\; 2.04 \;=\; 8.29 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with required suction lift (1 mark):** Required $H_s = 5$ m ≤ Maximum permissible $H_s^{max} = 8.29$ m. Therefore, priming will be **sufficient** at sea level with a safety margin of 3.29 m.

**Step 5 — Part (ii): maximum permissible suction lift (1 mark):** Already calculated as **8.29 m**.

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The pump can be installed up to 8.29 m above the sump water level. Any installation above this height will cause cavitation and loss of prime, even with proper priming.

**Step 7 — Practical recommendation (1 mark):** To improve safety margin, reduce friction by increasing suction pipe diameter or shortening its length. Avoid sharp bends in the suction line.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**Q. (a)** Compare **centrifugal pumps** and **positive-displacement pumps** with respect to their priming requirements. Why can a reciprocating pump self-prime while a centrifugal pump cannot? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison table concept (3 marks):**

| Feature | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Kinetic-to-pressure energy conversion | Direct volume displacement |
| Fluid density dependence | High (head inversely related) | Low |
| Self-priming ability | No (requires external priming) | Yes |
| Suction lift capability | Limited (~7–8 m max at sea level) | Can exceed 8 m easily |
| Valve dependency | None on suction side | Foot / suction valve mandatory |

**Why reciprocating pumps self-prime (2 marks):** A reciprocating pump creates a positive displacement of volume. The piston or plunger physically enlarges the suction chamber volume, creating a near-vacuum that draws water up the suction pipe. Atmospheric pressure pushes water into this vacuum regardless of the fluid's density being pumped. The expanding chamber's action is essentially independent of whether air or water is present initially — both can be displaced.

**Why centrifugal pumps cannot self-prime (2 marks):** A centrifugal impeller imparts velocity to whatever fluid is in the casing. Since air is ~816 times less dense than water, the momentum transfer from impeller blades to air is too feeble to generate a pressure rise sufficient to lift the water column. The impeller simply spins the air without effectively evacuating it.

---

**(b)** A centrifugal pump impeller has a tip diameter of **0.4 m**, rotates at **1450 rpm**, and has a tangential velocity component of water leaving the impeller $C_{\theta 2} = 22$ m/s. The radial-velocity component is $C_{r2} = 6$ m/s. Assuming no pre-whirl and ideal flow, calculate: **(i)** the impeller tip speed, **(ii)** the Euler head developed, and **(iii)** the pressure rise when the pump is primed with water versus when it is dry (air-filled). Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D_2 = 0.4$ m
- $N = 1450$ rpm
- $C_{\theta 2} = 22$ m/s
- $C_{r2} = 6$ m/s
- No pre-whirl: $C_{\theta 1} = 0$
- $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate the impeller tip speed $U_2$ (1 mark):**

$$
U_2 \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, D_2 \, N}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 1450}{60}
$$

$$
U_2 \;=\; \dfrac{1822.12}{60} \;\approx\; 30.37 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Euler head $H$ (1 mark):**

$$
H \;=\; \dfrac{U_2 \, C_{\theta 2}}{g} \;=\; \dfrac{30.37 \times 22}{9.81}
$$

$$
H \;=\; \dfrac{668.14}{9.81} \;\approx\; 68.11 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure rise for water (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta P_w \;=\; \rho_w \, g \, H \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 68.11
$$

$$
\Delta P_w \;\approx\; 6.68 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \;=\; 6.68 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 4 — Pressure rise for air (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta P_a \;=\; \rho_a \, g \, H \;=\; 1.225 \times 9.81 \times 68.11
$$

$$
\Delta P_a \;\approx\; 818.6 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 0.00819 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 5 — Ratio and comment (2 marks):**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P_w}{\Delta P_a} \;=\; \dfrac{1000}{1.225} \;\approx\; 816
$$

**Comment:** The pressure rise with water is **816 times greater** than with air. Although the Euler head $H = 68.11$ m is identical in both cases, the dry pump's pressure rise (~0.008 bar) is utterly insufficient to overcome even a 1 m water column (~0.098 bar). This quantitatively demonstrates **why priming is mandatory** for any centrifugal pump.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing head with pressure** — The Euler head $H$ is the same for water and air, but the *pressure* $\Delta P = \rho g H$ is not. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rad/s** — A frequent mistake is using $U_2 = \pi D N$ directly without dividing by 60.
> 3. **Ignoring vapor pressure head $H_v$** — At temperatures above 25 °C, $H_v$ becomes non-negligible and must be subtracted in $H_s^{max}$ calculations.
> 4. **Mixing suction lift sign convention** — Always take $H_s$ as *positive* when pump is above sump and *negative* (flooded suction) when below.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer** — KTU evaluators deduct up to 0.5 marks for missing SI units.
> 6. **Writing "priming removes air completely"** — Correction: priming *displaces* air to less than 1 % of internal volume; perfect vacuum is not required.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
