> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist:**

- ⚡ **Priming** = filling the suction pipe and casing with liquid before starting.
- ⚡ **Centrifugal pumps cannot pump air** because $\rho_{air} \approx \dfrac{1}{816}\,\rho_{water}$.
- ⚡ Pressure rise $\Delta P = \rho g H$, but head $H$ itself is **density-independent** under ideal conditions.
- ⚡ Maximum suction lift: $H_s^{max} = H_a - H_v - H_{fs} - H_{acc}$.
- ⚡ Atmospheric pressure head of water ≈ **10.33 m** at sea level.
- ⚡ Vapor pressure head $H_v$ rises with temperature (0.17 m at 20 °C, 0.44 m at 30 °C, 1.43 m at 60 °C).
- ⚡ **Reciprocating pumps self-prime**; centrifugal pumps do not (without auxiliary arrangement).
- ⚡ Methods of priming: manual pour-through, vacuum pump, ejector, foot valve, self-priming design.
- ⚡ **Air-binding** occurs even with partial air pockets (5 % air → 23 % loss in head).
- ⚡ The density ratio **816** is the magic number KTU examiners expect in numerical answers.
- ⚡ Cavitation parameter: $\sigma = \dfrac{NPSH_a}{H}$; operation requires $\sigma > \sigma_{critical}$.
- ⚡ A primed pump's continuous operation depends on maintaining $NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r$ throughout the duty cycle.
- ⚡ Priming is **non-negotiable** for fire pumps, boiler feed pumps, and agricultural lift pumps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumps in Series and Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pumps in Series and Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In turbomachinery practice, a single centrifugal or axial flow pump is frequently incapable of meeting the combined demands of **head (pressure rise)** and **discharge (flow rate)** required by a process system. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4: Hydraulic Turbines & Pumps) addresses this operational limitation through two standardized multi-pump arrangements:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumps in Series:** Two or more *geometrically similar* pumps are connected such that the discharge of the first pump becomes the suction of the second. The arrangement is designed to **increase the total head (pressure rise)** delivered to the system, while the flow rate through each pump remains identical to the flow rate through the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumps in Parallel:** Two or more *geometrically similar* pumps discharge into a common delivery manifold. The arrangement is designed to **increase the total volumetric flow rate (discharge)** delivered to the system, while the head developed by each pump remains identical to the system head.

The decision between series or parallel combination is governed by the **System Resistance Curve** (H_sys vs Q) of the piping network into which the pumps deliver fluid.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a team of people transferring water buckets from a sump to an overhead tank through a vertical pipe:

- **Series (Bucket Relay):** Person A scoops water and pours it into Person B's bucket at an intermediate height. Person B then lifts that water to the tank. *Each person handles the same quantity of water, but the total height the water climbs is the sum of heights lifted by each person.* This is **pumps in series** — the **head adds up**, but the **flow remains the same**.

- **Parallel (Multiple Taps from One Main):** Picture three identical taps connected to a common supply pipe. Each tap delivers the same pressure (head), but the **total water flowing out** is the sum of the flow from each tap. This is **pumps in parallel** — the **head stays the same**, but the **discharge adds up**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics Used:**
> - **Head (H):** measured in **metres (m)** of fluid column.
> - **Discharge (Q):** measured in **m³/s** or **L/s**.
> - **Power (P):** measured in **Watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**.
> - **Specific Speed (N_s):** a dimensionless index (in SI: rpm·√m³/s · m^(−3/4)) that classifies pumps as radial, mixed, or axial flow.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic is a **high-weightage Module-4 favorite** because it tests the student's ability to:
1. Combine performance curves graphically.
2. Apply **Affinity Laws** (Fan/Pump Laws).
3. Determine the **Operating Point** of a multi-pump system against a system curve.

A typical Part-B question (14 marks) requires you to **graphically plot combined curves** and **mathematically verify** using affinity laws.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pumps in Series — Analytical Logic

When *n* identical pumps are connected in series, the same fluid element passes sequentially through each impeller. Therefore:

- The **discharge** through each pump equals the system discharge.
- The **head** developed by the combination equals the algebraic sum of heads at that discharge.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):
1. Pump-1 delivers discharge **Q** at head **H₁(Q)**.
2. Pump-2 receives this same discharge **Q** and adds further head **H₂(Q)**.
3. For *identical pumps*, **H₁ = H₂**, giving total head **H_total = n · H**.
4. The combined H-Q curve is obtained by **multiplying the ordinate (H) of the single-pump curve by n** at every value of Q.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation in Series:** Cavitation risk at the second pump suction. The suction head of pump-2 is the discharge head of pump-1, which may approach the vapour pressure of the fluid. Always check **Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHA)** for the downstream pump.

## 2.2 Pumps in Parallel — Analytical Logic

When *n* identical pumps discharge into a common manifold, the head across each pump is identical (equal to the system head), but their discharges sum up.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):
1. Each of *n* pumps develops the same head **H** at its individual discharge **Q**.
2. The system head curve fixes the value of **H** (operating point head).
3. Total discharge = sum of individual discharges at that common head.
4. The combined H-Q curve is obtained by **multiplying the abscissa (Q) of the single-pump curve by n** at every value of H.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Warning:** Parallel pumps only "add flow" effectively when the system resistance curve is **relatively flat** (low static head). For a steep system curve (high static lift), the second pump adds very little extra flow because it must work against the same elevated head.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Discharge Relation | Head Relation | Power Relation (η same) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Pump | $Q_1$ | $H_1$ | $P_1 = \rho g Q_1 H_1$ |
| 2 | *n* identical Pumps in **Series** | $Q_{series} = Q_1$ | $H_{series} = n \cdot H_1$ | $P_{series} = n \cdot P_1$ |
| 3 | *n* identical Pumps in **Parallel** | $Q_{parallel} = n \cdot Q_1$ | $H_{parallel} = H_1$ | $P_{parallel} = n \cdot P_1$ |

### Affinity Laws (Pump Laws) — Mandatory for KTU

| Law | Relation | Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| **Flow Law** | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Speed change |
| **Head Law** | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$ | Speed change |
| **Power Law** | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}$ | Speed change |
| **Diameter Law** | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3}$ | Geometrically similar pumps |
| **Diameter-Head Law** | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2}$ | Geometrically similar pumps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Combined pump stations are ubiquitous in **municipal water supply schemes**, **boiler feed systems in thermal power plants**, **fire-fighting networks**, **cooling water circulation in process industries**, and **irrigation lift schemes**. Selecting between series and parallel determines whether the engineer prioritizes pressure or volume delivery.

## 2.4 Determination of Operating Point

The **operating point** of any pump (single or combined) is the intersection of:
- The **Pump Characteristic Curve** ($H_P$ vs $Q$), and
- The **System Resistance Curve** ($H_S$ vs $Q$).

For a typical friction-dominated system:

$$
H_S = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}
$$

where:
- $H_{static}$ = static lift (frictionless head), in **m**.
- $K$ = a resistance coefficient combining pipe friction, fittings, and velocity-head losses, in **m/(m³/s)²**.
- $Q$ = discharge in **m³/s**.

The operating point is found by solving $H_P(Q) = H_S(Q)$ — graphically or algebraically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Head Equation for *n* Pumps in Series

Let $H_1(Q)$ denote the head delivered by a single pump at discharge $Q$.

For two identical pumps in series, the discharge through pump-1 is $Q$, and the discharge through pump-2 is also $Q$ (continuity of incompressible flow in a closed series loop).

Total head delivered to the system:

$$
H_{series,2} = H_1(Q) + H_2(Q)
$$

Since the pumps are identical (same characteristic curve):

$$
H_2(Q) = H_1(Q)
$$

Therefore:

$$
H_{series,2} = H_1(Q) + H_1(Q) = 2 H_1(Q)
$$

Generalizing for *n* identical pumps in series:

$$
\boxed{H_{series,n}(Q) = n \cdot H_1(Q)}
$$

The discharge remains:

$$
\boxed{Q_{series,n} = Q_1}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Combined Discharge Equation for *n* Pumps in Parallel

For two identical pumps in parallel, both pumps operate against the same system head $H$.

Individual discharges (read from each pump's H-Q curve at head $H$):

$$
Q_1(H) \quad \text{and} \quad Q_2(H)
$$

By continuity at the common delivery manifold:

$$
Q_{parallel,2} = Q_1(H) + Q_2(H)
$$

For identical pumps:

$$
Q_1(H) = Q_2(H)
$$

Hence:

$$
Q_{parallel,2} = 2 Q_1(H)
$$

Generalizing:

$$
\boxed{Q_{parallel,n}(H) = n \cdot Q_1(H)}
$$

The head remains:

$$
\boxed{H_{parallel,n} = H_1}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Affinity Law Application for Parallel Pumps

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**

> A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 0.05$ m³/s against a head $H_1 = 20$ m when running at $N_1 = 1450$ rpm. The pump speed is increased to $N_2 = 1750$ rpm. Two such pumps are then connected in **parallel** and discharge into a system whose resistance curve is given by $H_S = 10 + 4000 Q^2$ (with $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m). Determine:
> (a) The head and discharge of the single pump at the new speed.
> (b) The combined operating point of the two parallel pumps.

### Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Apply the Affinity (Head) Law:**

$$
\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2
$$

$$
H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 20 \cdot \left(\frac{1750}{1450}\right)^2
$$

$$
H_2 = 20 \cdot (1.2069)^2 = 20 \cdot 1.4566
$$

$$
\boxed{H_2 \approx 29.13 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Affinity (Flow) Law:**

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

$$
Q_2 = 0.05 \cdot \left(\frac{1750}{1450}\right) = 0.05 \cdot 1.2069
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_2 \approx 0.0603 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

### Solution — Part (b)

**Step 3 — Construct the combined parallel-pump curve.**

For two identical pumps in parallel, at any common head $H$, the combined discharge is:

$$
Q_{combined}(H) = 2 \cdot Q_{single}(H)
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the operating point** by equating combined pump head with system head:

$$
H_{combined}(Q) = H_S(Q)
$$

$$
H_{single}\!\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right) = 10 + 4000 Q^{2}
$$

To solve, we assume the combined-pump characteristic near rated conditions is reasonably approximated by a parabolic form:

$$
H_{single}(q) \approx H_{shut} - k q^{2}
$$

where $H_{shut}$ is the shut-off head (head at $Q = 0$) and $k$ is a curve-shape constant. For a typical radial pump, the shut-off head at $N_2 = 1750$ rpm is approximately $H_{shut} \approx 1.3 \cdot H_{rated}$:

$$
H_{shut} \approx 1.3 \cdot 29.13 \approx 37.87 \text{ m}
$$

The constant $k$ is determined from the rated operating point of the single pump:

$$
H_2 = H_{shut} - k \cdot Q_2^{2}
$$

$$
29.13 = 37.87 - k \cdot (0.0603)^{2}
$$

$$
k = \frac{37.87 - 29.13}{(0.0603)^{2}} = \frac{8.74}{0.003636}
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 2403.7 \text{ m/(m}^3/\text{s)}^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate combined pump head with system head.**

The single-pump head at discharge $q = Q/2$:

$$
H_{single}\!\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right) = 37.87 - 2403.7 \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)^{2} = 37.87 - 600.9 \cdot Q^{2}
$$

Setting equal to system head:

$$
37.87 - 600.9 \cdot Q^{2} = 10 + 4000 \cdot Q^{2}
$$

$$
37.87 - 10 = 4000 \cdot Q^{2} + 600.9 \cdot Q^{2}
$$

$$
27.87 = 4600.9 \cdot Q^{2}
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \frac{27.87}{4600.9} = 0.006058
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{operating} \approx 0.0778 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Step 6 — Find the operating head.**

$$
H_{operating} = 10 + 4000 \cdot (0.0778)^{2}
$$

$$
H_{operating} = 10 + 4000 \cdot 0.006053
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{operating} \approx 34.21 \text{ m}}
$$

**Verification:** Discharge per pump at this head = $Q_{operating}/2 \approx 0.0389$ m³/s, which lies on the individual pump curve. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Series (×2) | Parallel (×2) |
|---|---|---|
| Combined Shut-off Head | $2 \cdot H_{shut}$ | $1 \cdot H_{shut}$ |
| Combined Best-Efficiency Flow | $Q_{BEP,single}$ | $2 \cdot Q_{BEP,single}$ |
| Best-Efficiency Head | $2 \cdot H_{BEP,single}$ | $H_{BEP,single}$ |
| System Curve Slope Effect | Major impact on flow | Major impact on flow gained |
| Cavitation Concern | Critical (downstream pump NPSH) | Low (each pump has fresh suction) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — Series vs Parallel Configuration

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the *fluid-path architecture* of both configurations and how they interact with the system resistance curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sump / Reservoir] --> B1[Pump 1 Suction]
    B1 --> C1[Pump 1 Impeller]
    C1 --> D1[Intermediate Delivery Pipe]
    D1 --> E1[Pump 2 Suction]
    E1 --> F1[Pump 2 Impeller]
    F1 --> G1[Common Delivery to System]
    G1 --> H1[System Resistance Curve HS]

    A2[Sump / Reservoir] --> B2[Pump A Suction Manifold]
    B2 --> C2[Pump A Impeller]
    C2 --> D2[Delivery Manifold Common Node]
    A2 --> E2[Pump B Suction Manifold]
    E2 --> F2[Pump B Impeller]
    F2 --> D2
    D2 --> G2[Common Delivery to System]
    G2 --> H2[System Resistance Curve HS]
```

## 4.2 Combined Characteristic Curve Construction — Block Architecture

This block diagram maps the *graphical construction procedure* used by KTU examiners to grade curve-combination problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Plot Single Pump H vs Q Curve]
    P2[Step 2a: For Series - Multiply H ordinate by n]
    P3[Step 2b: For Parallel - Multiply Q abscissa by n]
    P4[Step 3: Plot System Resistance Curve HS]
    P5{Step 4: Choose Configuration?}
    P6[Step 5a: Series Intersection - Same Q Higher H]
    P7[Step 5b: Parallel Intersection - Same H Higher Q]
    P8[Step 6: Read Operating Point Q op and H op]

    P1 --> P5
    P5 -->|Series| P2
    P5 -->|Parallel| P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P6
    P4 --> P7
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Choosing Series vs Parallel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[System Demand Analysis]
    S2{Is Static Head dominant?}
    S3{Is Friction Loss dominant?}
    S4[Choose Pumps in Series]
    S5[Choose Pumps in Parallel]
    S6[Combine Both Arrangements]
    S7[Use Variable Speed Drive]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 -->|Yes, high lift needed| S4
    S2 -->|No| S3
    S3 -->|Yes, high flow needed| S5
    S3 -->|Both high lift and high flow| S6
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between pumps operating in series and pumps operating in parallel, with reference to head and discharge characteristics."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pumps in Series | Pumps in Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge | Same as single pump: $Q_s = Q_1$ | $n$ times single pump: $Q_p = n Q_1$ |
| Head | $n$ times single pump: $H_s = n H_1$ | Same as single pump: $H_p = H_1$ |
| System Curve Interaction | Adds head at constant flow | Adds flow at constant head |
| Application | High lift, low flow systems | High flow, low lift systems |
| Cavitation Risk | Higher (downstream pump) | Lower |

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for H-Q relations, 1 mark for application comparison.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"State the affinity laws for a centrifugal pump and explain their significance."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a geometrically similar pump operating at different speeds:

1. **Flow Law:** $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ — discharge varies linearly with speed. *(1 mark)*
2. **Head Law:** $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$ — head varies with square of speed. *(1 mark)*
3. **Power Law:** $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}$ — power varies with cube of speed. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Significance:** Enables prediction of pump performance at off-design speeds without retesting; forms the basis of variable-speed pumping energy optimization. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

### Question:
> Two identical centrifugal pumps, each delivering $0.10$ m³/s at a head of $30$ m at $1450$ rpm, are connected in **series** and discharge into a system whose head is given by $H_S = 15 + 2000 Q^{2}$ (H in m, Q in m³/s).
>
> **(a)** Plot the combined characteristic curve of the two pumps in series and the system resistance curve. Determine the operating point. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the speed of each pump is now raised to $1750$ rpm, determine the new operating point using affinity laws. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Single-pump characteristic has rated point $(Q_1, H_1) = (0.10, 30)$ at $1450$ rpm.

**Step 2:** For two identical pumps in series, the combined shut-off head doubles:

$$
H_{shut,series} = 2 \cdot H_{shut,single}
$$

Assuming $H_{shut,single} \approx 1.3 \cdot H_{rated} = 1.3 \cdot 30 = 39$ m:

$$
H_{shut,series} \approx 78 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Combined rated point in series:

$$
(Q_{series}, H_{series}) = (0.10, 60) \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \text{m}
$$

**Step 4:** Combined series curve (parabolic approximation):

$$
H_{series}(Q) = 78 - 1800 Q^{2}
$$

(where $1800$ is obtained from passing through $(0.10, 60)$: $78 - 1800(0.01) = 60$ ✓)

**Step 5:** Equate to system curve:

$$
78 - 1800 Q^{2} = 15 + 2000 Q^{2}
$$

$$
63 = 3800 Q^{2}
$$

$$
Q_{op}^{2} = 0.01658 \Rightarrow Q_{op} \approx 0.1288 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_{op} = 15 + 2000 \cdot (0.1288)^{2} \approx 15 + 33.17 \approx 48.17 \text{ m}
$$

**Operating Point:** $\boxed{Q_{op} \approx 0.129 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad H_{op} \approx 48.2 \text{ m}}$ *(3 marks for computation, 2 marks for curve explanation, 2 marks for graphical interpretation)*

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Apply affinity laws to find new rated single-pump performance at $N_2 = 1750$ rpm:

$$
Q_{1,new} = 0.10 \cdot \left(\frac{1750}{1450}\right) = 0.10 \cdot 1.2069 \approx 0.1207 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_{1,new} = 30 \cdot \left(\frac{1750}{1450}\right)^{2} = 30 \cdot 1.4566 \approx 43.70 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** New combined series curve (shut-off doubles, rated point doubles):

$$
H_{series,new}(Q) = 2 \cdot (1.3 \cdot 43.70) - k_{new} Q^{2}
$$

Computing new $k_{new}$:

$$
2 \cdot (1.3 \cdot 43.70) - k_{new} \cdot (0.1207)^{2} = 2 \cdot 43.70
$$

$$
113.62 - k_{new} \cdot 0.01457 = 87.40
$$

$$
k_{new} = \frac{26.22}{0.01457} \approx 1799.6 \approx 1800 \text{ m/(m}^3/\text{s)}^2
$$

Hence $H_{series,new}(Q) \approx 113.62 - 1800 Q^{2}$.

**Step 3:** Equate to system curve:

$$
113.62 - 1800 Q^{2} = 15 + 2000 Q^{2}
$$

$$
98.62 = 3800 Q^{2}
$$

$$
Q_{op,new}^{2} = 0.02595 \Rightarrow Q_{op,new} \approx 0.1611 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_{op,new} = 15 + 2000 \cdot (0.1611)^{2} \approx 15 + 51.91 \approx 66.91 \text{ m}
$$

**New Operating Point:** $\boxed{Q_{op,new} \approx 0.161 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad H_{op,new} \approx 66.9 \text{ m}}$

*(1 mark affinity law Q, 1 mark affinity law H, 2 marks new series curve, 2 marks solving operating point, 1 mark interpretation)*

---

## Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

### Question:
> Two geometrically similar centrifugal pumps are to operate in **parallel**. Each pump has a shut-off head of $40$ m and delivers $0.08$ m³/s at a head of $30$ m.
>
> **(a)** Derive the equation of the combined H-Q curve when operated in parallel and find the operating point when connected to a system $H_S = 5 + 1500 Q^{2}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the operating point obtained in part (a) with that obtained if the **same two pumps were connected in series**. Comment on which configuration is more suitable. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Determine the single-pump parabolic characteristic using two points: shut-off $(Q=0, H=40)$ and rated $(Q=0.08, H=30)$.

Single-pump curve: $H_{single}(q) = 40 - k q^{2}$

At rated point: $30 = 40 - k \cdot (0.08)^2 \Rightarrow k = \dfrac{10}{0.0064} = 1562.5$

$$
H_{single}(q) = 40 - 1562.5 q^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Combined parallel characteristic.

For two pumps in parallel at common head $H$:

$$
q_{each} = \sqrt{\frac{40 - H}{1562.5}}
$$

Total discharge:

$$
Q_{parallel}(H) = 2 q_{each} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{40 - H}{1562.5}}
$$

Solving for $H$:

$$
H_{parallel}(Q) = 40 - \frac{1562.5}{4} Q^{2} = 40 - 390.625 Q^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate to system curve:

$$
40 - 390.625 Q^{2} = 5 + 1500 Q^{2}
$$

$$
35 = 1890.625 Q^{2}
$$

$$
Q_{op}^{2} = 0.01852 \Rightarrow Q_{op} \approx 0.1361 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_{op} = 5 + 1500 \cdot (0.01852) \approx 5 + 27.78 \approx 32.78 \text{ m}
$$

**Operating Point:** $\boxed{Q_{op} \approx 0.136 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad H_{op} \approx 32.8 \text{ m}}$ *(7 marks: 2 for single-pump curve, 2 for parallel curve derivation, 2 for solving, 1 for stating result)*

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** For the same two pumps in series, the combined curve is:

$$
H_{series}(Q) = 2 \cdot H_{single}(Q) = 80 - 3125 Q^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Equate to the same system curve:

$$
80 - 3125 Q^{2} = 5 + 1500 Q^{2}
$$

$$
75 = 4625 Q^{2}
$$

$$
Q_{series,op}^{2} = 0.01622 \Rightarrow Q_{series,op} \approx 0.1273 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_{series,op} = 5 + 1500 \cdot (0.01622) \approx 5 + 24.32 \approx 29.32 \text{ m}
$$

**Operating Point (Series):** $\boxed{Q \approx 0.127 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad H \approx 29.3 \text{ m}}$

**Step 3:** Comparison and Recommendation:

| Configuration | Q (m³/s) | H (m) | Flow per Pump |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel | 0.136 | 32.8 | 0.068 |
| Series | 0.127 | 29.3 | 0.127 |

Since the system curve is **friction-dominated** ($H_S$ rises steeply with $Q$, as seen from the coefficient 1500), the parallel arrangement delivers **higher discharge** while keeping each pump operating closer to its BEP (best efficiency point rated at $q = 0.08$ m³/s).

**Conclusion:** For this friction-dominated system, **parallel operation is more suitable** because it provides approximately 7% more flow. Series operation, while delivering lower head than the pumps' shut-off head, is less efficient here.

*(2 marks series derivation, 2 marks series operating point, 2 marks comparison table, 1 mark justified conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing which axis to scale:** Students often multiply the wrong axis when combining curves. *Series = multiply H (vertical); Parallel = multiply Q (horizontal).*
> 2. **Forgetting shut-off head scaling:** The shut-off head of $n$ pumps in series is $n$ times the single-pump shut-off head — do NOT assume rated head for the new boundary.
> 3. **Wrong affinity-law pairing:** When speed changes, ALL three laws (Q, H, P) must be applied consistently. Mixing up exponents (using $N^2$ for $Q$ instead of $N$) costs full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring unit consistency:** $Q$ must be in m³/s and $H$ in m before substituting into the system curve equation — otherwise numerical errors propagate.
> 5. **No verification step:** Always verify the operating point by plugging back into both curves; mismatch indicates algebraic error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series** = same $Q$, head multiplied by $n$ → used when **high pressure / high lift** is needed.
- **Parallel** = same $H$, discharge multiplied by $n$ → used when **high flow rate** is needed.
- **Combined H-Q curves:** Series obtained by vertical scaling ($H \to nH$); parallel obtained by horizontal scaling ($Q \to nQ$).
- **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^{2}$, $P \propto N^{3}$ — applicable to geometrically similar pumps.
- **Operating Point** = intersection of pump characteristic curve and system resistance curve.
- **System Curve:** $H_S = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$ — friction-dominated systems favour parallel; static-head systems favour series.
- **Cavitation Check (NPSH):** Always verify NPSHA > NPSHR for the downstream pump in series configuration.
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Each pump should ideally operate near its BEP for maximum efficiency; in combined systems, operating points may shift away from BEP.
- **Shaded-power Rule:** For identical pumps in any configuration with the same efficiency, total power is $n$ times the individual pump power.
- **KTU 14-mark Question Pattern:** Expect (a) curve derivation + operating point, (b) affinity-law application or comparison with the alternative configuration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Fans

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fan (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *fan* is a rotary turbo-machine that continuously transfers kinetic energy to a gaseous working fluid (typically air) through a rotating impeller, producing a **moderate rise in static pressure** — generally limited to about **1.25 kPa (≈ 5 inches of water column)** or roughly **0.05 bar (~5% of atmospheric pressure)**. Fans belong to the family of turbomachines and are differentiated from compressors and pumps by the very low pressure rise and high volumetric flow rates they handle.

According to the KTU PEMET411 syllabus (Module 4), fans are classified based on the **direction of flow** through the impeller and the **geometry of the blades**:

1. **Centrifugal (Radial) Flow Fans** — flow exits radially (perpendicular to shaft).
2. **Axial Flow Fans** — flow remains parallel to the axis of rotation.
3. **Mixed Flow Fans** — combination of radial and axial components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Exhaust vs. The Helicopter Rotor"**
>
> Imagine two ways to push air inside a room:
>
> - **Centrifugal Fan** is like swinging a **bucket of water over your head** in a circle. Water (air) is flung outward by centrifugal force, gains kinetic energy, and is discharged sideways. The energy of motion is then partially converted to pressure as the swirling air slows down in a surrounding casing (volute or scroll).
>
> - **Axial Flow Fan** is like a **rotating ceiling fan blade or a helicopter rotor**. Air is "swept" along the axis, gaining velocity and pressure as it passes through successive rows of blades (rotor + stator). The air enters and leaves moving in essentially the same direction.
>
> - **Mixed Flow Fan** is like a **mixer-grinder blade** — it gives air both a sideways push *and* a forward push simultaneously.

## 1.3 Why Fans are Studied Separately from Compressors

| Parameter | Fan | Compressor |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure Rise | < **5% of atmospheric** (≈ 0.05 bar) | > 5% (often very high ratios) |
| Fluid | Air / Low-density gases | Air / Refrigerants / Process gases |
| Compression Effects | Negligible (incompressible flow assumed) | Significant (compressible flow) |
| Typical Applications | HVAC, ventilation, boilers, cooling towers | Gas turbines, jet engines, refrigeration |
| Energy Density | Low (volumetric flow rate is high) | High (work input per kg is high) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Because pressure rise is small, fans are analyzed using **incompressible flow** assumptions — density ($\rho$) is treated as constant. This drastically simplifies Euler's equation and the fan laws compared to compressible turbo-machinery.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must be Memorized)

- **Volume Flow Rate ($Q$):** measured in **m³/s** or **m³/h** (or CFM in industry).
- **Static Pressure ($\Delta p_s$ or $H_s$):** measured in **Pa (N/m²)** or **mm of water column (mmWC)**.
- **Dynamic Pressure ($\Delta p_d$):** $\frac{1}{2} \rho C^2$, where $C$ is the discharge velocity.
- **Total Pressure ($\Delta p_t = \Delta p_s + \Delta p_d$).**
- **Power ($P$):** shaft power in **W or kW**.
- **Fan Efficiency ($\eta_f$):** $\dfrac{\rho \cdot Q \cdot \Delta p_t}{P_{shaft}}$.
- **Specific Speed ($N_s$):** $\dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(gH)^{3/4}}$ (metric, single-stage).

> [!NOTE]
> These seven metrics are the **backbone of every fan-related numerical in KTU exams**. Memorize both the symbol *and* the SI unit for each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fans (KTU 2024 Module 4 Emphasis)

### A. Centrifugal Fans (Radial Flow)

Centrifugal fans are further classified by the **shape of the impeller blade**, which directly affects the fan's pressure–flow characteristic and efficiency:

| Type | Blade Shape | $\beta_2$ Range | Power Curve | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Radial Blade (Paddle)** | Straight, radial | 90° | Rising (overload) | Dusty air, industrial exhaust |
| **Backward Curved / Backward Inclined** | Curved away from rotation | 30°–60° | Non-overload | Clean air, HVAC, efficient systems |
| **Forward Curved (Sirocco / Multivane)** | Curved in direction of rotation | > 90° | Steeply rising | High flow, low pressure (e.g., domestic AC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backward Curved blades are NON-OVERLOADING** — power demand *decreases* beyond design flow, providing inherent safety against motor burnout. Forward curved blades are OVERLOADING — power rises sharply with flow, risking motor stall if not properly matched.

### B. Axial Flow Fans

1. **Propeller Fan** — single-stage, mostly used for high flow / very low pressure.
2. **Tube-Axial Fan** — propeller + cylindrical housing, slight pressure rise.
3. **Vane-Axial Fan** — propeller + stationary guide vanes (stators) after rotor, higher efficiency and pressure.

### C. Mixed Flow (Diagonal) Fans

Combine features of both — flow exits at 45°–60° to the axis. Compact, used in industrial ventilation and automotive turbochargers.

## 2.2 Euler's Work Equation (The Heart of Every Fan)

For a fan (or any turbo-machine), the **work done per unit mass of fluid** is given by the Euler Turbomachinery Equation:

$$
W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}
$$

For a fan with **radial inlet** (no pre-swirl): $V_{w1} = 0$, so:

$$
W = U_2 V_{w2}
$$

Where:
- $U_2$ = blade tip speed at outlet = $\dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ (m/s)
- $V_{w2}$ = whirl (tangential) component of absolute velocity at outlet (m/s)
- $D_2$ = impeller outer diameter (m)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for KTU:** The Euler equation is used in nearly every 14-mark problem involving fan work, head, or power. The assumption $V_{w1}=0$ is **standard for fans with radial inlet** and must be clearly stated in your solution to score full marks.

## 2.3 Velocity Triangles — The "Engineering Compass" of Fan Analysis

For both inlet (1) and outlet (2) sections, four velocities are tracked:

| Symbol | Meaning | Geometric Relation |
|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Absolute velocity of fluid | — |
| $U$ | Blade velocity (= $\pi DN/60$) | Tangential to rotation |
| $V_r$ | Relative velocity (w.r.t. blade) | $C = U + V_r$ |
| $V_w$ | Whirl (tangential component of $C$) | $V_w = U + V_r \cos\beta$ |
| $V_f$ | Flow (meridional) component | $V_f = V_r \sin\beta$ |
| $\alpha$ | Absolute flow angle | $\tan\alpha = V_f / V_w$ |
| $\beta$ | Relative flow angle (blade angle) | $\tan\beta = V_f / (U - V_w)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always draw the outlet velocity triangle first for centrifugal fans. Use $W = U_2 V_{w2}$ with the assumption that the absolute velocity $C_2$ is **radial** at exit (i.e., $\alpha_2 = 90°$) unless otherwise specified — this is the most common KTU problem setup.

## 2.4 Centrifugal Fan — Outlet Velocity Triangle (When $C_2$ is Radial)

If $C_2$ is purely radial at exit, then $C_2 = V_{f2}$ and the velocity triangle reduces to:

$$
V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2
$$

Substituting in Euler's equation:

$$
W = U_2 \left( U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2 \right)
$$

> The flow component is $V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}$ where $B_2$ is the outlet blade width.

So work per unit mass becomes a **quadratic function of flow** $Q$ — a key result for fan characteristic curves.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | Blade tip speed at outlet | m/s |
| 2 | $W = U_2 V_{w2}$ | Euler's work per unit mass (radial inlet) | J/kg |
| 3 | $W = g H_f$ | Work → Head conversion (for fluid column) | J/kg |
| 4 | $P_{shaft} = \rho Q g H_f$ | Shaft power (ideal, no losses) | W |
| 5 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho Q W}{\eta_f}$ | Actual shaft power | W |
| 6 | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\rho Q g H_f}{P_{shaft}}$ | Fan (manometric) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\rho Q g H_f}{P_{shaft,ideal}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C^2$ (for dyn. press.) | Dynamic pressure | Pa |
| 9 | $H_t = H_s + \dfrac{C_2^2}{2g}$ | Total head = static + dynamic head | m of fluid |
| 10 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(g H)^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (metric) | — |
| 11 | $C_{throat} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{throat}}$ | Mean velocity at throat | m/s |
| 12 | $\text{Manometric Pressure} = \rho g (H_s - H_{suction})$ | Useful pressure rise | Pa |
| 13 | $Q \propto N, \; H \propto N^2, \; P \propto N^3$ | Fan affinity laws (speed change) | — |
| 14 | $Q \propto D^3, \; H \propto D^2, \; P \propto D^5$ | Fan affinity laws (diameter change) | — |
| 15 | $\sigma = \dfrac{N_s \sqrt{Q}}{(gH)^{3/4}}$ (NPSH-like) | Suction specific speed | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Don't memorize by rote — understand the dimensional pattern.** The fan laws form a self-consistent geometric series: exponents of $N$ are 1, 2, 3; exponents of $D$ are 3, 2, 5 (reverse order). This is from dimensional analysis of Euler's equation, where $W \sim U^2 \sim (DN)^2$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC systems in buildings** — backward curved centrifugal fans for clean, quiet, efficient air delivery.
- **Boiler FD/ID fans** — forced-draft (FD) and induced-draft (ID) fans handle combustion air and flue gases; centrifugal type with radial blades (to handle dust).
- **Mining & Tunnel Ventilation** — axial flow fans (vane-axial) for high flow at moderate pressure.
- **Electronic Cooling** — small axial fans (computer, server racks).
- **Wind Tunnels** — large axial or centrifugal fans for low-turbulence, high-volume flow.
- **Jet Engine Bypass** — the front fan of a turbofan is essentially a multi-stage axial fan.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Equation for Fan Work

### Statement
**"Show that the work done per unit mass of fluid by a fan is $W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$."**

### Step 1 — Define the System
Consider a fan with a rotating impeller. A fluid particle enters the impeller at section 1 (inlet) and leaves at section 2 (outlet). Apply the **angular momentum theorem** (Euler's turbomachinery equation) to a control volume fixed to the impeller.

### Step 2 — Angular Momentum Equation
The torque $T$ on the impeller equals the rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid:

$$
T = \dot{m} \left( r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1} \right)
$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate (kg/s) and $r$ is the radius.

### Step 3 — Convert Torque to Work
Work done per unit time (shaft power) is $P_{shaft} = T \omega$, where $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s. So:

$$
P_{shaft} = \dot{m} \, \omega \left( r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Recognize Blade Speed
Blade speed at any radius is $U = r \omega$. Therefore $\omega r_2 = U_2$ and $\omega r_1 = U_1$:

$$
P_{shaft} = \dot{m} \left( U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Work Per Unit Mass
Divide both sides by $\dot{m}$:

$$
\boxed{W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}}
$$

### Step 6 — Special Case for Fans
For a **radial inlet** (no pre-whirl, i.e., fluid enters purely radial), $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$
\boxed{W = U_2 V_{w2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-cited equation in KTU fan problems**. Always state the radial-inlet assumption explicitly in your answer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Centrifugal Fan with Radial Exit ($C_2$ radial)

### Given
- Radial exit condition: $C_2$ is purely radial → $C_2 = V_{f2}$ (no whirl component in absolute velocity).
- Blade angle at exit: $\beta_2$.
- Outlet flow area: $A_2 = \pi D_2 B_2$.

### Step 1 — Velocity Triangle Relations
From the outlet velocity triangle of a centrifugal fan:

$$
V_{r2} \cos\beta_2 = U_2 - V_{w2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2
$$

### Step 2 — Radial Exit
If $C_2$ is radial, $V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$. Also $V_{r2} \sin\beta_2 = V_{f2}$ (flow component), so:

$$
V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Euler
$$
W = U_2 V_{w2} = U_2 \left( U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2 \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Express in terms of Flow Rate
The flow component is $V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{A_2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}$. So:

$$
\boxed{W = U_2^2 - U_2 \cot\beta_2 \cdot \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}}
$$

This shows that **work is a linear decreasing function of flow** $Q$ when $\beta_2$ is fixed. This is the mathematical foundation of the *fan pressure–flow characteristic curve*.

## 3.3 Derivation: The Three Fan Affinity Laws (Speed Laws)

### Statement
**"Derive the fan laws relating flow, head, and power to speed."**

### Step 1 — Dimensional Reasoning
The Euler equation gives $W = U_2 V_{w2} = U_2^2 \cdot \phi$, where $\phi$ is a dimensionless flow coefficient.

### Step 2 — Flow Coefficient
$$
\phi = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{U_2} = \dfrac{Q}{A_2 U_2}
$$

For geometrically similar fans, $\phi$ is constant. If $D$ is fixed and $A_2 \propto D^2$:

$$
Q \propto U_2 D^2 \propto N D^3
$$

For a single fan (D constant): $\quad \boxed{Q \propto N}$

### Step 3 — Head (Work per unit mass)
$$
H = \dfrac{W}{g} \propto U_2^2 \propto N^2 D^2
$$

For a single fan: $\quad \boxed{H \propto N^2}$

### Step 4 — Power
$$
P = \rho Q g H \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3
$$

$$
\boxed{P \propto N^3}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine
The **Three Fan Laws** for constant $D$:

$$
\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2, \quad \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hall-mark of KTU problems:** Given a fan at one speed, predict its performance at another speed. Always verify the **system resistance curve remains unchanged** — these laws apply to *fan characteristics*, not system curves.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked-Out Numerical Examples

### Example 1 — Basic Power Calculation

**Question:** A centrifugal fan delivers **$Q = 4$ m³/s** of air at a static pressure rise of **$\Delta p_s = 1200$ Pa**. The mechanical efficiency is **$\eta_m = 0.85$** and fan efficiency is **$\eta_f = 0.75$**. Calculate (a) air power, (b) shaft power, (c) shaft power input.

> **Solution:**

**(a) Air Power (Useful Power Output):**
$$
P_{air} = Q \cdot \Delta p_s = 4 \times 1200 = 4800 \; \text{W} = 4.8 \; \text{kW}
$$

**(b) Shaft Power (Power into impeller):**
$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{air}}{\eta_f} = \dfrac{4.8}{0.75} = 6.4 \; \text{kW}
$$

**(c) Power Input (Motor power to shaft):**
$$
P_{input} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{6.4}{0.85} = 7.53 \; \text{kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of efficiencies:** Air power → divided by fan (fluid) efficiency → shaft power → divided by mechanical efficiency → electrical power input. This nesting is *frequently tested* in KTU.

---

### Example 2 — Fan Laws Application

**Question:** A fan running at **$N_1 = 1450$ rpm** delivers **$Q_1 = 5$ m³/s** against a head of **$H_1 = 50$ mmWC**, requiring **$P_1 = 1.5$ kW**. If the speed is increased to **$N_2 = 1750$ rpm**, find the new $Q_2$, $H_2$, and $P_2$.

> **Solution:**

**Apply the three fan laws directly:**

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 5 \times \dfrac{1750}{1450} = 6.03 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2 = 50 \times \left( \dfrac{1750}{1450} \right)^2 = 72.7 \; \text{mmWC}
$$

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3 = 1.5 \times \left( \dfrac{1750}{1450} \right)^3 = 2.64 \; \text{kW}
$$

---

### Example 3 — Specific Speed Classification

**Question:** A fan has $N = 1200$ rpm, $Q = 10$ m³/s, and $H = 60$ mmWC. Find specific speed. Identify whether it is centrifugal or axial.

> **Solution:**

**Convert units to SI for KTU standard:**

$H = 60 \; \text{mmWC} = 0.060 \; \text{m of water}$, use $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

Head in pressure: $\Delta p = \rho g H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.060 = 588.6 \; \text{Pa}$.

For **metric specific speed** (using $gH$):

$$
N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(gH)^{3/4}} = \dfrac{1200 \times \sqrt{10}}{(9.81 \times 0.060)^{3/4}}
$$

Compute denominator: $9.81 \times 0.060 = 0.5886$. Then $(0.5886)^{3/4} \approx 0.681$.

$$
N_s = \dfrac{1200 \times 3.162}{0.681} \approx 5572
$$

> **Classification:** $N_s \approx 5500$ falls in the range **4000–12000 → Axial Flow Fan**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU:**
> - $N_s < 4000$: Centrifugal (radial/forward/backward)
> - $4000 < N_s < 12000$: Mixed flow
> - $N_s > 12000$: Pure axial flow

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fan Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Motor Drive Unit] -->|Electrical Power| B[Shaft Coupling]
    B -->|Torque Transmission| C[Impeller Rotor]
    C -->|Kinetic Energy Transfer| D[Fluid Flow Path]
    D -->|Inlet Duct| E[Inlet Volute or Guide Vanes]
    E -->|Pre-whirl Control| F[Impeller Eye Inlet]
    F -->|Radial Acceleration| G[Impeller Blade Channel]
    G -->|Outlet Velocity Triangle| H[Outlet Volute or Diffuser]
    H -->|Kinetic-Pressure Conversion| I[Discharge Duct]
    I -->|Static Pressure Rise| J[Process or Duct System]
    D -->|Bypass or Recirculation| K[Auxiliary Cooling]
    L[Control Damper] -->|Throttling| I
    M[Speed Controller VFD] -->|Frequency Modulation| A
```

## 4.2 Centrifugal Fan — Velocity Triangle (Mimicking Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Absolute Velocity C2] -->|Tangential| B[Whirl Component Vw2]
    A -->|Radial| C[Flow Component Vf2]
    D[Blade Velocity U2] -->|Tangential Direction| E[Relative Velocity Vr2]
    E -->|Tangential Component| F[Vr2 cos Beta2]
    E -->|Radial Component| C
    B -->|Euler W = U2 times Vw2| G[Work Done per Unit Mass]
    F -->|Vr2 cos Beta2 = U2 minus Vw2| G
```

## 4.3 Fan Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fans Turbo Machines] --> B{Flow Direction}
    B -->|Radial Exit| C[Centrifugal Fans]
    B -->|Axial Exit| D[Axial Flow Fans]
    B -->|Diagonal Exit| E[Mixed Flow Fans]
    C --> F{Blade Geometry}
    F -->|Beta2 = 90| G[Radial Paddle Blade]
    F -->|Beta2 less than 90| H[Backward Curved Inclined]
    F -->|Beta2 greater than 90| I[Forward Curved Sirocco]
    D --> J{Casings and Vanes}
    J -->|No Casing| K[Propeller Fan]
    J -->|Tube Casing| L[Tube Axial Fan]
    J -->|Stator Vanes| M[Vane Axial Fan]
```

## 4.4 Performance Curve (Schematic) — Pressure vs Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flow Rate Q axis] -->|Increases Rightward| B[Static Pressure axis]
    C[Radial Blade Curve] -->|Rises Slowly| B
    D[Backward Curved Curve] -->|Drops Steadily| B
    E[Forward Curved Curve] -->|Rises Sharply| B
    F[System Resistance Curve Parabolic] -->|Quadratic Rise| B
    G[Operating Point Intersection] -->|Matched Design| B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Note:** The operating point is the intersection of the fan characteristic curve and the system resistance curve ($H_{sys} = k Q^2$). For a backward-curved fan, this intersection is **stable**; for a forward-curved fan, it can be unstable (surge risk) on the steep portion.

## 4.5 Component Map of a Centrifugal Fan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Bell Mouth] --> B[Shaft Inlet Sleeve]
    B --> C[Impeller Eye]
    C --> D[Rotating Blades on Hub]
    D --> E[Outlet Blade Tips]
    E --> F[Cutwater or Volute Tongue]
    F --> G[Volute Scroll Casing]
    G --> H[Discharge Flange]
    I[Bearing Housing] -->|Supports| B
    J[Drive Motor] -->|Coupling| I
    K[Inlet Guide Vanes Optional] -->|Pre-whirl Control| C
```
