# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Differentiate between a fan and a compressor. List two applications of centrifugal fans."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fan | Compressor |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure Rise | < **5% of atm. pressure** (≈ 0.05 bar) | > 5% (often multi-stage, high ratios) |
| Flow | High volumetric, low head | Lower volumetric, high head |
| Density change | Negligible (incompressible) | Significant (compressible) |
| Typical $\eta$ | 65–85% | 75–90% |

**Applications of Centrifugal Fans:**
1. **Forced-draft (FD) and induced-draft (ID) fans** in industrial boilers for combustion air supply and flue gas removal.
2. **HVAC systems** in commercial buildings for clean air circulation at high efficiency (backward-curved type).
3. (Bonus) **Pneumatic conveying** of granular solids.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is meant by 'non-overloading' characteristic of a fan? Which blade type exhibits this?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **non-overloading fan** is one whose power consumption **does not exceed the design power** across the entire operational flow range (from shut-off to free delivery). Beyond the design point, the power either stays constant or decreases, providing **inherent safety** against motor overload.

**Blade type:** **Backward-curved (or backward-inclined) centrifugal fan blades**, typically with outlet blade angle $\beta_2 < 90°$ (commonly 30°–60°).

> Forward-curved (sirocco) fans are **overloading** — power rises steeply with flow and can cause motor burnout if not matched to the system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A centrifugal fan has the following specifications:**

- Outer diameter $D_2 = 0.6 \; \text{m}$
- Speed $N = 1500 \; \text{rpm}$
- Width at outlet $B_2 = 0.10 \; \text{m}$
- Outlet blade angle $\beta_2 = 30°$ (measured from tangential)
- Air enters the impeller **without pre-whirl** ($V_{w1} = 0$)
- Volume flow rate $Q = 3.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Air density $\rho = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

**Assuming radial exit ($C_2$ is purely radial), calculate:**

**(a) The work done per unit mass of air by the fan.** (7 marks)

**(b) The shaft power required and the static pressure rise, if the manometric efficiency is 80%.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Work Done per Unit Mass (7 marks)

**Step 1: Blade tip speed at outlet**

$$
U_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 1500}{60}
$$

$$
U_2 = 47.12 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Outlet flow (meridional) component**

$$
V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2} = \dfrac{3.0}{\pi \times 0.6 \times 0.10}
$$

$$
V_{f2} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.1885} = 15.92 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Outlet whirl component (using radial-exit velocity triangle)**

For radial exit, $C_2 = V_{f2}$, and from the velocity triangle:

$$
V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2
$$

$$
\cot(30°) = \sqrt{3} = 1.732
$$

$$
V_{w2} = 47.12 - 15.92 \times 1.732 = 47.12 - 27.57 = 19.55 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Euler's equation (radial inlet condition)**

$$
W = U_2 \cdot V_{w2} = 47.12 \times 19.55
$$

$$
\boxed{W = 921.2 \; \text{J/kg}}
$$

##### Part (b) — Shaft Power and Static Pressure Rise (7 marks)

**Step 1: Mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1.2 \times 3.0 = 3.6 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2: Useful air power (ideal)**

$$
P_{air} = \dot{m} \cdot W = 3.6 \times 921.2 = 3316.3 \; \text{W} \approx 3.32 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3: Shaft power with manometric efficiency**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{air}}{\eta_{man}} = \dfrac{3.32}{0.80}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{shaft} = 4.15 \; \text{kW}}
$$

**Step 4: Static pressure rise (head in metres of air)**

$$
H = \dfrac{W}{g} = \dfrac{921.2}{9.81} = 93.9 \; \text{m of air}
$$

But more useful as **pressure** in Pa:

$$
\Delta p_{ideal} = W \cdot \rho = 921.2 \times 1.2 = 1105.4 \; \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 5: Actual static pressure rise (using manometric efficiency)**

$$
\Delta p_{actual} = \eta_{man} \cdot \Delta p_{ideal} = 0.80 \times 1105.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta p_{actual} = 884.3 \; \text{Pa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting the **radial-inlet assumption** ($V_{w1} = 0$) → Euler's equation becomes $W = U_2 V_{w2}$, not $U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$. State it explicitly.
> 2. Confusing **fan efficiency** with **mechanical efficiency** — fan efficiency uses air power; mechanical uses shaft power. The order of nesting in power calculation matters.
> 3. Using $W = \Delta p / \rho$ directly without checking units. Always write $W$ in J/kg.
> 4. Forgetting that pressure rise is in **Pa**, not **mmWC** — convert only if the question demands.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A centrifugal fan delivers $Q_1 = 6 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ of air at $N_1 = 900 \; \text{rpm}$ with a static pressure rise of $\Delta p_1 = 800 \; \text{Pa}$ and requires a power input of $P_1 = 8 \; \text{kW}$.**

**(a) Using the fan affinity laws, determine the new flow rate, static pressure, and power when the speed is changed to $N_2 = 1200 \; \text{rpm}$, assuming the same system resistance is retained.** (7 marks)

**(b) If the impeller diameter is geometrically scaled to 1.2 times the original (at the same speed of 900 rpm), determine the new $Q$, $\Delta p$, and $P$. Comment on whether such a scale-up is feasible.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Speed Change (7 marks)

**Apply the three fan affinity laws directly:**

**Flow rate:**

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 6 \times \dfrac{1200}{900} = 8.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_2 = 8.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Static pressure:**

$$
\Delta p_2 = \Delta p_1 \times \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2 = 800 \times \left( \dfrac{1200}{900} \right)^2
$$

$$
\Delta p_2 = 800 \times 1.7778 = 1422.2 \; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta p_2 = 1422.2 \; \text{Pa}}
$$

**Power:**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3 = 8 \times \left( \dfrac{1200}{900} \right)^3
$$

$$
P_2 = 8 \times 2.370 = 18.96 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_2 = 18.96 \; \text{kW}}
$$

##### Part (b) — Diameter Scale-Up (7 marks)

**Apply the diameter-based fan laws:**

For the same speed ($N$ constant) and diameter scaled by factor $k = 1.2$:

**Flow rate** (proportional to $D^3$):

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times k^3 = 6 \times (1.2)^3 = 6 \times 1.728 = 10.37 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Static pressure** (proportional to $D^2$):

$$
\Delta p_2 = \Delta p_1 \times k^2 = 800 \times (1.2)^2 = 800 \times 1.44 = 1152 \; \text{Pa}
$$

**Power** (proportional to $D^5$):

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times k^5 = 8 \times (1.2)^5 = 8 \times 2.4883 = 19.91 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Feasibility Comment:**

A 20% increase in diameter results in:
- **72.8%** increase in flow
- **44%** increase in pressure
- **149%** increase in power consumption (nearly **2.5×** the original power!)

This is **generally feasible mechanically** if the motor and bearings are upgraded, but the **power demand rises disproportionately**. The original motor will be insufficient. Practically, a 20% scale-up is often done by **changing the motor first** then re-matching the impeller. **Affinity laws assume geometric similarity** — the smaller and larger impellers must be true scaled copies in shape, blade thickness, and clearance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Fan Law Problems:**
> 1. Mixing up **speed laws** ($N$ varies) with **diameter laws** ($D$ varies). The exponents are different: $N$-laws use 1, 2, 3; $D$-laws use 3, 2, 5 (reverse).
> 2. Applying affinity laws to **system curve** instead of fan curve. The laws are *fan* laws — they predict new fan performance, not new system resistance.
> 3. Forgetting that **efficiency is assumed constant** in fan law derivations (a simplifying assumption). In practice, efficiency drops slightly off-design.
> 4. Not specifying the **basis of geometric similarity** (all linear dimensions scaled by same factor — $D, B, \beta$ unchanged).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Fans**

### A. Definitions
- ✅ Fan = turbomachine with pressure rise < **0.05 bar (≈ 5% atm)**
- ✅ Incompressible flow assumption valid for fans
- ✅ Euler work: $W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$; for radial inlet: $W = U_2 V_{w2}$
- ✅ Three fan laws: $Q \propto N, \; \Delta p \propto N^2, \; P \propto N^3$ (constant $D$)
- ✅ Three diameter laws: $Q \propto D^3, \; \Delta p \propto D^2, \; P \propto D^5$ (constant $N$)

### B. Blade Types
- ✅ **Radial blade** ($\beta_2 = 90°$) — radial exit, simple, dusty applications.
- ✅ **Backward curved** ($\beta_2 = 30°$–$60°$) — non-overloading, efficient, HVAC.
- ✅ **Forward curved** ($\beta_2 > 90°$) — overloading, high flow, low pressure.

### C. Velocity Triangles (Centrifugal)
- ✅ Outlet: $V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2$ (radial exit case)
- ✅ Inlet: assume $V_{w1} = 0$ unless stated otherwise
- ✅ $U_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$
- ✅ $V_{f2} = Q / (\pi D_2 B_2)$

### D. Specific Speed Classification
- ✅ $N_s < 4000$ → Centrifugal
- ✅ $4000 < N_s < 12000$ → Mixed flow
- ✅ $N_s > 12000$ → Axial flow

### E. Power & Efficiency Chain
- ✅ Air power: $P_{air} = \rho Q g H = \rho Q W$
- ✅ Shaft power: $P_{shaft} = P_{air} / \eta_f$
- ✅ Input power: $P_{input} = P_{shaft} / \eta_m$

### F. Common KTU Numerical Setup
- ✅ Given: $D_2, N, B_2, \beta_2, Q, \rho$ → find $W, P, \Delta p$
- ✅ Given: $N_1, Q_1, \Delta p_1, P_1$ → find $Q_2, \Delta p_2, P_2$ at $N_2$
- ✅ Given: $D_1$ and scale factor → find new performance using $D$-laws
- ✅ Given: $N, Q, H$ → find $N_s$ and identify fan type

### G. Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Mixing up $N$-law and $D$-law exponents.
- ❌ Forgetting to convert mmWC to Pa (or vice versa) when needed.
- ❌ Confusing manometric and mechanical efficiency.
- ❌ Not stating "radial inlet" or "radial exit" assumption explicitly.

### H. One-Line Revision Quotes
- 🧠 *"Backward curved = non-overload; Forward curved = overload."*
- 🧠 *"Power is the cube of speed, but only if D is held constant."*
- 🧠 *"Fan is to air what pump is to water — at low pressure."*
- 🧠 *"Specific speed decides the type: low = radial, high = axial."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blowers and Compressors: Classification and Working Principles of Fans, Blowers and Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Blowers and Compressors: Classification and Working Principles of Fans, Blowers and Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Turbo Blowers and Compressors** are turbomachines that impart **kinetic energy** to a gaseous working fluid (usually air) and convert it into **pressure energy**, thereby enabling the transport or compression of gases. The classification is fundamentally based on the **pressure rise (compression ratio)** they deliver and the **mechanism of energy transfer** (positive displacement vs. rotodynamic).

The three broad categories of gas-handling turbomachines are distinguished by their **delivery pressure** and the **specific speed regime** of operation.

| Machine | Typical Pressure Rise | Compression Ratio | Flow Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fans** | Up to **0.1 bar (10 kPa)** | $r_p \leq 1.1$ | High volume, low pressure |
| **Blowers** | **0.1 – 10 bar** | $1.1 < r_p < 10$ | Moderate volume, moderate pressure |
| **Compressors** | **> 10 bar** | $r_p > 10$ | Low to moderate volume, high pressure |

Where the **compression ratio** is defined as:
$$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Blower-Analogy:** Imagine a household scenario with three devices:
> - A **table fan** blows a lot of air, but you barely feel any pressure buildup (it is a *fan*).
> - A **vacuum cleaner** motor creates noticeable suction pressure to pick up dust (it is a *blower*).
> - A **diesel engine turbocharger** compresses intake air into the engine cylinder at very high pressure (it is a *compressor*).
>
> All three use rotating blades, but the **amount of pressure energy** they pump into the air is what classifies them. The blades **add momentum**, and the casing or diffuser **converts that momentum into pressure**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Air Density:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at sea level, 15°C)
> - **Gas Constant for Air:** $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
> - **Specific Heat Ratio for Air:** $\gamma = 1.4$
> - **Power Unit:** $\text{kW}$ or $\text{hp}$ ($1 \text{ hp} = 0.7457 \text{ kW}$)
> - **Pressure Unit (SI):** $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{bar}$ ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$)
> - **Volume Flow Unit:** $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ or $\text{CMM}$ (Cubic Meter per Minute)

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme Classification Logic

The KTU syllabus requires students to classify these machines based on **two parallel taxonomies**:

1. **Based on the Principle of Energy Transfer:**
   - **Positive Displacement (PD) Machines** — fixed volume trapped and discharged.
   - **Dynamic / Rotodynamic Machines** — continuous momentum exchange with a rotor.

2. **Based on the Direction of Flow through the Rotor:**
   - **Radial Flow (Centrifugal)**
   - **Axial Flow**
   - **Mixed Flow**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification — Fans, Blowers, and Compressors

#### 2.1.1 Fans (Low-Pressure Machines)

Fans are devices that move **large volumes of gas** (air, combustion gases, or vapors) against **very low pressures**. The total pressure rise across a fan is generally **less than 0.1 bar (about 1.1% compression ratio)**.

**Two main sub-classifications:**

**(a) Centrifugal Fans (Radial Flow)**
- Air enters axially at the **eye (hub)** of the impeller.
- The rotating impeller slings the air outward by **centrifugal action**.
- The air then passes through a **scroll casing** (volute) and a **diffuser** which converts kinetic energy → pressure energy.
- Best suited for **high pressure, low flow** applications (e.g., industrial ventilation, boiler FD/ID fans).

**Sub-types of Centrifugal Fans:**
- **Backward Curved / Backward Inclined Blades** — high efficiency, non-overloading power characteristic.
- **Forward Curved Blades** — high flow at low speed, used in domestic HVAC.
- **Radial Blade Fans** — used for dust-laden or dirty air.

**(b) Axial Flow Fans**
- Air flows **parallel to the axis of rotation**.
- The rotor blades act like **aircraft wings**, generating **aerodynamic lift** that pushes air forward.
- Comprises a **hub**, **blades**, and a **casing** (sometimes with **guide vanes** or **stator blades** to remove swirl).
- Best suited for **high flow, low pressure** (e.g., mine ventilation, cooling towers).

**Sub-types of Axial Flow Fans:**
- **Propeller Fans** — no guide vanes, used in wall/exhaust applications.
- **Tube-Axial Fans** — enclosed in a cylinder, light-duty industrial.
- **Vane-Axial Fans** — with inlet/outlet guide vanes, higher pressure rise.
- **Mixed Flow Fans** — combine radial and axial flow, compact design.

#### 2.1.2 Blowers (Medium-Pressure Machines)

Blowers operate in the **0.1 bar to 10 bar** range. They can be either **positive displacement** (Roots blower) or **centrifugal** (multistage).

**(a) Positive Displacement (PD) Blowers**
- **Roots Blower:** Two figure-8 shaped lobes rotate in opposite directions inside a casing. Air is trapped between the lobes and the casing and is "pushed" to the discharge side **without internal compression**.
- **Screw Compressor / Blower:** Two intermeshing helical screws trap and compress air between them.

**(b) Centrifugal Blowers (Turbo Blowers)**
- These are essentially **high-pressure centrifugal compressors with low compression ratios** (typically $r_p = 1.2$ to $4$).
- Use **radial or backward-curved impellers** rotating at high tip speeds.
- Multi-staging is used to achieve higher pressure rises.

#### 2.1.3 Compressors (High-Pressure Machines)

Compressors achieve **compression ratios greater than 10** (or pressures above 10 bar). They are subdivided into:

**(a) Positive Displacement Compressors**
- **Reciprocating Compressors** — piston-cylinder mechanism, single or multi-stage.
- **Rotary Compressors** — screw, vane, lobe, scroll types.

**(b) Dynamic (Turbo) Compressors**
- **Centrifugal Compressors** — radial outward flow, often multi-stage with intercooling.
- **Axial Flow Compressors** — series of rotating and stationary blade rows (stages).

### 2.2 Working Principles — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Working of a Centrifugal Fan/Blower/Compressor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Turbomachine Equation** governs the energy transfer in all dynamic machines:
> $$W = U_2 \cdot C_{\theta 2} - U_1 \cdot C_{\theta 1}$$
> For a centrifugal machine with **radial inlet** ($C_{\theta 1} \approx 0$):
> $$W = U_2 \cdot C_{\theta 2}$$

**Step-by-step Energy Transfer Logic:**

1. **Inlet (Eye):** Gas enters axially at the impeller eye with **no pre-swirl** (in simple machines).
2. **Impeller Action:** The rotating impeller (at tip speed $U_2$) imparts **tangential velocity** $C_{\theta 2}$ to the gas via **centrifugal force**.
3. **Volute/Diffuser:** The high-velocity gas exiting the impeller tip is decelerated in the **volute casing** (or vaneless/vaned diffuser). By **Bernoulli's principle**, this kinetic energy drop appears as a **static pressure rise**.
4. **Discharge:** Compressed gas exits the volute through the discharge nozzle.

#### 2.2.2 Working of an Axial Flow Fan/Compressor

The axial flow machine consists of:
- **Rotor (Moving Blades):** Acts like an airfoil, generating **lift** that produces a pressure rise across the blade row.
- **Stator (Stationary Blades / Guide Vanes):** Removes the tangential (swirl) component of velocity and converts it into additional pressure rise. Also directs flow onto the next rotor row at the correct angle.

**Velocity Triangle Analysis (Per Stage):**
$$\Delta h_{stage} = U \cdot (C_{\theta 2,rotor} - C_{\theta 1,rotor})$$

A typical multi-stage axial compressor can have **10 to 20 stages**, with overall pressure ratios of 5:1 to 30:1.

#### 2.2.3 Working of a Positive Displacement Blower (Roots Type)

1. Two **identical lobed rotors** rotate in **opposite directions** in close mesh with the casing.
2. A **fixed volume of gas** is trapped between the lobes and the casing.
3. The trapped gas is **carried to the discharge port** without internal compression.
4. Discharge happens at the **delivery pressure** of the system (back-pressure determines discharge pressure).
5. **No internal compression** → requires **external cooling** for high-pressure applications.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas needed for KTU university exam problems on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Pressure (Compression) Ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$ | Euler's Turbomachine Equation (work per unit mass) | $\text{J/kg}$ |
| 3 | $W = U_2 C_{\theta 2}$ | Euler's equation (radial inlet, $C_{\theta 1}=0$) | $\text{J/kg}$ |
| 4 | $U_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | Blade tip speed at outlet | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 5 | $\eta_{isentropic} = \dfrac{(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}{T_{02}/T_{01} - 1}$ | Isentropic efficiency of compressor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{actual} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot W_s}{\eta_{isentropic}}$ | Actual power required | $\text{kW}$ |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 8 | $\dot{V}_{displacement} = L \cdot A \cdot N$ | PD compressor displacement volume flow (reciprocating) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Specific heat relation for ideal gas | $\text{J/(kg·K)}$ |
| 10 | $T_{02} = T_{01} + \dfrac{W}{C_p}$ | Total temperature rise (adiabatic) | $\text{K}$ |
| 11 | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ | Ideal gas density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 12 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(H)^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (for dynamic compressors) | $\text{rpm, m}^3/\text{s, m}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Fans** are used in HVAC, mine ventilation, boiler FD/ID/PA fans, electronic cooling, wind tunnels.
> - **Blowers** are used in sewage aeration, pneumatic conveying, cement kilns, copper smelting, fish farming aeration.
> - **Compressors** are used in gas turbine engines, air separation plants (oxygen/nitrogen), refrigeration, natural gas pipelines, scuba tanks, and air tools.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Turbomachine Equation (Energy Transfer Per Unit Mass)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important derivation** in turbomachinery. It applies to all dynamic fans, blowers, and compressors. The KTU board examiner expects a clean, vector-based derivation.

**Setup:** Consider a control volume around the rotor of a dynamic machine. Gas enters at station 1 (inlet) with absolute velocity $C_1$ and exits at station 2 (outlet) with absolute velocity $C_2$. The rotor rotates with tangential velocity $U$ at radius $r$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Angular Momentum Theorem (Euler's Turbomachine Equation):**

The torque on the rotor is equal to the rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid:
$$T = \dot{m} (r_2 C_{\theta 2} - r_1 C_{\theta 1})$$

Since $U = \omega r$, we have $U \cdot r = \omega r^2$, so $r_2 = U_2/\omega$ and $r_1 = U_1/\omega$. Substituting:

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega} (U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1})$$

**Step 2 — Power Transferred to the Fluid:**

Power $P = T \cdot \omega$:

$$P = \dot{m} (U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1})$$

**Step 3 — Work Done Per Unit Mass (Specific Work):**

$$W = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$$

$$\boxed{W = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification for Radial Inlet (typical of centrifugal fans):**

At the inlet of a centrifugal machine, gas has no tangential component (purely radial entry):
$$C_{\theta 1} = 0 \implies W = U_2 C_{\theta 2}$$

**Step 5 — Generalization to Axial Machines:**

For an axial machine, $U_1 = U_2 = U$ (constant mean radius), so:
$$W = U(C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the **mathematical backbone** of all dynamic turbomachine analysis. The KTU examiner expects students to identify the inlet and outlet velocity components from the velocity triangle and substitute correctly.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fans vs. Blowers vs. Compressors

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison table is a high-yield summary expected in **2-mark KTU short-answer questions**.

| Parameter | Fan | Blower | Compressor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Rise (ΔP)** | < 0.1 bar (10 kPa) | 0.1 – 10 bar | > 10 bar |
| **Compression Ratio ($r_p$)** | ≤ 1.1 | 1.1 – 10 | > 10 |
| **Typical Flow Rates** | Very High (10⁴–10⁶ m³/h) | Moderate (10²–10⁴ m³/h) | Low to Moderate (1–10³ m³/h) |
| **Specific Speed ($N_s$)** | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| **Mechanism** | Mostly dynamic (axial/centrifugal) | Dynamic (multi-stage centrifugal) or PD (Roots) | PD (reciprocating/rotary) or Dynamic (multi-stage) |
| **Density Change Through Machine** | Negligible (<1%) | Small (1–20%) | Significant (>20%) |
| **Energy Transfer per kg** | Low (≈ 5–15 kJ/kg) | Medium (≈ 15–150 kJ/kg) | High (≈ 150–500 kJ/kg) |
| **Typical Applications** | HVAC, mine ventilation | Aeration, pneumatic conveying | Gas turbines, air separation, pipelines |
| **Example Machines** | Propeller fan, centrifugal fan | Roots blower, multi-stage centrifugal blower | Reciprocating piston, centrifugal, axial flow compressor |
| **Compressibility Effects** | Often neglected | Considered (but minor) | Fully considered (uses gas tables) |
| **Multi-staging** | Rarely | Sometimes (centrifugal blowers) | Almost always (PD) or typically (dynamic) |
| **Intercooling** | Not needed | Sometimes used | Mandatory for high $r_p$ |

### 3.3 Comparison: Positive Displacement vs. Dynamic Machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the ability to **distinguish** these two fundamental categories. This matrix covers it.

| Feature | Positive Displacement (PD) | Dynamic (Rotodynamic) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Traps fixed mass, reduces volume | Continuous momentum exchange with rotor |
| **Pressure vs. Flow** | Pressure depends on **system back-pressure**; flow nearly **constant** at given speed | Pressure and flow are **interdependent** (operating point on curve) |
| **Surge/Choking** | No surge; can overpressure (needs relief valve) | Susceptible to **surge** at low flow |
| **Compressible Flow** | Handled in cylinder analysis | Uses velocity triangles and Euler's equation |
| **Volumetric Efficiency** | Key parameter ($\eta_v$) | Not applicable |
| **Typical Range** | High pressure, moderate flow | Wide range, high flow |
| **Examples** | Reciprocating, screw, Roots, scroll | Centrifugal, axial, mixed flow |
| **Starting Torque** | High (must overcome discharge pressure) | Low (no discharge pressure at start) |

### 3.4 Sample Analytical Problem (Model Solution)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A centrifugal compressor handles air at inlet conditions of 1 bar and 300 K. The impeller tip diameter is 0.5 m and the rotational speed is 15000 rpm. Assuming radial inlet and a slip factor of 0.9, with a flow coefficient $\phi = 0.25$ and work coefficient $\psi = 0.6$, calculate: (a) the specific work, (b) the isentropic work, and (c) the isentropic efficiency if the pressure ratio achieved is 4.

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$
- $D_2 = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- $N = 15000 \text{ rpm}$
- Slip factor: $\sigma = 0.9$
- $\phi = 0.25$ (flow coefficient)
- $\psi = 0.6$ (work coefficient)
- $r_p = 4$

**Step (a): Specific Work**

Tip speed:
$$U_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 15000}{60} = 392.7 \text{ m/s}$$

Specific work using work coefficient:
$$W_s = \psi \cdot U_2^2 = 0.6 \times (392.7)^2 = 0.6 \times 154214 = 92528 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_s \approx 92.53 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Step (b): Isentropic Work**

For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$:
$$T_{01} = 300 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{02s} = T_{01} \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times (4)^{0.4/1.4}$$
$$T_{02s} = 300 \times (4)^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.486 = 445.8 \text{ K}$$

$$W_s^{isentropic} = C_p (T_{02s} - T_{01}) = 1005 \times (445.8 - 300) = 1005 \times 145.8$$
$$\boxed{W_s^{isentropic} \approx 146.5 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Step (c): Isentropic Efficiency**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **inversion of the ratio** for compressors compared to turbines. For compressors, isentropic work is the **minimum theoretical work**; actual work is always **greater** than isentropic work.
> $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{W_{isentropic}}{W_{actual}}$$

$$\eta_{isen} = \frac{146.5}{92.53} = 1.583$$

This gives $\eta_{isen} > 1$, which is physically impossible. This indicates the assumed work coefficient $\psi$ is unrealistic for the stated pressure ratio, OR the given data is for a different configuration. The student should be alert to this and note: **"The data given is inconsistent — a centrifugal compressor achieving $r_p = 4$ would require a much higher specific work than $\psi U_2^2 = 92.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$."** A realistic value of $\psi$ for such a pressure rise would be in the range of **1.0 to 1.4**, which is the **K二级: validation step** expected by KTU board examiners.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram is rendered as a **functional architecture flow** rather than a physical drawing, as physical schematics of fans/compressors require complex 3D illustrations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Gas-Handling Turbomachines] --> B[Principle of Operation]
    A --> C[Pressure Rise Class]
    A --> D[Flow Direction]

    B --> B1[Positive Displacement PD]
    B --> B2[Dynamic Rotodynamic]

    C --> C1[Fans DP less than 0.1 bar]
    C --> C2[Blowers DP 0.1 to 10 bar]
    C --> C3[Compressors DP greater than 10 bar]

    D --> D1[Axial Flow]
    D --> D2[Radial Flow Centrifugal]
    D --> D3[Mixed Flow]

    B1 --> B1A[Reciprocating]
    B1 --> B1B[Rotary Screw Vane Lobe Scroll]
    B1 --> B1C[Roots Blower]

    B2 --> B2A[Centrifugal Impeller]
    B2 --> B2B[Axial Flow Stages]
    B2 --> B2C[Mixed Flow]

    C1 --> C1A[Centrifugal Fan]
    C1 --> C1B[Axial Flow Fan]
    C1 --> C1C[Mixed Flow Fan]

    C2 --> C2A[Roots Blower PD]
    C2 --> C2B[Centrifugal Blower Multi-stage]
    C2 --> C2C[Screw Blower]

    C3 --> C3A[Reciprocating Compressor]
    C3 --> C3B[Rotary Compressor]
    C3 --> C3C[Centrifugal Compressor]
    C3 --> C3D[Axial Flow Compressor]

    D1 --> D1A[Propeller Fan]
    D1 --> D1B[Tube Axial]
    D1 --> D1C[Vane Axial]
    D1 --> D1D[Axial Compressor]

    D2 --> D2A[Backward Curved Centrifugal Fan]
    D2 --> D2B[Forward Curved Centrifugal Fan]
    D2 --> D2C[Radial Blade Centrifugal]
    D2 --> D2D[Centrifugal Compressor]
```

### 4.2 Working Principle Block Diagram — Centrifugal Compressor

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inlet Air 1 bar, 300 K] --> B[Impeller Eye Radial Inlet]
    B --> C[Rotating Impeller Tip Speed U2]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Force Imparts Tangential Velocity Ctheta2]
    D --> E[High Velocity Gas Exits Impeller Tip]
    E --> F[Volute Casing or Vaned Diffuser]
    F --> G[Kinetic Energy Converted to Pressure]
    G --> H[Discharge Port P2 greater than P1]
    H --> I[Compressed Air to System]
```

### 4.3 Working Principle Block Diagram — Axial Flow Compressor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Inlet Air] --> B[Inlet Guide Vanes IGV]
    B --> C[Rotor Row 1 R1 - Lift and Acceleration]
    C --> D[Stator Row 1 S1 - Swirl Removal and Diffuser]
    D --> E[Rotor Row 2 R2 - Further Pressure Rise]
    E --> F[Stator Row 2 S2 - Continue Static Pressure Rise]
    F --> G[More Rotor Stator Stages]
    G --> H[Outlet Guide Vanes OGV]
    H --> I[High Pressure Compressed Air Discharge]
```

### 4.4 Working Principle Block Diagram — Roots Blower (Positive Displacement)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> B[Trapped Between Lobe A and Casing]
    B --> C[Lobe Rotation Carries Fixed Volume to Discharge Side]
    C --> D[No Internal Compression]
    D --> E[Discharge at System Back-Pressure]
    E --> F[External Cooler Required for High Pressure]
    F --> G[Compressed Air Delivery]
```

### 4.5 Energy Conversion Chain — Euler's Equation Visualized

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Mechanical Shaft Power] --> B[Rotor Torque T]
    B --> C[Angular Momentum Change of Fluid]
    C --> D[Specific Work W equals U2Ctheta2 minus U1Ctheta1]
    D --> E[Kinetic Energy at Impeller Exit]
    E --> F[Diffuser Volute Deceleration]
    F --> G[Static Pressure Rise DP]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Classify turbomachines used for gas handling. How are fans, blowers, and compressors distinguished from each other?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Turbomachines used for gas handling are classified into three categories based on the **pressure rise** they deliver:

1. **Fans:** Pressure rise less than 0.1 bar (≈ 10 kPa). Used for moving large volumes of air at low pressure, e.g., mine ventilation, HVAC.
2. **Blowers:** Pressure rise between 0.1 bar and 10 bar. They can be positive displacement (Roots blower) or dynamic (multi-stage centrifugal blower). Used in aeration tanks, pneumatic conveying.
3. **Compressors:** Pressure rise greater than 10 bar. Achieve high compression ratios, used in gas turbines, air separation, pipelines. Can be reciprocating, rotary, centrifugal, or axial flow.

> Key distinguishing parameter: **compression ratio $r_p = P_2/P_1$**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*State and explain Euler's turbomachine equation. What does each term represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Euler's Turbomachine Equation** gives the **work done per unit mass** of fluid flowing through a dynamic turbomachine:

$$W = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Specific work done on the fluid ($\text{J/kg}$)
- $U_2, U_1$ = Tangential (blade) velocities at outlet and inlet ($\text{m/s}$)
- $C_{\theta 2}, C_{\theta 1}$ = Tangential components of absolute velocity at outlet and inlet ($\text{m/s}$)

For a **centrifugal machine with radial inlet** ($C_{\theta 1} = 0$):
$$W = U_2 C_{\theta 2}$$

This equation is derived from the **angular momentum theorem** and is fundamental to all axial and centrifugal turbomachine analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a centrifugal compressor**. Derive the **Euler's turbomachine equation** and discuss how the energy transfer occurs from rotor to fluid.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A centrifugal compressor compresses air from **1 bar, 300 K** to a pressure ratio of **3.5**. The mass flow rate is **5 kg/s**. Assuming isentropic compression, calculate:
  (i) The isentropic temperature at outlet.
  (ii) The isentropic specific work.
  (iii) The isentropic power required.
  (iv) The actual power required if the isentropic efficiency is **82%**.

  Given: $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Derivation (7 marks):**

A centrifugal compressor consists of an **impeller** (rotor), a **diffuser** (vaneless or vaned), and a **volute casing**. Air enters axially at the impeller eye, is accelerated radially outward by the rotating blades, and exits the impeller tip at high velocity. The diffuser/volute converts this kinetic energy into static pressure.

**Derivation of Euler's Equation:**

Applying the **angular momentum theorem** to a control volume around the rotor:
$$T = \dot{m} (r_2 C_{\theta 2} - r_1 C_{\theta 1})$$

Multiplying by angular velocity $\omega$ to get power, then dividing by $\dot{m}$ to get specific work:
$$W = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$$

(Detailed derivation is in Section 3.1.)

For radial inlet, $C_{\theta 1} = 0$, so $W = U_2 C_{\theta 2}$. The product $U_2 C_{\theta 2}$ represents the **rate of angular momentum transfer** from the rotor to the fluid, which is the energy input per unit mass.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $r_p = 3.5$, $\dot{m} = 5 \text{ kg/s}$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.82$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$.

**(i) Isentropic Outlet Temperature:**

$$T_{2s} = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times (3.5)^{0.4/1.4}$$
$$T_{2s} = 300 \times (3.5)^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.4356 = 430.7 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_{2s} \approx 430.7 \text{ K}}$$

**(ii) Isentropic Specific Work:**

$$W_s = C_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = 1005 \times (430.7 - 300) = 1005 \times 130.7$$
$$\boxed{W_s \approx 131.3 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(iii) Isentropic Power Required:**

$$P_{isen} = \dot{m} \cdot W_s = 5 \times 131.3 = 656.6 \text{ kW}$$
$$\boxed{P_{isen} \approx 656.6 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iv) Actual Power Required:**

For a compressor: $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{W_{isen}}{W_{actual}}$
$$W_{actual} = \frac{W_{isen}}{\eta_{isen}} = \frac{131.3}{0.82} = 160.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$P_{actual} = \dot{m} \cdot W_{actual} = 5 \times 160.1 = 800.7 \text{ kW}$$
$$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 800.7 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **classification of fans** based on flow direction. Compare **centrifugal fans** and **axial flow fans** in terms of construction, working principle, and applications.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a velocity triangle, derive the **Euler work per stage** for an axial flow compressor. An axial flow compressor stage has the following data:
  - Mean blade speed: $U = 220 \text{ m/s}$
  - Inlet absolute velocity tangential component: $C_{\theta 1} = 150 \text{ m/s}$
  - Outlet absolute velocity tangential component: $C_{\theta 2} = 320 \text{ m/s}$
  - Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 25 \text{ kg/s}$

  Calculate the **(i) stage work**, **(ii) power developed**, and **(iii) the stage pressure rise** if the stage loading coefficient $\psi = 0.55$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification & Comparison (7 marks):**

Fans are classified into two main types based on flow direction:

1. **Centrifugal Fans (Radial Flow):** Air enters axially, exits radially outward. Constructed with an impeller (backward-curved, forward-curved, or radial blades) and a scroll casing (volute).
   - **Working:** Centrifugal action imparts radial velocity; the volute casing converts kinetic energy to pressure.
   - **Applications:** Boiler FD/ID fans, industrial dust collection, high-pressure HVAC.

2. **Axial Flow Fans:** Air flows parallel to the axis. Constructed with a hub, blades, and casing; often with guide vanes.
   - **Working:** Blades act as airfoils, generating aerodynamic lift that produces axial thrust and pressure rise.
   - **Applications:** Mine ventilation, cooling towers, tunnel ventilation, high-volume low-pressure HVAC.

| Feature | Centrifugal Fan | Axial Flow Fan |
|---|---|---|
| Flow direction | Radial outward | Axial |
| Pressure rise | Moderate to high | Low to moderate |
| Flow rate | Moderate | Very high |
| Space | Requires radial space | Compact (axial) |
| Efficiency | 70–85% | 80–90% |
| Guide vanes | Not always | Often present |

**Part (b) — Velocity Triangle & Numerical (7 marks):**

**Velocity Triangle Derivation (Euler's Equation for Axial Flow):**

For an axial flow stage, the mean radius is constant, so $U_1 = U_2 = U$. Applying Euler's equation:
$$W_{stage} = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1} = U(C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})$$

**Given:** $U = 220 \text{ m/s}$, $C_{\theta 1} = 150 \text{ m/s}$, $C_{\theta 2} = 320 \text{ m/s}$, $\dot{m} = 25 \text{ kg/s}$, $\psi = 0.55$.

**(i) Stage Work:**

$$W_{stage} = U (C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1}) = 220 \times (320 - 150) = 220 \times 170$$
$$\boxed{W_{stage} = 37400 \text{ J/kg} = 37.4 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(ii) Power Developed:**

$$P = \dot{m} \cdot W_{stage} = 25 \times 37400 = 935000 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{P = 935 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Stage Pressure Rise:**

Using stage loading coefficient $\psi = W_{stage}/U^2$:
$$W_{stage} = \psi \cdot U^2 = 0.55 \times (220)^2 = 0.55 \times 48400 = 26620 \text{ J/kg}$$

Note: The two computed work values differ (37.4 kJ/kg vs 26.6 kJ/kg) because the velocity triangle data and the loading coefficient may not be from the same operating point. The KTU board would accept either method, but the student should **clearly state both approaches** and identify the discrepancy.

Assuming $W_{stage} = 37.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$ from velocity triangle:
$$T_{02} - T_{01} = \frac{W_{stage}}{C_p} = \frac{37400}{1005} = 37.21 \text{ K}$$

If inlet stagnation temperature $T_{01} = 300 \text{ K}$:
$$T_{02} = 337.21 \text{ K}$$

Pressure rise assuming isentropic:
$$\frac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} = \left(\frac{T_{02}}{T_{01}}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left(\frac{337.21}{300}\right)^{3.5} = (1.124)^{3.5} = 1.519$$

$$\boxed{P_{02}/P_{01} \approx 1.52 \text{ (i.e., 52% pressure rise per stage)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up compressor and turbine efficiency formulas:** Compressors use $\eta_{isen} = W_{isen}/W_{actual}$; turbines use the inverse. **2 marks lost** if this is wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting to use stagnation (total) temperatures** in Euler's equation — Euler's work is based on total (stagnation) enthalpy, not static. **3 marks lost**.
> 3. **Not specifying flow direction** when classifying fans — KTU requires explicit mention of "axial" or "radial" or "mixed" flow. **1 mark lost** per missing specification.
> 4. **Using $P_1$ and $P_2$ instead of $P_{01}$ and $P_{02}$** in pressure ratio when the machine has high kinetic energy at outlet. The pressure ratio of a compressor stage is based on stagnation (total) pressures.
> 5. **Confusing the work coefficient $\psi$ formula:** $\psi = W/U^2$ for axial flow, but $\psi = W/(U_2^2)$ for centrifugal. Note the difference in the reference velocity.
> 6. **Stating Euler's equation without derivation:** KTU requires the derivation (angular momentum theorem) for full marks. A bare statement gets only 2 of 5 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield for KTU Board Exams**

- ✅ **Three Classes of Gas-Handling Machines:** Fan (ΔP < 0.1 bar), Blower (0.1–10 bar), Compressor (> 10 bar). Distinguish using **compression ratio $r_p$**.
- ✅ **Two Fundamental Principles:** Positive Displacement (PD) and Dynamic (Rotodynamic). PD uses trapped volume; Dynamic uses momentum exchange.
- ✅ **Euler's Turbomachine Equation:** $W = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$. This is the **single most tested equation** in the entire course.
- ✅ **Radial Inlet Simplification:** $C_{\theta 1} = 0 \implies W = U_2 C_{\theta 2}$.
- ✅ **Centrifugal Machine Components:** Impeller, Diffuser, Volute Casing.
- ✅ **Axial Machine Components:** Rotor + Stator (stages). Each stage ≈ 5–8% pressure rise.
- ✅ **Fan Sub-Types:** Centrifugal (backward/forward curved, radial) and Axial (propeller, tube-axial, vane-axial).
- ✅ **Roots Blower:** PD type, **no internal compression**, requires external cooling.
- ✅ **Compressor Efficiency Formula:** $\eta_{isen} = W_{isen}/W_{actual}$ (inverted from turbine).
- ✅ **Stagnation vs. Static Properties:** Use stagnation (total) values in Euler's equation and efficiency calculations.
- ✅ **Tip Speed Formula:** $U_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$ — a frequent KTU question.
- ✅ **Real Gas Effects:** Use $\gamma$ and $C_p$ for air: $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$.
- ✅ **Power Calculation:** $P = \dot{m} W_{actual} = \dot{m} W_{isen}/\eta_{isen}$.
- ✅ **PD vs. Dynamic Trade-off:** PD machines deliver constant flow regardless of pressure; Dynamic machines deliver constant pressure approximately, with flow varying with system resistance.
- ✅ **KTU Numerical Favourite:** "Calculate isentropic work, actual work, and power for a compressor with given $r_p$, $\dot{m}$, $\eta_{isen}$" — practice this format.
- ✅ **Important Units:** Always use SI (Pa, m³/s, kg/s, kW). Convert bar to Pa by multiplying by $10^5$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** Questions on this topic typically combine a **theoretical explanation (7 marks)** with a **numerical on Euler's equation or isentropic compression (7 marks)**. Master the velocity triangle analysis and the isentropic process formulas ($T_2/T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $W = C_p \Delta T$) — these are the **two numerical pillars** of the entire module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Compressors: Stage Velocity Triangles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Compressors: Stage Velocity Triangles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage Velocity Triangles of a Centrifugal Compressor** are the geometric vector representations of the **absolute velocity (V)**, **relative velocity (W)**, and **blade velocity (U)** at the **inlet (eye/inducer)** and **outlet (impeller tip)** of a radial/centrifugal impeller. They are the fundamental analytical tools used to determine the work transfer, pressure rise, and flow behaviour in a centrifugal compressor stage.

For a centrifugal compressor, the velocity triangles are drawn at **two key stations**:
- **Station 1 — Inlet (Eye of the Impeller):** Gas enters axially (or with pre-whirl) into the rotating impeller.
- **Station 2 — Outlet (Impeller Tip):** Gas leaves the impeller at a high radius with substantial tangential velocity, which is then converted to pressure in the diffuser.

The **relative velocity (W)** is the velocity of the fluid *as seen by an observer rotating with the impeller*, while the **absolute velocity (V)** is the velocity as seen by a stationary observer fixed to the casing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Merry-Go-Round

Imagine a child standing at the **centre (hub)** of a spinning merry-go-round and throwing a tennis ball **radially outward** (away from the centre).

- To a **stationary parent** standing on the ground, the ball travels *radially and slightly tangentially* (because the merry-go-round has dragged it sideways during its flight). This combined path is the **absolute velocity (V)**.
- To a **child riding the merry-go-round**, the ball appears to leave *almost straight radially* (the rotational component is subtracted out). This is the **relative velocity (W)**.
- The **tangential speed of the merry-go-round at the radius where the ball was released** is the **blade velocity (U)**.

The vector triangle formed by these three velocities — $\vec{V} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ — is the **velocity triangle**. In a centrifugal compressor, the impeller *adds energy* to the fluid, which manifests as a change in the absolute tangential component (whirl velocity, $V_w$) between inlet and outlet — exactly what Euler's turbomachinery equation captures.

## 1.3 Nomenclature — Universal KTU Convention

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $U$ | Blade (peripheral) velocity | m/s |
| $V$ | Absolute velocity of fluid | m/s |
| $W$ | Relative velocity of fluid (w.r.t. blade) | m/s |
| $V_f$ | Flow (meridional / radial) component of V | m/s |
| $V_w$ | Whirl (tangential) component of V | m/s |
| $W_f$ | Flow component of relative velocity | m/s |
| $W_w$ | Whirl component of relative velocity | m/s |
| $\alpha$ | Absolute flow angle (from tangential) | degrees |
| $\beta$ | Relative flow angle (from tangential) | degrees |
| $r$ | Radius (inlet $r_1$, outlet $r_2$) | m |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity of impeller | rad/s |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | rpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention for Angles:** $\alpha$ is measured from the **tangential direction**, while $\beta$ is measured from the **tangential direction in the relative frame** (i.e., measured from the blade surface). This convention is strictly followed in all KTU board questions and must NOT be reversed by students.

## 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus

Under Module 4 of PEMET411, the **Stage Velocity Triangles** form the analytical foundation for:
- Deriving **Euler's work equation** for compressors.
- Calculating the **ideal and actual stage work**.
- Introducing the concept of **slip factor ($\sigma$)** for radial-bladed impellers.
- Establishing the relationship between **blade angles ($\beta_1, \beta_2$)** and machine performance.
- Designing the **vaneless and vaned diffusers** downstream of the impeller.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inlet Velocity Triangle (Station 1 — Eye of the Impeller)

At the impeller eye, the gas is drawn axially into the rotating eye. The components are:
- Blade velocity: $U_1 = \omega r_1$
- Absolute velocity: $V_1$ with components $V_{f1}$ (axial/meridional) and $V_{w1}$ (pre-whirl, tangential)
- Relative velocity: $W_1$ with components $W_{f1}$ and $W_{w1}$

**Vector closure relation:**
$$\vec{V_1} = \vec{W_1} + \vec{U_1}$$

**Component-wise breakdown (in the tangential and meridional directions):**
- Tangential: $V_{w1} = U_1 + W_{w1}$, so $W_{w1} = V_{w1} - U_1$
- Meridional: $V_{f1} = W_{f1}$ (the flow component is identical in both frames)

> [!NOTE]
> **For pure axial entry (no pre-whirl):** $V_{w1} = 0$, so $W_{w1} = -U_1$ (negative sign indicates the relative whirl points *opposite* to blade motion). This is the **most common KTU exam assumption**.

## 2.2 The Outlet Velocity Triangle (Station 2 — Impeller Tip)

At the impeller outlet, the gas leaves at radius $r_2$ with blade velocity $U_2 = \omega r_2$. The components are:
- $V_2$ — absolute velocity leaving the impeller
- $W_2$ — relative velocity leaving the impeller (tangent to the blade)
- $V_{f2}$ — radial (meridional) outflow component
- $V_{w2}$ — whirl component (tangential, in direction of blade motion)

For a **radial-bladed impeller** (backward-curved blades with $\beta_2 = 90°$ from tangential, i.e., purely radial exit in relative frame), $W_2$ is purely radial and $W_{w2} = 0$, giving the famous result $V_{w2} = U_2$.

## 2.3 Euler's Turbomachinery Equation (Stage Work)

The specific work done by the impeller on the fluid per unit mass is:
$$W_s = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$

For the **common KTU assumption** of axial entry ($V_{w1} = 0$):
$$W_s = U_2 V_{w2} = U_2^2 \quad (\text{for radial blades})$$

## 2.4 Slip Factor — Why Real Compressors Underperform the Ideal

Real fluids do not follow the impeller blades perfectly; they **slip** relative to the impeller due to relative flow, friction, and passage geometry. The **slip factor $\sigma$** is defined as:

$$\sigma = \frac{V_{w2, \text{actual}}}{V_{w2, \text{ideal}}}$$

For a **radial-bladed impeller**, the ideal outlet whirl equals $U_2$, so:
$$V_{w2, \text{actual}} = \sigma \cdot U_2$$

**Stodola's empirical formula (commonly tested in KTU):**
$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos\beta_2}{Z}$$

where $Z$ is the number of blades and $\beta_2$ is the blade angle at outlet.

**Wiesner's approximation (also frequently tested):**
$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{0.63 \pi}{Z}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \pi D N / 60$ | Blade peripheral velocity | All velocity triangle problems |
| 2 | $\vec{V} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ | Vector closure (absolute = relative + blade) | Triangle construction |
| 3 | $V_f = V \cos\alpha$ | Flow component from absolute velocity | Resolving velocities |
| 4 | $V_w = V \sin\alpha$ | Whirl component from absolute velocity | Resolving velocities |
| 5 | $W_f = W \cos\beta$ | Flow component from relative velocity | Resolving velocities |
| 6 | $W_w = W \sin\beta$ | Whirl component from relative velocity | Resolving velocities |
| 7 | $W_s = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$ | Euler's work equation (specific work) | Energy transfer calculation |
| 8 | $W_s = U_2 V_{w2}$ (if $V_{w1}=0$) | Simplified Euler equation | Most common case |
| 9 | $\sigma = 1 - \pi\cos\beta_2 / Z$ | Stodola's slip factor | Real-stage work calculation |
| 10 | $\sigma = 1 - 0.63\pi / Z$ | Wiesner's slip factor | Quick estimation for radial blades |
| 11 | $V_{w2, \text{actual}} = \sigma U_2$ | Actual whirl at outlet | Slip-corrected work |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{stage}} = \Delta h_s / W_s$ | Stage isentropic efficiency | Performance evaluation |
| 13 | $\tan\alpha_1 = V_{f1} / (U_1 - W_{f1}\cot\beta_1)$ | Absolute inlet flow angle | Triangle solving |
| 14 | $\tan\beta_2 = W_{f2} / (U_2 - V_{w2})$ | Relative outlet flow angle | Triangle solving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTATION RULE:** When writing absolute values, magnitudes, or determinants inside markdown tables, **NEVER use the unescaped vertical bar `|`** — it breaks the table. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead. Example: write $\vert V_{w2} \vert$ not $\vert V_{w2} \vert$ inside any table.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

The velocity triangle analysis of centrifugal compressors underpins the design of:
- **Jet engines** (centrifugal compressor stage of small turbojet/turbofan engines).
- **Refrigeration and HVAC systems** (R-134a, R-410A centrifugal chillers).
- **Natural gas pipeline compressors** (multi-stage centrifugal boosters).
- **Process industries** (ethylene oxide, LNG, air separation units).
- **Superchargers and turbochargers** for internal combustion engines.

The slip factor corrections originated from the precise experimental measurements by **Aurel Stodola** (Swiss engineer, 1920s) and remain embedded in modern CFD-validated correlations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Euler's Turbomachinery Equation from the Velocity Triangle

**Starting point — the angular momentum theorem applied to a control volume fixed to the impeller:**

The torque on the fluid equals the rate of change of angular momentum:
$$T = \dot{m}(r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$$

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by the angular velocity $\omega$ to obtain the power transfer:**
$$P = T \omega = \dot{m}\,\omega(r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $U = \omega r$ at any radius, so $\omega r_2 = U_2$ and $\omega r_1 = U_1$:**
$$P = \dot{m}(U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1})$$

**Step 3 — Divide by mass flow rate to get specific work (work per unit mass):**
$$W_s = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$

This is **Euler's turbomachinery equation**, the cornerstone of all turbomachinery energy-transfer analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention note for KTU:** In a *compressor* (energy added to fluid), $W_s > 0$. In a *turbine* (energy extracted from fluid), $W_s < 0$. The equation itself is sign-neutral; the sign emerges from the actual velocity triangle geometry.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Work for Radial-Bladed Impeller with Axial Entry

**Assumptions (standard KTU problem setup):**
1. Pure axial inlet — $V_{w1} = 0$
2. Radial blades at outlet — $\beta_2 = 90°$ measured from tangential
3. Incompressible or ideal gas behaviour

**Step 1 — Apply axial entry condition to Euler's equation:**
$$W_s = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 \cdot 0 = U_2 V_{w2}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve the outlet velocity triangle. Since $\beta_2 = 90°$, the relative velocity $W_2$ is purely radial (meridional):**
$$W_{w2} = W_2 \sin\beta_2 = W_2 \sin 90° = W_2$$
$$W_{f2} = W_2 \cos\beta_2 = 0$$

Wait — we must be careful. The convention used in KTU is $\beta$ measured from the **tangential direction**. So for a radial blade (perpendicular to tangential), $\beta_2 = 90°$ means $W_2$ has its whirl component $W_{w2} = W_2 \sin\beta_2 = W_2$, which contradicts the radial-blade assumption. **The KTU convention is actually that $\beta$ is measured from the meridional (radial) direction** for relative angles, OR the radial-blade condition is given as $W_2$ being purely meridional with $V_{w2} = U_2$.

**Resolution used in KTU textbooks:** For a radial impeller exit, $W_2$ has no whirl component, hence $V_{w2} = U_2$ (since $V_{w2} = U_2 + W_{w2} = U_2 + 0$).

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_{w2} = U_2$ into Euler's equation:**
$$W_s = U_2 \cdot U_2 = U_2^2$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of rotational speed and impeller diameter:**
$$U_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$
$$W_s = \left(\frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}\right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Slip correction for the real impeller:**
$$W_{s,\text{actual}} = \sigma \cdot U_2^2$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: General Outlet Velocity Triangle (Backward-Curved Blades)

For **backward-curved blades** (commonly used in centrifugal compressors, $\beta_2 < 90°$ from the tangential in KTU convention, with the relative velocity leaving *against* the direction of rotation tangentially), the outlet triangle analysis proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Identify all components at outlet:**
- Blade velocity: $U_2$ (tangential, direction of rotation)
- Relative velocity $W_2$ at angle $\beta_2$ to the tangential
- Absolute velocity $V_2$ at angle $\alpha_2$ to the tangential
- Meridional (flow) component: $V_{f2} = W_{f2}$ (no slip in radial direction)

**Step 2 — Apply the vector closure in tangential and meridional components:**

Tangential direction:
$$V_{w2} = U_2 + W_{w2} = U_2 - W_2 \cos\beta_2$$
*(Note: for backward-curved blades, $W_2$ has a tangential component *opposing* the rotation, hence the negative sign when $\beta_2$ is measured from the tangential in the direction of rotation.)*

Meridional direction:
$$V_{f2} = W_{f2} = W_2 \sin\beta_2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $W_2$ using the continuity-derived meridional velocity:**
$$W_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{\sin\beta_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find $V_{w2}$:**
$$V_{w2} = U_2 - \frac{V_{f2} \cos\beta_2}{\sin\beta_2} = U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Euler's equation to find specific work:**
$$W_s = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} = U_2(U_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2) - U_1 V_{w1}$$

For axial entry ($V_{w1} = 0$):
$$W_s = U_2^2 - U_2 V_{f2} \cot\beta_2$$

This is the **general KTU result for the specific work of a centrifugal compressor stage** with backward-curved blades and axial entry.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pressure Rise Across the Stage

For an adiabatic compression with isentropic efficiency $\eta_c$:
$$W_s = \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} \cdot \eta_c^{-1} \quad \text{(for incompressible-like approximation)}$$

For an ideal gas with significant temperature rise, the stage isentropic relation gives:
$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \left(1 + \frac{\eta_c W_s}{c_p T_1}\right)^{\gamma / (\gamma - 1)}$$

Combining with the slip-corrected Euler equation:
$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \left(1 + \frac{\eta_c \, \sigma \, U_2^2}{c_p T_1}\right)^{\gamma / (\gamma - 1)}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A centrifugal compressor running at 15,000 rpm handles air at the inlet with $V_{f1} = 60$ m/s and $V_{w1} = 0$. The inlet mean diameter is 0.18 m, the outlet diameter is 0.36 m, and the impeller has 18 backward-curved blades with $\beta_2 = 60°$ (from meridional). The radial outflow velocity is $V_{f2} = 50$ m/s. Find: (a) inlet velocity triangle, (b) outlet velocity triangle, (c) ideal specific work, (d) actual specific work using Stodola's slip factor.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate blade velocities:**
$$U_1 = \frac{\pi (0.18)(15000)}{60} = 141.37 \text{ m/s}$$
$$U_2 = \frac{\pi (0.36)(15000)}{60} = 282.74 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Inlet triangle (axial entry, $V_{w1} = 0$):**
- $V_{f1} = 60$ m/s
- $W_{f1} = V_{f1} = 60$ m/s
- $W_{w1} = V_{w1} - U_1 = 0 - 141.37 = -141.37$ m/s
- $W_1 = \sqrt{W_{f1}^2 + W_{w1}^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 141.37^2} = 153.6$ m/s
- $\beta_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{W_{f1}}{|W_{w1}|}\right) = \tan^{-1}(60/141.37) = 23.0°$

**Step 3 — Outlet relative velocity:**
$$W_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{\sin\beta_2} = \frac{50}{\sin 60°} = 57.74 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Outlet absolute whirl component:**
$$V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2 = 282.74 - 50 \cot 60° = 282.74 - 28.87 = 253.87 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Outlet absolute velocity magnitude:**
$$V_2 = \sqrt{V_{f2}^2 + V_{w2}^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + 253.87^2} = 258.75 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Ideal specific work (Euler, no slip):**
$$W_{s,\text{ideal}} = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} = (282.74)(253.87) - (141.37)(0) = 71,773 \text{ J/kg} = 71.77 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Stodola slip factor** (KTU convention: $\beta_2$ here is from meridional, equivalent to $90° - \beta_{2,\text{tangential}}$):
$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos\beta_2}{Z} = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos 60°}{18} = 1 - \frac{\pi (0.5)}{18} = 1 - 0.0873 = 0.9127$$

**Step 8 — Actual specific work:**
$$W_{s,\text{actual}} = \sigma \cdot W_{s,\text{ideal}} = 0.9127 \times 71.77 = 65.50 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

This is the **typical KTU 14-mark problem structure** combining inlet/outlet triangle drawing, Euler's equation, and slip correction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inlet Velocity Triangle (Axial Entry) — Vector Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Origin at Eye Centre] --> B[Vector U1 along tangential direction]
    A --> C[Vector W1 at angle beta1 from negative tangential]
    A --> D[Vector V1 = W1 + U1 purely axial]
    B --> E[U1 tip on tangential axis]
    C --> F[W1 tip at upper-left quadrant]
    D --> G[V1 tip on meridional axis]
    E --> H[Triangle closes: V1 = U1 + W1]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

## 4.2 Outlet Velocity Triangle (Backward-Curved Blades) — Vector Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Origin at Impeller Tip Centre] --> Q[Vector U2 along positive tangential]
    P --> R[Vector W2 at angle beta2 from meridional in the relative frame]
    P --> S[Vector V2 = U2 + W2]
    Q --> T[U2 tip on positive tangential]
    R --> U[W2 tip in upper-half plane]
    S --> V[V2 tip in upper-right quadrant]
    T --> W[Triangle closure: V2 = U2 + W2]
    U --> W
    V --> W
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Centrifugal Compressor Stage

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Inlet Duct - Axial Entry V1] --> B[Pre-rotation Vanes Optional - Controls Vw1]
    B --> C[Impeller Eye - Inlet Velocity Triangle Station 1]
    C --> D[Rotating Impeller - Work Transfer U2 Vw2 - U1 Vw1]
    D --> E[Impeller Tip - Outlet Velocity Triangle Station 2]
    E --> F[Vaneless Diffuser - Converts V2 to Pressure]
    F --> G[Vaned Diffuser - Further Pressure Recovery]
    G --> H[Volute or Collector Casing]
    H --> I[Outlet to Next Stage or Process]
    D --> J[Slip Factor sigma Reduces Actual Vw2]
    J --> E
```

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Comparison: Inlet vs Outlet Triangle Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INLET_STN1[Inlet Station 1 - Eye of Impeller]
        I1[Meridional Vf1 downward into page]
        I2[Blade U1 tangential]
        I3[Relative W1 backward-tilted]
        I4[Absolute V1 = W1 + U1]
    end
    subgraph OUTLET_STN2[Outlet Station 2 - Impeller Tip]
        O1[Meridional Vf2 radially outward]
        O2[Blade U2 tangential larger than U1]
        O3[Relative W2 backward-curved tilt]
        O4[Absolute V2 = U2 + W2 with large Vw2]
    end
    INLET_STN1 --> |Energy Added by Rotation| OUTLET_STN2
```

## 4.5 Text-Based ASCII Velocity Triangle (Printable for Exam Rough Work)

**INLET TRIANGLE (Axial Entry, $V_{w1} = 0$):**
```
              • W1
             /|
            / |
           /  | W_f1 = V_f1
          /   |
         /β1  |
        •-----•----→  U1 (tangential, direction of rotation)
        Origin
              ↑
              V1 (= W1 + U1, purely axial/meridional)
```

**OUTLET TRIANGLE (Backward-Curved, $\beta_2$ from meridional):**
```
                    • V2
                   /|
                  / |
                 /  | V_f2
                /   |
               /α2  |
        •-----•----•----→  U2 (tangential)
        Origin
              \
               \  W2 (backward-curved)
                \
                 • W2_tip
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (Conceptual)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define the term **slip factor** in a centrifugal compressor. State Stodola's formula for slip factor and explain why it is necessary in the work calculation.

**Model Answer:**
The slip factor $\sigma$ is the ratio of the actual whirl velocity at the impeller exit to the ideal whirl velocity assuming perfect fluid adherence to the blades:
$$\sigma = \frac{V_{w2,\text{actual}}}{V_{w2,\text{ideal}}}$$
Stodola's empirical formula is:
$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos\beta_2}{Z}$$
where $Z$ is the number of blades and $\beta_2$ is the blade angle at outlet. It is necessary because real fluid slips relative to the impeller due to relative flow and channel geometry, reducing the actual whirl velocity and hence the specific work output compared to the ideal Euler prediction.

### Question A2 (Definition/Short Derivation)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** State **Euler's turbomachinery equation** and explain the physical significance of each term. What is its simplified form for axial inlet and radial-bladed impeller?

**Model Answer:**
Euler's equation for specific work done by the impeller on the fluid is:
$$W_s = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$
where $U_1, U_2$ are blade (peripheral) velocities at inlet and outlet, and $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ are the whirl (tangential) components of absolute velocity at inlet and outlet. The term $U_2 V_{w2}$ represents the angular momentum flux at outlet and $U_1 V_{w1}$ at inlet; their difference gives the net work done per unit mass. For axial entry ($V_{w1} = 0$) and radial blades ($V_{w2} = U_2$):
$$W_s = U_2^2$$

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Velocity Triangle Construction and Work Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, Modified for Module 4]**

A centrifugal compressor running at **16,000 rpm** handles air at the inlet. The inlet mean diameter is **0.20 m** and the outlet diameter is **0.40 m**. The impeller has **20 backward-curved blades** with outlet blade angle $\beta_2 = 55°$ measured from the meridional direction. The radial outflow velocity is $V_{f2} = 45$ m/s, and the inlet is axial with $V_{f1} = 55$ m/s.

**(a)** Draw the inlet and outlet velocity triangles and determine the blade velocities, relative velocities, and absolute velocities at both stations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the specific work done by the impeller using Euler's equation, the slip factor using Stodola's formula, and the actual specific work delivered to the air. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — Triangle Construction**

**Step 1 — Blade velocities:**
$$U_1 = \frac{\pi (0.20)(16000)}{60} = 167.55 \text{ m/s}$$
$$U_2 = \frac{\pi (0.40)(16000)}{60} = 335.10 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Inlet triangle (axial entry, $V_{w1} = 0$):**
- $V_{f1} = W_{f1} = 55$ m/s
- $W_{w1} = V_{w1} - U_1 = 0 - 167.55 = -167.55$ m/s
- $W_1 = \sqrt{55^2 + 167.55^2} = 176.37$ m/s
- $\beta_1 = \tan^{-1}(55/167.55) = 18.17°$ from tangential
- $V_1 = V_{f1} = 55$ m/s (purely meridional)

**Step 3 — Outlet relative velocity:**
$$W_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{\sin\beta_2} = \frac{45}{\sin 55°} = \frac{45}{0.8192} = 54.93 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Outlet whirl component:**
$$V_{w2} = U_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2 = 335.10 - 45 \cot 55° = 335.10 - 45(0.7002) = 335.10 - 31.51 = 303.59 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Outlet absolute velocity:**
$$V_2 = \sqrt{V_{f2}^2 + V_{w2}^2} = \sqrt{45^2 + 303.59^2} = \sqrt{2025 + 92167} = 306.91 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Outlet absolute flow angle:**
$$\alpha_2 = \tan^{-1}(V_{f2}/V_{w2}) = \tan^{-1}(45/303.59) = 8.43°$$

**Diagram (described):** The inlet triangle is a right triangle with $V_1$ vertical (meridional), $U_1$ horizontal (tangential), and $W_1$ as the hypotenuse in the second quadrant. The outlet triangle has $U_2$ horizontal, $W_2$ at $55°$ from vertical (meridional) in the second quadrant, and $V_2$ as the resultant nearly along the tangential direction.

**(b) Part (b) Solution — Work and Slip Calculation**

**Step 7 — Ideal specific work (Euler):**
$$W_{s,\text{ideal}} = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} = (335.10)(303.59) - (167.55)(0) = 101,719 \text{ J/kg} \approx 101.72 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8 — Stodola's slip factor** (using $\beta_2$ from meridional; in KTU convention this is equivalent to $\cos\beta_2$ for the Stodola formula):
$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos\beta_2}{Z} = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos 55°}{20} = 1 - \frac{\pi (0.5736)}{20} = 1 - 0.0901 = 0.9099$$

**Step 9 — Actual whirl velocity:**
$$V_{w2,\text{actual}} = \sigma \cdot U_2 = 0.9099 \times 335.10 = 304.91 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 10 — Actual specific work:**
$$W_{s,\text{actual}} = U_2 V_{w2,\text{actual}} = 335.10 \times 304.91 = 102,171 \text{ J/kg} \approx 102.17 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Refinement using direct Stodola:** When the slip factor is applied to the entire Euler work term, the actual work is $W_{s,\text{actual}} = \sigma \cdot W_{s,\text{ideal}} = 0.9099 \times 101.72 = 92.56$ kJ/kg. **KTU accepts either methodology** provided the working is shown clearly. The most common approach is the latter (multiply final work by $\sigma$).

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Slip Factor Deep Dive and Diffuser Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model, Adapted]**

A centrifugal compressor has an impeller with **24 blades** and outlet blade angle $\beta_2 = 65°$ from the meridional direction. The impeller tip diameter is **0.45 m** and runs at **12,000 rpm**. The air enters axially at the inlet (mean diameter 0.22 m) with $V_{f1} = 50$ m/s, and leaves with $V_{f2} = 48$ m/s.

**(a)** Using Stodola's formula, calculate the slip factor. Compare the result with Wiesner's approximation. Discuss why slip occurs in a real impeller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the specific work assuming (i) no slip, (ii) Stodola's slip, and (iii) Wiesner's slip. Comment on the design implications for high-pressure-ratio centrifugal compressors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — Slip Factor Calculation**

**Step 1 — Stodola's slip factor:**
$$\sigma_{\text{Stodola}} = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos 65°}{24} = 1 - \frac{\pi (0.4226)}{24} = 1 - 0.0553 = 0.9447$$

**Step 2 — Wiesner's approximation:**
$$\sigma_{\text{Wiesner}} = 1 - \frac{0.63\pi}{24} = 1 - \frac{1.979}{24} = 1 - 0.0825 = 0.9175$$

**Step 3 — Physical reason for slip:**
Slip occurs because the fluid cannot follow the blade curvature exactly due to:
- **Relative flow** — the fluid has inertia in the relative frame and tends to continue along its incoming path.
- **Finite number of blades** — leaving passages between blades where the fluid experiences pressure-driven cross-flow.
- **Frictional losses** at the blade surfaces.
- **Passage geometry** (curvature and area changes) inducing secondary flows.

The result is that the actual relative velocity $W_2$ has a slightly different direction than the blade angle, causing the absolute whirl $V_{w2}$ to fall below the ideal $U_2$.

**(b) Part (b) — Three Work Calculations**

**Step 4 — Blade velocities:**
$$U_1 = \frac{\pi (0.22)(12000)}{60} = 138.23 \text{ m/s}$$
$$U_2 = \frac{\pi (0.45)(12000)}{60} = 282.74 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Outlet whirl (no slip, ideal radial exit with $V_{w2}=U_2$):**
$$V_{w2,\text{ideal}} = U_2 - V_{f2}\cot 65° = 282.74 - 48(0.4663) = 282.74 - 22.38 = 260.36 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Specific works:**
- (i) **No slip:** $W_{s,\text{no slip}} = U_2 V_{w2,\text{ideal}} = 282.74 \times 260.36 = 73,617$ J/kg $\approx 73.62$ kJ/kg
- (ii) **Stodola:** $W_{s,\text{Stodola}} = 0.9447 \times 73.62 = 69.55$ kJ/kg
- (iii) **Wiesner:** $W_{s,\text{Wiesner}} = 0.9175 \times 73.62 = 67.55$ kJ/kg

**Step 7 — Design implication:**
For high-pressure-ratio centrifugal compressors, slip directly reduces the pressure rise per stage. Designers compensate by:
- Increasing rotational speed (limited by material strength and tip-speed limits).
- Using more blades (Z) to reduce slip.
- Selecting optimal blade angles (typically $\beta_2$ between 30°–60° from meridional).
- Adding diffuser stages to recover kinetic energy as pressure.

Wiesner's approximation is more conservative (predicts lower work) and is preferred for preliminary conservative designs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Angle convention confusion:** KTU measures $\beta$ from the meridional direction for relative angles. Students from other universities may use tangential convention — **read the question carefully** and state your convention explicitly.
> 2. **Sign of $W_{w1}$:** For axial entry, $W_{w1} = -U_1$ (negative). Some students forget the negative sign and get the wrong relative whirl.
> 3. **Slip factor formula selection:** Using Stodola when $\beta_2$ is given from meridional requires $\cos\beta_2$ in the formula — students sometimes erroneously use the value from tangential, leading to wrong slip factors.
> 4. **Units consistency:** Rotational speed in **rpm** must be converted to **rad/s** via $N\pi/30$ when computing $U$. Alternatively, $U = \pi D N / 60$ gives m/s directly. Mixing up the two is a common error.
> 5. **Forgetting the slip correction:** Computing only the *ideal* work without applying $\sigma$ results in **3-mark penalty** in KTU 14-mark problems.
> 6. **Drawing the triangle incorrectly:** A clear, scaled, labelled diagram with all angles and vectors marked is **mandatory** for full marks in 14-mark questions. An incorrect or absent diagram loses 2–3 marks even if calculations are right.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A centrifugal compressor **velocity triangle** is the vector diagram showing $\vec{V} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ at impeller inlet (station 1) and outlet (station 2).
- [x] Blade velocity: $U = \pi D N / 60$ (m/s, with $D$ in m, $N$ in rpm).
- [x] **Euler's equation** is universal: $W_s = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$.
- [x] For **axial inlet** + **radial blades**: $W_s = U_2^2$ (the famous result).
- [x] For **backward-curved blades** + axial inlet: $W_s = U_2^2 - U_2 V_{f2} \cot\beta_2$.
- [x] **Stodola's slip factor:** $\sigma = 1 - (\pi \cos\beta_2) / Z$.
- [x] **Wiesner's slip factor:** $\sigma = 1 - 0.63\pi / Z$ (for radial blades, quick estimate).
- [x] **KTU angle convention:** $\alpha$ and $\beta$ measured from the meridional direction in some textbooks, from the tangential in others — **always state the convention explicitly in the solution**.
- [x] **Flow component** is identical in absolute and relative frames: $V_f = W_f$.
- [x] **Pre-whirl** ($V_{w1} \neq 0$) reduces work for a compressor — designers usually aim for axial or near-axial entry.
- [x] **Slip** arises from relative flow, finite blade count, friction, and passage geometry.
- [x] **Diffuser design** (vaneless/vaned) recovers the kinetic energy of $V_2$ as static pressure rise.
- [x] A **clear, well-labelled velocity triangle diagram** earns 2–3 bonus marks in 14-mark KTU questions.
- [x] **Meridional direction** is the direction of bulk flow (axial at inlet, radial at outlet for a centrifugal impeller).
- [x] **Whirl component** is tangential to the impeller rotation circle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slip Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: SLIP FACTOR IN CENTRIFUGAL MACHINES

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **SLIP FACTOR (σ) — KTU 2024 Canonical Definition**
> *"The slip factor is defined as the ratio of the actual whirl component of absolute velocity at the impeller exit to the theoretical whirl component of absolute velocity at the impeller exit, assuming an infinite number of blades (i.e., a frictionless, guided flow). It is a dimensionless correction factor that accounts for the finite number of blades in a centrifugal impeller."*

Mathematically:

$$\sigma = \frac{V_{w2,\text{actual}}}{V_{w2,\text{ideal, }\infty\text{ blades}}}$$

In Euler's turbomachinery equation, the theoretical head/energy transfer is corrected by the slip factor:

$$W_{\text{actual}} = \sigma \cdot U_2 \cdot V_{w2,\text{ideal}}$$

When the inlet is pre-whirl free ($V_{w1} = 0$), the Euler work simplifies dramatically:

$$W = \sigma \cdot U_2^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board Exam Insight:** The slip factor is **always less than unity** ($\sigma < 1$) because real flow cannot be guided by infinite blades. Therefore, the *actual* work done by a centrifugal impeller is **always less** than the ideal Euler work. Typical values lie between **0.85 and 0.95** for well-designed industrial impellers.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shopping Cart Wheel"

Imagine pushing a 4-wheeled shopping cart versus a 20-wheeled train of carts across a parking lot with curved rails. With infinite wheels (a continuous rail), every parcel of "fluid" follows the curved path perfectly. But with a finite number of wheels, the fluid parcel between wheels drifts outward due to inertia — it cannot make the sharp turn dictated by the blade. **The fluid slips relative to the blade angle.** This drift is the *slip*.

In a real impeller, the relative flow does not exit at the designed blade angle $\beta_2$; instead, it exits at a smaller flow angle $\alpha_2$ (often called $\beta_2'$). The difference is the **deviation angle** $\delta = \beta_2 - \alpha_2$.

---

## 2. Physical Origin of Slip — Why Does It Happen?

| Cause | Physical Mechanism | Effect on Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Finite blade number | Relative eddies (secondary flow) form in blade passages | Reduces effective whirl velocity |
| Pressure gradient | Pressure side (convex) has higher pressure than suction side | Causes cross-flow & deviation |
| Boundary layer | Friction retards flow near blades | Decreases effective turning |
| Inlet pre-whirl | Angular momentum exchange at inlet | Modifies effective slip |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "PFB"** — *Pressure, Friction, and Boundedness* are the three physical causes of slip.

---

## 3. Velocity Triangle with and without Slip

**With infinite blades (ideal):** The flow perfectly follows the blade angle $\beta_2$ at exit, giving $V_{w2,\infty} = U_2$ (when $V_{w1} = 0$).

**With finite blades (real):** The relative whirl leaves the impeller tangentially displaced. From the exit velocity triangle, using the deviation angle $\delta$:

$$V_{w2,\text{actual}} = U_2 - V_{f2}\tan\delta$$

For a radial exit blade ($\beta_2 = 90°$):

$$V_{w2,\text{actual}} = U_2 - V_{f2}\tan\delta$$

The slip factor in its general geometric form is therefore:

$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{V_{f2}}{U_2}\tan\delta$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Euler's Ideal Equation (∞ blades):**
The energy transfer per unit mass in the absence of slip:

$$W_{\text{ideal}} = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$

For $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$W_{\text{ideal}} = U_2 V_{w2} = U_2^2$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Slip Correction:**
Replace $V_{w2}$ with $\sigma \cdot V_{w2,\text{ideal}}$:

$$W_{\text{actual}} = \sigma \cdot U_2 \cdot V_{w2,\text{ideal}}$$

**Step 3 — Deviation Angle Approach:**
From the velocity triangle:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{U_2 - V_{w2,\text{actual}}}{V_{f2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{V_{f2}}{U_2}\tan\delta$$

**Step 4 — Physical Slip Factor Correlations:**
The deviation angle $\delta$ is empirically correlated to blade count, blade angle, and flow angle, giving rise to the famous slip factor models (Stodola, Pfleiderer, Wiesner, Stanitz, etc.).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-grade slip factor correlations**. Memorize Stodola, Pfleiderer, and Wiesner as the three highest-weight formulas.

| # | Author(s) | Slip Factor Formula | Applicable Range / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stodola (1915)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{\pi}{N}\sin\beta_2 \cdot \cot\alpha_2$ | First classical model; for radial/forward blades |
| 2 | **Pfleiderer (1939)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{0.6}{N}\cos\beta_2 \cdot \cot\alpha_2$ | Most widely used; best for $\beta_2 \in (50°, 90°)$ |
| 3 | **Carter / Eules (1940s)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{0.63\pi}{N}\dfrac{\cos\beta_2}{\sin\alpha_2}$ | Modified Pfleiderer with $\pi$ factor |
| 4 | **Balje (1950s)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{1.2}{N}\dfrac{\cos\beta_2}{\sin\alpha_2}$ | Extended blade range |
| 5 | **Stanitz (1952)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{1.2}{N}$ | Incompressible radial blades only |
