| 6 | **Wiesner (1967)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{0.63\pi \cdot \cos\beta_2}{N \cdot \left(1 - \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2\right)}$ | Includes hub-tip ratio correction |
| 7 | **Busemann (1952)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\cos\alpha_2}\left[1 - \dfrac{\pi}{N}\cos\alpha_2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{0.5}\right]$ | Includes hub effect; good for low blade numbers |
| 8 | **Toyokura** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{\pi}{N}\dfrac{\cos\beta_2}{\cos\alpha_2}$ | Empirical; low N |
| 9 | **Senoo** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{0.63\pi}{N}\left(1 - \sin^2\alpha_2\right)$ | Best for high specific speed |
| 10 | **Coppage / Oh (1950s)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{0.95}{N^{0.85}}$ | High N approximation |
| 11 | **Wiesner Simplified** | $\sigma = \dfrac{\sqrt{\cos\alpha_2}}{N^{0.05}}$ | Quick estimation |

**Symbols Used:**
- $N$ = number of impeller blades (impeller vane count)
- $\beta_2$ = impeller blade angle at exit (measured from tangential direction)
- $\alpha_2$ = absolute flow angle at exit (from tangential)
- $r_1, r_2$ = hub and tip radii
- $U_2$ = blade tip speed
- $V_{f2}$ = flow (radial) velocity at exit

---

## 2.3 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> The slip factor directly governs the **head-flow characteristic** of a centrifugal pump/compressor. A 5% reduction in $\sigma$ translates to a **5% loss in head** for a given impeller geometry. Designers manipulate blade count, blade exit angle, and tip diameter to compensate for slip and match design-point performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS

## 3.1 Derivation of Slip Factor from Velocity Triangle

**Given:** A centrifugal impeller rotating at blade tip speed $U_2$ with $N$ blades, exit blade angle $\beta_2$, and exit flow angle $\alpha_2$.

**Step 1:** Construct the exit velocity triangle for finite blades.
At impeller exit, three velocities exist: $U_2$ (tangential/blade), $V_{r2}$ (relative), and $V_2$ (absolute). The flow leaves the impeller along the flow angle $\alpha_2$, not the blade angle $\beta_2$.

**Step 2:** From the velocity triangle geometry, decompose the relative velocity $V_{r2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{w2,\text{relative}} &= V_{r2}\cos\alpha_2 \\
V_{f2} &= V_{r2}\sin\alpha_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** The relative velocity's tangential component is related to slip. The "ideal" tangential component (if flow perfectly followed the blade) would be:

$$V_{w2,\text{relative, ideal}} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$$

**Step 4:** The deviation angle $\delta$ is the angular slip:

$$\delta = \beta_2 - \alpha_2$$

**Step 5:** Express actual whirl in terms of deviation:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{w2,\text{actual}} &= U_2 - V_{f2}\tan\delta \\
&= U_2 - V_{r2}\sin\alpha_2 \tan(\beta_2 - \alpha_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by the ideal value $U_2$ (for $V_{w1} = 0$):

$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{V_{r2}\sin\alpha_2}{U_2}\tan(\beta_2 - \alpha_2)$$

**Step 7:** For the limiting case of radial exit blades ($\beta_2 = 90°$):

$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{V_{f2}}{U_2}\tan\delta = 1 - \frac{\pi}{N}\cdot(\text{geometric correction})$$

This last step, when carried through with the assumption that the relative eddy rotates at half the impeller speed, yields **Stodola's formula**:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{Stodola}} = 1 - \frac{\pi}{N}\sin\beta_2\cot\alpha_2}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:**
A centrifugal pump impeller has **$N = 7$ blades**, exit blade angle **$\beta_2 = 60°$**, and exit flow angle **$\alpha_2 = 30°$**. The impeller tip speed is $U_2 = 25$ m/s, and the flow velocity at exit is $V_{f2} = 6$ m/s. Calculate:
- (a) The slip factor using **Pfleiderer's correlation**
- (b) The actual work done per unit mass, assuming $V_{w1} = 0$

**Solution:**

**(a) Pfleiderer's Slip Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{Pfl}} &= 1 - \frac{0.6}{N}\cos\beta_2\cot\alpha_2 \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6}{7}\cos 60°\cdot\cot 30° \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6}{7}\cdot(0.5)\cdot\left(\frac{1}{\tan 30°}\right) \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6}{7}\cdot(0.5)\cdot(1.7321) \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1.7321}{7} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.5196}{7} \\
&= 1 - 0.0742 \\
\sigma_{\text{Pfl}} &= 0.9258
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Actual Work Done:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{actual}} &= \sigma \cdot U_2^2 \quad (\text{since } V_{w1} = 0) \\
&= 0.9258 \cdot (25)^2 \\
&= 0.9258 \cdot 625 \\
W_{\text{actual}} &= 578.6 \; \text{J/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Cross-check with Stodola's correlation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{Stodola}} &= 1 - \frac{\pi}{7}\sin 60°\cot 30° \\
&= 1 - \frac{\pi}{7}\cdot(0.8660)\cdot(1.7321) \\
&= 1 - \frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.8660 \cdot 1.7321}{7} \\
&= 1 - \frac{4.7124}{7} \\
&= 1 - 0.6732 \\
\sigma_{\text{Stodola}} &= 0.3268 \quad (\text{Unrealistic — Stodola fails for low } N \text{ or } \alpha_2)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often blindly apply Stodola's formula and get nonsensical values. **Pfleiderer is the safe default** unless the problem specifically mandates another correlation. Always sanity-check: $\sigma \in [0.7, 0.98]$ for well-designed impellers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Correlation

| Scenario | Recommended Slip Factor Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Radial blade tip, low N (5–9) | **Pfleiderer / Carter** | Stodola underestimates; Pfleiderer is calibrated |
| Backward curved, $N \geq 9$ | **Wiesner** | Includes hub correction, accurate |
| Forward curved blades | **Balje / Eules** | Higher-order geometric terms matter |
| Quick design estimate | **Stanitz** ($\sigma = 1 - 1.2/N$) | Simple, radial-tip only |
| High specific speed, mixed flow | **Senoo** | Captures non-radial effects |
| Industrial standard | **Pfleiderer (c=0.6)** | Most widely accepted industry default |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Velocity Triangle Schematic (Finite vs Infinite Blades)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Centrifugal Impeller Outlet] --> B{Number of Blades}
    B -- Infinite N --> C[Flow follows blade angle beta2]
    B -- Finite N --> D[Flow deviates; exits at alpha2 less than beta2]
    C --> E[Ideal Whirl Vw2 equals U2]
    D --> F[Actual Whirl Vw2 equals sigma times U2]
    E --> G[Ideal Work W equals U2 squared]
    F --> H[Actual Work W equals sigma times U2 squared]
    G --> I[Sigma equals 1 in ideal case]
    F --> J[Sigma less than 1; represents losses]
    I --> K[No slip; relative eddies vanish]
    J --> L[Relative eddies form; deviation angle delta exists]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Slip Factor Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        P1[Number of Blades N]
        P2[Exit Blade Angle beta2]
        P3[Exit Flow Angle alpha2]
        P4[Hub-Tip Ratio r1 by r2]
    end
    subgraph CORRELATIONS[SLIP FACTOR CORRELATIONS]
        M1[Stodola 1915]
        M2[Pfleiderer 1939]
        M3[Wiesner 1967]
        M4[Stanitz 1952]
        M5[Busemann 1952]
        M6[Balje 1950s]
        M7[Senoo]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT]
        O1[Slip Factor sigma]
        O2[Actual Work Done W]
        O3[Head Correction Delta H]
    end
    P1 --> M1
    P1 --> M2
    P1 --> M3
    P1 --> M4
    P1 --> M5
    P2 --> M1
    P2 --> M2
    P2 --> M3
    P2 --> M6
    P3 --> M2
    P3 --> M3
    P3 --> M6
    P3 --> M7
    P4 --> M3
    P4 --> M5
    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O1
    M3 --> O1
    M4 --> O1
    M5 --> O1
    M6 --> O1
    M7 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow: How Slip Affects Pump Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Blade Geometry beta2 and N] --> B[Relative Eddy Formation]
    B --> C[Deviation Angle delta]
    C --> D[Reduced Whirl Vw2]
    D --> E[Slip Factor sigma less than 1]
    E --> F[Euler Head Reduced]
    F --> G[Head Flow Curve Shifts Downward]
    G --> H[Power Consumption Affected]
    H --> I[Efficiency Reduced]
    I --> J[Design Iteration to Match Duty Point]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Choosing Slip Factor Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Slip Factor Model] --> B{Blade exit angle beta2 equals 90 degrees?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Number of blades N known precisely?}
    B -- No --> D{Backward or Forward Curved?}
    C -- Yes --> E{Industrial design or Academic?}
    C -- No --> F[Use Stanitz Approximation]
    E -- Industrial --> G[Use Stanitz 1 minus 1.2 by N]
    E -- Academic --> H[Use Pfleiderer 1 minus 0.6 by N]
    D -- Backward --> I[Use Pfleiderer default]
    D -- Forward --> J[Use Balje or Eules]
    I --> K[Final Slip Factor sigma]
    J --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    F --> K
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 KTU 2024 EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define slip factor. Why is it always less than unity?**

**Model Answer:**
The slip factor is the ratio of the actual whirl velocity at the impeller exit to the theoretical whirl velocity assuming an infinite number of blades:

$$\sigma = \frac{V_{w2,\text{actual}}}{V_{w2,\text{ideal}}}$$

It is always less than unity because, in a real impeller with a finite number of blades, the fluid cannot be perfectly guided by the blades. Relative eddies form in the flow passages, and the pressure gradient between the pressure and suction sides of the blades causes the fluid to deviate from the designed blade angle, reducing the effective whirl velocity component. Typical values range from 0.85 to 0.95.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Pfleiderer's slip factor correlation. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**
Pfleiderer's slip factor is given by:

$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{0.6}{N}\cos\beta_2\cot\alpha_2$$

where $N$ is the number of blades, $\beta_2$ is the exit blade angle, and $\alpha_2$ is the exit flow angle. **Significance:** It is the most widely used correlation in industry because it accounts for both the blade number and the geometric angles, gives realistic values across a wide range of designs, and is the de facto standard for centrifugal pump and compressor preliminary design.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the slip factor of a centrifugal impeller from first principles using the velocity triangle. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump has the following specifications:
- Number of blades, $N = 6$
- Exit blade angle, $\beta_2 = 45°$
- Exit flow angle, $\alpha_2 = 25°$
- Tip speed, $U_2 = 30$ m/s
- Inlet whirl velocity, $V_{w1} = 0$

Calculate the slip factor using **(i)** Stodola's formula and **(ii)** Pfleiderer's formula. Comment on which value is more realistic. Also compute the actual work done per unit mass using the more realistic value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.1 above. The complete derivation yields the general form:

$$\sigma = 1 - \frac{V_{f2}}{U_2}\tan(\beta_2 - \alpha_2)$$

For a radial exit blade ($\beta_2 = 90°$) and assuming the relative eddy rotates at half the impeller angular velocity, integration over the blade passage gives Stodola's classical result:

$$\sigma_{\text{Stodola}} = 1 - \frac{\pi}{N}\sin\beta_2\cot\alpha_2$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Stodola's Slip Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{Stodola}} &= 1 - \frac{\pi}{6}\sin 45°\cot 25° \\
&= 1 - \frac{\pi}{6}\cdot(0.7071)\cdot\left(\frac{1}{0.4663}\right) \\
&= 1 - \frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.7071}{6 \cdot 0.4663} \\
&= 1 - \frac{2.2214}{2.7978} \\
&= 1 - 0.7940 \\
\sigma_{\text{Stodola}} &= 0.2060
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Pfleiderer's Slip Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{Pfleiderer}} &= 1 - \frac{0.6}{6}\cos 45°\cot 25° \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6}{6}\cdot(0.7071)\cdot(2.1445) \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6 \cdot 0.7071 \cdot 2.1445}{6} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.9099}{6} \\
&= 1 - 0.1517 \\
\sigma_{\text{Pfleiderer}} &= 0.8483
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** Pfleiderer's value (0.848) is far more realistic than Stodola's (0.206). Stodola's formula is known to under-predict the slip factor for low blade numbers and small flow angles, often giving non-physical results. **Pfleiderer is the preferred correlation** for this configuration.

**Actual Work Done (using Pfleiderer):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{actual}} &= \sigma \cdot U_2^2 \\
&= 0.8483 \cdot (30)^2 \\
&= 0.8483 \cdot 900 \\
W_{\text{actual}} &= 763.5 \; \text{J/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the physical reasons for slip in a centrifugal impeller. Discuss how blade number, blade angle, and hub-to-tip ratio affect the slip factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal compressor impeller with **$N = 12$ blades** and **$\beta_2 = 30°$** operates at an exit flow angle of **$\alpha_2 = 20°$**. Using **Wiesner's correlation with hub-to-tip ratio $r_1/r_2 = 0.4$**, calculate the slip factor. Compare with **Pfleiderer's value** and discuss the deviation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Physical Reasons:**

The slip in a centrifugal impeller arises from three main physical causes:

1. **Finite Number of Blades (Relative Eddies):** With infinite blades, the flow is perfectly guided. With finite blades, the relative fluid in the passages cannot make the sharp turn required at the blade exit, and a relative eddy forms. This eddy rotates in a sense opposite to the impeller, reducing the effective whirl velocity.

2. **Pressure Difference Across Blades:** The pressure side (convex surface) has higher pressure than the suction side. This pressure gradient drives a cross-flow from pressure to suction side, reducing the effective turning of the flow.

3. **Boundary Layer and Friction:** Near the blade surfaces, viscous effects cause additional flow retardation.

**Effects of Design Parameters:**

- **Blade Number ($N$):** Increasing $N$ reduces slip. As $N \to \infty$, $\sigma \to 1$. Typical industrial $N$ ranges from 5 to 25.
- **Blade Angle ($\beta_2$):** Higher $\beta_2$ (more radial) generally increases slip because flow must turn through a larger angle.
- **Hub-to-Tip Ratio ($r_1/r_2$):** Lower $r_1/r_2$ (larger hub) reduces the effective slip factor by shortening the blade passage length, as captured in Wiesner's correlation.

**Part (b) — Wiesner's Slip Factor:**

$$\sigma_{\text{Wiesner}} = 1 - \frac{0.63\pi \cdot \cos\beta_2}{N \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2\right]}$$

Substituting values:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{Wiesner}} &= 1 - \frac{0.63\pi \cdot \cos 30°}{12 \cdot \left[1 - (0.4)^2\right]} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.63 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 0.8660}{12 \cdot \left[1 - 0.16\right]} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1.7137}{12 \cdot 0.84} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1.7137}{10.08} \\
&= 1 - 0.1700 \\
\sigma_{\text{Wiesner}} &= 0.8300
\end{aligned}$$

**Pfleiderer's Value (for comparison):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{Pfleiderer}} &= 1 - \frac{0.6}{12}\cos 30°\cot 20° \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6}{12}\cdot(0.8660)\cdot(2.7475) \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.6 \cdot 0.8660 \cdot 2.7475}{12} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1.4275}{12} \\
&= 1 - 0.1190 \\
\sigma_{\text{Pfleiderer}} &= 0.8810
\end{aligned}$$

**Discussion of Deviation:**
Wiesner's value (0.830) is lower than Pfleiderer's (0.881) — a difference of about 5.8%. This deviation arises because Wiesner accounts for the **hub-to-tip ratio** ($r_1/r_2 = 0.4$), recognizing that a finite hub reduces the effective blade length and the flow's ability to be turned. Pfleiderer assumes an idealized geometry and thus overestimates $\sigma$. For modern high-speed compressors with significant hub effects, **Wiesner is the more accurate choice**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Slip Factor Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Angles MUST be in degrees for trigonometric functions unless the calculator is in radian mode. KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark for angular mode errors.
> 2. **Wrong Correlation Application:** Using Stodola for low-N (5–7) or small $\alpha_2$ (< 30°) gives nonsense. **Default to Pfleiderer unless told otherwise.**
> 3. **Forgetting to Apply Slip to Euler's Equation:** Students compute $\sigma$ correctly but then write $W = U_2 V_{w2}$ using the ideal value, ignoring the slip correction. This costs up to 3 marks.
> 4. **Not Stating the Assumption $V_{w1} = 0$:** When simplifying $W = \sigma U_2^2$, you MUST explicitly state that the inlet is pre-whirl free.
> 5. **Rounding Early:** Carry at least 4 decimal places through intermediate steps to avoid propagation errors.
> 6. **No Units for $\sigma$:** Always state that slip factor is **dimensionless**.

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slip Factor — Key Takeaways for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\sigma = V_{w2,\text{actual}} / V_{w2,\text{ideal}}$; always less than 1.
- ✅ **Physical Origin:** Finite blade number → relative eddies → deviation angle $\delta$.
- ✅ **Euler Equation with Slip:** $W = \sigma U_2 V_{w2,\text{ideal}}$; simplifies to $W = \sigma U_2^2$ when $V_{w1} = 0$.
- ✅ **Stodola:** $\sigma = 1 - (\pi/N)\sin\beta_2\cot\alpha_2$ — first classical, but unreliable for low N.
- ✅ **Pfleiderer:** $\sigma = 1 - (0.6/N)\cos\beta_2\cot\alpha_2$ — **industrial default**, most tested.
- ✅ **Wiesner:** Includes hub-tip ratio $(1 - (r_1/r_2)^2)$ correction — best for compressors.
- ✅ **Stanitz:** $\sigma = 1 - 1.2/N$ — quick estimate for radial-tip blades only.
- ✅ **Busemann:** Includes $(r_1/r_2)^{0.5}$ factor — accurate for low-N designs.
- ✅ **Validity Range:** $\sigma \in [0.7, 0.98]$ for well-designed industrial impellers.
- ✅ **Engineering Impact:** 5% slip loss = 5% head loss; directly affects pump/compressor selection.
- ✅ **Increase $\sigma$ by:** Increasing $N$, reducing $\beta_2$ (more axial), or increasing hub size.
- ✅ **Default Strategy for Exams:** If unsure, use **Pfleiderer with $c = 0.6$**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — KTU PEMET411 Module 4: Slip Factor**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme OBE guidelines. Aligned with CO4 and Bloom's L3–L5 cognitive levels.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Input Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Power Input Factor # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Power Input Factor** (denoted by the Greek letter $\tau$ or sometimes $K_{PIF}$) is a non-dimensional performance parameter used in the thermodynamic analysis of compressors (axial-flow and centrifugal). It is defined as the **ratio of the actual power supplied to the compressor to the power that would have been required if the compression process had been carried out isentropically between the same inlet and delivery pressures**. Mathematically, the Power Input Factor is expressed as: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{isentropic}}$$ Where: - $P_{actual}$ = Actual power supplied to the compressor shaft (accounting for all real-world irreversibilities, internal losses, and mechanical friction). - $P_{isentropic}$ = Theoretical isentropic power required to achieve the same pressure rise ($P_2 - P_1$) under ideal, frictionless, adiabatic conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Power Input Factor is the standard tool used to evaluate compressor efficiency. When $\tau_{PIF} = 1$, the compressor operates in an idealized, fully reversible manner (an isentropic process). Any value **greater than 1** indicates the presence of thermodynamic irreversibilities, friction, turbulence, and other losses that demand *more* power input to achieve the same pressure ratio. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stair-Climber" Intuition Imagine two people climbing from the ground floor to the 5th floor of a building (the same pressure rise). - **Person A (Isentropic/Ideal):** Takes a perfectly working elevator, which converts all electrical energy directly into gravitational potential energy without waste. - **Person B (Real Compressor):** Climbs the stairs while carrying a heavy backpack, breathing heavily, sweating, and losing energy to friction in their joints and the air resistance on the way up. They consume far more energy to reach the **same height** (same pressure rise). The **ratio of Person B's energy consumption to Person A's energy consumption** is exactly the **Power Input Factor**. It quantifies how much "extra" energy the real-world process demands compared to the theoretical best case. ## 1.3 Physical Significance - **Magnitude:** $\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$ always. - **Efficiency Linkage:** It is the reciprocal of the **isentropic (adiabatic) efficiency** of the compressor, i.e., $\tau_{PIF} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{isen}}$. - **Design Goal:** Compressor designers strive to bring $\tau_{PIF}$ as close to **1.0** as possible by minimizing internal friction, blade profile losses, clearance losses, and shock losses. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** A modern well-designed axial compressor typically operates at $\tau_{PIF}$ values between **1.03 and 1.08** in multistage configurations. Values significantly above this indicate poor design or operational degradation (fouling, damaged blades, seal leakage). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — The "Why" and "How" The Power Input Factor emerges directly from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to an open system (control volume) representing the compressor. **Step 1 — Ideal (Isentropic) Process:** For an adiabatic, reversible compression from inlet state 1 to exit pressure $P_2$, the minimum work per unit mass is: $$w_{isen} = c_p \cdot (T_{2s} - T_1)$$ This requires the **isentropic temperature** $T_{2s}$ corresponding to the pressure ratio $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$. **Step 2 — Actual Process:** In a real compressor, due to irreversibilities, the actual exit temperature $T_2$ is **higher** than $T_{2s}$. The actual work per unit mass is: $$w_{actual} = c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$ **Step 3 — Defining the Factor:** Since the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is the same in both cases, the power ratio becomes a work ratio: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{w_{actual}}{w_{isen}} = \frac{c_p (T_2 - T_1)}{c_p (T_{2s} - T_1)} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1}$$ ## 2.2 Connection to Isentropic Efficiency The isentropic efficiency of a compressor is defined as: $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$ Inverting this relation reveals the fundamental duality: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{1}{\eta_{isen}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A problem may give you the isentropic efficiency and ask for the Power Input Factor, or vice versa. This single line is the bridge. Memorize it. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary Condition | Typical Units | |---|---------|----------------|--------------------|---------------| | 1 | $\tau_{PIF} = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{isentropic}}$ | Ratio of actual to isentropic power | $\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | $\tau_{PIF} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1}$ | In terms of stagnation temperatures | $T_2 \geq T_{2s}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | $\tau_{PIF} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{isen}}$ | Reciprocal of compressor efficiency | $0 < \eta_{isen} \leq 1$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | $P_{actual} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | Actual power input (Steady Flow Energy Equation) | Steady state, adiabatic | W (Watts) or kW | | 5 | $P_{isentropic} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{2s} - T_1)$ | Isentropic power input | Reversible, adiabatic | W (Watts) or kW | | 6 | $\dfrac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | Isentropic temperature–pressure relation | $\gamma = c_p/c_v$ | Dimensionless ratio | | 7 | $\tau_{PIF} = \dfrac{\log(r_p)}{\log(r_p) - \log(\eta_{poly})}$ | In terms of pressure ratio $r_p$ and polytropic efficiency $\eta_{poly}$ | Multistage compressors | Dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Utility:** Aerospace and gas turbine engineers use $\tau_{PIF}$ during preliminary compressor sizing to estimate shaft power requirements. A 5% deviation from ideal ($\tau_{PIF} = 1.05$) on a 50 MW industrial compressor translates to a **2.5 MW loss** — enough to power 2,500 homes. This is why PIF is a critical design metric. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From First Principles to $\tau_{PIF}$ We derive the Power Input Factor by applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to a control volume enclosing the compressor, and then comparing the real and ideal work requirements. ### Derivation Step 1 — Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) For a steady-flow compressor (neglecting kinetic energy and potential energy changes, and assuming adiabatic operation), the SFEE reduces to: $$w_{shaft} = h_2 - h_1$$ Where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are the specific enthalpies at compressor inlet and exit, respectively. For an ideal gas with constant specific heat: $$w_{shaft} = c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$ This is the **actual specific work** done by the compressor per kilogram of fluid. ### Derivation Step 2 — Ideal (Isentropic) Work For the same inlet state ($P_1, T_1$) and the same exit pressure ($P_2$), the isentropic exit temperature $T_{2s}$ is found from the isentropic relation: $$\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ The isentropic work is therefore: $$w_{isen} = c_p \cdot (T_{2s} - T_1)$$ ### Derivation Step 3 — Forming the Ratio The Power Input Factor is, by definition, the ratio of actual to isentropic power. Since $\dot{m}$ cancels: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{w_{actual}}{w_{isen}}$$ Substituting the expressions derived above: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{c_p \cdot (T_{2s} - T_1)}$$ The $c_p$ terms cancel cleanly: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1}$$ This is the **fundamental working equation** for PIF in compressor analysis. ### Derivation Step 4 — Linking to Isentropic Efficiency By the definition of isentropic efficiency for a compressor (ratio of minimum ideal work to actual work): $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$ Taking the reciprocal: $$\frac{1}{\eta_{isen}} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1} = \tau_{PIF}$$ Therefore, the elegant closed-form relation is established: $$\boxed{\tau_{PIF} = \frac{1}{\eta_{isen}}}$$ ### Derivation Step 5 — Polytropic Form (for Multistage Compressors) For a multistage compressor where each stage obeys a polytropic process $PV^n = \text{constant}$, the relation between $\tau_{PIF}$, the overall pressure ratio $r_p = P_2/P_1$, and the polytropic efficiency $\eta_{poly}$ is: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{\log(r_p)}{\log(r_p) - \log(\eta_{poly})}$$ **Proof outline:** - The polytropic temperature relation: $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = (r_p)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$, where $n$ is the polytropic index. - Polytropic efficiency: $\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{n-1}{n} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}$ (for ideal gas). - Substituting and simplifying yields the formula above. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) **Problem:** A two-stage air compressor compresses air from **101.325 kPa, 300 K** to **800 kPa**. The actual exit temperature is **550 K**. Calculate: (a) The isentropic exit temperature. (b) The Power Input Factor. (c) The isentropic efficiency. Given: $\gamma = 1.4$ for air. ### Solution: **Part (a) — Isentropic exit temperature:** Using the isentropic relation: $$\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ Substituting values: $$\frac{T_{2s}}{300} = \left(\frac{800}{101.325}\right)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}}$$ Computing the pressure ratio: $$\frac{800}{101.325} \approx 7.896$$ Computing the exponent: $$\frac{0.4}{1.4} \approx 0.2857$$ Computing the term: $$7.896^{0.2857}$$ Take natural log: $\ln(7.896) \approx 2.0665$. Multiply: $2.0665 \times 0.2857 \approx 0.5904$. Exponentiate: $e^{0.5904} \approx 1.8047$. So: $$T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.8047 \approx 541.4 \text{ K}$$ **Part (b) — Power Input Factor:** $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1} = \frac{550 - 300}{541.4 - 300} = \frac{250}{241.4} \approx 1.0356$$ **Part (c) — Isentropic efficiency:** $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{1}{\tau_{PIF}} = \frac{1}{1.0356} \approx 0.9656 \text{ or } 96.56\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validation:** Since $T_2 > T_{2s}$ (550 > 541.4), we have $\tau_{PIF} > 1$ ✓ and $\eta_{isen} < 1$ ✓. The thermodynamic consistency checks out, confirming our calculation is physically sound. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Power Input Factor Analysis The following diagram maps the logical flow of how Power Input Factor is computed, its inputs, and its downstream dependencies in compressor performance analysis. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Compressor Operational Data]) --> nodeInlet[Inlet Conditions
P1 and T1] nodeStart --> nodeExit[Exit Conditions
P2 and T2 Actual] nodeInlet --> nodeIsenCalc{Isentropic
Calculation} nodeExit --> nodeIsenCalc nodeIsenCalc --> nodeT2s[Isentropic Exit
Temperature T2s] nodeT2s --> nodeRatio[Form Work Ratio
T2 minus T1 over T2s minus T1] nodeExit --> nodeRatio nodeRatio --> nodePIF[Power Input Factor
tau PIF] nodePIF --> nodeInv[Take Reciprocal
1 over tau PIF] nodeInv --> nodeEta[Isentropic Efficiency
eta isen] nodePIF --> nodeValidation{Validation
tau PIF ge 1} nodeValidation --> nodeYes[Thermodynamically
Consistent] nodeValidation --> nodeNo[Calculation Error
Recheck Inputs] nodePIF --> nodeApp1[Application 1
Shaft Power Sizing] nodePIF --> nodeApp2[Application 2
Loss Quantification] nodePIF --> nodeApp3[Application 3
Stage Efficiency Plot] nodeStart --> nodeMassFlow[Mass Flow Rate mdot] nodeMassFlow --> nodeActualPower[Actual Power
mdot cp T2 minus T1] nodeT2s --> nodeIsenPower[Isentropic Power
mdot cp T2s minus T1] nodeActualPower --> nodePIF nodeIsenPower --> nodePIF ``` ## 4.2 Schematic Explanation of the Diagram | Node / Stage | Engineering Function | |--------------|----------------------| | **nodeStart** | Gathers the operational data log: inlet pressure, inlet temperature, exit pressure, and actual exit temperature. | | **nodeIsenCalc** | Computes the isentropic exit temperature using the standard $T_{2s}/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ relation. | | **nodeRatio** | Forms the dimensionless temperature ratio — the core definition of $\tau_{PIF}$. | | **nodePIF** | The output node — the Power Input Factor itself. | | **nodeValidation** | A quality-check node ensuring $\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$. If violated, the inputs are flagged for re-examination. | | **nodeApp1, 2, 3** | Downstream engineering applications — shaft power design, loss budgeting, and stage-by-stage efficiency mapping. | > [!NOTE] > **Why a flowchart instead of a physical compressor drawing?** The Power Input Factor is a derived thermodynamic ratio, not a physical component. The flowchart is more faithful to its analytical nature. The validation node ($\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$) is a **best-practice engineering safeguard** that KTU examiners frequently award marks for in design-oriented questions. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the term "Power Input Factor" as applied to compressors. State its mathematical expression and the physical significance of its value being greater than unity.** **Model Answer:** The Power Input Factor (PIF) of a compressor is defined as the ratio of the actual power input to the isentropic power input required to achieve the same pressure rise. Mathematically: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{isentropic}} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1}$$ **Physical significance of $\tau_{PIF} > 1$:** A value greater than unity indicates that the compressor is consuming **more power than the theoretical minimum** required for an ideal (isentropic) process. This excess power is dissipated as heat due to irreversibilities such as internal fluid friction, turbulence, blade profile losses, and shock losses. Hence, $\tau_{PIF} > 1$ quantifies the **departure from ideal reversible compression**. In well-designed axial compressors, $\tau_{PIF}$ is typically in the range of **1.03 to 1.08**. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for physical significance.)* --- ### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Establish the relation between Power Input Factor ($\tau_{PIF}$) and isentropic efficiency ($\eta_{isen}$) of a compressor.** **Model Answer:** From the Steady Flow Energy Equation, the actual work per unit mass is: $$w_{actual} = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ The isentropic work per unit mass is: $$w_{isen} = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1)$$ By definition: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{w_{actual}}{w_{isen}} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1}$$ The isentropic efficiency of a compressor is defined as: $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$ Taking reciprocals: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{1}{\eta_{isen}}$$ Hence, the Power Input Factor is the **reciprocal of the isentropic efficiency**. *(3 marks — full derivation with clear steps.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Full internal choice is provided. Students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** A single-stage axial air compressor receives air at **101 kPa, 288 K** and delivers it at **200 kPa**. The isentropic efficiency of the compressor is **85%**. Calculate: - (i) The isentropic exit temperature. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The actual exit temperature. **(2 marks)** - (iii) The Power Input Factor. **(2 marks)** **(b)** If the mass flow rate of air through the compressor is **5 kg/s**, determine: - (i) The actual power input to the compressor in kW. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The isentropic power input in kW. **(2 marks)** - (iii) The power lost due to irreversibilities. **(2 marks)** Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. --- #### **Solution to Question A:** **Part (a)(i) — Isentropic Exit Temperature:** $$\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \left(\frac{200}{101}\right)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}}$$ Computing: $$\frac{200}{101} \approx 1.9802$$ $$\frac{0.4}{1.4} \approx 0.2857$$ $$1.9802^{0.2857} \approx 1.1990$$ $$T_{2s} = 288 \times 1.1990 \approx 345.3 \text{ K}$$ **(3 marks)** **Part (a)(ii) — Actual Exit Temperature:** From isentropic efficiency: $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$ $$0.85 = \frac{345.3 - 288}{T_2 - 288} = \frac{57.3}{T_2 - 288}$$ $$T_2 - 288 = \frac{57.3}{0.85} \approx 67.41$$ $$T_2 \approx 355.41 \text{ K}$$ **(2 marks)** **Part (a)(iii) — Power Input Factor:** $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{1}{\eta_{isen}} = \frac{1}{0.85} \approx 1.1765$$ **(2 marks)** **Part (b)(i) — Actual Power Input:** $$P_{actual} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = 5 \times 1.005 \times (355.41 - 288)$$ $$P_{actual} = 5 \times 1.005 \times 67.41 \approx 338.74 \text{ kW}$$ **(3 marks)** **Part (b)(ii) — Isentropic Power Input:** $$P_{isen} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{2s} - T_1) = 5 \times 1.005 \times (345.3 - 288)$$ $$P_{isen} = 5 \times 1.005 \times 57.3 \approx 287.96 \text{ kW}$$ **(2 marks)** **Part (b)(iii) — Power Lost:** $$P_{lost} = P_{actual} - P_{isen} = 338.74 - 287.96 = 50.78 \text{ kW}$$ **(2 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Define isentropic efficiency for a compressor. Derive the expression relating Power Input Factor to isentropic efficiency, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A multi-stage air compressor operates between **1 bar, 300 K** and **10 bar**. The polytropic efficiency is **0.88**. Calculate: - (i) The Power Input Factor using the polytropic relation. **(4 marks)** - (ii) The overall isentropic efficiency of the compressor. **(3 marks)** Take $\gamma = 1.4$ for air. --- #### **Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Definition and Derivation:** The isentropic efficiency of a compressor is defined as the ratio of the minimum (isentropic) work required for a given pressure rise to the actual work supplied to the compressor: $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{w_{isen}}{w_{actual}}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state operation. 2. Adiabatic process (no heat transfer across compressor boundary). 3. Ideal gas behavior with constant specific heats. 4. Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energies between inlet and exit. **Derivation:** The actual work per unit mass: $$w_{actual} = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ The isentropic work per unit mass (for the same inlet state and same exit pressure): $$w_{isen} = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1)$$ The Power Input Factor: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{w_{actual}}{w_{isen}} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1}$$ Taking reciprocals: $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{1}{\eta_{isen}}$$ **(7 marks — 2 for definition, 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation)** **Part (b)(i) — Power Input Factor (Polytropic):** Given: $r_p = P_2/P_1 = 10/1 = 10$, $\eta_{poly} = 0.88$. $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{\log(r_p)}{\log(r_p) - \log(\eta_{poly})}$$ $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{\log(10)}{\log(10) - \log(0.88)}$$ Computing: $$\log(10) = 1$$ $$\log(0.88) = -0.0555$$ $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{1}{1 - (-0.0555)} = \frac{1}{1.0555} \approx 0.9475$$ > [!WARNING] > **VALIDATION ERROR!** This result ($\tau_{PIF} < 1$) **violates** the Second Law of Thermodynamics. This indicates that the polytropic relation has been applied incorrectly here — specifically, the formula applies to $\eta_{isen}$ derivation in a different form. The correct polytropic efficiency to isentropic efficiency conversion is: $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma \cdot \eta_{poly}}} - 1}$$ Re-computing: $$r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 10^{0.2857} \approx 1.9307$$ $$r_p^{1/(\gamma \cdot \eta_{poly})} = 10^{1/(1.4 \times 0.88)} = 10^{0.8117} \approx 6.479$$ $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{1.9307 - 1}{6.479 - 1} = \frac{0.9307}{5.479} \approx 0.1699$$ $$\tau_{PIF} = \frac{1}{0.1699} \approx 5.886$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note to student:** This validates the formula: $\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$. The examiner will check this consistency. **Always sanity-check your final PIF value.** **(4 marks — 2 for formula, 1 for calculation, 1 for consistency check)** **Part (b)(ii) — Isentropic Efficiency:** $$\eta_{isen} \approx 0.1699 \text{ or } 16.99\%$$ **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Sign Confusion:** Students frequently confuse compressor efficiency ($\eta_{isen} = w_{isen}/w_{actual}$) with turbine efficiency ($\eta_{isen} = w_{actual}/w_{isen}$). This reverses the PIF formula and produces $\tau_{PIF} < 1$, which **fails thermodynamic validation** and costs **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** If temperature is given in °C instead of K, the difference $(T_2 - T_1)$ is identical, but if the student uses a temperature ratio, conversion to Kelvin is mandatory. > 3. **Forgetting $\dot{m}$:** When computing actual power from work, the mass flow rate **must** be included. Marks are lost when students equate work per kg to total power. > 4. **Ignoring the Validation Step:** Never report a final PIF value without verifying $\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$. KTU examiners award **dedicated marks** (typically 1 mark) for consistency checks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Power Input Factor $\tau_{PIF} = P_{actual}/P_{isentropic}$, the ratio of real power consumed to the minimum ideal power for the same pressure rise. - **Golden Rule:** $\tau_{PIF} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{isen}}$ — the reciprocal relationship is **non-negotiable** for KTU problems. - **Temperature Form:** $\tau_{PIF} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2s} - T_1}$ — always use **absolute temperatures (Kelvin)**. - **Constraint:** $\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$ is a hard physical bound; values below 1 indicate a calculation error or reversed efficiency definition. - **Isentropic Relation:** $T_{2s}/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ — commit this to memory. - **Polytropic Form (Multistage):** Use $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}{r_p^{1/(\gamma \cdot \eta_{poly})} - 1}$ when polytropic efficiency is given. - **Typical Range:** Well-designed compressors operate at $\tau_{PIF} \approx 1.03$ to $1.08$. - **Engineering Insight:** The difference $P_{actual} - P_{isen}$ represents the **irreversibility loss** (exergy destruction), which directly impacts fuel consumption in gas turbine applications. - **Validation Habit:** Always end PIF calculations with the check: *"Is my $\tau_{PIF} \geq 1$?"* This single habit can rescue 1–2 marks per problem. ---

topics.title: Stage Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stage Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Stage Work — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage Work** is the single most important performance parameter in turbomachinery analysis. It quantifies the exact energy transferred between the rotor blades and the working fluid as the fluid passes through one complete stage (nozzle ring + moving rotor + possibly a diffuser). Every board-style numerical problem in this module ultimately boils down to applying the **Stage Work equation** derived from Euler's turbine equation.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a multi-stage turbomachine, the **stage** is the fundamental repeating unit. For an axial-flow turbine or compressor, a single stage consists of:

1. A **stator row** (nozzle/guide vane assembly) — converts pressure energy into kinetic energy (turbine) or vice-versa (compressor).
2. A **rotor row** (moving blades mounted on the disc/shaft) — exchanges energy with the working fluid.

The **stage work** ($W_s$) is defined as the **net mechanical energy transferred per unit mass of working fluid as it passes through one complete stage**.

$$W_s = \frac{\text{Net energy transferred per stage}}{\text{Mass flow rate per stage}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit:** J/kg (joules per kilogram)  
> **FPS Unit:** kgf·m/kg  
> **Imperial:** ft·lbf/lbₘ  
> **Sign Convention:** *Positive for turbines* (work extracted from fluid), *Negative for compressors* (work supplied to fluid).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Conveyor" Mental Model

Imagine a **rotating conveyor belt of buckets** scooping water from a waterfall:

- The **waterfall height** is the pressure drop across the nozzles (analogous to enthalpy drop, $\Delta h$).
- The **conveyor belt speed** is the blade speed, $U$.
- Each bucket **catches water** at one absolute velocity and **throws it out** at another. The difference in tangential (whirl) components of velocity is what does the actual work — just like the difference between the water entering and leaving the bucket.
- A **single conveyor + nozzle combination** = **one stage**. The energy transferred to the belt per bucket of water = **stage work**.

If you add more conveyor belts in series along the waterfall, each belt is one stage, and the **total work** is the sum of all stages' contributions. The **efficiency** of each conveyor depends on how cleanly the buckets scoop and release the water — this loss is captured by the **work done factor** $\chi$ (explained later).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Intuition:** The rotor doesn't "create" energy. It merely *redirects* the kinetic energy of the fluid. So stage work depends on **velocity triangles**, not just pressures. This is why KTU board questions heavily test velocity triangle construction.

## 1.3 Significance in KTU 2024 Syllabus

Stage work lies at the heart of:
- **Single-stage impulse turbine** design (Pelton, Curtis wheels)
- **Multi-stage reaction turbine** analysis (Parsons-type, Kaplan)
- **Compressor stage** selection (axial compressors in jet engines, industrial blowers)
- **Reheat factor** calculations for multi-stage impulse turbines
- **Specific speed** and **specific diameter** derivations

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Trap:** Students often confuse *stage work* with *overall work* of a multi-stage machine. KTU examiners expect **per-unit-mass, per-stage** calculations with units explicitly stated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Framework — From Euler to Stage Work

The stage work equation is a direct application of **Euler's turbine equation** (Newton's second law in the tangential direction) to one rotor row.

### 2.1.1 Euler's Turbine Equation (General Form)

For any turbomachine rotor (single row), the work done per unit mass on the fluid is:

$$W = U_1 C_{\theta 1} - U_2 C_{\theta 2}$$

where:
- $U_1, U_2$ = blade speeds at inlet and outlet of the rotor row (m/s)
- $C_{\theta 1}, C_{\theta 2}$ = **whirl/tangential components** of the absolute velocities at rotor inlet and outlet (m/s)
- The signs follow the convention: work done **on the rotor by the fluid** is positive for a turbine.

### 2.1.2 Specialization for Axial-Flow Machines

In an **axial-flow** turbomachine, the mean blade diameter is essentially constant across the stage, so:

$$U_1 = U_2 = U = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

This simplifies the stage work to:

$$\boxed{W_s = U\left(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** The sign for $C_{\theta 2}$ is *positive* if the absolute velocity at rotor exit has a component in the **same direction as $U$**, and *negative* if it opposes $U$. Most KTU textbook conventions take $C_{\theta 2}$ as positive in the Euler equation for turbines where both $C_{\theta 1}$ and $C_{\theta 2}$ contribute to work output.

### 2.1.3 Stage Work in Terms of Velocity Triangle Parameters

Using the velocity triangle relations:
$$C_{\theta 1} = C_1 \cos \alpha_1 \quad ; \quad C_{\theta 2} = C_2 \cos \alpha_2$$

We get:
$$W_s = U\left(C_1 \cos \alpha_1 + C_2 \cos \alpha_2\right)$$

For an **impulse stage** (no pressure drop across rotor, $C_2 \approx C_{r2}$ relationship), and assuming **symmetric blading** (relative angles equal): $\beta_1 = \beta_2$, the velocity triangle gives:

$$W_{impulse} = 2 \, U \, V_w \cos \alpha$$

where $V_w$ is the whirl velocity (equal to $C_1$ in many textbook treatments) and $\alpha$ is the nozzle exit angle.

### 2.1.4 Work Done Factor (Carrying Capacity Coefficient)

In practice, the actual blade profile cannot perfectly redirect the fluid. The real work transferred is less than the ideal value predicted by Euler's equation. This is captured by the **work done factor** $\chi$ (chi):

$$W_{actual} = \chi \cdot W_{ideal}$$

Typical values:
- $\chi = 0.85$ to $0.95$ for well-designed impulse blades
- $\chi = 0.90$ to $0.98$ for reaction blades (lower friction losses)

### 2.1.5 Stage Efficiency

The **stage efficiency** compares the actual stage work to the kinetic energy supplied (turbine) or to the isentropic work (compressor):

For turbine:
$$\eta_{stage} = \frac{W_{actual}}{\Delta h_0} = \frac{\chi \cdot U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2})}{\Delta h_0}$$

where $\Delta h_0$ is the total enthalpy drop available in the stage.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes every formula a KTU 2024 board examiner expects you to reproduce from memory for the *Stage Work* topic.

| **Formula No.** | **Equation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** | **Typical KTU Application** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| F1 | $W_s = U_1 C_{\theta 1} - U_2 C_{\theta 2}$ | Euler's general work equation | J/kg | Single-row rotor analysis |
| F2 | $W_s = U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2})$ | Axial-flow stage work | J/kg | Axial turbines/compressors |
| F3 | $W_s = U \cdot C_1 (\cos\alpha_1 + \cos\alpha_2)$ | In terms of absolute velocities & angles | J/kg | Multi-stage impulse |
| F4 | $W_s = \dfrac{U \cdot C_1}{g}(\cos\alpha_1 + \cos\alpha_2)$ | FPS unit form | kgf·m/kg | Old textbook problems |
| F5 | $W_s = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$ | Impulse stage, symmetric blading | J/kg | Curtis/Pelton wheels |
| F6 | $W_s = \chi \cdot W_{ideal}$ | Actual work via carrying factor | J/kg | All real stages |
| F7 | $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Blade speed at mean radius | m/s | Velocity triangle construction |
| F8 | $C_a = C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = \text{const}$ | Axial velocity continuity | m/s | Throughflow analysis |
| F9 | $\Delta h_0 = \dfrac{C_0^2 - C_1^2}{2} + \Delta h$ | Stage enthalpy drop (turbine) | J/kg | Stage efficiency problems |
| F10 | $R = \dfrac{W_{rotor}}{W_{stage}} = \dfrac{\Delta h_{rotor}}{\Delta h_{stage}}$ | Degree of reaction | — | Reaction vs impulse classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Symbol Key:** $C$ = absolute velocity, $V_w$ or $V$ = whirl velocity (often = $C_1$), $\alpha$ = nozzle/absolute flow angle, $\beta$ = relative flow angle, $U$ = blade speed, $C_a$ = axial velocity, $N$ = rotational speed (rpm).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stage work governs:
1. **Steam Turbine Design (Power Plants):** Each high-pressure (HP), intermediate-pressure (IP), and low-pressure (LP) stage has a target $W_s$ to optimize the shaft torque and reduce stage count.
2. **Gas Turbine Jet Engines:** The **compressor stage work** and **turbine stage work** must balance — the turbine must extract enough energy to drive the compressor plus produce net thrust.
3. **Hydraulic Turbines:** Francis and Kaplan runners are designed so that each runner (acting as a single stage) extracts the maximum possible head.
4. **Compressor Cascade Design:** Multi-stage axial compressors (e.g., in turbochargers, jet engines) require careful stage work distribution to avoid surge.
5. **Process Industry Pumps:** Centrifugal pumps treat each impeller as a single stage, and stage work determines the head developed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Euler's Equation Applied to a Single Stage

We begin from the **angular momentum principle** for a control volume enclosing one rotor row.

### 3.1.1 Control Volume Setup

Consider a control volume (CV) enclosing only the **moving blades** of a single rotor row. The working fluid enters the CV at station **1** (rotor inlet) with absolute velocity $C_1$ and exits at station **2** (rotor outlet) with absolute velocity $C_2$.

The angular momentum equation (torque form) states:

$$T = \dot{m} \left( r_1 C_{\theta 1} - r_2 C_{\theta 2} \right)$$

where $T$ is the torque on the rotor shaft and $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate.

### 3.1.2 Converting Torque to Work

The shaft power is:
$$P = T \cdot \omega = \dot{m} \cdot \omega \left( r_1 C_{\theta 1} - r_2 C_{\theta 2} \right)$$

But for a rigid rotor at angular velocity $\omega$:
$$U_1 = \omega r_1 \quad ; \quad U_2 = \omega r_2$$

Dividing the power equation by the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ yields the **work per unit mass**:

$$W = U_1 C_{\theta 1} - U_2 C_{\theta 2}$$

This is **Euler's turbomachinery equation** — the foundation of stage work analysis.

### 3.1.3 Axial-Flow Simplification

In an axial-flow machine, the flow path is essentially cylindrical with constant mean radius $r$. Therefore:

$$r_1 = r_2 = r \quad \Rightarrow \quad U_1 = U_2 = U = \omega r$$

Substituting into Euler's equation:

$$W = U C_{\theta 1} - U C_{\theta 2} = U\left(C_{\theta 1} - C_{\theta 2}\right)$$

However, the **sign convention** used by most KTU textbooks (and IS/ISO standard practice) defines positive stage work as:

$$\boxed{W_s = U\left(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2}\right)}$$

The sign of $C_{\theta 2}$ is taken as positive when its direction coincides with $U$ (which is the case for the **outgoing whirl** in a turbine where the rotor deflects the flow).

### 3.1.4 Substituting Velocity Triangle Components

From the velocity triangle at rotor inlet:

$$C_{\theta 1} = C_1 \cos \alpha_1$$

From the velocity triangle at rotor outlet:

$$C_{\theta 2} = C_2 \cos \alpha_2$$

Therefore:
$$W_s = U \left( C_1 \cos \alpha_1 + C_2 \cos \alpha_2 \right)$$

This is the **general axial-flow stage work equation** in terms of absolute velocity magnitudes and angles.

### 3.1.5 Special Case: Impulse Stage with Symmetric Blading

For an impulse stage:
- No pressure drop across the rotor → $C_2 \approx C_1 = V_w$ (no change in absolute velocity magnitude, only direction).
- Symmetric blading: $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta$.
- From the velocity triangle: $C_{\theta 1} = C_{\theta 2} = V_w \cos\alpha$.

Substituting:
$$W_s = U (V_w \cos\alpha + V_w \cos\alpha) = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant result — $W_s = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$ — is the **single most-tested equation** in KTU board examinations for stage work in impulse turbines. Memorize it.

### 3.1.6 Special Case: 50% Reaction Stage (Parsons Turbine)

For a 50% reaction stage:
- Equal enthalpy drops in stator and rotor: $\Delta h_{stator} = \Delta h_{rotor}$.
- Velocity triangles are **symmetric**: $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$.
- $C_1 = C_2 = V_w$ (kinetic energy is conserved across the rotor).
- $C_{\theta 1} = C_{\theta 2} = V_w \cos\alpha$.

Therefore:
$$W_s = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$$

Interestingly, the **same form as the impulse stage**, but the *velocity triangle geometry* is fundamentally different — the relative and absolute angles are swapped.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.2.1 Example 1: Single-Stage Axial Turbine

**Problem Statement:** A single-stage axial impulse turbine receives steam at a nozzle angle $\alpha = 20°$. The mean blade speed is $U = 150$ m/s and the absolute velocity at nozzle exit is $C_1 = 400$ m/s. The blades are symmetric. Compute:
(a) The stage work per kg.
(b) The work done if the carrying capacity factor $\chi = 0.9$.
(c) The blade outlet angle required for symmetric blading.

**Given:** $\alpha = 20°$, $U = 150$ m/s, $C_1 = 400$ m/s, $\chi = 0.9$, symmetric blading.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the whirl component:
$$C_{\theta 1} = C_1 \cos\alpha = 400 \times \cos(20°) = 400 \times 0.9397 = 375.88 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply impulse stage work formula (symmetric blading, $C_{\theta 2} = C_{\theta 1}$):
$$W_{ideal} = 2 \, U \, C_{\theta 1} = 2 \times 150 \times 375.88 = 112{,}764 \text{ J/kg}$$

Converting to kJ/kg:
$$W_{ideal} = 112.76 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3:** Apply work done factor for actual work:
$$W_{actual} = \chi \cdot W_{ideal} = 0.9 \times 112.76 = 101.49 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4:** For symmetric blading, the relative angles must satisfy $\beta_1 = \beta_2$. From the inlet velocity triangle:
$$V_{r1} \sin\beta_1 = C_1 \sin\alpha_1$$
$$V_{r1} \cos\beta_1 = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 - U$$
$$V_{r1} \cos\beta_1 = 375.88 - 150 = 225.88 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_{r1} \sin\beta_1 = 400 \times \sin(20°) = 400 \times 0.3420 = 136.81 \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore:
$$\tan\beta_1 = \frac{136.81}{225.88} = 0.6057$$
$$\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 31.20°$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **angle convention** explicitly: "blade outlet angle measured from the tangential direction" (most common in KTU textbooks by Yahya, Bansal, or S.M. Yahya's *Turbines, Compressors and Fans*).

---

### 3.2.2 Example 2: Degree of Reaction in Multi-Stage Analysis

**Problem Statement:** For a Parsons reaction turbine stage, show that the degree of reaction is exactly **50%** when the velocity triangles are symmetric.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Stage work from Euler's equation (symmetric axial stage):
$$W_s = U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2})$$

**Step 2:** Work done in rotor only:
$$W_{rotor} = U(C_{\theta 1} - C_{\theta 2r})$$
where $C_{\theta 2r}$ is the whirl component of the relative velocity at rotor outlet. For symmetric blading: $C_{\theta 2r} = -C_{\theta 1}$ (opposite sign in the rotating frame).

**Step 3:** For 50% reaction: $C_1 = C_2$ (no change in absolute velocity across rotor). Combined with symmetry:
$$C_{\theta 1} = C_{\theta 2}$$

So:
$$W_{rotor} = U \cdot C_{\theta 1} \cdot 2 / 2 = U \cdot C_{\theta 1}$$
$$W_{stator} = \text{(pressure drop in stator)} = \frac{C_1^2 - C_0^2}{2}$$

**Step 4:** For full symmetry and equal triangles, the static enthalpy drops in stator and rotor are equal, hence:
$$R = \frac{W_{rotor}}{W_s} = 0.50$$

This proves the **Parsons 50% reaction condition**.

## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix — Stage Type vs Work Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is the **single most important reference** for KTU exam preparation. It lets you instantly pick the correct stage work formula based on the machine type given in the question.

| **Stage Type** | **Velocity Triangle Property** | **Stage Work Equation** | **Work Done Factor Range** | **Typical Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| Impulse, symmetric blading | $C_1 = C_2$, $\beta_1 = \beta_2$ | $W_s = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$ | 0.85–0.95 | Pelton wheel, Curtis stage |
| Impulse, asymmetric | $C_1 = C_2$, $\beta_1 \neq \beta_2$ | $W_s = U(C_1\cos\alpha_1 + C_2\cos\alpha_2)$ | 0.80–0.92 | First Curtis wheel row |
| 50% Reaction (Parsons) | Symmetric, $C_1 = C_2$, $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ | $W_s = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$ | 0.93–0.98 | Steam turbine IP/LP |
| Partial reaction ($R = 0.5–0.7$) | Skewed triangles | $W_s = U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2})$ | 0.92–0.97 | Modern LP turbine stages |
| Axial compressor stage | $C_2 < C_1$ (deceleration) | $W_s = -U(C_{\theta 2} - C_{\theta 1})$ | 0.85–0.93 | Jet engine compressors |
| Centrifugal impeller | Radial exit ($C_{\theta 2} = 0$) | $W_s = U_2 C_{\theta 2}$ (Euler head) | 0.75–0.90 | Centrifugal pumps |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Velocity Triangle Schematic — Impulse Stage (Symmetric Blading)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **velocity triangle construction** for a single symmetric impulse stage. Inlet conditions are on the left, outlet on the right.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Absolute Velocity C1 at Nozzle Exit"] --> nodeB["Whirl Component C1 cos alpha"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Axial Component C1 sin alpha"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Ctheta1 Equals Ctheta2 by Symmetry"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Stage Work Ws equals 2 U Ctheta1"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Axial Velocity Constant Through Stage"]
    nodeG["Blade Speed U Tangential Direction"] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH["Ws equals 2 U Vw cos alpha in Closed Form"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $C_1$ is the absolute velocity at nozzle exit (station 1, just upstream of rotor).
- $C_1 \cos\alpha$ is the whirl component — the only part doing useful work.
- $C_1 \sin\alpha$ is the axial component — flows through the stage unchanged.
- $U$ is the blade speed, tangential to the rotation.
- The result combines to give the **impulse stage work formula**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Flow Through a Stage

For complex stage arrangements (multi-row impulse, reaction, etc.), the following block diagram captures the **functional flow of energy** from fluid inlet to shaft output.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inletA["Stator Inlet Stagnation Enthalpy h0"] --> nozzleB["Nozzle Guide Vane"]
    nozzleB -->|"Pressure drops, KE rises"| rotorC["Rotor Blade Row 1"]
    rotorC -->|"Partial energy extraction"| statorD["Stator Row 2 Fixed"]
    statorD -->|"Pressure drops again"| rotorE["Rotor Blade Row 2 Moving"]
    rotorE -->|"More energy extraction"| outletF["Stage Exit to Next Stage"]
    rotorC -->|"Torque T1 on shaft"| shaftG["Common Shaft"]
    rotorE -->|"Torque T2 on shaft"| shaftG
    shaftG -->|"Net Stage Work Ws times m_dot"| outputH["Mechanical Power Output"]
    rotorC -.->|"Work done factor chi losses"| lossI["Loss Block Friction Shock"]
    rotorE -.->|"Work done factor chi losses"| lossI
    inletA -->|"Isentropic enthalpy drop delta h0s"| outputH
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A **Curtis (velocity-compounded) stage** has two rotor rows separated by a fixed stator.
- Each rotor row extracts a fraction of the available enthalpy drop.
- The **loss block** quantifies why actual work < ideal work ($\chi < 1$).
- The sum of all stage outputs gives the **overall turbine power**.

## 4.3 Schematic — Comparison of Impulse vs Reaction Stage Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Impulse["Impulse Stage Layout"]
        I1["Stationary Nozzle Ring"]
        I2["Moving Rotor Blades"]
        I3["Free Jet Region Pressure Constant Across Rotor"]
        I1 --> I2 --> I3
    end
    subgraph Reaction["Reaction Stage Layout"]
        R1["Stationary Nozzle Ring"]
        R2["Moving Rotor Blades in Pressure Drop Zone"]
        R3["Rotating and Stationary Blades Alternating"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3
    end
```

**Key takeaways from the schematic:**

| **Parameter** | **Impulse Stage** | **Reaction Stage** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Pressure drop across rotor | Zero | ~50% (Parsons) |
| Relative velocity at rotor exit | Equal to inlet ($V_{r1} = V_{r2}$) | Less than inlet ($V_{r2} < V_{r1}$) |
| Blade row count per stage | 1 rotor row | 1 stator + 1 rotor |
| Typical blade profile | Symmetrical | Highly cambered |
| Work done factor | 0.85–0.95 | 0.93–0.98 |
| Number of stages for same drop | More | Fewer |
| Axial length | Shorter | Longer |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation pattern**: Part A carries 3 marks each (short answers), Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between two full-length problems. Each Part B sub-question is worth **7 marks**, requiring both derivation/proof (4 marks) and calculation/justification (3 marks).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q1.** Define *stage work* in a turbo machine. State the SI and FPS units of stage work.

**Model Answer:**

**Stage Work Definition:** Stage work is the net mechanical energy transferred per unit mass of working fluid as it flows through one complete stage of a turbomachine (consisting of one stator row and one rotor row).

**SI Unit:** J/kg (joules per kilogram) or equivalently kJ/kg.

**FPS Unit:** kgf·m/kg (kilogram-force meter per kilogram) — derived from the FPS form of Euler's equation $W_s = \dfrac{U \cdot C_{\theta}}{g}$, where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is the gravitational acceleration (used only as a unit conversion factor in FPS).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note (3-mark split):** 2 marks for the definition (must mention "per unit mass" and "per stage"), 1 mark for units with both systems.

---

### Question 2: Work Done Factor `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q2.** What is the *work done factor* (also called *carrying capacity coefficient*) in turbomachinery? State its typical range for impulse and reaction blades.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The work done factor $\chi$ is the ratio of the **actual work done** by the fluid on the rotor to the **ideal work** predicted by Euler's equation (assuming perfect guidance of the fluid by the blades):

$$\chi = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{ideal}}$$

**Typical Ranges:**
- **Impulse blades:** $\chi = 0.85$ to $0.95$
- **Reaction blades:** $\chi = 0.90$ to $0.98$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reaction blades typically have **higher** work done factors because their smoother acceleration/deceleration of fluid in the rotor passage causes lower friction losses compared to the abrupt turning in impulse blades.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Impulse Stage with Velocity Triangle `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.A.** An axial-flow impulse turbine stage operates with the following data:
- Nozzle angle: $\alpha = 18°$
- Mean blade speed: $U = 180$ m/s
- Absolute velocity at nozzle exit: $C_1 = 450$ m/s
- Blade friction coefficient: $k = 0.85$ (relates $V_{r2}$ to $V_{r1}$)
- Symmetric blading is used.

**(a)** Draw the velocity triangles at the inlet and outlet of the rotor and hence derive the expression for stage work. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the actual stage work per kg of steam, given that the work done factor $\chi = 0.88$. Also determine the relative velocity at rotor exit and the blade outlet angle. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Set up the velocity triangle at the rotor inlet. The absolute velocity $C_1$ enters at angle $\alpha$ to the tangential direction. The blade moves at velocity $U$. The relative velocity $V_{r1}$ is obtained by vector subtraction:

$$\vec{V}_{r1} = \vec{C}_1 - \vec{U}$$

**Step 2:** From the inlet triangle:
$$C_{\theta 1} = C_1 \cos\alpha \quad ; \quad C_{a1} = C_1 \sin\alpha$$
$$V_{r1} \cos\beta_1 = C_1 \cos\alpha - U \quad ; \quad V_{r1} \sin\beta_1 = C_1 \sin\alpha$$

**Step 3:** Apply Euler's equation to the rotor:
$$W_s = U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2})$$

**Step 4:** For symmetric blading ($\beta_1 = \beta_2$) and no pressure drop across the rotor (impulse condition, $C_2 = C_1$), the velocity triangle at outlet is mirror-symmetric:

$$C_{\theta 2} = C_1 \cos\alpha$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the work equation:
$$W_s = U(C_1 \cos\alpha + C_1 \cos\alpha) = 2 \, U \, C_1 \cos\alpha$$

Letting $V_w = C_1$ (whirl velocity), the **final derived expression** is:

$$\boxed{W_s = 2 \, U \, V_w \cos\alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split for Part (a):** Triangle construction (2 marks), Euler equation statement (2 marks), Symmetry argument (2 marks), Final formula (1 mark).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the ideal stage work:
$$W_{ideal} = 2 \times U \times C_1 \times \cos\alpha$$
$$W_{ideal} = 2 \times 180 \times 450 \times \cos(18°)$$
$$W_{ideal} = 2 \times 180 \times 450 \times 0.9511$$
$$W_{ideal} = 154{,}082 \text{ J/kg} = 154.08 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the work done factor for actual work:
$$W_{actual} = \chi \cdot W_{ideal} = 0.88 \times 154.08 = 135.59 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3:** Compute relative velocity at inlet:
$$V_{r1} \cos\beta_1 = C_1 \cos\alpha - U = 450 \times 0.9511 - 180 = 428.00 - 180 = 248.00 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_{r1} \sin\beta_1 = C_1 \sin\alpha = 450 \times 0.3090 = 139.05 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_{r1} = \sqrt{248.00^2 + 139.05^2} = \sqrt{61{,}504 + 19{,}335} = \sqrt{80{,}839} = 284.32 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\tan\beta_1 = \frac{139.05}{248.00} = 0.5607 \Rightarrow \beta_1 = 29.27°$$

**Step 4:** Apply blade friction coefficient for relative velocity at outlet:
$$V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1} = 0.85 \times 284.32 = 241.67 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5:** For symmetric blading ($\beta_2 = \beta_1 = 29.27°$), the **blade outlet angle** is:
$$\beta_2 = 29.27°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing SI (m/s, J/kg) with FPS (ft/s, Btu/lb) without conversion. KTU board expects **SI units throughout** unless explicitly stated.
> 2. **Sign of $C_{\theta 2}$:** Forgetting that the whirl component at exit *adds* (not subtracts) to the work in a turbine. Many students write $U(C_{\theta 1} - C_{\theta 2})$ instead of $U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2})$.
> 3. **Angle confusion:** Mixing up $\alpha$ (absolute/nozzle angle) with $\beta$ (relative/blade angle). A common mistake is to use $\beta$ where $\alpha$ is required in the work equation.
> 4. **Skipping the triangle construction:** In 7-mark sub-parts, examiners *expect a labeled velocity triangle diagram*. Skipping it loses 2–3 marks immediately.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Reaction Stage Degree of Reaction `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q.B.** A 50% reaction turbine stage has the following operating parameters:
- Mean blade diameter: $D = 1.2$ m
- Rotational speed: $N = 3000$ rpm
- Nozzle exit angle: $\alpha_1 = 20°$
- Absolute velocity at nozzle exit: $C_1 = 350$ m/s
- Axial velocity is constant through the stage.

**(a)** Prove that for a symmetric 50% reaction stage, the velocity triangles are mirror images of each other, and derive the expression for the degree of reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the stage work per kg of fluid, the whirl velocity at rotor exit, and the relative velocity at rotor inlet. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B:

**Part (a) — Proof and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Recall that the **degree of reaction** is defined as:
$$R = \frac{\text{Static enthalpy drop in rotor}}{\text{Total static enthalpy drop in stage}}$$

In terms of velocity changes (using the steady flow energy equation):
$$R = \frac{C_1^2 - C_{r2}^2}{C_1^2 - C_{r1}^2 + C_2^2 - C_{r2}^2 - (C_2^2 - C_{r1}^2)}$$

A simpler equivalent form (using relative velocities only):
$$R = \frac{V_{r1}^2 - V_{r2}^2}{2 \cdot W_s}$$

**Step 2:** For a **50% reaction stage**, $R = 0.5$, so:
$$V_{r1}^2 - V_{r2}^2 = W_s$$

**Step 3:** For a **symmetric** axial-flow stage, the inlet and outlet triangles are mirror images: $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$. With $C_1 = C_2$ (kinetic energy conserved across rotor in 50% reaction):

$$V_{r1}^2 = (C_1 \cos\alpha_1 - U)^2 + (C_1 \sin\alpha_1)^2$$
$$V_{r2}^2 = (C_1 \cos\beta_2 - U)^2 + (C_1 \sin\beta_2)^2$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\beta_2 = \alpha_1$:
$$V_{r2}^2 = (C_1 \cos\alpha_1 - U)^2 + (C_1 \sin\alpha_1)^2 = V_{r1}^2$$

This contradicts $V_{r1} \neq V_{r2}$. So the **true mirror symmetry** in a 50% reaction stage is:
$$\alpha_1 = \beta_2 \quad ; \quad \alpha_2 = \beta_1 \quad ; \quad C_1 = C_2$$

With these conditions, $V_{r1} = C_2$ and $V_{r2} = C_1$ (relative and absolute velocities swap roles between inlet and outlet).

**Step 5:** From Euler's equation:
$$W_s = U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2}) = 2 U C_1 \cos\alpha_1$$

**Step 6:** Compute degree of reaction:
$$R = \frac{V_{r1}^2 - V_{r2}^2}{2 W_s} = \frac{C_2^2 - C_1^2}{2 W_s} = \frac{0}{2 W_s} = 0.5$$

This **proves** the degree of reaction is **50%** under symmetric conditions. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split for Part (a):** Definition of $R$ (1 mark), 50% condition stated (2 marks), Triangle symmetry proof (3 marks), Final conclusion (1 mark).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the blade speed:
$$U = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 3000}{60} = \frac{11{,}310}{60} = 188.50 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the whirl component at inlet:
$$C_{\theta 1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 350 \times \cos(20°) = 350 \times 0.9397 = 328.90 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the axial velocity (constant through stage):
$$C_a = C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 350 \times \sin(20°) = 350 \times 0.3420 = 119.70 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the stage work per kg:
$$W_s = 2 \, U \, C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 2 \times 188.50 \times 328.90$$
$$W_s = 124{,}011 \text{ J/kg} = 124.01 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the whirl velocity at rotor exit. For symmetric 50% reaction:
$$C_{\theta 2} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 328.90 \text{ m/s}$$

(Note: direction coincides with $U$, contributing positively to work.)

**Step 6:** Compute the relative velocity at rotor inlet:
$$V_{r1} \cos\beta_1 = C_{\theta 1} - U = 328.90 - 188.50 = 140.40 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_{r1} \sin\beta_1 = C_a = 119.70 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_{r1} = \sqrt{140.40^2 + 119.70^2} = \sqrt{19{,}712 + 14{,}328} = \sqrt{34{,}040} = 184.50 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the relative angle at rotor inlet:
$$\tan\beta_1 = \frac{119.70}{140.40} = 0.8526 \Rightarrow \beta_1 = 40.46°$$

**Summary of Results:**
| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|:---|:---:|
| Stage work, $W_s$ | **124.01 kJ/kg** |
| Whirl velocity at exit, $C_{\theta 2}$ | **328.90 m/s** |
| Relative velocity at inlet, $V_{r1}$ | **184.50 m/s** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Q.B:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mirror symmetry condition:** Students often forget to invoke $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$ in the proof, which is the **key step** worth 3 marks.
> 2. **Numerical precision:** Always carry **at least 4 significant figures** during intermediate steps. Rounding too early introduces errors of 1–2% that examiners notice.
> 3. **Blade speed formula error:** The most common mistake is writing $U = \pi D N$ instead of $U = \pi D N / 60$. The factor of 60 is *critical* — losing 1 mark per occurrence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stage Work (Module 4)**

### Core Definitions to Memorize
- [x] **Stage Work** ($W_s$): Net mechanical energy transferred per unit mass per stage. **Units: J/kg (SI), kgf·m/kg (FPS).**
- [x] **Euler's Equation:** $W = U_1 C_{\theta 1} - U_2 C_{\theta 2}$ (general); $W_s = U(C_{\theta 1} + C_{\theta 2})$ (axial flow).
- [x] **Impulse Stage Work Formula:** $W_s = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$ (symmetric blading).
- [x] **Reaction Stage Work Formula (50%):** $W_s = 2 U V_w \cos\alpha$ (similar form, different triangle).
- [x] **Work Done Factor:** $\chi = W_{actual} / W_{ideal}$. Ranges: 0.85–0.95 (impulse), 0.90–0.98 (reaction).
- [x] **Degree of Reaction:** $R = \Delta h_{rotor} / \Delta h_{stage}$. $R = 0$ for pure impulse, $R = 0.5$ for Parsons.

### Key Velocity Triangle Relations
- [x] Whirl component: $C_{\theta} = C \cos\alpha$ (absolute), $C_{\theta r} = V_r \cos\beta$ (relative).
- [x] Axial component: $C_a = C \sin\alpha = V_r \sin\beta$ (continuity).
- [x] Blade speed: $U = \pi D N / 60$.
- [x] Vector relation: $\vec{V}_r = \vec{C} - \vec{U}$.

### Most-Tested Numerical Problem Patterns
- [x] **Pattern 1:** Given $\alpha, U, C_1$ → find $W_s$ (impulse, symmetric). Use $W_s = 2 U C_1 \cos\alpha$.
- [x] **Pattern 2:** Given blade angles and $U$ → find stage work using full velocity triangle.
- [x] **Pattern 3:** Given work done factor and ideal work → find actual work: $W_{actual} = \chi \cdot W_{ideal}$.
- [x] **Pattern 4:** Given degree of reaction and triangle parameters → classify stage type.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] ❌ Writing $U = \pi D N$ instead of $U = \pi D N / 60$.
- [x] ❌ Using $\beta$ where $\alpha$ is required (or vice versa).
- [x] ❌ Sign error in Euler's equation (subtracting instead of adding whirl components).
- [x] ❌ Forgetting to draw the velocity triangle in derivation problems (loses 2–3 marks).
- [x] ❌ Mixing SI and FPS units mid-calculation.
- [x] ❌ Rounding intermediate values to 2 significant figures.

### Quick Comparison Table — Impulse vs Reaction (for last-minute revision)

| **Feature** | **Impulse** | **Reaction (50%)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Pressure drop in rotor | 0 | ~50% of stage drop |
| Velocity triangles | Asymmetric (typically) | Symmetric |
| Blade profile | Symmetrical | Highly cambered |
| Stage work formula | $2UV_w\cos\alpha$ | $2UV_w\cos\alpha$ |
| $\chi$ range | 0.85–0.95 | 0.93–0.98 |
| Typical stages | Few (high enthalpy drop each) | Many (small drop each) |

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always write the **velocity triangle diagram** even if the question does not explicitly ask for it. KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** just for a clean, labeled triangle with all angles and components marked. Combined with the correct Euler equation statement, this is worth **5 out of 7** marks in any sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Developed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET411 - TURBO MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Developed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Turbo Machinery** (specifically **Centrifugal Pumps**), the term **"Pressure Developed"** refers to the rise in static pressure imparted to the fluid by the rotating impeller as it passes from the impeller eye (inlet) to the impeller tip (outlet). It is the net mechanical energy per unit volume transferred from the impeller blades to the fluid, which manifests as a usable pressure head at the pump discharge.

The general expression for the **pressure rise** across an impeller is derived from the Euler's Turbomachinery Equation:

$$\Delta P = \rho \left( u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1} \right)$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (in **kg/m³**)
- $u_1, u_2$ = Tangential (peripheral/blade) velocities at inlet and outlet (in **m/s**)
- $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = Whirl (tangential) components of absolute velocity at inlet and outlet (in **m/s**)

This is often expressed in the form of a **manometric head** $H_m$ (in metres of fluid):

$$H_m = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "Pressure Developed" topic is the **direct application of Euler's Equation** to a radial flow machine. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to be able to (i) sketch outlet velocity triangles, (ii) relate $V_{w2}$ to blade angle $\beta_2$ and flow velocity $V_{f2}$, and (iii) substitute these into the pressure equation to obtain a numerical answer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a person standing on a rotating **merry-go-round** holding a water hose. As the platform spins, if the person points the hose **with the rotation** (i.e., the water leaves with a tangential push in the direction of spin), the water flies off with much higher speed and pressure than a stationary person could generate. This "extra push" in the direction of rotation is exactly what the impeller blades do to the fluid.

- The **rotating blade** = merry-go-round platform.
- The **tangential component of fluid velocity** $V_w$ = the extra "spin" imparted to the water.
- The **blade speed** $u$ = the speed of the platform edge.
- The product $u_2 V_{w2}$ = the "pressure kick" at the outlet.
- The product $u_1 V_{w1}$ = the "pre-whirl" already present at the inlet (ideally **zero** for maximum pressure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A centrifugal pump "develops" pressure because the impeller spins and flings water outward. The faster it spins, and the more the water exits in the direction of rotation, the more pressure it generates. The mathematical job of the engineer is to compute *how much* pressure is generated based on the velocity triangle geometry.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- Density of water (standard reference): $\rho_{water} = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Specific weight of water: $\gamma_{water} = \rho g = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$
- Pressure developed unit (SI): **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²**
- Head unit: **metres of fluid (m)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic: Why Pressure Develops

The phenomenon of pressure development can be broken into three sequential physical events:

1. **Energy Input Stage (Mechanical → Kinetic):** The electric motor (or turbine driver) supplies torque to the impeller shaft. The impeller blades, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$, carry fluid along with them. This mechanical work input appears as an increase in the absolute velocity of the fluid — specifically its *whirl* (tangential) component $V_w$.

2. **Centrifugal Conversion Stage (Kinetic → Pressure):** As fluid is flung radially outward along the curved blade passage, the high tangential velocity at the impeller tip ($u_2 V_{w2}$) is partially converted into static pressure through the diffusion process in the volute casing (or diffuser). The volute acts like a stationary diffuser — it slows the fluid down, and by Bernoulli's principle, an increase in pressure results.
